session title: Canceled: Audio Directors Roundtable Day 2
description: This roundtable is a forum for attendees to build relationships and networks, to encourage and share best practices, to talk about topics such as, creative ownership, leadership strategies, mentoring, advocating for resources, cross departmental collaboration, the future of game audio and the best ways to build and maintain strong, diverse audio teams.
takeaway: Department heads take away new creative perspectives, strategies for management, solutions to uncommon issues, and a sense of perspective from their peers and team members. Department members take away a better understanding of directorial challenges and creative opportunities that they can bring back to their colleagues.
intended audience: While the discussion is led by audio directors, it revolves around the entire departments experience. The audience consists of any member of the audio department including sound designers of all varieties (junior, senior and technical), audio QA, music and dialog editors, as well as directors themselves.

session title: Canceled: Responsive Physics in VR: Preserving Player Intent in 'UNDERDOGS'
description:
Physics can make VR games feel real, or completely break immersion. In this talk, Yotam Harris, technical director and game designer on UNDERDOGS, shares how his team kept players feeling in control inside a physics-driven combat system.

Rather than forcing realism, they focused on preserving player intent — making sure actions felt immediate, consistent, and aligned with what players meant to do. Topics include input-driven movement that looks physical but remains reliable, directional intent overrides for combat, and techniques to meet player expectations when physics or perception would otherwise interfere.

This session is for gameplay programmers, technical designers, and VR developers building immersive systems where physical simulation and player control need to coexist.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to design physics-heavy gameplay systems that preserve player intent and responsiveness. The talk covers techniques like input-space interpretation, intent correction during physical interactions, and context-driven systems for grabbing, throwing, and movement — all aimed at making simulated characters feel responsive and under control.
intended audience: This talk is for gameplay programmers, technical designers, and VR developers working with physics-based characters. Attendees should be familiar with concepts like physics simulation, force-driven character control, and runtime interaction systems. A working understanding of how rigged bodies behave in real-time engines will help make the most of the session.

session title: Canceled: The Enemy Awaits: Async Patterns for Behavior
description: What do you do when your Junkdog needs to fetch, bark, and dodge punches all at once? This talk explores how one indie team built a custom async framework for AI. While standard solutions worked at first, they quickly broke down under real-world demands, being particularly bad at mid-level decision logic. Using native async/await, the team built a modular, readable system where behaviors are cancellable, composable tasks. The result: more control, less boilerplate, and AI code that's fun to read and write. Walk through real examples from UNDERDOGS and leave with fresh ideas for designing smarter, cleaner game bots.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to model AI using async/await to create modular behaviors. Through examples from UNDERDOGS, they see how this approach replaces convoluted behavior trees with composable tasks that return values, handle exceptions, and support cancellation - unlocking greater clarity, scalability, and control over solutions like behavior trees and FSMs.
intended audience: This talk is for game developers, behavior engineers, and technical designers interested in building smarter, more maintainable behaviors. Attendees should be comfortable reading code and familiar with common AI structures like behavior trees or state machines. No prior knowledge of async/await is required - core concepts are introduced clearly throughout.

session title: Canceled: The Future of Gaming Marketplaces: Personalization, Platforms, and Player-Centric Design
description: Gaming marketplaces are at a crossroads. As player expectations shift and new entertainment paradigms emerge, developers and platform teams must rethink how games are discovered, purchased, and experienced. This talk explores how reduced attention spans, mobile-first behaviors, and community-driven engagement are reshaping marketplace design. We'll dive into building seamless, personalized, and resilient store experiences across Console, Web, PC, and Mobile. Drawing from hands-on experience and industry data, this session offers actionable strategies for empowering creators through optimized publishing flows, navigating platform dependencies, and embracing direct-to-consumer models in light of emerging regulations.
takeaway:
How reduced attention spans and short-form content are influencing marketplace UX.
The role of personalization and community in driving engagement and conversion.
Strategies for building cohesive store experiences across platforms.
Empowering creators through optimized publishing flows and listing tools.
Case studies from leading platforms and games.
The rise of direct-to-consumer models and the impact of regulatory shifts
intended audience: Game developers, product managers, UX designers, platform engineers, and business strategists focused on digital commerce, player engagement, and monetization.

session title: GDC 101
description:
Welcome to the GDC Festival of Gaming! Hosted by GDC's Natalie Sam (Marketing Director) and Sam Warnke (Conference Producer, Curated Program), this essential crash course is designed to help you make the most of your GDC experience. You'll learn strategies that work: choosing the sessions that matter to you, mastering the Festival Hall neighborhoods, and hearing personal tips and practical advice to set you up for success.




From understanding what's new with the GDC Festival of Gaming to navigating Moscone Center like a seasoned pro, this session gives you the confidence and knowledge to approach each day with purpose. Whether you're new to GDC or returning after our evolution, you'll leave knowing exactly how to navigate the week ahead.
takeaway: Attendees will leave equipped with GDC survival skills, strategic session planning techniques, and practical tips to navigate the festival experience successfully from day one.
intended audience: First-time GDC attendees, newcomers to the game development industry, students, and anyone who wants to build a solid foundation for navigating their GDC experience effectively.

session title: Tabletop Free Play & Playtesting Lounge
description:
Grab a board game and let the games begin! Stop by the lounge and check out a game from our Free Play board game library or take a moment to playtest something brand new. Enjoy a little friendly competition and share feedback directly with the designers!

Check out a gallery of the schedule of featured games here (https://unpub.softr.app/gdc-2026).

Brought to you in partnership with Gamescape (https://www.gamescapesf.com/), Golden Gate Gamemakers (https://goldengategamemakers.com/), and Unpub (https://www.unpub.org/)!
location: Tabletop Free Play & Playtesting Lounge, West Hall Level 3
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Adobe Substance 3D State of the Ecosystem (Presented by Adobe)
description:
Stay up to date with the latest innovations across the Adobe Substance 3D ecosystem. Dive into the latest product updates and releases, including a focused look at OpenPBR and how it's shaping the future of material standards. The Substance 3D team will also tease upcoming features, with an exclusive live demo in Substance 3D Painter led by Lucrezia Romeo, Character Artist specializing in video games, offering a firsthand look at what's coming next.
Finally, the team will share a preview of ongoing and upcoming research projects, providing a glimpse into the future direction of the Substance 3D tools and technology.
As an added bonus, the first 350 attendees will get a bag with a custom design by Nikie Monteleone (limited edition – don't miss it!).
takeaway: Get the latest announcements about the Substance 3D suite and find out how the Substance 3D ecosystem can improve your workflow.
intended audience: Game developers, technical artists, visual designers, production engineers or anyone working on workflows, integration, or art production will gain insights into game pipelines and techniques for producing consistent, high-quality visuals efficiently.
speakers: Wes McDermott(Adobe), Jérémie Noguer(Adobe), Lucrezia Romeo(Independent)

session title: Bringing Back a Legend: The Global Multi-Platform Publishing Strategy of Delta Force (Presented by Tencent Games)
description: Delta Force has been a Steam top 10 mainstay, and Team Jade has learned a lot from this journey. The team behind the game built a global publishing pipeline from scratch to cultivate and support a global fanbase. This microtalk session covers the creation of a marketing roadmap to achieve a high impact-to-cost ratio, conducting PR to create a narrative and maintain exposure, and finding, engaging, and retaining content creators to bring organic traction and content to the game and community.
takeaway: Attendees will learn actionable strategies and useful tips for marketing their game, no matter the team or game size. They will understand how to combine product milestones with marketing beats, build a strong PR presence, and create a robust influencer ecosystem.
intended audience: This talk is aimed at marketing managers, PR and community professionals, or anyone interested in marketing a game. It offers valuable insights for attendees of all experience levels, providing both a general overview and practical tips learned from real-world publishing scenarios.
speakers: Jingshen Wei(Team Jade / Tencent Games), Lingjin Meng(Team Jade / Tencent Games), Qinfu Zhao(Team Jade / Tencent Games)

session title: Hitting 60 FPS in UE5 and Keeping It There (Presented by Epic Games)
description:
Epic Games' Matt Oztalay can talk about how to get your project running at your target frame rate until he's hoarse (and in fact has!). Wouldn't it be better for you (and Matt's vocal cords) if you started your project at your target framerate and kept it that way?

In this session, we'll take a journey through Unreal Engine 5's development to see how Epic has been improving performance of the engine; the tools you need to build your project at your target frame rate from the ground up; the features and tools to monitor and diagnose your project's performance; and how you can get back to frame rate if (when) you get off track.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about performance improvements to Unreal Engine features, how to build their projects for 60 fps targets, the Unreal Engine tools to help monitor and diagnose performance, and methods and tools to get their projects back on target.
intended audience: Developers who're targeting high framerates with Unreal Engine projects.

session title: How AI is Reshaping Game Creation (Presented by Google Cloud)
description: Step into the studio with leading game makers who are crafting the next generation of interactive entertainment. In this session, gaming industry leaders will share how AI is augmenting tools, engines, and game dev processes, transforming how game creators build and operate live service games. Go beyond theory and discover how AI is becoming an enabler for game companies, providing a true opportunity to reinvent the very complex game dev process from initial concept art to post-production.
takeaway: Attendees explore how AI augments the full development lifecycle, from automating concept art iteration to optimizing post-production workflows. They gain insights into integrating AI within existing engines to streamline asset creation, enhance live service operations, and reduce technical debt, ultimately transforming complex development pipelines into agile, highly scalable production environments.
intended audience: This session is designed for studio leadership, technical artists, and production managers looking to modernize their development pipelines. It is particularly valuable for those overseeing large-scale live services. Attendees should have a foundational understanding of game engine architecture and current asset production workflows to fully appreciate the implementation strategies discussed.
speakers: Catherine Hawayek(Google Cloud), Jeff Skelton(Electronic Arts), Adam Smith(Unity), Muneki Shimada(Sony Interactive Entertainment)

session title: Toxic Players After Punishment: Rehabilitation and Prosocial Play (Presented by GameSafe)
description:
Toxic behavior remains a persistent challenge in multiplayer games, and most industry responses still rely on detection followed by punishment. While effective at enforcement, this approach often ends with bans or mutes—overlooking opportunities to reduce repeat offenses, retain players, and support long-term community health.

In this talk, the speaker reframes player punishment as a design and community problem rather than a purely moderation task. Drawing on player-centered research, the talk highlights common drivers of toxic behavior and shows how players frequently link their actions to game systems, incentives, and social friction.

The talk then explores where current moderation systems fall short from a live-service perspective and shares practical examples of prosocial community practices already used in games, including peer feedback, mentoring, norm-setting, and constructive designer–player interaction. The session concludes with high-level design principles for building healthier, more resilient multiplayer communities.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to move beyond detection-and-punishment toward rehabilitation of toxic players, drawing on criminology, player research, and real community practices. The session highlights how players interpret punishment, where current systems fall short, and what emerging prosocial practices suggest for rethinking responses to punished players.
intended audience: Session targets game designers, community managers, trust and safety teams, and live-ops leaders working on multiplayer titles. Attendees benefit if they grapple with toxicity, enforcement, or player well-being. Familiarity with basic moderation tools and multiplayer game systems is helpful but not required; no advanced research or statistics background is needed.

session title: Balancing TCGs with Power Sorting
description: People always ask, 'How do you power balance thousands of cards in a Trading Card Game?' It seems like an impossibly daunting task. In this session, you will learn how the best in the business do it, and why your game is actually easier to balance the more cards it has. Learn why algorithmic balance, the most common thing people try first, fails. Instead try comparative power sorting; a fairly simple but powerful technique that leverages some cards in your game to help balance others. Moreso, this technique works for any game that has lots of game objects with complex abilities that need balance, but can't achieve it algorithmically.
takeaway: Learn how to use comparative power sorting to power balance trading card games. Learn about how to create a vanilla curve to get you started. Learn why algorithmic balancing always breaks. Learn some techniques around avoiding dangerous card designs. Learn how to manage and avoid power creep. Plus, get some guided practice costing cards within the session.
intended audience: This is for individuals with some trading card game knowledge. This is probably best suited for those who are working on a TCG or aspire to one day. Also, if you have made a TCG and found it had balance issues, this talk will give you the tools to avoid those pitfalls next time.

session title: 'Battlefield 6': Game Feel is the Message
description:
What makes a first-person shooter game actually great? Join Jac Carlsson, 3C and Game Feel designer on Battlefield's latest title as well as lifelong professional dancer/choreographer, for a dynamic exploration of the art and science of 'game feel'. This session places special emphasis on the responsive, kinetic sensation of 'gun feel' at the core of Battlefield 6's first-person combat.






Game feel isn't a feature you print on the back of the box, it's that elusive experience when player intent and the qualitative aspect of the game's response meld seamlessly. Drawing on his background in movement, Jac delves into how the body and perception is surprisingly central to every moment of play, and why input as a physical mechanic sets games apart as a medium.






This lecture reframes game feel theory: why aligning perception and gameplay, technically and audio-visually, is at the heart of the medium, unlocking new creative thinking in design. Jac demystifies practical game feel design in combat mechanics and procedural animation, breaking down how the team crafted a responsive, tactile, and satisfying experience. He also explores how 'perceived latency' and the 'behavioral quality' in visuals and audio is just as critical as technical performance.






Game feel is a multidisciplinary field. Whether you're an engineer, designer, animator, artist, or sound designer, you'll gain insight into what makes great FPS game feel emerge by design.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with actionable insights on how to think about, recognize, analyze, and directly improve game feel in their own projects. The lecture will deepen their creative understanding of game feel theoretically as well as practically, and why it's the beating heart of modern games. If you care about making responsive, memorable, and meaningful experiences, this talk will change how you see, and design game feel.
intended audience: This session is for designers, animators, engineers and largely any creative in game development interested in game feel, gunplay, and audio-visually satisfying interaction in games with emphasis on first person shooter combat. Attendees should have a basic understanding of first person shooter mechanics, but no deep technical expertise is required, just curiosity about the craft of game feel.

session title: Brilliant Jerks: The Cost of Toxic Performance
description: We've all worked with them: exceptionally skilled individuals whose toxic behavior is excused because of their perceived value. But brilliance isn't worth the cost when it damages team dynamics, erodes trust, and drives away talent. In this talk, Executive Producer Fleur Marty shares a candid, real-world case study and explores the hidden toll of enabling 'brilliant jerks' in game development. Attendees learn how to recognize the warning signs, why tolerating toxic talent harms both culture and outcomes, and how to coach (or decisively address) those who refuse to change. Whether leading a small team or a large studio, this session offers practical tools and a compelling call to action: it's time to stop making excuses and start building healthier, higher-performing teams.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to identify and manage toxic high performers, with actionable strategies for coaching behavioral change, setting accountability frameworks, and navigating terminations when necessary. They'll leave equipped to protect team health, reinforce studio culture, and make courageous decisions that prioritize long-term success over short-term output.
intended audience: This talk is primarily intended for managers and leaders, but will be of value to anyone interested in fostering the best possible team culture.

session title: Bringing Narrative to 'Disney Dreamlight Valley'
description: How do you create a world where Buzz Lightyear can live in harmony with Scar from The Lion King — without one trying to defeat the other? In this session, Josh Labelle, Creative Director of Disney Dreamlight Valley, explores the creative and production strategies behind building a vibrant, IP-rich narrative game. From quest pipelines and dialogue workflows to character voice preservation and unexpected crossover moments, this talk offers a behind-the-scenes look at crafting stories in a shared universe. Ideal for narrative designers and producers navigating complex live service games.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to balance multiple IPs in a live service game, maintain authentic character voices, and build a scalable narrative pipeline that delivers consistent content updates without sacrificing quality or tone.
intended audience: This talk is intended for narrative designers, writers, producers, and studio leads working on IP-based or live service games. Familiarity with narrative pipelines or licensed IP collaboration is helpful but not required.

session title: 'Clair Obscur: Expedition 33': An Audio Postmortem
description: Expedition 33's sound was produced by a rather small and young team. We regularly tackled new challenges for the first time, trying to find the best way to handle everything, often thinking we had no idea what we were doing. But we did it anyway, and in the end, we learned that what matters the most isn't to do everything the right way, but to do it with guts. This presentation will go through the pillars of Expedition 33's sound and explain how we did it, from an artistic, technical and production point-of-view, giving attendees an in-depth and honest view on how the sound of Clair Obscur was produced.
takeaway: Attendees will get an in-depth and honest look into the audio production of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33. They will learn useful production tips and philosophy about how a small and young team can ship a game of this scale, concrete sound design techniques to create sound effects with a strong identity, as well as practical integration tips in Unreal Engine 5.
intended audience: This talk is aimed at any audio professional from any level who wants to learn from an unusual audio production and know more about Unreal Audio, and more broadly about anyone interested in game audio development.
speakers: Raphael Joffres(Sandfall Interactive), Maxance Playez(Sandfall Interactive), Olivier Penchenier(Sandfall Interactive)

session title: From Reliable to Respected: Breaking Free of the Good Job Trap
description:
Many accomplished professionals carry conditioning from school into their careers—praised for being reliable, following rules, and doing excellent work. Initially, this strategy delivers promotions, recognition, and success. But over time, it stops working. Recognition fades, opportunities pass by, and high performers feel stuck, frustrated, and overwhelmed—trapped by their own reliability.

Cecile Hemery explores navigating the 'Good Job Trap': the transition from reliable to respected, from striving to please to becoming a self-led leader who defines success, influence, and impact on their own terms. Drawing from 15 years in gaming leadership and coaching over 250 professionals, she shares why traditional 'good work' strategies fail at senior levels and provides a practical framework—through interactive discussions, polling, and exercises—for breaking free from people-pleasing patterns to rediscover voice, clarity, and greater impact in careers.
takeaway: Attendees will recognize when people-pleasing patterns limit their leadership effectiveness, understand why traditional 'good work' strategies fail at senior levels, and reframe career frustrations as natural growth transitions. They gain clarity on their current leadership posture, learn to shift from external validation to internal recognition, and discover how to define success on their own terms.
intended audience: This workshop targets accomplished professionals interested in authentic leadership development and career growth. Ideal attendees include professionals wanting to evolve beyond people-pleasing patterns, managers seeking to understand and develop team members through leadership transitions, and anyone curious about moving from reliable approaches to confident leadership that creates genuine impact.

session title: Game Funding: Lessons from Outersloth
description: What's it like funding games? This talk will go over the stats, learnings, and reflections on starting and running Outersloth, an indie game fund created from the success of the game Among Us. Starting in 2022, it has successfully funded 20+ games of varying genres. See what happens behind-the-scenes when you submit a game, insight into self-publishing, and a call for more game funds to exist (or at least, for publishers to give better support.) This is NOT a talk about how to perfectly pitch a game or self-publish as an indie - it is a transparent reveal of the journey behind running a games fund, insights we've gained from submissions, and why self-publishing has become more important than ever.
takeaway: This talk is for developers who are interested in learning more about the business 'why' of self-publishing and insights from an indie studio dabbling in games funding. It will serve as a reflection point and guide for other studios that may be interested in funding other games, of varying budgets.
intended audience: No prerequisite knowledge is required for this session.

session title: GDC Encounters: Speed Networking Day 1
description:
**GDC's version of Speed Networking. A fast-track to new contacts, partnerships, and future collaborators. Join us on Monday, Tuesday, and Friday.**

**
**


**Monday, March 9th**

Session 1 | 10:30am-11:30am (60min)

Session 2 | 11:50am-12:50pm (60min)

Session 3 | 1:50pm-2:50pm (60min)

Session 4 | 3:10pm-4:10pm (60min)

Session 5 | 4:30pm-5:30pm (60min)
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: GDC Masters: 50 Years in Games and a Legacy for Creators
description:
'You're sitting with a friend who asks, 'How have you survived 55 years of making games and remained passionate about what we do? What are the top lessons that might help me?''

I'll share a series of answers, with short explanations for each one. Big issues in the industry. Challenges that affect teams. Topics that come up when you look in the mirror. Use the answers that feel right. Discard the approaches that aren't for you.
takeaway: Understand what it takes to survive and grow through fifty years of industry shifts—and why the fundamentals of game development have remained surprisingly constant since the 1970s.
intended audience:
Game developers seeking perspective on surviving -- and thriving through -- the industry's first five decades

Anyone interested in the origins of the game industry and their relevance to modern development

session title: Machine Learning Forum: Ask the Experts
description:
The GDC Machine Learning Summit Chairs have designed this Forum session for you. Why? To give you a place to ask any questions you might have about Machine Learning AI in the Video Games Industry. How? By gathering eminent panelists that will be on stage, and having a moderator to bounce questions around. This 60-minute Forum will start with the introduction of our panelists, the moderator will then kick off the session with a few questions while the audience gathers around the mics made available in the room, and then give priority to any questions from the audience. Come to talk about Simulation, Content Creation, Tools, Pipelines, Processes, Operations, Services, Advancement from Academia, and more!

This Forum is all yours—All You Can Ask!
takeaway: Although the moderator will have questions prepared for the panelists, priority will be given to the audience. This Forum will give attendees a good idea of what the hot questions of the moment are, what the current and future trends in Machine Learning look like, and offer attendees the opportunity to have their very own questions answered.
speakers: Martin Singh-Blom(Embark Studios), Alessandro Sestini(Electronic Arts, SEED), Alexis Rolland(Ubisoft, La Forge), Alexandre Moufarek(Google DeepMind), Zhen Zhai(Blizzard Entertainment), Julien Merceron(Orion Productions)

session title: Matchmaker as Code: A Constraint-Based Approach to Cross-Platform Multiplayer
description:
Behind the cross-play curtain, many pitfalls lurk.
Matchmaking implementation, in particular, can quickly become unwieldy without the right foundation to support the added complexity.
Join this session to learn from the experience of Gameloft's Asphalt team in adapting from a rigid, legacy matchmaker to a fully flexible solution.
The talk will highlight the challenges involved in supporting common modern features such as parties, teams, and cross-play filters in a fast-paced iteration environment, and how a matchmaker-as-code solution helped us stay on top.
We will discuss insights into graph-based candidate evaluation algorithms and how they can improve and simplify the logic, as well as proven approaches for fast, safe iteration in production.
takeaway: Attendes learn strategies to solving complex matchmaking challenges by leveraging a Matchmaker as Code approach to tailor systems to unique design needs and discover practical approaches for safely evolving matchmaking logic within the fast-paced, ever-changing demands of a live multiplayer environment.
intended audience: This session is ideal for developers and systems designers, preferably with some experience in matchmaking design/implementation, looking to modernize matchmaking systems for live multiplayer games with complex and changing feature sets. We will be discussing some algorithmical details.

session title: Modernizing the Rendering of 'Minecraft'
description:
Minecraft's charming, minimalistic art style has always been one of the aspects that defines the brand, but over time we've seen a growing desire from players for evolutions to the graphical capabilities of the game. While third-party mods are available on Minecraft Java to achieve these visual experiences, enhanced graphics were unavailable to most Minecraft players until recently with the release of Vibrant Visuals: a cross platform, 100% real-time, physically based evolution of one of the most successful games of all time.

This session explores the team's journey since 2018, discussing the development of the RenderDragon engine, the evolution of the game's art pipeline to PBR, and the development of Vibrant Visuals. It aims to share our learnings from when we upgraded our graphics engine, how we shipped a high-fidelity experience across such a wide variety of devices, and how we openly developed the upgrade with our players.
takeaway: Attendees will get details on how Minecraft manages its rendering and how it has evolved to facilitate Vibrant Visuals. They will also learn about the trade-offs and optimizations we had to make to achieve our performance targets on a wide variety of platforms.
intended audience: Graphics programmers, engine architects or technical artists. This talk will cover technical details of rendering architecture, shading pipelines, and cross-platform considerations for scaling across a variety of hardware.

session title: Thriving Players Workshop
description: This workshop will feature speakers Weszt Hart, Riot Games, and Natasha Miller, Blizzard Entertainment, leading attendees through identifying common risks or roadblocks to prosocial interaction in games, give best practices on mitigating the risks in a way that increases social reward, and provide design/research tools to help increase the likelihood of prosocial interactions occurring in your player community.
takeaway: Attendees should leave the workshop with best practices in social design, as well as some practical tools and applications they can use at their game studios when designing social systems for thriving.
intended audience: Anyone interested in improving the social experience of their players and game community would benefit from this workshop. However, designers and games researchers of any level will likely benefit the most from the workshop.
speakers: Natasha Miller(Blizzard Entertainment), Matthew Soeth(Thriving in Games Group), Weszt Hart(Riot Games), Josh Chapdelaine(Thriving in Games Group), Ariana Barcenas Kanemoto(Blizzard Entertainment)

session title: Tools Roundtable Day 1: Engineering
description:
The Tools Technology Roundtables' focus on discussion of best practices, novel techniques, and technical trade-offs for building game development tools and services. Game development tools are constantly evolving, so come to share what works (and what doesn't) at your studio, and listen to others discuss how they keep their creative teams producing awesome games.


Day 1: 'Engineering', focuses on tools programming, testing, deployment, frameworks, scripting, automation, extensibility, diagnostics, and telemetry.

Day 2: 'Assets' focuses on export/import, interchange, persistence, collaboration, search and organization, development models (branching and/or trunking), revision control, and outsourcing.

Day 3: 'Build' focuses on processing assets through to the game in all its forms: build systems, caching, distribution, scalability, optimization, disc mastering and patching.
takeaway:
A better understanding of current techniques and trade-offs for building game development tools, plug-ins, pipelines, and services.
Advice in selection between various techniques, languages, and frameworks.
Advice in handling increasingly complex teams, asset pipelines, and requirements.
intended audience: Engineers, technical artists, and technical producers.

session title: Uplifting Latine Game Devs Here, There, and Everywhere Roundtable (Presented by the IGDA)
description: Uplifting Latine Game Devs Here, There, and Everywhere is a solutions-oriented roundtable focused on actionable ways to support Latine developers regardless of where you are. This session will highlight everyday actions, large and small, that can expand opportunities for Latine developers, from amplifying their voices in online spaces, to mentoring, to tangible ways to give visibility to game projects. Attendees will share experiences, explore scenarios, and walk away with meaningful tools and resources to support the growth of Latine game development globally.
takeaway: Participants will leave with practical, tangible ways to uplift Latine game developers in their own communities, at their companies, and in global gaming spaces.
intended audience: This session is intended for anyone interested in building a more inclusive and equitable games industry, including developers, managers, publishers, educators, and allies.

session title: UX Mobile Porting Challenges in 'Prince of Persia: The Lost Crown' Case Study
description:
The porting team approach to adapting Prince of Persia: The Lost Crown on Mobile.
The team identified early on the challenges linked to a platformer and combat metroidvania for Mobile. Alexis Denancé presents how the team approached this puzzle and implemented various features & adaptations to enrich the game experience and make it enjoyable to a larger audience while delivering on the true HD experience that brought Prince of Persia: The Lost Crown to win the Innovation in Accessibility Game Award in 2024.
He details at length all the 3Cs adaptation, additional features, as well as the Free Trial format and Playtest learnings during production.
takeaway:
In this lecture the audience will learn :
- how to approach the porting of complex controls scheme from PC/Consoles to Mobile.
- implement specific features tailored for to adapt your game from HD to Mobile.
intended audience:
This lecture is intended for teams planning to port a PC/Consoles game on Mobile, or considering it.
More specifically, Game Designers and UX Designers.

session title: VFX Roundtable Day 1
description:
On Day 1, Ellie and Jason will facilitate the roundtable covering general topics and revisit topics from the VFX Summit earlier in the week.

The roundtable on Day 2 will focus on VFX through an ARTISTIC LENS. Jason will focus the discussion on your questions concerning the artistic aspects of VFX. This may include art education (as students or veterans), art direction, or whatever else comes up - see you there!

On Day 3, the roundtable will give a TECHNICAL LENS for creating VFX. Ellie will guide the conversation through such as graph based effect authoring, effect lighting and optimization, new mesh based techniques, VR/AR/XR and/or whatever attendees want to go deep on.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with an increased awareness of visual effects techniques, approaches and technology, as well as a greater insight into how different artists and studios approach visual effects challenges.
intended audience: This is for visual effects artists, technical artists, students, graphics programmers and anyone else with an interest in the art and creation of visual effects.
speakers: Jason Keyser(VFX Apprentice), Ellie Koorlander(Creative Assembly)

session title: XR in 2026: The State of the Industry + Community Networking
description:
Step into the future of Extended Reality (XR) with this dynamic session that combines an insightful presentation on the state of the XR industry in 2026 with an engaging networking opportunity for professionals shaping the future of VR, AR, and Mixed Reality.

The first half of the session will deliver a comprehensive industry outlook, exploring the future of XR across key areas such as emerging platforms, market trends, and the evolving ecosystem. Gain actionable insights into monetization strategies, retention techniques, and distribution models that are driving ROI in XR development and business. Learn how industry leaders are navigating challenges and capitalizing on opportunities in this rapidly growing space.

The second half transitions into a vibrant networking experience, where developers, business leaders, and innovators can connect, share ideas, and collaborate on the next wave of XR advancements. Whether you're focused on gaming, enterprise solutions, or creative applications, this session is your chance to build meaningful relationships and exchange knowledge with peers who are shaping the future of immersive technology.

Join us to stay ahead of the curve, discover new opportunities, and strengthen your place in the XR ecosystem.

session title: From the Ground Up: Rethinking Quality in Games
description: Quality can no longer be just about finding bugs. As games grow larger and systems more complex, the way we think about Quality must shift. In this talk, we will share how teams at Ubisoft are changing the game by blending tech and testing, upskilling roles, and using automation and data to focus on what really matters. Expect real stories, honest lessons, and a clear look at how we are raising the bar for modern Quality teams. If you are wondering what Quality teams should look like today and where it's headed next, this talk is for you.
takeaway: This session explores how Ubisoft's Quality teams are building future-ready workflows using automation, data, and Gen AI. Attendees will see how evolving team structures, early investments in tech capabilities, and targeted upskilling are driving measurable gains in testing efficiency, collaboration, and development outcomes across live game projects.
intended audience: This session is useful for Producers, Quality Leaders, and Developers working on AAA or live games who want to strengthen their team's approach to Quality and build long-term success into their workflows.

session title: Lunch and Entertainment in the Gardens
description:
Join us for lunch and entertainment in the GDC Gardens.

Stone Stage entertainment presented by MAGWEST (https://www.magwest.org/):


• 11:00 AM - 12:30 PM: Ultra Combo
• 12:50 - 2:00 PM: 88bit






Review the full week's schedule here (https://gdconf.com/lunch-and-entertainment-in-the-gardens/).
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Player-Oriented Monetization: Curating Monetization as Boutique Experience (Presented by Tencent Games)
description: Monetization, especially in free-to-play titles, is a hugely important aspect of Live Service revenue generation. This session focuses on the monetization ecosystem and content design in 'Delta Force', specifically the foundational logic behind its cross-platform content structure and its innovative attempt to create its gameplay-integrated monetization, which benefits players' retention. At the same time, the 'Delta Force' team have also gained some novel insights through seasonal content cadence optimization. With some concrete examples, we will demonstrate our approach to establishing our IP value, involving players directly into the game and fueling social media virality.
takeaway: Participants will learn actionable insights into how 'Delta Force', as a GaaS game, implemented framework planning for its commercial architecture and this framework was optimised. The session will also provide our approaches to consistently deliver novelty and delight to players by being reactive with cosmetic content production.
intended audience: This session is ideal for Game Designers, Systems Designers, Character Artists, Hard-surface Artists and other roles related to game content development, particularly those interested in FPS ecosystem and content pipelines, requiring no specialized prerequisite knowledge.

session title: AI-Powered Anti-Cheat Operations in Extraction Shooter Games (Presented by GameSafe)
description:
With the global success of Tencent's extraction shooter games, security threats have become increasingly severe. Unlike traditional tactical shooters, this genre combines diverse gameplay elements such as offensive engagements, stealthy looting, and quick extraction, along with an integrated economy system—leading to more complex security challenges. In response, Tencent's Game Security team has continuously leveraged large-scale replay data, player risk profiles, and AI models to drive technological upgrades, building a more comprehensive, in-depth, and real-time security protection framework to safeguard game operations.
This session will share innovative results from practical implementation.
takeaway: Attendees will gain critical insights into the unique security risks of extraction shooter games and their AI-powered solutions. These insights will empower teams to proactively develop and deploy defensive capabilities against potential threats.
intended audience: Game industry professionals, developers and operators.

session title: Building the Dark Fantasy Materials of 'DOOM: The Dark Ages' (Presented by Adobe)
description: Explore the material creation pipeline behind DOOM: The Dark Ages as Kat Tamburello guides you through the procedural systems and artistic strategies that enabled a small team to build a dark fantasy world. This session examines how the team built an extensive library of tiling materials using Substance-based workflows to support the dynamic damage reveal system, rapid iteration on multiple biomes, and the massive scale of the Dragon and Atlan map. Attendees will gain insight into how these techniques streamlined production, ensured visual consistency, and empowered the team to meet the ambitious scope and complexity demanded by the world of DOOM: The Dark Ages.
takeaway: Learn how a small team used Substance-based workflows to craft extensive tiling materials. Key insights include rapid iteration, and efficient production techniques that supported the game's massive scale and visual consistency.
intended audience: Game developers, technical artists, visual designers, production engineers or anyone working on workflows, integration, or art production will gain insights into game pipelines and techniques for producing consistent, high-quality visuals efficiently.

session title: Tuning Your Unreal Engine 5 Game for Mobile Deployment (Presented by Epic Games)
description:
Mobile devices span a wide range of capabilities, from budget options to flagship models. How do you build a single Unreal Engine 5 project that runs well across this spectrum?

This talk covers the settings, features, and workflows needed to scale performance and quality across iOS and Android. We'll explore device profiles and scalability systems, quality presets and console variable tuning, mobile-specific optimizations, and testing and profiling workflows to validate performance across devices. Learn how to configure your project to scale across the mobile ecosystem.
takeaway: Attendees will learn device profile configuration, scalability quality levels, mobile feature enablement strategies, and testing workflows for validating performance across iOS and Android devices.
intended audience: Developers working on mobile games, including those currently using Unreal Engine 5 and those considering it for mobile deployment.

session title: Actionable Intelligence: Practical Advice for Game Developers Working with AI Under the SAG-AFTRA IMA
description: A group of industry veterans with expertise and knowledge on the SAG-AFTRA Interactive contract give practical advice on how to engage with AI under the SAG-AFTRA Interactive Agreement.
takeaway: This talk will empower developers and performers with the knowledge they need to safely engage with AI and work under the SAG-AFTRA Interactive Media Agreement.
intended audience: The talk is intended for game developers and performers who have questions about how to work with AI under the SAG-AFTRA Interactive Media Agreement.
speakers: Bo Crutcher(Blindlight), Jennifer Hale(Independent), William Chip" Beaman(Halp Network)

session title: Creating a Culture of Sustainability in Tabletop Games
description: Creators in the tabletop industry hold the keys to reducing the carbon footprint of board games. Learn how board games negatively impact the climate crisis and what designers, publishers, and manufacturers can do to reduce their environmental impact. Along with the ways that we can change the culture of sustainability for ourselves and our players. We will discuss both practical and idealist methods to challenge the status quo of tabletop game production and distribution.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about sustainable production methods and earth-friendlier materials for tabletop games and game merch, as well as ways we as an industry can improve the culture of sustainability around physical games.
intended audience: The target audience of this session is tabletop game designers, developers, and publishers who are interested in decreasing the carbon footprint of board game production. While some practical experience in tabletop game production will provide added context, no prerequisite knowledge is necessary.

session title: Etudes and Actors: Owlcat's Tooling for Highly Branched Narrative
description:
Owlcat Games is famous for making sprawling RPGs with tons of player choice and reactivity. Such games often have hundreds or even thousands of individual variables to represent current narrative state: quests, previous decisions, etc. Essentially a bunch of global variables managed with ad-hoc logic.

This approach scales poorly, and so we designed a better system, which organizes game state into a hierarchy of sub-states that are automatically turned on and off while taking into account potential conflicts.

This talk will share the experience of building and using this 'Etude'-based system, and show examples of using it in both Unity and Unreal engines.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about a novel approach to handling narrative state, along with implementation details — enough to implement their own version, or mine this approach for ideas.
intended audience: Narrative and quest designers, gameplay and tool programmers. Some technical details will be given, but no specific programming background is required to understand the main points.

session title: From Beautiful to Playable: Texture Compression Secrets Every Game Artist Should Know
description: This session demystifies export formats, engine import settings, and platform-specific texture handling - providing practical guidance for maximizing visual quality while keeping performance and memory in check. Attendees learn how to avoid common pitfalls, like texture bleeding and bloated builds, and explore advanced techniques such as gradient mapping and Signed Distance Fields. Whether targeting high-end PCs or mobile devices, this talk equips artists and technical developers with the insights needed to optimize texture pipelines and ensure stunning visuals don't come at a hidden cost. Real-world examples and actionable workflows make this a must-attend for anyone involved in game art production.
takeaway: Get the scoop on texture compression and when to use each type like a pro. You'll pick up slick tips on tackling texture issues, saving memory, and making materials pop so their art looks great and runs smoothly across any platform.
intended audience: This session is for any artist working in Unreal—character artists, FX, UI, environment artists, and more. Anyone importing textures into Unreal, no matter their specialty, will learn how to avoid pitfalls and optimize assets for better visuals and performance across all platforms. No advanced engine knowledge required!

session title: Generative Recommender Post-Trained by Reinforcement Learning in Video Games
description: The exponential growth of online gaming generates vast amounts data but also presents unique challenges for recommendation systems—including fluctuating player interests, complex social graphs, and diverse content types. This lecture introduces a novel generative recommendation framework built on large language models and enhanced by reinforcement learning (RL). Designed specifically for gaming ecosystems, it unifies multiple recommendation tasks - such as content, product, social suggestions - into a single architecture, significantly reducing engineering overhead while enabling rapid feature deployment. The RL post-training enables near real-time adaptation to evolving player behavior, optimizing long-term engagement and experience diversity. The solution not only supports dynamic configuration optimization within gameplay but also enhances content delivery across game communities. It demonstrates substantial improvements in retention and engagement compared to current online solutions. Finally, the talk will address scalability, latency, and fairness challenges encountered in real-world deployment.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to design a unified, generative recommender system using large language models and reinforcement learning (RL), specifically tailored for gaming environments. They will explore concrete strategies for handling diverse recommendation scenarios - such as game configuration, content feed recommendation, and social suggestions - within this unified architecture. The system dynamically adapts via RL to optimize key metrics like retention and engagement, while also addressing production challenges such as latency and bias at scale.
intended audience: This session targets platform architects, ML engineers, data scientists, and product leads building recommendation systems for online games. Foundational knowledge of machine learning concepts is recommended. Game designers seeking deeper insights into advanced personalization mechanics (friend systems, UGC discovery, dynamic advertising) will also benefit significantly.

session title: Optimizing a Large, Time-Critical Problem: Occlusion Culling at 'Roblox'
description: How do you occlusion cull fully dynamic, user-generated content? We don't know what parts of the scene are static, since we free creators to change anything at any time. We want to empower amateur authors to bring their visions to life, not to burden them with constructing occlusion geometry that they must redo whenever they make other changes to their scenes. We present how we solved this problem at Roblox, for fully dynamic occlusion culling with no manual authoring. We also discuss spatiotemporal antialiasing in software rasterization, limiting software rasterization to where it helps, and avoiding multithreaded stalls. We show how our feedback loops automatically adjust to unknown scenes and hardware.
takeaway: You will see an example of how thinking differently about a problem can give you a better solution. You will also see a way to add spatiotemporal antialiasing to software rasterization at very little cost, methods to spend rasterization time only where it helps, and examples of modifying multithreaded code to avoid stalls.
intended audience: This talk is for programmers needing to write time critical code to solve large problems on a wide range of hardware. The scenario will be most familiar to graphics engineers, but is easily understood by all. There is no graphics API usage; the methods and solutions shown are entirely on the CPU.

session title: Recent Bets & Outcomes from Supercell's 'Clash Royale'
description: After a decade live, Clash Royale has experienced record-breaking highs and dramatic lows. In this session, Aleksandar Marković (Head of Clash Royale) and James Back (Game Design Lead) look back at the past five years with an honest focus on what the team tried – what worked, and what didn't. They'll walk through key updates, experiments, mistakes, and course corrections from 2021–2025, sharing how bets around novelty, accessibility, and streamlining shaped the game's direction, from steep declines to its strongest year yet. Aleksandar and James outline the team's three big ideas that now guide their approach, while acknowledging the challenges of evolving a long-running live game and the continual learning that comes with it.
takeaway: Attendees leave with actionable lessons on three themes: finding the overlap between what players want and what works for the game; simplifying to make space to grow the audience; and sticking to a multi-year strategy over long iterations. They see failures and fixes across content, core systems, economy, modes, and more.
intended audience: Designed for teams working on core, competitive free-to-play games. It suits attendees familiar with live game systems, economies, and modes. Prior knowledge of Clash Royale is not required; the focus stays on principles drawn from content, simplification, and long-horizon platform strategy.

session title: Six Figures in 30 Days: How We Turned a Funding Crisis into a Kickstarter Success
description:
We hadn't intended to ever run a Kickstarter campaign, but we came to the conclusion that we needed some temporary funding and some additional proof points that we were on the right track.

Niina will take you through the seven biggest lessons she learned executing a Kickstarter campaign in order to:
-Prove market demand for our product
-Create a sense of momentum for our product and company
-Earn some quick operating money without the full process of a formal round of investment funding
-Increase customer awareness for Stars Reach, i.e., the sales funnel.
takeaway: Arm yourself with the basics of running a successful Kickstarter campaign and which key factors need your careful attention. Prepare in advance to set yourself up for success. Understand your minimum and optimal preparation time, campaign duration, and post campaign steps.
intended audience: This presentation benefits indie developers and potential indie developers who might have to look after their own marketing launch. Also, helpful to beginner-to-intermediate Marketing professionals.

session title: The Tension: Designing for Discomfort in 'Wanderstop'
description: Wanderstop exists both within and without Cozy Game genre conventions. It contains beautiful landscapes, funny characters and an easy tea making simulation. It does not make the player feel productive or at ease. Lead designer Steve Margolin talks through the design process balancing narrative and mechanics and navigating the tension between expectation and reality. They share anecdotes from development and breakdown the many mechanics that make the player feel uncomfortable. Discomfort is an often maligned design goal, going against general best practice of 'finding the fun.' In this talk, Steve advocates for the value of discomfort and its place in the designer's toolkit.
takeaway: Using lessons learned from the design process of Wanderstop, attendees will come away from this talk with a greater understanding of discomfort as a design goal, when and how to effectively use it and what benefits it can have on a project.
intended audience: Game designers of all disciplines and experience will benefit from this talk exploring a divisive gameplay goal. Additionally, anyone interested in learning how to navigate audience expectations and genre conventions will find insight. Playing Wanderstop is not required to benefit from the talk.

session title: Welcome Back: Updating the Returning Player Experience in 'Diablo Immortal'
description: Looking at the returning player experience in Diablo Immortal, a mobile game with over three years of content, this talk dives into how the team identifies the biggest pain points our players face, develops a strategy based on those pain points, and designs within that strategy to alleviate the problems. We will also look at how the impact of those decisions is measured, how that helps validate the design, and enables the team to start the cycle over. Through the lens of design, this presentation showcases the importance of utilizing data to craft an experience that will support your returning players' needs.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how the Diablo Immortal team approaches the returning player experience and how data is utilized to flesh out solutions to problems once they have been identified. This is useful for live service teams and games, especially teams looking to understand how to cater to returning players.
intended audience: This talk is for anyone interested in understanding how returning player issues can be identified and solved in live service games. The focus will be largely on the design aspect of a returning player experience, but can be useful for all areas of development.

session title: You Are Not a Lawyer: 20 Legal Hacks You Would Know If You Were
description: Before attendees 'Ask Video Game Lawyers Anything,' this session equips indie and emerging studios with the top legal lessons they wish they had known earlier. Experienced games lawyers, who advise studios from the inside and as external counsel, walk through common pitfalls around contracts, hiring across borders, IP ownership and assignments, record-keeping, marketing claims, taxes, and more. Using real-world examples, they show how small decisions in the early stages of a project can create expensive problems later, or avoid them entirely. The session also demystifies when and how to seek legal advice, how to prepare for those conversations, and what can be handled in-house versus with outside counsel. Spot legal risks early and handle issues proactively, before they become crises.
takeaway: Attendees gain a concrete checklist of legal red flags for indie and small studios, practical guidance on contracts, IP ownership, cross-border hiring, taxes, and truthful marketing, and a clearer sense of when and how to engage legal counsel so they prevent problems instead of paying to clean them up.
intended audience: This session targets indie and small studio founders, producers, and leads involved in deals, hiring, or IP decisions. No legal background is required, but basic familiarity with game development pipelines and business operations helps. Anyone curious about making legal issues less intimidating and more manageable benefits from attending.
speakers: Ryan Black(DLA Piper (Canada) LLP), Yan Perng(Blizzard Entertainment), Angelo Alcid(Microsoft Corp.), Brandon Huffman(Odin Law and Media)

session title: Metaperf: Industrializing Performance Optimization for 'Delta Force' (Presented by Tencent Games)
description:
The Delta Force development team tackles the challenge of cross-platform performance optimization for highly complex, large-scale gameplay—particularly achieving 32v32 real-time battles on mobile devices.
Their in-house tool, Metaperf, overcomes the limitations of traditional instrumentation by combining hybrid sampling and AI-driven attribution to deliver high-frequency, low-overhead diagnostics across PC, mobile, and console platforms.
The system automatically classifies stuttering patterns through frame-by-frame analysis and intelligent correlation, precisely identifying bottlenecks in high-concurrency and large-scene environments.
Validated over 30 months of production, Metaperf has processed 15,000+ game sessions and identified 3,000+ performance defects.
By empowering QA and engineering teams with expert-level analytical capabilities, it establishes an industrialized, intelligent workflow for performance optimization—setting a new benchmark for large-scale, cross-platform game development.
takeaway:
Attendees gain insight into building a unified cross-platform performance profiling pipeline integrating data from PC, mobile, and console.
Through Delta Force case studies, they learn low-overhead hybrid sampling, AI-driven stutter attribution, and data processing methods that transform manual profiling into intelligent, industrial-scale performance optimization.
intended audience: This session is intended for performance engineers, technical directors, and QA leads who work on large-scale PC and console titles. Attendees should have basic knowledge of profiling and CPU/GPU performance tools. Engine developers and tool designers seeking to build scalable, data-driven performance analysis pipelines will gain the most from this talk.
speakers: Mingtong Chen(Team Jade / Tencent Games), Qingyu Tan(Team Jade / Tencent Games)

session title: Delta Force Audio: Building a Unified Soundscape Across Modes, Platforms, and Live Service (Presented by Tencent Games)
description: This session shares practical audio design experiences from Delta Force. It explores how the team implemented the 'Sound-as-Information' philosophy as a core design principle,and used it to enable the tactical dynamics and gameplay loop for the Extraction Mode. The talk details methods for optimizing audio for 64-player battles on mobile, shares insights on creating authentic Chinese voice-over for internationally-themed characters, and composing music for monetization features that enhance emotional resonance. Attendees will gain valuable strategies for audio development in multi-platform, live-service games.
takeaway: Attendees will learn the methodology behind a audio system that turns sound into core gameplay intelligence. They will also gain practical strategies for multi-platform audio optimization and authentic voice localization for global character rosters, applicable to their own live-service projects.
intended audience: This session targets sound designers, technical sound designers, audio directors, and professionals working on multi-platform game audio. Basic knowledge of audio middleware like Wwise is helpful, but no advanced programming skills are required.
speakers: Xiangli Wang(Tencent Games), Xiaorong Chen(Tencent Games)

session title: Four Artists Use Substance 3D to Texture 'Clair Obscur: Expedition 33' (Presented by Adobe)
description: Sandfall Interactive's Lead Character Artist Alan Reynaud and Environment Artist Mathieu Costat will share how they approached texturing Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 with only a small, predominantly junior team. They will explain how they managed to texture a large number of assets, from main characters to a wide range of environment props, in a short amount of time, all while ensuring consistent artistic direction. They will also present a live demo showing how simple tips and fluid collaboration across departments can save time and elevate a project's overall quality. Finally, they will provide insights into the studio's workflows used for the game and take a look at the game's latest update.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how a small team efficiently textured a large volume of characters and environment assets while maintaining a cohesive artistic style. The session offers practical tips and insights on streamlined collaboration in game production.
intended audience: Game developers, technical artists, visual designers, production engineers or anyone working on workflows, integration, or art production will gain insights into game pipelines and techniques for producing consistent, high-quality visuals efficiently.
speakers: Alan Reynaud(Sandfall Interactive ), Mathieu Costat(Sandfall Interactive)

session title: From Independence to Intervention: What Players Really Want from Community Safety (Presented by GameSafe)
description:
The gaming industry's evolution from products to platforms has fundamentally transformed player safety expectations, yet current 'safety by design' approaches often miss the mark. Recent research reveals a sophisticated paradox: players simultaneously demand high autonomy and extensive oversight from leaders, prioritizing safety over privacy. This challenges the long-held assumption that gaming communities prefer independence over intervention.

This panel examines how player expectations align with current industry safety approaches through the Trust & Safety Triforce framework: Policy, Tooling, and Education. It draws on real-world implementation examples, including evolving community standards, player verification and trust tiers, large-scale voice moderation, and moderator training programs. Attendees learn practical lessons tested across Discord, Call of Duty, Minecraft, and more to design safety systems that protect players without undermining their autonomy.
takeaway: Attendees will gain concrete frameworks for transforming trust and safety from reactive compliance into proactive competitive advantage, including implementation strategies for consistent reporting, tooling, actionable guidelines, safety-by-design principles, and moderator support systems that measurably improve player retention and lifetime value.
intended audience: Studio executives, community leadership, trust and safety professionals, product managers, and anyone involved in player experience design. No prerequisite knowledge required, though familiarity with trust and safety and community management challenges will enhance understanding of advanced implementation strategies.
speakers: Rachel Kowert(University of Cambridge), Mike Pappas(Modulate), Savannah Badalich(Discord), Maria Tamellini(GamerSafer), Matthew Soeth(Thriving in Games Group (TIGG))

session title: The Future of Playable Worlds with Google DeepMind (Presented by Google Cloud)
description: Google is redefining the gaming landscape by bridging the gap between foundational AI research and enterprise-scale cloud infrastructure. By integrating Google DeepMind's breakthrough models with Google Cloud's robust gaming stack, we empower developers to build 'living games' that are more immersive, efficient to produce, and profitable to scale.
takeaway: Attendees will explore Google DeepMind's foundational models including SIMA and Genie. Learn to deploy large-scale neural networks in game engines, automate environment generation, and use agent-based AI to create scalable, responsive, and infinitely extensible gameplay experiences.
intended audience: This session is intended for lead engineers, AI researchers, and technical architects interested in the practical application of cutting-edge neural models. It suits professionals aiming to bridge the gap between research and production. Prerequisites include familiarity with cloud computing architectures and a basic understanding of machine learning model deployment strategies.
speakers: Ivan Ramirez(Google Cloud), Targoon Siripanichpong(Google Cloud), Jamie Pourturk(Google Cloud), Alexandre Moufarek(Google DeepMind), Piermaria Mendolicchio(Google DeepMind)

session title: Game UX Mindset Masterclass: Principles and Methods
description: Making fun and engaging video games is hard, and with thousands of games being released every year, the competition is fierce. Although there is no known recipe for crafting successful games, some clear ingredients do exist, which game developers can use to make their own successful recipe. User experience (UX) and cognitive science disciplines combined provide developers with those ingredients --across all types of games, platforms used, or audiences targeted-- to guide them along the development process. Experiencing a game happens in the player's mind. This is why understanding the human brain (especially its limitations) while it is perceiving and interacting with a game is paramount to accomplishing your developers' goals faster and more efficiently. This workshop covers the main characteristics and limitations of the brain (perception, attention, memory), learning principles and how to apply them in games to onboard players efficiently, usability principles to make your game easy to learn, engageability principles (motivation, emotion, gameflow) to make your game engaging, and will touch on UX strategy, methods, and mindset (including ethical considerations).
takeaway:
Understand the main characteristics and limitations of the human brain (in terms of perception, attention, memory, motivation, and emotion), and how they impact game development.
Learn what makes a game engaging (in terms of motivation, emotion, and game flow) and how to foster these components in your game.
Have a clear and concrete understanding of what having a UX mindset and developing a UX strategy entail, from production pipeline to ethical considerations.
intended audience: Anyone can benefit from knowing more about the brain and about user experience. This Masterclass will however benefit designers, artists, gameplay/UI programmers, producers, and junior UX practitioners even more.

session title: Player Trust as the Foundation of Retention, Engagement, and Long-Term Success
description: In live games, trust determines whether players feel safe to stay, speak up, and believe the team has their best interests at heart. Engagement, retention, and sentiment don't exist in isolation — they are downstream from trust. Community management isn't support; it's foundational infrastructure for a healthy live game.
takeaway:
A practical playbook for building, maintaining, and ensuring trust through the design lifecycle
A shortlist of observable trust signals beyond sentiment scores
intended audience: Anyone interested in improving the trust of their players and game community would benefit from this session. However, designers, devleopers, and community managers of any level will likely benefit the most from the session.

session title: Animation (Adjacent) Microtalks
description: How could you, as an animator, communicate your needs to a different department more clearly? What do animation-adjacent departments wish animators knew about their work in order to facilitate that communication? These animation microtalk speakers will present the questions they wish animators would ask to make day to day collaboration smoother and expectations clearer.
takeaway: While targeted at animators, attendees from all disciplines should be able to obtain a fresh slice of understanding for each representative discipline's needs.
intended audience: The intended audience is primarily animators of all levels, and any other developer who is curious about this kind of glimpse into one of the representative departments.
speakers: Euna Park(That's No Moon), Shayna Moon(CD Projekt RED), Mark Slutsky(Compulsion Games), Kathleen McGovern(Santa Monica Studio), Brendan Russert(Riot Games), Moira Rausch(Independent)

session title: Ask Video Game Lawyers Anything (Legal AMA): This Normally Costs Money! Day 1
description:
This roundtable session allows you to ask any burning legal questions* you may have from a group of industry lawyers. This is a group of video game enthusiasts who happen to be lawyers (or, sure, vice versa). They met at video game and lawyer conventions (yes, video game lawyer conventions are a thing) and have been speaking for years about legal topics near and dear to their favourite digital pastime. The topics to be discussed if nobody's brave: influencer advertising; loot boxes; IP law; corp stuff; the rise of AI; web3 (if we have to); trademarks and other intellectual property; but we'd love to answer YOUR questions and give out legal info on video game and law topics.

* The speakers can't give specific legal advice; and aren't insured for that! Use hypotheticals, people! Of course there's a disclaimer here... we are lawyers, after all.
takeaway: An engaging and entertaining look inside the discussions video game lawyers have with clients, as well as an appreciation of the issues that arise in games. Above all else, talking to lawyers can be scary and we want to demystify that! Getting good legal advice is critical to games success.
intended audience: Anyone interested in the games business! We put these on to make interacting with lawyers approachable and non-scary; we hope you come with legal questions that have been on your mind!
speakers: Ryan Black(DLA Piper (Canada) LLP), Yan Perng(Blizzard Entertainment), Angelo Alcid(Microsoft Corp.), Brandon Huffman(Odin Law and Media)

session title: 'Clair Obscur: Expedition 33': Delivering a Wide Scope of Features & Content When You Only Have Four Programmers
description: Starting as a one-person effort, the programming team for Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 then ramped up to four members over the last three years of production. With a scope of 30+ hours of story content and an equal amount of side content, the technical team aimed to give designers maximum creative freedom by enabling them to create and combine gameplay elements. This session will explore the technical choices made to support that goal, the challenges encountered along the way, and concrete examples drawn from gameplay systems and content of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33.
takeaway: Attendees will discover how the scope of features and content of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 was delivered by a small team with limited programming resources. Starting from production constraints, they will learn approaches for using Unreal Engine with a 'vanilla-first' mindset, how the programmers can empower designers to build game content, and how some of the systems of the game were implemented under these constraints. They will also learn production tips and methodologies useful when working as a small team.
intended audience: This talk is mostly aimed at gameplay programmers, tech designers, as well as project managers interested in optimizing programming priorities in small teams.
speakers: Tom Guillermin(Sandfall Interactive), Florian Torres(Sandfall Interactive)

session title: Establishing the Standards and Practices of Game Preservation Roundtable
description:
'Game Preservation' is a deep subject covering numerous fields of work from emulation to the archiving of development assets all the way to the remastering and re-releasing of games. However, despite so many contributing to this field, one universal question remains: 'What is Game Preservation, and how do you do it?'.

This session serves to bring preservationists and archivists together to discuss a shared definition for 'Game Preservation', and share ideas on standards that institutions and companies internationally can adopt.

As a Roundtable, attendees can submit questions relating to any and all forms of game preservation during the session. Some examples:
1. How can we improve the working relationship between institutions and companies?
2. What are the best tools for X workflow?
3. Which areas of Game Preservation are underdiscussed?
takeaway: Attendees will be able to share their personal experiences in Game Preservation, as well as be able to contribute back to the industry at large. They can expect to leave with an expanded understanding of the current processes of institutions and companies, and what pain points exist for each and how they may be able to be addressed today and in the future.
intended audience: This session is open for any and all who work in the field of Game Preservation whether at a studio, a publisher, an institution or those who function as freelancers and enthusiasts.
speakers: Garrett Fredley(PlayStation), Andrew Borman(The Strong National Museum of Play)

session title: Flavors of Challenge: The 8 Flavors of Difficulty and How They Can Be Combined to Make Better Difficult Games
description: The past decade has seen an explosion of interest in hyper-difficult games such as Cuphead, Spelunky, Getting Over It, and of course, the FromSoftware games. But when we examine this new crop of difficult games, we find they have limited similarities; there is no single 'difficulty' lever that is being cranked up but instead a complex balance of gameplay and aesthetic elements. Flavors of Challenge seeks to identify the discrete elements that make difficult games difficult and asks why these punishing games are the favorite of many. Over the course of the talk, we will discuss what designers can do to make their difficult games more rewarding and profile several games that combine the 'Flavors of Challenge' in interesting ways.
takeaway: 'Flavors of Challenge' will provide attendees with a new lens to analyze difficult games. It will also give designers strategies to make their difficult games more rewarding and accessible. In addition, attendees will gain an increased appreciation for some of the most difficult games of the past decade.
intended audience: This talk is for all designers that are skeptical of difficult games or who want to learn how to better structure challenges within their games. Experience playing difficult games will be helpful but is not required.

session title: From Intermediate to Advanced: Charting Your Career in Game Audio
description: We all know that breaking into the game industry is difficult, but what do you do once you've already 'made it?' This panel explores the space between the intermediate and advanced stages of a freelance career in game audio, filling the gap left behind by the abundance of presentations and resources aimed at beginners. Our panel of established game audio freelancers will discuss pricing strategy, navigating professional relationships as they become increasingly more personal, and the challenges that come with success, among other topics. Attendees will leave with practical advice on how to nurture and grow a career well into maturity, with a focus on balance, stability and emotional intelligence.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a thorough understanding of the inherent struggles in growing an established career, and practical advice on how to manage them. Professionals newer to the industry will get a head start on confronting issues that they likely have not considered, while more established individuals will gain new perspective on familiar obstacles and insight on how to move to the next stage of their career.
intended audience: This session targets game audio professionals with 2+ years of experience and advanced beginners looking to proactively prepare for their future.
