It's ideal for sound designers; composers; audio programmers, working towards leadership roles; freelancers, planning for long-term sustainability; and mid-career professionals, facing transition challenges. It is not recommended for creators completely new to the field.
speakers: Star Victoria Power(Slide20XX), Ryan Ike(Independent), Akash Thakkar(Independent), Katherine Tole(Plant Based Audio)

session title: Global Pipelines Roundtable
description: As production demands grow and workflows diversify, developing and maintaining a robust, adaptable pipeline is more critical than ever. This casual roundtable invites pipeline developers, TAs, and technical leads to discuss evolving pipeline trends, common challenges across studios, and share practical strategies for building efficient, scalable systems. Topics span from the VFX Reference Platform to cloud-native workflows, storage strategies, and MLOps integration. Join this conversation to exchange ideas and experiences with peers who are shaping the future of production technology.
takeaway: Participants will gain insight into emerging technologies and pipeline standards, discover shared pain points across studios, and walk away with actionable ideas for improving or future-proofing their own production pipelines.
intended audience: This roundtable is ideal for pipeline developers, technical artists, production engineers, and anyone responsible for studio infrastructure. Individuals working in cloud services, virtual production, or machine learning workflows will also benefit from shared knowledge and best practices.
speakers: David Vega(The Digital Production Pipeline Association), Fran Zandonella Benjamin(The Digital Production Pipeline Association)

session title: Godot Roundtable
description:
The Godot roundtable is all about our favorite open source game engine: Godot!

From game jams to Battlefield 6's official community level editor, Godot is rising in adoption across the industry. This roundtable is a place to discuss common questions regarding adoption, development, studio pipelines, and commercial workflows that come up when considering using Godot for a project. We'll gather questions from the room and discuss them in detail as a group.
Example questions:
- How does one get involved with Godot code contributions?
- How can Godot be used in game development education?
- What are the big drawbacks with Godot adoption for a commercial production today?

Any experience level is welcome!
takeaway: Attendees should walk away feeling like they have a broader network of people interested in Godot and a better familiarity with where it currently exists in the game development ecosystem. People who are interested in contributing to the engine should feel like they have clarity on the right way to pursue that.
intended audience: The audience is anyone interested in Godot. That includes people interested in adopting Godot for commercial production, people interested in Godot as a prototyping/hobby engine, people interested in contributing to Godot as an open source project, teachers interested in an affordable and accessible teaching aid, and anyone else who may be curious about the engine.

session title: How I Raised X Million
description: With the industry in flux, founding studios is harder than ever. From the rapidly changing funding landscape, to studio closures, to new tech and tooling, to new player patterns of Gen A growing up on Roblox and Fortnite it's hard to know how to succeed. Join our rockstar panel of game studio founders who beat the odds to raise a combined total of over $40 Million to date. From navigating investor pitches (and 'push back') to building investor trust and overcome knee-jerk reactions to balancing creative vision with business realities, these microtalks and panel offers candid stories and practical strategies for turning bold ideas into thriving studios. We will hear hard-earned lessons from the game studio founders, successes as well as failures. Whether you're an aspiring founder or an established game developer looking to scale, this conversation will arm you with the inspiration and tactics to secure capital and bring your studio to life.
takeaway: Attendees learn the importance of having a well thought out Growth and Financial plan to support a great product - it's more than being passionate about games and a big idea. When to turn down investment. How to find investment in unexpected places. What role technical founders have in fundraising.
intended audience: Aspiring founders and experienced developers looking to scale their game studios. Founders wanting to know more about the tradeoffs, critical success factors, and detailed tips about the funding journey. As well as investors and VC's wanting to understand the founder's journey.
speakers: Nicole Lazzaro(XEODesign), Jenny Xu(Talofa Games), Lauren Frazier-Silva(Ramen VR)

session title: How to Advocate for Yourself as a Neurodivergent Game Dev Roundtable (Presented by the IGDA)
description: Navigating the workplace or job interviews can be especially challenging for neurodivergent game developers. Differences in communication, stress management, or adapting to change can lead to misunderstandings or feelings of isolation in professional environments. This roundtable explores practical strategies for self-advocacy at work and during interviews. Participants are encouraged to share personal experiences or hypothetical scenarios, learning techniques and insights from one another in a supportive, peer-led discussion. This roundtable is hosted by the IGDA Neurodivergent in Games SIG.
takeaway: By the end of the roundtable, participants will feel more confident in advocating for themselves as neurodivergent professionals, both in the workplace and during job interviews. They will learn how to recognize their unique strengths and develop strategies for navigating challenging situations, helping them feel better understood, valued, and supported.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended for anyone interested in improving their skills in advocating for themselves as a neurodivergent professional.

session title: How to Win a VES Award
description:
For over 20 years, the Visual Effects Society has had an award for 'Outstanding Visual Effects in a Real Time Project'. Only 9 people, worldwide have won this category multiple times.

Join us as these outstanding artists and leaders discuss their work, thoughts, planning and execution of the best effects in the world, and how they navigated their careers such that they positioned themselves at studios that are accomplishing the best work in the industry, worthy of realizing a VES award.
takeaway: We hope to inspire real time visual effects for artists across the industry to strive for, to navigate their careers around, and modify their day to day approach such that their default mode is to create award worth work, and that this will result in them also winning a VES award one day.
intended audience: Video Game Real-Time Visual Effects Artists and the VFX Summit.
speakers: David Johnson(Undertone FX, Inc.), Sandy Lin-Chiang(Insomniac Games), Eben Cook(Naughty Dog), Janne Pulkkinen(Remedy Entertainment)

session title: Leaders Working at the Heart of the Team 2026
description: There is a critical facet of game development that needs exploring: the emotional and elusive feel of being a great leader. In order to maintain the health of a project the leader must have their finger firmly on the pulse of the team and be able to diagnose situations that often call for drastically different solutions. In this session, we will investigate facets of soft skills and share specific situations that we have encountered throughout our careers and how soft skills have saved the team and shipped awesome games. Topics include: The three P's of passion: motivating your team, Leadership as a service, Calm urgency, and Decision Deferred: the worst decision of all.
takeaway: Attendees will gain an increased knowledge and understanding of how to use the soft skills a leader has, and how focusing on their team will make their games more successful. This will be covered through practical examples and discussion.
intended audience: The target audience is leaders with 4+ years experience in the games industry. It will also be valuable for new leaders/producers/project managers and disciplinary leads.
speakers: Grant Shonkwiler(Shonkventures), Kate Edwards(Geogrify/SetJetters), Seth Sivak(Bad Robot Games), Dave Ranyard(Dream Reality Interactive), Jess Case(Schell Games)

session title: Making Player Data Work Roundtable Day 1: Pre-Production
description:
The role of player feedback in game development can be divisive and complex. Some studios totally embrace players' engagement in design through early access, open development, routine user research, and community engagement. Other studios limit player feedback to just post-launch, or perhaps dismiss feedback completely.

How can teams use the 'voice of the player' rationally, effectively and deliberately?

Are there challenges you face that player feedback could overcome?

Can you make a greater success of the feedback and data you already have?

How does the 'voice' of player data change through development?

These three roundtables present a valuable opportunity to surface your challenges, viewpoints and experience on the use of player feedback to inform game design. Learn from the experiences of fellow designers, researchers, data scientists, community and player champions, and take away new perspectives on the value of the players' voice in decision-making.




This roundtable covers pre-production. How can player data inform product direction, greenlight processes, or prototype validation? Bring your questions, experiences and challenges to this roundtable focusing on player data strategy in pre-prod and early-development.
takeaway:
An open-discussion on approaches, lessons, challenges and opportunities in the use of player feedback. Bring your questions on how to capture, comprehend, action and measure the impact of insights from real players.

Each of three roundtables covers a differing phase of game development.
intended audience: Game designers, producers and quality-owners with advice and opportunities. Researchers, data scientists, accessibility and community advocates with practical experience of player research, seeking broader horizons and a chance to learn from the successes of others.

session title: Narrative Portfolio Review
description: As a game writer or narrative designer, your portfolio is one of the most critical tools for getting hired... So bring yours here! Whether you're an industry hopeful or a veteran writer or narrative designer looking to spruce up your samples, this portfolio workshop will provide individualized feedback on your materials from Senior and Lead-level narrative industry peers. Please bring either a printed copy of your portfolio or have it digitally accessible via your laptop or tablet. As you will be meeting 1:1 with a reviewer, spots are very limited and will be assigned on a first-come first-serve basis.
takeaway: Attendees will gain actionable, personalized feedback on their portfolio materials in a 1:1 meeting with a Senior or Lead-level game writer or narrative designer industry peer. Attendees will leave with clear next steps for the refinement or improvement of their materials so they can feel empowered and motivated.
intended audience: This workshop is for game writers/narrative designers of all levels who already have a portfolio and are looking to receive peer-review on their materials. Please be aware that you must have either a hard copy of your portfolio or a device by which we can access it.
speakers: Anna C. Webster(Independent), Alexa Ray Corriea(Gearbox), Ashley Poprik(Independent), Emma Kidwell(Independent), John Ryan(Independent), Laura Michet(Heart Machine), Sam Maggs(Critical Role), Alice Camp(Microfun), Aysha Farah(Independent), Karrie Shao(Pacific Drive), Kelsie Mhoon(Obsidian Entertainment), Nessa Cannon(Independent), Lis Moberly(20th Century Games, The Walt Disney Company), Books Schwartz, Sam Winkler(Gearbox Software)

session title: Reinforcement Learning in 'FC26': Shipping Human-Like Goalkeepers with a Designer-First Approach
description: In this talk, we will explore the integration of a designer-first reinforcement learning approach into EA SPORTS FC 26. The approach focuses on creating human-like AI behaviors for the game's goalkeeper. Unlike traditional reinforcement learning methods that demand extensive resources and time, our new approach enables game developers to train AI agents overnight. This talk will highlight how we address common issues when applying reinforcement learning to game production, such as fixing undesired behaviors, and how to build a robust testing system featuring over 300 'unit-test' scenarios, ensuring continuous validation. Our new goalkeeper AI not only outperforms the legacy game's built-in AI with a 10% improvement in ball saving rate, but trains 50% faster than standard reinforcement learning methods, while being less robotic than other DRL or traditional AI approaches. During the talk we will show several videos comparing the performance of the goalkeeper trained with reinforcement learning and the old game's CPU AI. The videos will also showcase the difference in quality between the two approaches, demonstrating that our approach produces more human-like behavior. By sharing these insights, we aim to demonstrate how reinforcement learning can be successfully applied in video game production for player-facing gameplay to create realistic and engaging AI, targeting developers' needs.
takeaway: Attendees will learn practical techniques for training human-like game agents with reinforcement learning for player-facing features. Moreover, attendees will discover how to build an efficient production pipeline that enables overnight training, semi-automated debugging, and robust regression testing to support rapid iteration. Finally, attendees will learn best practices for integrating tester feedback into the training loop to create more controllable and designer-aligned game AI.
intended audience: The lecture is aimed at game AI experts and practitioners, machine learning (especially reinforcement learning) practitioners for games, and machine learning researchers.
speakers: Alessandro Sestini(Electronic Arts, SEED), Mike Jones(Electronic Arts (EA) Canada)

session title: The Black Designers' Dilemma
description: How do different disciplines solve the same game-breaking problem — and what happens when bias enters the room? In this candid collaborative session, five Black professionals across narrative, systems, production, and executive roles explore how design decisions get made under pressure. Using real-world examples, panelists reflect on how their roles approach challenges differently and how cultural perspectives shape creative collaboration. This isn't about performing the right answer — it's about surfacing the real thinking behind cross-functional problem-solving. Attendees will gain insight into both technical trade-offs and social dynamics that affect every team. A must-attend for anyone interested in leadership, inclusion, or surviving late-stage development with your design integrity intact.
takeaway: Attendees gain insight into how narrative, systems, and production roles approach late-stage design challenges differently, and how Black professionals navigate bias while collaborating. Concrete examples reveal decision-making tradeoffs, communication breakdowns, and practical strategies for improving cross-functional teamwork in high-pressure development environments.
intended audience: This session is for all game development professionals, from students to seasoned developers, across disciplines. Anyone interested in how teams solve complex design problems and how cultural perspectives influence collaboration will benefit from this candid, behind-the-scenes look at decision-making in real-world production environments.
speakers: Carl Varnado(Rancho Solano Prep), Pamela Illuore(Playstation), Jabari Ali(Hidden Genius), Jarory DeJesus(Coquito Games), Evan Narcisse(Brass Lion Entertainment)

session title: Using Telemetry (the Right Way) is a Game-Changer for Game Tools
description:
Telemetry can be a game-changer in helping you develop better tools, but without watching users work, it can also be deeply misleading.

Using real-world examples, this talk offers practical and actionable advice on what telemetry can and cannot tell you, what to consider when adding a telemetry system to your tools, and how to use it to track improvements to the user experience of your tools over time.
takeaway: Attendees will learn what to consider when introducing a telemetry system to their tools, how to make good use of the data, common pitfalls to avoid, and how to include telemetry data in the tools development process.
intended audience: Programmers, specifically tools and engine developers, tools designers, technical artists and designers, technical directors, technology managers, tools QA experts and other tools experts.

session title: Beyond the Build: Turning Your Studio into a Supercomputer (Presented by Incredibuild)
description:
In modern game development, the local workstation has become a critical bottleneck for studio throughput. Whether it's massive shader compilations, heavy bake cycles, or complex GPU-driven tasks, waiting for local hardware is the primary enemy of iterative development and results in significant resource waste.

This session dives into the architecture of Incredibuild, demonstrating how to seamlessly distribute highly parallel GPU and CPU tasks across your entire network. We will explore how to bypass the 'dependency nightmare' and show how even the most powerful resources can be pooled and shared to benefit the entire studio.

Stop thinking in terms of 'my machine' and start thinking in terms of The Grid.
takeaway: Attendees will hear from a developer about how Incredibuild helped to improve local & build pipelines by 95%. How easily it was to integrate into their infrastructure and editors (UE, Unity, and others), as well as the challenges they overcame to ensure a stable, scalable developer experience across the studio.
intended audience:
This panel is focused on game developers, publishers, technical decision makers, engine/tools teams, and any technical directors looking to find easy & fast ways to improve CI/CD/local build times. It will also benefit anyone involved with managing infrastructure, DevOps/SRE engineers.
No prerequisite knowledge is required - just an interest in learning more about what it takes to improve your development cycle without purchasing & managing expensive rack-mounted hardware.

session title: From Code to Game: Empowering Developers with JetBrains Rider and TeamCity (Presented by JetBrains)
description:
Your ideas, your code, your game. JetBrains tools are here to make the journey smooth, fast and enjoyable.

Together, let's see how JetBrains Rider helps you write the code you need. With impressive engine-specific inspections, Rider will suggest best practices in Unity and Unreal Engine. AI functionality can help learn new APIs or handle boilerplate for you. Rich debugging helps you understand how your game is working, on desktop, mobile or console. And integration with serialised data bridges the gap between the editor and your code. We'll learn some tips and tricks and see what's new, including exciting new Godot support.

And we'll see how TeamCity simplifies continuous integration and keeps your development pipeline running like clockwork.

This session will show you how JetBrains helps you bring your game ideas to life, faster and smarter than ever.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to be more productive writing code and following engine best practices thanks to intuitive and innovative features in JetBrains Rider. They will also understand how TeamCity can help make it easy to build their Unity, Unreal Engine and Godot games.
intended audience: Developers working with C#, C++ and GDScript. Working with Unity, Unreal Engine, Godot and custom engines. AAA studios and Indies.

session title: High-Fidelity on PC and Mobile: The 'Delta Force' Character Production Pipeline (Presented by Tencent Games)
description:
Ensuring games are available across multiple platforms is increasingly essential in an age where game budgets and competition for player attention continues to cinrease. However, this creates issues when trying to port assets between platforms with large variations in specification.
The team behind PC and Mobile hit Delta Force have created tools and processes that dramatically speak up the coversion of assets from PC to mobile, without impact to visible asset quality, game performance and without needing a large increase in project staff.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about the key artistic and technical points involved in maintaining quality and importing character model assets across PC and mobile. They will learn how to set up asset pipelines, set up quality control mechanisms and how to accurately optimize and adapt to the differences between PC and mobile games, and ensure that the 3D character assets perform well on both platforms.
intended audience: The intended audience primarily includes game art developers in the industry including, but not limited to, 2D character artists, 3D character artists, 3D character technical artists, and animators. It will prove especially useful to develoers working on cross-platform projects.

session title: Trust, Attest, Server-Authoritative: Modern iOS Anti-Cheat for Multiplayer (Presented by GameSafe)
description: A practical guide to modern iOS anti-cheat engineering. Learn how to combine Apple's attestation APIs (App Attest, DeviceCheck) with runtime integrity checks and server-authoritative design to detect tampering and automation in multiplayer games — without harming performance or legitimate users.
takeaway:
• Understand modern iOS cheat vectors and system limitations.
• Learn how to integrate App Attest and DeviceCheck for device attestation.
• Design server-authoritative game logic to reduce client trust.
• Combine attestation, runtime signals, and behavior analytics into a unified risk model.
• Manage rollout, false positives, and quota limits safely in production.
intended audience: Game security engineers, iOS developers, multiplayer backend engineers, and technical product managers. Attendees should be familiar with Swift or Objective-C, and general multiplayer architecture.

session title: Surprise and Delight as Social Infrastructure
description: Great communities are designed, not moderated into existence. In this talk, Nate Sawatzky, head of Trust and Safety at Supercell, shares how to build healthier player environments by making community design part of the game loop. Drawing from his experience at Disney and Club Penguin, he will explore how evolving lore, in-game updates, and playful interactions can set expectations in a fun way, reinforce norms, and create surprise and delight that strengthens community belonging. Sawatzky will also explore how player creativity reshapes intended systems, revealing new pathways to fun, stronger social connections, and smarter feature evolution
takeaway: Design a repeatable community loop, encode norms through content and UX, treat surprise as infrastructure, anticipate exploits with lightweight threat modeling, and channel player subversion into safe, rewarding outlets.
intended audience: Anyone interested in improving game design environments, including: Game Designers, Developers, Community Managers, and Data Scientists.
speakers: Nathan Sawatzky(Thriving in Games Group Inc)

session title: Economic Policy and Ecosystem Workshop
description:
The game industry is an engine of economic opportunity. Success can come from anywhere! Governments around the world are eager to encourage the creation of game-related jobs, businesses and IP. This workshop will explore how governments, economic development agencies, and industry associations can work together to support the growth of their local industry and set investment policies and strategies that make a true, lasting impact.
The workshop will be conducted in a hands-on format, with a focus on interactive group sessions to maximize knowledge sharing and exchange between participants. Specific granular topics will be proposed and prioritized as part of the workshop format, determined by the most pressing needs of those in the room… from how best to nurture talent pipelines to incubating new startups, from investment and tax credit regimes to migration policies and FDI, and so on.
takeaway: Workshop participants will walk away with practical examples of how their ongoing efforts can best generate economic impact and support game industry success. Further, attendees will have a rare opportunity to connect with (and be inspired by) their peers doing similar ecosystem and economic policy work around the world.
intended audience: This workshop is intended for government representatives, economic development agencies, and industry associations that are focused on supporting and growing their regional game industry. Other industry leaders that are involved in such efforts as advisors, board members, and volunteers are welcome as well.
speakers: Jason Della Rocca(Execution Labs), Kristian Roberts(Nordicity), Johanna Nylander(Swedish Games Industry (Dataspelsbranschen))

session title: Beyond Creating Joy, What Else Can Games Do for Our Next Generation? (Presented by GameSafe)
description: In this session, Zheng Lei, head of Tencent's Minor Protection System, shares how the innovative 'Gift-for-Good' model builds an ecological bridge connecting player goodwill with youth development needs. The 'Campus Tech Festival' with League of Legends, he demonstrates how to seamlessly integrate philanthropic elements into gameplay. The talk delves into three core design philosophies: respecting players, establishing feedback loops, and online-offline integration, showcasing how over 40 million players across 70+ games have created tangible impact through STEM education and sports initiatives over five years. This provides the industry with replicable pathways for creating shared value.
takeaway: Audiences will gain actionable frameworks for designing mechanisms that organically integrate gaming with public welfare initiatives, such as converting daily login rewards into support for philanthropic activities; learn to establish warm feedback loops that enhance engagement through personalized certificates and stories of beneficiaries; and master specific strategies for creating online-offline integrated ecosystems, like transforming game IP into real-world educational experiences.
intended audience: This session benefits game producers, live operations leads, and CSR specialists seeking to deeply integrate social value creation into game design. No prerequisites are required as it provides both strategic frameworks and implementable cases suitable for various game business models.

session title: Bones & Booleans
description: The giant d20 returns to GDC! Our panel of seasoned veterans are here to share hard-earned experiences, give tips & tricks and answer questions about all things Animation & Presentation implementation and tech in game engines.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with a few tips & tricks that they can integrate into their workflows & pipelines, and have the opportunity to ask a panel of experts questions about anything related to Animation & Cinematics technology, pipelines & workflows.
intended audience: Topics will be focused on various aspects of technical animation & cinematics, but we encourage artists to join. Most panelists expertise will be centered around UE, but there is a range of experience across other engines & toolsets.
speakers: Frank Gordon(Compulsion Games), Nathaniel Winckler(That's No Moon), Matt Lake(Independent), Thomas Van Cise(That's No Moon)

session title: Constructing Minietopia from a Micro Perspective: Concept Design and Implementation of 'Animula Nook' (Presented by Tencent Games)
description:
The artists behind Animula Nook, a cozy Lilliput fantasy life simulation, will share the visual design and technical solutions they employed to construct a believable, warm, and healing micro-world.

This session is divided into two parts: how to achieve an authentic and convincing micro-scale perception, and how to effectively convey a sense of warmth and comfort through visual design.

Attendees will gain insights into the team's intricate micro-visual and player interaction designs, as well as the cost-effective technical implementations used. Furthermore, the talk will cover the stylized rendering of various hair and fuzzy materials, and the high-fidelity implementation solutions across different gaming platforms.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how a cross-platform stylized cartoon-rendered game was conceptually designed around a Lilliput fantasy theme, and how tailored visual implementation solutions were developed for different platforms.
intended audience: The intended audience includes Technical Artists, Environment Artists, and Character Artists, as well as anyone interested in stylized game visual design or practical rendering technical solutions. The session requires no specific prerequisite knowledge.
speakers: Hong Kang(Tencent Games), Xingwen Dong(Tencent Games)

session title: Reusable Material Systems: Vinyl Flooring for Scalable Environments (Presented by Adobe)
description: Explore the process of creating a convincing vinyl flooring material with Substance 3D Designer from start to finish. Vincent Dérozier will walk through his workflow, starting from a clean, simple base surface, and progressing toward worn and aged variations suitable for games. He will demonstrate how to leverage the power of Substance 3D Designer to build versatile textures that can be easily reused and adapted across multiple projects.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to create parametric, production-ready materials in Substance 3D Designer, and will gain practical techniques for crafting versatile, reusable textures.
intended audience: Game developers, technical artists, visual designers, production engineers or anyone working on workflows, integration, or art production will gain insights into game pipelines and techniques for producing consistent, high-quality visuals efficiently.

session title: AI Models for 2D Generation
description: Ubisoft La Forge shares its pioneering work in generative AI for 2D image creation, built through close collaboration between artists and R&D teams. This session explores Ubisoft's research around a creator-first approach that respects artistic intent while addressing real production challenges such as accelerating visual ideation and reducing iteration cycles or repetitive tasks. From data preparation to training and human evaluation methods, attendees will gain insights into how Ubisoft has developed workflows using proprietary data, internal and open-source tools. They will also understand how models can be fine-tuned with minimal data, making them accessible to smaller studios. The talk will showcase various AI models developed at Ubisoft and how they are integrated into game production pipelines to support artists working on concept arts, texturing and materials creation.
takeaway: Attendees learn how Ubisoft fine-tunes generative AI models for concept art, textures, and game items thumbnail using proprietary data and open-source tools. They gain practical insights into workflows, artist-led evaluation, and how LoRA models can be trained with just 30 images—making AI integration feasible even for small studios.
intended audience: The intended audience is technical artists, tech-savvy artists, machine learning enthusiasts or researchers interested in having a glimpse at applications of AI in the industry. There is no prerequisite knowledge required for this session.

session title: Antisemitism in the Games Industry Roundtable (Presented by the IGDA)
description: Antisemitism has dramatically increased across the world. Many Jewish game developers have been unable to speak up. If they do, they are met with further antisemitic behavior. This is especially true online where misinformation is shared and corrections are met with resistance - all without consideration for the effect such behavior might have on Jews in the community. This is a community-driven conversation where we'll share our experiences with antisemitism from our industry and come up with solutions to combat it.
takeaway: Jewish attendees will come away with their feelings heard about how antisemitism has affected them. Allies may come away with more sympathy for their Jewish colleagues. Everyone will learn more about what they can do to combat antisemitism.
intended audience: This roundtable is open to Jewish game developers and those who want to learn more about antisemitism and hear about the experience of the Jewish community.

session title: 'Apex Legends' Dev Support: Getting Bandwidth Back by Letting People Do Their Best Work
description: Game development teams face tight deadlines that are stymied by production bottlenecks, communication hurdles, and dynamic workflows breaking. Respawn's Apex Legends team adopted a dedicated developer support team model that allows it to overcome these hurdles by reducing the time to resolution of problems and having experts on troubleshooting its systems. Today, the Developer Support team handles upwards of 4000 issues per year with an average response time of less than 2 minutes and an average resolution time of less than 1 hour, ensuring that the Apex Legends development team can get back on track quickly. What's more, we have an escalation rate of less than 10% meaning that 90% of those issues do not require an engineering resource to resolve it, giving valuable focused production time back to the engineering team. With compelling anecdotes and hard data, this talk will focus on the team's formation, use, and impact.
takeaway: Attendees will walk away with an understanding of how to start and grow a Developer Support team in their own studios.
intended audience: This talk is targeted at studio leadership--especially in design, art, engineering, and production--or those interested in content creation velocity and reducing randomization of key resources for troubleshooting. There are no prerequisites.

session title: Changing our Paradigm of Leading Change
description: This session reframes and re-energizes the seemingly endless efforts placed on managers and their teams to create a better, more productive team culture. Too often, what are posed as solutions for this ultimately make things more burdensome and frustrating. A new paradigm for planning and conversing for change is advocated for and participants are invited to practice its usage in the session immediately
takeaway: Attendees are offered a new method of collaborative structuring and dialogue toward creating sustainable change. Promising to bridge the views of creative and engineering interests within teams and tackle seemingly intractable performance challenges with teammates, this session can change how the attendee looks at and feels about their work and, perhaps, their life.
intended audience: Managers of all levels, individual contributors to teams, and anyone who feels they have hit a wall in their own motivation and inspiration to do remarkable work.

session title: Controlled Chaos: 12 Principles of Design for VFX
description: Is your work missing something special? Not quite jumping off the screen? This lecture dives into how the 12 principles of design—evolved from Disney's legendary animation fundamentals—can strengthen and sharpen your VFX work. We'll explore how concepts like rhythm, balance, and negative space can help organize visual elements in a clear and functional manner. Through iconic design examples, VFX breakdowns, and a demonstration transforming a first-pass effect, attendees will learn how these evolved principles support visuals that read clearly, inform, and dazzle. Whether you're new to VFX or looking to sharpen your eye, this session will refine and energize your work.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to apply the 12 principles of design to visual effects, gaining a clear understanding of how these practical tools enhance clarity, communication, and appeal. Through examples and demonstration, attendees will learn how foundational design choices can transform effects from basic to impactful—making their work more intentional, readable, and expressive.
intended audience: The target audience for this lecture are VFX artists from beginners to seasoned veterans, but visual artists from all disciplines will find something useful from this presentation to strengthen and refine their work.

session title: Developers in China & IGDA SZ Meet Up (Presented by the IGDA)
description:
This informal session aims to connect game developers and industry professionals from China with the global game development community at GDC. As the IGDA Shenzhen Chapter, we'll facilitate meaningful conversations about the Chinese game development landscape, share insights on cross-cultural collaboration opportunities, and introduce IGDA's resources and community to developers who may be new to the organization.




Open networking for Chinese developers and international attendees interested in the Chinese market

Brief introduction to IGDA membership benefits and global chapter activities

Casual discussions on current trends in China's gaming industry

Q&A on collaboration opportunities between Chinese and international studios

Information sharing about IGDA Shenzhen Chapter initiatives and how to get involved




This will be conducted in a relaxed, conversational format to encourage genuine connections and community building.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Does it Make You Feel Like a Wandering Ronin? Creative Direction for 'Ghost of Yōtei'
description:
One of our core challenges at Sucker Punch is building an open-world game that goes toe-to-toe with games built by much larger teams with much larger budgets. We've had some success doing this, and a large part of that success comes from harnessing the creative energy of our whole team, rather than expecting a small group of people to make all the creative decisions about the game.
That creates a challenge for us as Creative Directors. Most of the time, we're not telling the team what to do—rather, we're inspiring them with a creative vision, building an enthusiastic consensus, and standing back as the team uses their judgement to reach those creative targets.
The pieces don't always fall into place so cleanly, though—and sometimes we're much more top-down in our creative direction. Join us as we walk through the creative direction process for Ghost of Yōtei.
We'll talk about all the mechanisms we used for communicating vision: presentations, play tests, razors, pillars, trailers, demos, internal AMAs, et al. We'll give specific examples of providing a direction or vision, then watching the team build on that to create an iconic part of the game. We'll also provide specific examples of exerting more control during the game's development for some crucial asset like a game trailer, and other times as a last resort when a vision mismatch popped up. And finally, we'll identify the major inflection points where we course-corrected our vision to match the things we learned during the game's development.
takeaway: Attendees will take away techniques for how to communicate a vision that inspires teammates to reach towards a shared goal. Coordinating creative energy can be tough, this talk will show people first hand examples of how the Creative Directors at Sucker Punch Productions go about it.
intended audience: This is a general interest session; no technical knowledge is required. While the talk is about working in Creative Direction, many of the techniques shared are broadly applicable. No matter what role you have in game development, it's valuable to be able to define and communicate a creative goal.
speakers: Nate Fox(Sucker Punch Productions), Jason Connell(Sucker Punch Productions)

session title: Game AI Roundtable Day 1
description:
The Game AI Roundtable is organized into three sessions, with each session having its own focus.

Session 1 centers on traditional, non-ML game AI. Topics include behavior trees, utility systems, navigation, tools, and other established techniques. This session avoids machine learning (which is covered on day 3) to keep the conversation targeted towards current mainstream game AI.

Session 2 is a fast-paced Q&A session aimed at all experience levels. Designers, students, programmers, animators, producers, and anyone else curious about game AI are encouraged to bring questions. The goal is to answer as many questions as possible and cover a broad range of topics rather than dive deeply into any single one.

Session 3 focuses on machine learning and generative AI, including LLMs. Discussion will cover current uses, challenges, ethics, and the evolving role of these technologies in games.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a clearer understanding of current game AI practices, from established techniques to emerging ML and LLM approaches. Each session offers a different perspective, with day 1 focusing on practical methods.
intended audience: Day 1 is aimed at programmers and technical designers who are familiar with game AI.

session title: Game Designer's Notebook
description: There are many game design topics that don't require an hour to discuss, but can be useful, especially for experienced game designers, as quick deep dives or punchy bits of food for thought. The Game Designer's Notebook asks seasoned game designers to open up a few pages from their notebook, and share insights and explorations into contemporary design issues in the live service game design arena. Part microtalk, part design review, the GDN is the session at the GDC Live Service Summit where game designers get to jump in, go deep and jump out.
takeaway: Game Designers should walk away feeling something like 'oh wow. Cool. I get that. I can use that.' They should walk away feeling inspired about their discipline and their craft. They should gain valuable lessons that are useable in their day to day. They should leave saying things like 'I can't wait to try that.'
intended audience: This is mostly for game designers and product leaders - other adjacent disciplines like marketing, tech, UX, UI Art and Animation and Narrative are often discussed.
speakers: Kenny Shea Dinkin(-), Rohit Crasta(The New York Times), Jesse Schell(Schell Games)

session title: How Tabletop Games Could Help Us Improve the Pro-Social Design of Digital Games
description: Robert believes there is a lot digital game creators can learn from tabletop games. In this session Robert will present a number of player dynamics found in tabletop gaming that provide unique insight into pro-social behavior specifically. And will offer his perspective on how those lessons could help digital game developers create more pro-social experiences and communities for their own games.
takeaway:
Tabletop games offer strong models for pro-social design. Player dynamics can teach digital creators how to encourage cooperation, positive interaction, and community-minded play.

These lessons can improve digital game communities. Applying tabletop-inspired design principles can help developers build healthier player experiences and more supportive communities.
intended audience: All are welcome, especially digital game developers, designers, community managers, and live-ops teams should attend to learn how tabletop-inspired player dynamics can help them create more pro-social experiences and healthier game communities.

session title: Implementing a Built-In Multiplayer UGC Experience in 'Genshin Impact'
description:
Miliastra Wonderland, Genshin Impact's new User-Generated Content (UGC) gameplay module, is built directly into the game rather than as a separate application. This design maximizes the reuse and portability of existing, actively updated Genshin Impact content within Miliastra Wonderland, boosting both the development cycle and long-term UGC creation.

This talk explores the technical framework for implementing UGC on top of Genshin Impact's existing architecture, highlighting its effectiveness and the challenges of maintaining performance when development focus shifts from a single-player, developer-crafted foundation to a multiplayer UGC environment. Attendees gain insights into design decisions, optimization strategies, and best practices for enabling rich, player-created experiences in a large-scale live service.
takeaway: Attendees gain a technical understanding of key features for creating UGC experiences, learn how to extend complex systems atop an existing game foundation, and explore practical strategies for optimizing performance across multiple platforms and ensuring smooth UGC experiences in a large-scale live-service game like Genshin Impact.

session title: Indie Soapbox: Playing it Unsafe
description:
Indie Soapbox is the chance for indie devs from diverse backgrounds and projects to come together to share their thoughts, lessons and provocations about indie game dev in this long standing microtalk format. This session features a rich and varied set of themes and topics, all in a punchy format that is sure to provoke, inspire, and broaden your horizons.




Our chosen theme this year is '**Playing It Unsafe**': diving into the role of indie games in driving innovation and boundary pushing, where devs are exploring themes, conventions, gameplay, representations, etc. that aren't happening elsewhere, and how that breathes vitality into the rest of the industry.
takeaway: This broad spread of punchy, thought-provoking microtalks will leave attendees with new directions, questions, and fresh ways to rally together as indie game developers.
intended audience: With a varied line up and spread of topics, this microtalk session will have something for everyone, at all stages in their career.
speakers: Hot Pink(HotPink Games), Adanna Nedd(Independent), Sarah Slovak(Lunacy Studios), Akash Thakkar(Independent), Jenny Jiao Hsia(Hexecutable), Ryan Ike(Independent), Claire L. Evans(YACHT), Jona Bechtolt(YACHT), Cass Riordan(No Goblin)

session title: Level 50 and Above: Navigating Game Careers Beyond Midlife
description: What happens when you've spent decades in games—and the industry suddenly feels like it's moving on without you? In an era of upheaval and layoffs, this roundtable centers game developers aged 50 and above, offering space to reflect, reconnect, and reimagine what's next. We'll explore how to sustain creative drive, manage health and life changes, confront ageism, and navigate retirement or reinvention with confidence. Whether you're looking to stay, pivot, or simply be seen, this session is about honoring experience, finding purpose, and building futures that don't end at 50—but evolve with power, clarity, and intention.
takeaway: Learn how to build a sustainable creative career in games—from avoiding early burnout to reigniting purpose mid-career and shaping your future or career transitions later in life. This roundtable will explore mentorship, well-being, and long-term creative growth at every stage of your professional journey.
intended audience: This session is for current and late-career professionals and anyone interested in career development and longevity in the face of a changing industry.

session title: The OpenUSD Roundtable Day 1
description:
USD stands for 'Universal Scene Description', and it is a 3D file format Pixar created over a span of many years and movies, and was released open source to the public in 2016. Although technically it is more of an API than a file format, which is an ongoing discussion into the terminology and use of USD.

In this session, attendees can gather to talk about USD. Come and meet, discuss, and find out what everyone has been doing with USD.

Questions can be submitted during the roundtable. For example, these may include topics such as:
1. What are issues you have bumped into when implementing USD into your pipeline?
2. What would be required for USD to be more widely adopted?
3. How can studios, and vendors, best share Schemas?

Questions can be oriented towards technical issues, design, and artistic workflows, with day 1 having a larger focus on artistic workflows, and day 2 having a larger focus on technical issues.
takeaway: Attendees will share their experiences, challenges, and successes with USD. They can expect to leave with information shared by other studios who have used USD, and real world experiences from other teams, that they can then apply to their own thought process around USD, as well as in their own implementations of USD.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended for anyone with an interest in USD. This could be programmers, technical artists, designers, but also producers and technical directors who are looking into how USD is used, and what the pros and cons are that those in the industry have experienced with USD.

session title: The Power of Authenticity: Audio Challenges and Successes in 'Civilization VII'
description: Capturing human history has been a goal for the Civilization franchise since the first iteration. With each successive game in the franchise, the level of care into authentically representing a culture has increased. Our audio team faced challenges finding the most effective ways to accurately represent a culture's music, language, and sounds. These challenges led to new collaborations and design methodologies highlighting the unique sounds around the world and ensuring we capture as many details as possible. Our panel will consist of Dmytro Nebesh, sound designer at Firaxis games, Geoff Knorr, composer on Civilization VII, Finn Taylor, narrative designer and historian at Firaxis games, and Kadet Kuhne, Assoc. Audio Director at Firaxis games. We will discuss the challenges and importance of designing authentic cultural sounds for a game, and the positive results it can have for the overall game design and player immersion.
takeaway: Audience members will learn various approaches to tackle historical representations. Many games will use history as a backdrop, and understanding when, why, and how to present historical characteristics authentically is an essential part of the design process. Audiences will see how audio is an important and effective way to bring representation into a game, when the corresponding effort is put in.
intended audience: Sound designers, voice designers, composers, historians, and intermediate developers that are exploring ways to bring historical authenticity into their games, and work with other cultures and musicians from various backgrounds in the research and creation of culturally accurate representation.
speakers: Dmytro Nebesh(Firaxis Games), Geoff Knorr(Knorr Music LLC), Finn Taylor(Firaxis Games), Kadet Kuhne(Firaxis Games)

session title: The Rise of Independent Game Journalism
description:
After a few uncertain years for games journalism, 2025 has brought a fresh wave of opportunity. Independent media organizations are proving there is life and growth beyond corporate structures that struggle to survive in the age of social media. This panel brings together leaders from GamesBeat, Giant Bomb, Aftermath, and Game File to share their journeys and explore what independence means for game studios today.
Panelists will discuss the circumstances that led them to establish independent media brands, challenges they faced during the transition, and offer direct, actionable insights to game makers and their teams that want to break through the noise. They will also highlight how community support has fueled their resilience, how independence shapes editorial direction, and the new creative opportunities now possible. For developers, publishers, and industry professionals, the discussion presents an optimistic and practical outlook on the future of game coverage.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insight into how independent outlets adapt and sustain themselves, with lessons on community, business models, and credibility. Developers can apply these approaches to their own strategies while understanding how the future of media will impact the visibility of their games.
intended audience: Games Industry professionals interested in learning about the rapidly evolving landscape for games media and the challenges independent journalists are currently facing. Attendees will gain perspective on how these shifts affect coverage, credibility, and how stories reach players. No prior experience required.
speakers: Dean Takahashi(GamesBeat), Jeff Grubb(Giant Bomb), Nathan Grayson(Aftermath), Perrin Kaplan(Zebra Partners), Stephen Totilo(Game File)

session title: Live Sound Bath and Meditation (Presented by thatgamecompany)
description:
Tim Nixon (thatgamecompany) & Caio Jiacomini (thatgamecompany)

Quiet Room 213

Take a moment from the busy show to relax and enjoy the calming sounds of crystal singing bowls intertwined with the atmosphere of Sky: Children of the Light, before sinking into a short guided meditation to help refresh the mind and body. All are welcome.




**Doors Open at 12:30PM.**

session title: Leveling Up with Google Cloud: PlayStation's Global Migration to Spanner (Presented by Google Cloud)
description: At the heart of the PlayStation™ ecosystem, a mission-critical service is responsible for a single, vital function: confirming every player's access to their games and digital content. For a platform with over 350 million user accounts, this entitlement system must be flawless, instant, and always available. As PlayStation's user base and content library grew, the decade-old legacy data architecture was pushed to its breaking point. Faced with spiraling costs and performance bottlenecks that directly impacted their most loyal players, Sony Interactive Entertainment (SIE) needed more than just a new database—they needed a new paradigm. This is the story of how SIE migrated its planet-scale entitlement system to Google Cloud Spanner, a database uniquely designed for this class of problem, achieving a 10x reduction in storage and a 50% drop in costs, all with zero downtime.
takeaway: The audience learns that 'planet-scale' legacy systems can be modernized without service interruption. By migrating to Google Cloud Spanner, SIE achieved a 10x storage reduction and 50% cost savings, proving that global consistency and massive efficiency are possible through a cloud paradigm.
intended audience: The intended audience includes CTOs, cloud architects, and engineering leaders managing global-scale legacy systems. The takeaway is that migrating mission-critical data to Google Cloud Spanner eliminates performance bottlenecks and spiraling costs, delivering a 10x storage reduction and 50% savings with zero downtime.
speakers: Sailesh Krishnamurthy(Google Cloud), Shalin Shekhar(Sony Interactive Entertainment), Edward Chu(Sony Interactive Entertainment)

session title: Playing with the Shoggoth: Safety in Games with Generative AI (Presented by GameSafe)
description:
'As Generative AI transitions from production pipelines into core gameplay loops, developers face a paradigm shift in player safety. Integrating live Large Language Models (LLMs) moves the challenge beyond traditional content moderation into the realm of adversarial creativity. When players interact with unscripted AI, both the player and the AI actively probe for weaknesses, testing boundaries in ways static safety systems can not predict.
This session explores the unique safety issues encountered when the ''content'' is also a ''content creator''. It examines how real-time, high-scale LLM interactions differ from standard User-Generated Content (UGC) and highlights the designer's responsibility to protect players from the AI itself. Attendees will gain insights into player behavior patterns at scale and leave with a robust mental model to help them balance player creative freedom with evolving safety concerns in the era of generative play.'
takeaway: Attendees will get a behind the scenes look at some of the safety concerns in using live AI in games. Both from the perspective of players manipulating the AI, and also the perspective of how the AI can manipulate the players.
intended audience: Any developers interested in adding elements of live generative AI into their games or anyone curious about the safety implications of gen AI in gaming will benefit from this presentation. No special knowledge of generative AI is required.

session title: 'Roco Kingdom': Crafting a Comprehensive Soundscape for Over 500 Creatures (Presented by Tencent Games)
description:
In Roco Kingdom, there are hundreds of creatures called 'Jingling' in various forms and types. How can we bring these Jinglings to life from the perspective of audio?

With an extremely large eco-system that had over 500 species with their own evolutionary chain, the audio team needed to conduct comprehensive sound design for them, including: Jingling voices with 19 complex emotions, Action SFX that matched all emotion movements, Riding SFX of complex motion forms, singing behavior, music designed for signature jinglings and more!

Under the design premise of such quantity and fineness requirements, Morefun studio audio team and POSTRED jointly built feedback pipeline and multi-layered soundscape to support all the creature audio design work. This ultimately boosted the efficiency and let the teams find the balance between width and depth.
takeaway: Participants can learn how 'Roco Kingdom' does sound design for more than 500 creatures in limited time. They will learn how to implement sound for shaping various creatures as well as learn how to arrange the producing structure to ensure the smooth operation of large-scale design.
intended audience: The audience should be interested in sound design, attempting to broaden their understanding of efficient audio production. It might be highly attractive to professionals interested in the pet-collecting or monster-taming genres, as well as producers / audio leads looking for insight on how to organise current or future open-world projects.
speakers: Runhang Zeng(Morefun Studio / Tencent Games), Luka Lebanidze(POSTRED)

session title: Through the Looking Glass: The Art of 'South of Midnight' (Presented by Adobe)
description: Paulette Richardson and Chad Emond from Compulsion Games will share insights into the handcrafted art style behind the environments and characters of South of Midnight. Paulette will talk about the texturing process for props and environments using Substance 3D Designer and Substance 3D Painter. Chad will follow with a look at the characters of South of Midnight, taking you through the team's character pipeline and showcasing how Substance 3D Painter was used during the texturing process.
takeaway: Explore South of Midnight's environment and character art pipelines and discover how Substance tools supported Compulsion Games in creating a distinctive, handcrafted art style.
intended audience: Game developers, technical artists, visual designers, production engineers or anyone working on workflows, integration, or art production will gain insights into game pipelines and techniques for producing consistent, high-quality visuals efficiently.
speakers: Chad Emond(Compulsion Games), Paulette Richardson(Compulsion Games)

session title: Why 'Just Use an LLM' Fails for Voice Chat Moderation (Presented by Modulate)
description:
Voice chat has become one of the most powerful tools for player connection. It deepens immersion, strengthens communities, and keeps players engaged longer than almost any other feature. But for many studios—especially smaller teams—voice chat introduces a new and unexpected challenge: moderation at scale. While game developers may be tempted to address in-game comms moderation using LLMs, this approach overlooks fundamental challenges around cost, latency, and audio understanding.

In this session, Modulate's game engine and machine learning experts unpack the real-world tradeoffs behind the use and overreliance on LLMs for voice chat moderation. Speakers will share firsthand experience implementing voice chat moderation, both with and without LLMs: what worked, what didn't, and what operational burdens surprised them. Together, they will explore how teams often default to building internal tools to save money, only to discover that moderation infrastructure quietly drains time, talent, and focus away from making great games.

The session concludes with a practical discussion of how purpose-built solutions, not generalist LLMs, can help studios meet safety, legal, and cost challenges without reinventing a complex moderation platform from scratch—allowing teams to stay focused on creativity and game development.
takeaway:
Attendees will leave with:

- A clear understanding of the true costs of voice chat moderation
Beyond infrastructure, moderation requires training, tuning, policy development, and ongoing maintenance.

- A realistic framework for 'build vs. buy' decisions
Especially for indie and mid-size studios with limited bandwidth and live-service ambitions.

- Actionable guidance for sustainable leadership
How to avoid over-engineering non-game systems and protect team focus, budget, and morale.
intended audience: This session is great for indie and mid-size game studios, especially those shipping or planning live-service games, and social, multiplayer, or UGC-driven experiences. This session is also intended for studio leadership and decision-makers for live-ops games including game directors, producers, technical leads, and solo developers.

session title: 'Helldivers' and the Art of the Living Game: Designing for Community Ownership
description: In this talk, Cooper Fleishman explores what transforms a game from having players into having a true community, using Helldivers 2 as a case study in prosocial design. He argues that Arrowhead achieved this not through moderation policies or reward loops, but through deliberate systems like shared resources, emergent squad roles, and a GM-driven galactic war, which enabled player-driven movements such as the Malevelon Creek resistance and the liberation of Seyshel Beach.
takeaway:
Prosocial communities are designed, not just moderated. Helldivers 2 shows that deliberate game systems can foster real community behavior more effectively than moderation policies or reward loops alone.

Shared systems create player ownership. Mechanics like shared resources, emergent roles, and a live GM-driven war can spark collective action and memorable player-led movements.
intended audience: This is open to anyone interested in developing a better understanding of game design, prosociality, or how games like Helldivers 2 has built a strong community.

session title: Action-Minded Strategies for Climate Stress Roundtable: Community, Action, and Empowerment (Presented by the IGDA)
description:
The games industry holds a unique and powerful position in shaping the future of our planet's climate. Its cultural reach, educational potential through game design, and direct influence on carbon emissions make it one of the most impactful industries of our time. Yet, many professionals within it often feel lost, overwhelmed, or powerless within a system that seems to move forward regardless of environmental consequences.

In this session, climate psychologist, sustainability educator, and former co-chair of the IGDA Climate SIG, Dr. Clayton Whittle, creates space to explore climate challenges within the games industry and the pressures faced by its professionals as they consider their role in building a more sustainable future for games.

Participants will find a supportive environment to discuss these issues, engage with a practical toolkit for sustainable advocacy, and discover actionable strategies for climate-conscious design.

Grounded in the understanding that meaningful climate action requires community, this session emphasizes how collective support empowers real, lasting change across the games industry.
takeaway:
Understand Climate Stress: Recognize how the climate crisis triggers stress, anxiety, and feelings of helplessness — and learn why these reactions are both natural and manageable.
Build Resilience: Gain practical tools for coping with climate-related stress in both personal and professional contexts.
Take Meaningful Action: Discover actionable strategies to integrate sustainability and climate-conscious thinking into your work in game development.
Find Community: Connect with others who share your concerns and values, and explore how community can transform climate anxiety into collective empowerment.
Continue the Journey: Leave with pathways for ongoing engagement through the IGDA Climate SIG, including resources, toolkits, and a supportive professional network.
intended audience: Game developers interested in how the games industry can be a positive force for climate action. Professionals currently struggling with the personal or professional climate stress or anxiety.

session title: Audio Directors Roundtable Day 1
description: This roundtable is a forum for attendees to build relationships and networks, to encourage and share best practices, to talk about topics such as, creative ownership, leadership strategies, mentoring, advocating for resources, cross departmental collaboration, the future of game audio and the best ways to build and maintain strong, diverse audio teams.
takeaway: Department heads take away new creative perspectives, strategies for managment, solutions to uncommon issues, and a sense of perspective from their peers and team members. Department members take away a better understanding of directorial challenges and creative opportunities that they can bring back to their colleagues.
intended audience: While the discussion is led by audio directors, it revolves around the entire departments experience. The audience consists of any member of the audio department including sound designers of all varieties (junior, senior and technical), audio QA, music and dialog editors, as well as directors themselves.
speakers: Adele Cutting(Soundcuts Ltd), Mari Saastamoinen Minto(EA DICE)

session title: Automated Testing Roundtable Day 1
description:
Day 1: Process
Automated testing provides a team with stability across development, allowing early feedback, fast iteration and empowering QA. Yet investing in automation has a bad reputation in the industry, where just-ship-it is the mantra. Maybe, as the market becomes increasingly competitive, its time has finally come.

Day 1 consists of a discussion about the people that write the tests and the workflows used to reinforce them. How does the process work in your studio? Are the engineers responsible for automated tests, or QA? Are QA silo'd or integrated? What could be better? What doesn't work?

These roundtables bring people together from all experience levels and budgets to discuss how they currently, or would like to, use automated testing of any form. See atig.dev/gdc﻿ (https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/QZfJCKrY6nHBYOW65HnFRI5JWxt?domain=atig.dev) for more details and to join our discord!
takeaway: Attendees will discover common definitions around automated testing, how other studios are currently using it, what works well, and what does not work well. They will return to their studios and extol the benefits of automated testing, and that it does not have to be hard or time consuming to see returns on investment.
intended audience: Programmers and technical QA with experience or an interest in automated testing. No expertise necessary - all ability levels encouraged.

session title: 'Balatro' Community Playbook: From Launch to Letting Go
description:
How do you build a thriving game community, then step back and let it run itself?

In this session, Emma Smith-Bodie, Community Manager at Playstack, shares how indie hit Balatro cultivated a self-sustaining player community on Discord and Reddit. From creating the right foundations to knowing when to let go, she'll walk through practical tactics for attracting early players, maintaining long-term engagement, and handing over the reins to your fans.

Learn how to foster loyalty, spark genuine conversation, and recognize when your players are ready to lead. You will leave with actionable strategies and a new perspective on community building, one where stepping back is the ultimate sign of success.
takeaway:
Learn how setting up your Discord and Reddit with the right aims, utilizing early engagement tactics, and setting the tone play a role in attracting players to your community

Discover how to build loyalty with consistent, meaningful interactions whilst balancing official announcements with organic contributions

Explore the early signs of success when a community is ready to transition to a player-led space
intended audience: This talk is for community managers, indie devs, and publishers looking to build strong, player-led communities. No prior knowledge is required, just an interest in community strategy and a desire to create spaces that thrive beyond day-to-day management. Ideal for those launching new titles or evolving early-stage communities.

session title: Dev Trading Card Mixer
description: Attendees will create trading cards of themselves, highlighting and celebrating their skills and interests in the industry with artistic flair. Then those cards will be collected and distributed randomly back out to the attendees to enjoy, trade, and even find the creators of.
takeaway: The attendees should use this opportunity to celebrate their skill sets, and passion for the industry in ways business cards, resumes, and even cover letters cannot. And perhaps the passion they put onto the card will draw others to find them and learn more about what they have to offer the industry and each other.
intended audience: All are welcome. Especially those who are trying to feel empowered by the work they do, and find inspiring people to connect with.

session title: Fresh Grad to Art Director: My Journey at 'Love and Deepspace'
description: This session reveals how Infold Games built a top-tier romance art team for Love and Deepspace by developing fresh graduates into leaders through focused training, mentorship, and a developer mindset. It emphasizes essence-driven thinking, player-centered design, and the balance between artistic creativity and commercial goals in a demanding LiveOps setting. Attendees learn practical methods for nurturing artist growth, embracing feedback, collaborating across disciplines, and making design decisions that align with the game vision. This approach enables the creation of immersive, engaging worlds that achieve both artistic quality and business success.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insights into growing and leading art teams, maintaining creative focus under pressure, and using player-centered design to balance art and business goals. The session inspires applying cross-disciplinary skills and feedback-driven iteration to craft immersive game worlds that resonate deeply with players.
intended audience: Audiences of all experience levels are welcome.

session title: Game Audio Programmer Roundtable Day 1
description:
Day 1: 'Audio Programming Roundtable: Game Integration'
This roundtable session will focus on tools and techniques for integrating with game engines. Topics will center around technology choices, game audio engine design, and tool design. Newbies and experts are welcome to come and share their wisdom.

Day 2: 'Audio Programming Roundtable: Low-Level Topics'
This roundtable session will focus on low-level topics such as DSPs and mixing. Topics will center around algorithms, interesting effects to implement, and integrating those effects into workflows. Newbies and experts are welcome to come and share their wisdom.

Day 3: 'Audio Programming Roundtable: Free-for-all'
In this roundtable session we will cover any topics that we didn't get to in the previous two days. We will also have a Q&A session where your questions can be answered by expert audio programmers. Newbies and experts are welcome to come and share their wisdom.
takeaway: Attendees will have the opportunity to discuss audio programming, and to ask and answer questions with other people who are interested and knowledgeable - their colleagues.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended for anybody who is interested in game audio programming, in any discipline. Experts are encouraged to attend all three days to ask and answer questions, and everybody is welcome to come and join in the discussion - or just listen!
speakers: Guy Somberg(Blizzard Entertainment), Tomas Neumann(CD Projekt Red)

session title: Indie Unwind
description:
Wrap up your Independent Games Summit experience at 'Indie Unwind', a relaxed, informal session-into-gathering led by the Independent Games Summit Chairs, designed exclusively for indie game developers. Following the thought-provoking Indie Soapbox session, this is your chance to slow down, unwind, and prepare yourself for the rest of GDC week.

Connect with fellow indie devs, exchange ideas, share your experiences, or simply decompress in a welcoming and supportive environment. Let's celebrate our community, create new friendships, and recharge before heading back into the world of indie game creation.
takeaway: Indie developers will leave this session feeling more connected, grounded, and ready to navigate the challenges of indie game development.
intended audience: All indie devs are welcome.

session title: Production Roundtable Day 1: Process
description:
There are countless ways to produce a game. The Game Production Roundtable aims to move the industry forward by creating space for producers of all levels to come together to share their experiences, discuss challenges they're facing, and build relationships with their peers.

Each day, we'll cover topics across the spectrum of production, loosely following the themes of Process, People, and Career. Potential topics include: Supporting team motivation and fulfillment through production, identifying when a process isn't working, using scope and schedule as tools to make a better product, navigating changes to scope, cultivating team buy-in and ownership of processes, crunch, empowering the team, scheduling, advice for first-time producers, and how to continue developing your production skills.

Another component of the roundtable will focus on supporting the next generation of producers by allowing newcomers to ask questions that help prepare them for their production careers.
takeaway: Attendees will walk away with expanded knowledge and ideas on the ways that producers across the industry grapple with various challenges, and will build relationships with other producers to further inform and support their craft.
intended audience: All are welcome. Producers of all levels of experience who are interested in contributing to the craft's pool of knowledge, and in building relationships with others in the craft will benefit the most. Other disciplines who want to cultivate a better understanding of production are also invited to attend.
speakers: Nico Williams(Unknown Worlds), Shayna Moon(CD Projekt RED)

session title: Punching Across the Room in 'Batman: Arkham Shadow'
description: As ardent Arkham fans themselves, Camouflaj took the task of translating a fully featured version of the iconic IP to VR with great care. Countless hours of research into the lore and an extremely detailed analysis of the previous games was critical to their success. The very passionate (and very vocal) Arkham community was going to make or break how Batman: Arkham Shadow was received. Keeping it 'Authentic Arkham', one of Camouflaj's daily refrains, was non-negotiable. Perhaps most daunting was translating freeflow combat, whose influence can still be felt in modern games 17 years after Batman: Arkham Asylum's original release. In this talk, Ryan Darcey (Design Director) and Troy Johnsen (Principal Gameplay Engineer) discuss how this was achieved along with how they translated core support systems like locomotion and gadgets.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a detailed understanding of how Camouflaj translated Arkham's gameplay (e.g. Grappling, Gliding, Batarang, Explosive Gel, Detective Vision, etc.) from the flatscreen to VR, with an emphasis on Arkham's iconic freeflow combat.
intended audience: Developers pursuing AAA quality action/adventure games in VR will benefit most from attending this talk, but there are no prerequisites.
speakers: Ryan Darcey(Camouflaj | Meta), Troy Johnsen(Camouflaj | Meta)

session title: System Design Best Practices and Roles in Game Production Roundtable (Presented by the IGDA)
description:
This is a roundtable discussion about careers in system design. We will discuss the role of system design in different developmental stages and how it interacts with other design and development disciplines in a team structure. The opportunities and challenges of different career stages for system designers. How to navigate system design with the industry's fast-changing nature of genre/platform/audience preference.

We hope this will be a concentrated space for system designers from different stages of their career and development experience to discuss the love (and hate) of working in the discipline and how to navigate current job markets and funding climate as a system designer.
takeaway: Audiences should come out of this roundtable with an improved understanding of how the system design pipeline works in different development styles and cultures. Exchange system designer career development, sustainability, advancement, and effective mentorship tools and tips. As well as support and connections to survive and thrive in today's market.
intended audience: This roundtable is primarily aimed at working system/gameplay/combat/game designers, but the conversation can also benefit people who are interested in system design as a potential career, as well we game developers who want to understand the system design discipline better.

session title: The State of PC and Console Games in 2026
description: For the third year running, Newzoo presents the definitive PC & console market update — built on exclusive global datasets and player research. Join Ben Porter, Director of Consulting, for a data-driven look at the latest market sizing, platform shifts, regional highlights, and key opportunities for developers, publishers, and platform holders. This 60-minute session will also explore 1–2 special topics based on fresh 2026 data, ensuring maximum relevance. Attendees will leave with actionable insights to inform business strategy, monetization planning, and player engagement in the year ahead.
takeaway: An up-to-date, data-backed understanding of the PC & console games market in 2026, with clear, practical takeaways for product strategy, market positioning, monetization, and audience growth.
