intended audience: Senior decision-makers across the PC & console ecosystem — including developers, publishers, platform holders, investors, and strategic partners — who want to ground their decisions in market data and player insights.

session title: The Year in Live Service Games: 2026 Edition
description: In this long-running session (which ironically always finishes on time), we will shed light on the most important developments in the live service gaming market on mobile and PC/console through the lens of deep dives on some of the most important games and trends of the past year. Each segment of the talk will shed light on challenges and opportunities in the market and provide useful lessons to designers, product leaders, and strategists. The combination of information packed mini-lectures with unfortunately ample dad jokes will leave you groaning, but better informed about the state of live service games and their most important recent developments.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about key market and design trends in live service games, roughly equally divided between mobile casual games, mobile midcore games, and PC/console games, through the lens of 6-8 deep dives. Providing useful information about challenges and opportunities in key segments of the live service gaming market.
intended audience: Past feedback shows the session is useful for attendees at all levels of experience, but most useful for folks that are relatively new to live service gaming (even if they are very experienced game developers), particularly those that have a key role in shaping slate decisions or feature lineups.
speakers: Dave Rohrl(Mobile Game Doctor), Steve Meretzky(PeopleFun), Scott Hartsman

session title: What's New in Game History: 2026
description:
How do games live on after launch? Documenting the underrecognized last stage of the development ecosystem—game afterlives—this fast-paced panel spotlights the most vital new work in game history, preservation, and community practice. Four rapid-fire talks will cover fan-driven preservation, game history publishing, global institutional efforts, and a 'Spotlight Project' featuring The Black Games Archive.

Attendees will leave with concrete examples, resources, and insights into how games are documented, remembered, and given cultural meaning long after release. Whether you're a developer, educator, or enthusiast, this session will inspire you to think critically about the legacy of your work—and how today's industry choices become tomorrow's history.
takeaway: Discover the essential updates in game history you can't get anywhere else. Attendees will leave with a curated overview of the latest preservation efforts, research, and community projects—plus clear references and resources to explore long after the panel ends.
intended audience: This session is designed for industry professionals, historians, academics, and game enthusiasts interested in the heritage and long-term preservation of video games. It's equally valuable for cultural preservationists and industry strategists, offering insights into how games are remembered, archived, and interpreted. A basic familiarity with video game history and its cultural significance will help attendees get the most out of the session.
speakers: Laine Nooney(New York University), Phil Salvador(Video Game History Foundation), TreaAndrea Russworm(University of Southern California), Jason Scott(Internet Archive), Wes Fenlon(PC Gamer)

session title: 'Ragnarok Online 3': The Evolution and Practice of Elastic MMO Cloud Architecture Supporting Million CCU (Presented by Tencent Games)
description: This session, led by the Technical Director of Ragnarok Origin 3, explores the scalable architecture of a large-scale MMORPG on Tencent Cloud. It traces the evolution from a single-server setup to a horizontally scalable, cross-server architecture, detailing how the centralized AvatarService ensures seamless player state continuity during dynamic migration. The talk also covers the intelligent, Kubernetes-based auto-scaling system that automates operations, turning the complexity of supporting millions of concurrent users into stable, manageable infrastructure. Attendees will learn key design principles and cloud-native practices for modern MMO server architecture.
takeaway: Attendees learn a concrete architecture pattern for million-PCU MMOs on cloud: how to model avatar migration across scenes, centralize player state without killing performance, and build a telemetry-driven elastic scaling loop on Kubernetes that survives real launch-day traffic.
intended audience: This session primarily targets server engineers and technical leaders working on online games, as well as anyone interested in MMO backend technology. Attendees are expected to have a basic understanding of the client–server networking model.

session title: Aged by Adventure: Crafting the Time-Worn World of 'Indiana Jones and the Great Circle' (Presented by Adobe)
description:
Explores how MachineGames approached aging and texturing across diverse environments in Indiana Jones and the Great Circle. Ranging across the marble halls of the Vatican to flooded rivers in Thailand, ancient African ruins, and Marshall College, our focus was on conveying age, wear, and history in every material – both old and new.
Using Shanghai, Vatican and Marshall College as case studies, this presentation covers research, reference gathering, and material creation, including base materials, layered blends, and geo-decals for exploring how the team approached aging and wear of the environments. The talk dives into different approaches for weathering, aging, and damage, demonstrating how these elements shape surfaces and reinforce storytelling.
The presentation also highlights practical workflows in Substance 3D Painter, architectural module design, and environment dressing – showing how texturing, subtle wear, dust, and debris bring environments to life while balancing cinematic authenticity with playable, immersive spaces.
Attendees will gain insight into crafting believable, aged worlds through materials and surface storytelling.
takeaway: Get familiar with the MachineGames approach to environment art and texture creation. Learn from practical examples about the team's processes and approach to problem solving.
intended audience: Game developers, technical artists, visual designers, production engineers or anyone working on workflows, integration, or art production will gain insights into game pipelines and techniques for producing consistent, high-quality visuals efficiently.
speakers: Vytautas Katarzis(MachineGames), Antonio Vaz Figueiredo(MachineGames), Michael Tran(MachineGames)

session title: Weaponized Play: How Extremist Networks are Targeting Children in Online Games (Presented by GameSafe)
description:
'As online gaming reaches bigger audiences, it has also become a target for sadistic online extremists seeking to groom, manipulate, and recruit children. The same immersive and social features that make games engaging also create opportunities for exploitation. This is an evolving and escalating form of online child sexual exploitation and abuse (OCSEA): children are being deliberately targeted, with real-world consequences including psychological harm, radicalization, and violence.
In this session, panelists will examine how extremist networks are attempting to weaponize gaming spaces, where the threat vectors of terrorism and violent extremism overlap with OCSEA, which children are most at risk, and what tactics are used to perpetrate abuse and evade detection. Most importantly, the discussion will examine how gaming companies can disrupt these threats and protect young players. Drawing on real-world cases and interventions from panelists leading response initiatives, attendees will leave with actionable takeaways to combat this urgent threat.'
takeaway: Attendees gain practical insight into emerging abuse patterns in gaming environments and how they are manifesting. The session equips industry experts with concrete approaches to identifying high-risk behaviors, responding when cases are surfaced, and strengthening cross-team and cross-sector responses to protect young players from organized extremist exploitation.
intended audience: This session is designed for trust and safety professionals, platform policy specialists, and game developers responsible for player protection. Familiarity with online child safety, terrorism and violent extremism, moderation systems, and/or multiplayer gaming environments is recommended but not required.
speakers: Katia Potapov(Tech Coalition), Rita Fabi(Tech Coalition), Jang Kim(Roblox), Jessa Mellea(Global Internet Forum to Counter Terrorism (GIFCT))

session title: Cutscene Pipeline in 'Love and Deepspace' (Presented by Tencent Games)
description:
To craft an immersive romance, Love and Deepspace relies on cinematic, film-quality cutscenes—delivered within the relentless pace of live-service development. Our core challenge was building a pipeline flexible enough to support artists from film, games, and animation backgrounds, yet streamlined to eliminate friction and technical overhead. Empowering a team of over 100 artists to focus on storytelling, not troubleshooting, has been the backbone of Love and Deepspace's consistent content delivery—leading to award-winning success and over 70 million players worldwide.
This session unpacks our evolving cutscene pipeline—how raw scripts become polished performances with specialized tools, how we standardized collaboration between writers, mocap, and animators to bring AAA storytelling to mobile, and the challenges and solutions that kept our creative momentum high and console-quality experience achievable.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how Love and Deepspace scaled film-level cutscenes for mobile by keeping creative intent independent from engine tech, giving artists real-time control, and automating review. Discover how custom tools and smart workflow let over 100 artists deliver award-winning narrative at live-service speed.
intended audience: This talk is for studio leads, artists, engineers, and project managers interested in scaling narrative pipelines, art-tech collaboration, and delivering cinematic content efficiently without blocking creativity.
speakers: Xianyu Wang(Infold Games (Papergames)), Ji Zhang(Infold Games (Papergames))

session title: Opening Night at the Ballpark
description:
Opening Night at the Ballpark is your all-in kickoff. This is the moment for the B2B games community to come together for tabletop battles, surprise activations, classic ballpark bites, and when the sun sets, trailers and your fan-voted movie will light up the big screen.

Please find more information and a walking map to Oracle Park here: https://gdconf.com/opening-night-at-the-ballpark (https://gdconf.com/opening-night-at-the-ballpark/)

****
location: Oracle Park, 24 Willie Mays Plaza, San Francisco, CA 94107
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Live Sound Bath and Meditation (Presented by thatgamecompany)
description:
Tim Nixon (thatgamecompany) & Caio Jiacomini (thatgamecompany)

Quiet Room 213

Take a moment from the busy show to relax and enjoy the calming sounds of crystal singing bowls intertwined with the atmosphere of Sky: Children of the Light, before sinking into a short guided meditation to help refresh the mind and body. All are welcome.




**Doors Open at 9:00 AM.**

session title: Tabletop Free Play & Playtesting Lounge
description:
Grab a board game and let the games begin! Stop by the lounge and check out a game from our Free Play board game library or take a moment to playtest something brand new. Enjoy a little friendly competition and share feedback directly with the designers!

Check out a gallery of the schedule of featured games here (https://unpub.softr.app/gdc-2026).

Brought to you in partnership with Gamescape (https://www.gamescapesf.com/), Golden Gate Gamemakers (https://goldengategamemakers.com/), and Unpub (https://www.unpub.org/)!
location: Tabletop Free Play & Playtesting Lounge, West Hall Level 3
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: AI-Driven 3D Game Prototyping with Engine Integration (Presented by Tencent Games AI)
description: With the continuous evolution of AI technologies such as reasoning-based LLMs, it has become feasible to enable AI-driven workflows for key processes in 3D game development, significantly lowering the threshold for game creation. This session will demonstrate, through specific game case studies, how to transform simple requirements into game prototypes, generate core logic code, UI elements, and 3D scenes driven by game engines, covering the full development workflow supported by AI technologies. Finally, we will also share our understanding of the current capabilities and limitations of AI.
takeaway:
1. Learn to use an AI workflow for 3D game prototyping by separating logic and presentation, allowing AI to deeply engage in development.
2. Learn to use test-driven development to guarantee the reliability and quality of AI generated logic code.
3. Learn how AI collaborates on UI design, code generation, and synchronization with game logic.
4. Learn how to help AI better understand 3D space and participate in the creation of simple levels and scene interactions.
intended audience: Targeted towards an audience interested in AI-driven game development.

session title: Co-Creating Possibility: The Future of Mobile Gaming Performance (Presented by Arm)
description:
**Part 1 – Patrick Mendenall (Arm)**

Our global research confirms that while AI adoption accelerates development, deployment is often blocked by hardware and software fragmentation and unpredictable performance. The core challenge isn't your vision; it is the cost of navigating platform-specific complexity. You need a stable foundation to focus on the experience, not the device. To support this, we are evolving the Arm ecosystem into an accessible toolbox. We are designing solutions like KleidiAI to help you ship games without low-level optimization burdens. We are advancing Frame Advisor and our RenderDoc extensions to unify graphics and ML debugging. Furthermore, we are enabling Neural Graphics pipelines to unlock visual fidelity beyond traditional rendering limits, focusing on engine enablement to ensure readiness.

**Part 2 – Lukas Medek (Sumo Digital)**

What happens when advanced neural technology frees up performance budget on mobile? You make room for real innovation. In this session, Sumo Digital showcases a new case study demonstrating how Arm's neural technology enables the team to push the boundaries of real-time lighting and visual fidelity on mobile devices. By offloading selected workloads to neural acceleration, Sumo's developers can reallocate precious performance headroom to explore cutting-edge Unreal Engine features, including Megalights, ray tracing, and Actor Sequences, all while maintaining smooth, playable performance on mobile hardware.
takeaway: Attendees will discover how the Arm ecosystem is evolving to solve fragmentation and enable on-device AI. Learn how to leverage the 'Accessible Toolbox' - including KleidiAI, Frame Advisor, and Neural Graphics - to create high-quality, predictable performance and deploy amazing games without the burden of low-level optimization.
intended audience: Beginner developers who are curious about the future of mobile graphics, experienced engineers optimizing for performance, or studio leaders planning your next project.

session title: Designing Hands-First Games: A VR Developer's Guide to Getting It Right (Presented by Meta)
description: Building for hands requires a different mindset than controllers. In this session, Meta shares how to evaluate whether hands fit your project, design patterns that translate player intent into action, and pitfalls that trip up first-time implementers. Double Jack built hands into their game, Maestro, from day one and shares what worked, what didn't, and what surprised them.

session title: Developing Large Procedural Systems with Low Friction and Fast Generation (Presented by Epic Games)
description:
What happens when your world has hundreds of different procedural elements that need to talk to each other? How do you build a large system that is maintainable, fast, and enables multiple artists to work on it collaboratively?

This talk by Senior Tools Programmer Adrien Logut and Principal Technical Artist Chris Murphy aims to cover some of the key concepts behind achieving this in large Unreal Engine projects.

Key topics include the procedural content generation (PCG) Biome Core; runtime vs static serialization; methodologies to avoid source control contention; key performance considerations; and finally, some tips and tricks to get the most out of PCG.
takeaway: Attendees will come away with a better understanding of how to develop large scale PCG systems with a focus on biome development, runtime vs static serialization, methodologies to avoid source control contention, key performance considerations, and tips and tricks to get the most out of PCG.
intended audience: Technical artists and Engineers with a focus on Procedural Content.
speakers: Chris Murphy(Epic Games), Adrien Logut(Epic Games)

session title: Faster Frames, Lower Power: How 'Infinity Nikki' Leverages Adreno High Performance Memory (Presented by Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.)
description:
Modern mobile game development faces significant challenges around power efficiency, with memory access standing out as one of the most energy intensive operations on today's devices. To help developers address this, modern Adreno GPUs incorporate Adreno High Performance Memory—a fast, power efficient embedded memory solution designed to reduce energy consumption while simultaneously improving rendering throughput. By leveraging this specialized memory, developers can meaningfully lower power draw, extend battery life, and enhance overall application responsiveness.

In this session, attendees will gain a detailed understanding of how to access and utilize Adreno High Performance Memory through the latest Vulkan API extensions. The presentation will cover recommended integration strategies, highlight best practices for maximizing efficiency, and provide insight into a reference implementation within Unreal Engine 5. Additionally, they will hear how developers from Infold Games leveraged Adreno High Performance Memory in Infinity Nikki to achieve measurable improvements in both performance and power optimization on Adreno GPUs.
takeaway: Attendees will walk away with a clear understanding of what Adreno High Performance Memory brings to the table, how to integrate it in a modern game engine such as Unreal Engine 5 and how to best take advantage of it in their current and upcoming titles running Vulkan.
intended audience: This talk is designed for engine architects and graphics programmers who are interested in maximizing power-efficiency for their mobile applications using Vulkan. The audience should have some familiarity with the Vulkan API, particularly the topics of resource allocation and command buffer submission.
speakers: Chris Torne(Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.), Srihari Babu Alla(Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.), Yuhan Zhou(Infold Games)

session title: Making Sure Your Game Finds an Audience: Lessons from 28 Years of Sucker Punch (Presented by Community Clubhouse)
description:
It's never been easy to make a game that people discover, then buy, then play, then love. Every year it seems to get harder. And, like most of you, at Sucker Punch we can only keep making games if the games we make find an audience that loves them. Over our 28-year history we've developed some guidelines and models of thinking about our audience that have helped us hit that goal for the 10 games we've made.
This talk shares these ideas along with anecdotes about the circumstances that led us to discover them and how they've evolved since their inception. Having a good model of the audience for our games has played a big part in our history of successful games. I'm looking forward to sharing it.
takeaway: Unfortunately, there's no simple, foolproof way to guarantee your game will be enjoyed by millions of players, but there are simple ways to make this more likely. There are also easily-made mistakes that make a successful game much less likely. Attendees will walk away with examples of both positive and negative patterns, drawn from the experiences we've had at Sucker Punch. If nothing else, the audience will have a pretty good picture of Sucker Punch's game-making culture.
intended audience: The talk is most squarely aimed at creative decision-makers and production, though there should be spillover interest from adjacent disciplines. Which, if your studio is anything like Sucker Punch, means just about everyone.

session title: The Art & Science of Evergreen Games: Running Live Services That Last (Presented by King)
description: Live Service games succeed by evolving continuously, but only when that evolution stays anchored in what players love. This session brings together leaders behind long-running global live-service titles, Candy Crush Saga and Minecraft, to share how they keep games thriving year after year. The teams from King and Mojang will explore how they balance innovation with stability, modernize legacy systems, and adapt to shifting player expectations - all while maintaining trust and delivering consistent fun player experiences at scale. From multi-year updates to everyday live operations, this session offers a clear look at what it really takes to run, evolve, and future-proof two of the world's most enduring games. Through behind-the-scenes case studies and honest lessons learned, Eva Ryott, Ryan Cooper, and Romain Jemma will share how their teams make long-term decisions, structure collaboration across disciplines, and navigate complex technical and creative trade-offs to keep their games evolving with clarity and purpose.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to evolve core systems without disrupting the player experience, align product vision with design and technical planning, and manage change in long-running game ecosystems. All while delivering meaningful improvements that keep millions of players engaged and excited.
intended audience: Game developers and leaders working on live or long-running games—particularly product managers, game designers, technical leads, and producers. Anyone responsible for evolving core systems, aligning cross-disciplinary teams, and sustaining player engagement at scale.
speakers: Eva Ryott(King), Ryan Cooper(Mojang), Romain Jemma(King)

session title: The Future of Video Games is Play (Presented by Amazon Web Services)
description: Join Chris Melissinos, recipient of the 2013 Game Developers Choice Award 'Ambassador Award' and Principal Evangelist for Video Games and Immersive Technologies at AWS, as he explores the most significant transformation in the video game industry in over 50 years. Drawing from a lifetime of playing, collecting, and working within the gaming world, Melissinos will trace the original vision that sparked the industry, examine how video games have evolved into a defining art form, and analyze the forces currently reshaping the landscape. He will also share predictions for where the industry is headed. This keynote offers both historical perspective and forward-looking insights into what video games are today and what they can become tomorrow.
takeaway: This presentation explores the importance of video games, the path we have taken to where we are today, the headwinds that are changing the shape of the industry, and opportunities moving forward as we engage the largest game playing cohort in our history, across more devices than ever before and will offer advice on new segments, engagement loops, audiences, market opportunities, and how to shift our thinking from 'video games' to 'play'.
intended audience: Production, Engineering, Indie Game Leadership, Marketing, Strategy and Business Operations, Business Development

session title: Build 'Living Games' with AI
description: Evolve beyond Live Services into 'Living Games' that rapidly react to player behavior. Google demonstrates how Generative AI closes the loop between player behavior and content creation. Attendees will gain practical strategies for scalable implementation, solving IP challenges, and managing costs to build the next generation of endlessly replayable, responsive experiences.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA

session title: Designing Crush-Worthy Characters in 'Love and Deepspace'
description: This session explores how 'Love and Deepspace' achieved instant emotional connection and commercial success by translating the science of attraction into character design. Through intuitive visual cues, distinctive personalities, and layered backstories, Infold Games' creative pipeline builds characters that captivate players at first sight and foster meaningful, lasting engagement. Attendees will learn practical strategies ranging from applying psychological insights to encouraging player expression, offering a framework for creating memorable, emotionally resonant characters in expansive 3D worlds.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to create instantly captivating characters by combining emotional triggers, visual storytelling, and scalable design processes. They will understand key principles used by Infold Games to craft 'crush-worthy' characters and be inspired to elevate their own character design to foster deeper player connections.
intended audience: Audiences of all experience levels are welcome.

session title: DIY Protection for Game Developers: If You Create It, Protect It
description:
Game developers invest deeply in their creative work, but without a clear understanding of intellectual property (IP), that work is vulnerable to theft, misuse, or expensive legal disputes.

This session provides essential guidance to avoid mistakes that can derail the creative process or threaten the survival of an emerging studio. IP attorney and former software engineer, Benjamin Siders, guides attendees through the process of protecting their creative work, spotting legal risks and knowing when to call in professional legal help. He will cover common, and costly, mistakes developers make with collaborators, contracts and AI-generated content.

Using real-world examples and hypotheticals, Benjamin details what developers can manage on their own and where hidden traps often lie. Attendees will walk away with legal literacy, practical DIY strategies to reduce risk, and the confidence that their creations are protected, and that they are not unintentionally infringing on others' property.
takeaway: Attendees gain essential working knowledge of intellectual property law in game development. The session covers how to protect creative assets, avoid costly mistakes in collaboration and invention agreements, clarify ownership, register copyrights, safely use AI-generated content, and understand which legal steps are DIY, and when to call in an attorney.
intended audience: As presenter Benjamin Siders says, 'If you're going to create it, protect it.' This session is tailored for any developer seeking to avoid costly intellectual property mistakes, from established studios to individual creators. No prior legal training or IP knowledge is required.

session title: G.A.N.G. Demo Derby: Sound Design
description:
The G.A.N.G. Sound Design Demo Derby is a longstanding tradition at GDC, and has been a cornerstone event for new and emerging audio professionals. Attendees can submit 120 seconds of their best work for a detailed critique and feedback from a team of leading audio directors and professionals. They can also participate in an active discussion with fellow panelists and audience members. The Derby is open to GDC attendees across all disciplines.




Attendees can submit their demos using the following link: https://www.audiogang.org/demo-derby-submission/ (https://www.audiogang.org/demo-derby-submission/)
takeaway: Attendees will see how people evaluate a demo reel through witnessing the live evaluation of others' (or their own!) demo reel.
intended audience: Anyone interested in game sound design or interested in improving their own demo reels.
speakers: DJ Meadows(HNDCRFTD LLC), Adam Gubman(Moonwalk Audio), Justin Bell(Santa Monica Studio), Jay Weinland(Interactive Audio)

session title: Game Education Roundtable Day 1
description: Got a strong opinion about what should be taught in game dev education? Loved or hated some things about your own education? Have ideas for what industry needs from educators and researchers? Come to this roundtable and share! We'll bring your input directly back to the game education community during Friday's educator focused sessions. Take part in shaping the future of game developer education so that everyone can be better prepared to make tomorrow's games. The session will begin with a brief state-of-education summary by the hosts, then move into key questions for participants about what's great (and could be better) and how to best prepare future students for the industry.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with knowledge about the current state of game education, and have a chance to shape its future for the better. This is a chance to give back hard-earned knowledge to students (and their teachers) about what you need to know to prepare for working in the game industry.
intended audience: Anyone who wants to share knowledge and best practices that can improve game education for future students.
speakers: Katherine Isbister(University of California Santa Cruz), Gillian Smith(Worcester Polytechnic Institute)

session title: GDC Encounters: Speed Networking Day 2
description:
**GDC's version of Speed Networking. A fast-track to new contacts, partnerships, and future collaborators. Join us on Monday, Tuesday, and Friday.**





**Tuesday, March 10th**

**10:30am - 5:30pm**

Session 5 | 4:30pm-5:30pm (60min)
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Innovative Tools for the Innovative Indies Roundtable: Gamedev in a Distributed World (Presented by the IGDA)
description: Game development has been transformed in the last 5 to 10 years, especially for indie teams. Even AAA studios are struggling to adapt. Indies are agile enough to adapt their operational strategies to use the latest tools to increase the efficiency and quality of their game development pipeline. This session, hosted by the IGDA Indie SIG, will discuss the best tools and strategies for indie game developers to succeed in this new environment. Attendees will be encouraged to provide input on the strategies they plan to use and we will all learn together.
takeaway: Attendees of Innovative Tools for the Innovative Indies will walk away with a set of tangible strategies and tools, vetted by the indie game community, to help them succeed in the current game development environment.
intended audience: Innovative Tools for the Innovative Indies is intended for indie game development teams, freelancers, and contractors. All are welcome and encouraged to bring their best ideas.

session title: It's Not in the Writer's Manual: Q&A for New Writers
description: How do newer writers fit into game design and narrative design teams? Do writers coming from the worlds of film and comics have advantages getting work over prose writers? Is it easier to be a freelancer or employee? Do you have the skills to be a writer in this industry? How do you know when you've 'made it'? How do you put a writing team together? Especially for individuals with little experience and those looking to break in, this session encourages participants to ask veteran writers and narrative designers questions without definitive answers. Anyone working with writers is also encouraged to attend. Whether it's finding the right job, leading writers and improving team dynamics, or establishing rates, no question about the business of game writing is off limits.
takeaway: Attendees will be able to ask questions regarding their experiences in the industry. They may wish to become professional game writers or want insight into working with writers. Not all attendees ask questions, but the variety of responses and the ensuing conversations will benefit newer writers and narrative designers.
intended audience: This Q&A offers newer writers and those looking to break into the industry opportunities to ask veteran narrative designers and writers pressing questions about the business of game writing and how to become better professionals.
speakers: Toiya Kristen Finley(Schnoodle Media, LLC), Nessa Cannon(Independent), Matthew Weise(Fiction Control), Ashley Ruhl(Broadsword Online Games), Andrea Saravia Perez(Deviant Games)

session title: LiveOps Moneyball: 10 Monetization Trends Driving 1.5x Revenue Growth
description: Drawing on AppMagic's daily panel of hundreds top-grossing mobile games, this session uncovers ten LiveOps patterns — five offer mechanics and five event frameworks — that reliably lift revenue x1.5–2. It starts with a crisp market snapshot showing why few new releases become successful.
takeaway: Attendees gain a concise, data-grounded list of ten LiveOps patterns that currently drive x1.5-2 revenue growth, see how they're applied in genres from Match-3 to casual casino, and leave with decision cues and next-sprint implementation tips they can immediately test in their own games.
intended audience: This talk serves mobile F2P product managers, LiveOps and monetization leads, as well as senior game designers seeking data-driven tactics to push revenue and retention. Attendees should understand core free-to-play metrics and basic LiveOps terminology; no statistical background beyond reading ARPDAU and cohort charts is required.

session title: Making Games for Kids Roundtable
description: Children are not just mini versions of adult gamers. Kids' gaming preferences and abilities are affected by their developing cognitive and physical abilities. We will focus on the design of games for kids, including considering children's development at different ages and the implications for game mechanics, as well as on marketing, discovery, and business models. When the target audience is children - it is always tricky. But it's not impossible!
takeaway: Attendees will take away practical learnings from industry peers related to all aspects of interactive product development, not limited to developmental psychology milestones, best practices for game design for kids, and business management.
intended audience: Any and all who make games and interactive products for children (under 15) or aspire to do so!

session title: Preservation Community Meetup
description: Join The MADE for a Community Space meetup with the game history and preservation community. Network and meet with current members of the community and preservation experts.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Production Workshop Part 1: Conception to Alpha
description:
This year's Production Workshop examines every stage of the game development lifecycle, from Conception to Launch, highlighting the critical hard and soft skills required to successfully navigate them.

This session is a deep dive into solving complex, real-world problems. Far from a 'Production 101' course, this is a fully interactive day featuring roundtable exercises and collaborative discussions. Beyond the planned curriculum, participants are encouraged to workshop their own past and current challenges with the help of facilitators and peers.

The afternoon also features a panel of experienced producers representing a wide range of expertise, from Casual and Mobile games to AAA, Co-Dev partnerships, and Publishing. Following the panel, the day concludes with a 'Quick Fire' mentoring session where attendees will have the opportunity to sit down with the speakers for direct insights and advice.



**Hosts:** Brynley Gibson (Sharkmob), Dave Ranyard (Dream Reality Interactive), and Fleur Marty (Gearbox).

**Morning Session Schedule**




· 10:30am – 10:45am: Welcome and Introduction




· 10:45am – 12:20pm: Workshop Part 1: Conception to Alpha An interactive workshop identifying common development challenges during conception, pre-production, and early production, and the critical skills producers need to navigate them.




· 12:20pm – 1:50pm: Lunch Break




**Afternoon Session Schedule**




· 1:50pm – 3:30pm: Workshop Part 2: Alpha to Launch An interactive workshop focusing on the challenges faced during post-production and closing, emphasizing the specific producer toolkits required to cross the finish line.




· 3:30pm – 3:45pm: Coffee Break




· 3:45pm – 4:30pm: Panel: The Many Roles of the Producer (Moderated by Fleur Marty; Panelists: Daphné Moyal (Virtuos), Ella Vance (The New York Times), Shayna Moon (CD Projekt RED), Shelby Martin (2K) and Zachary Beaudoin (T-Minus Zero Entertainment). Experts from across the industry, covering everything from Indie to AAA, share their perspectives on the versatile and evolving nature of the Producer role.




· 4:30pm – 5:30pm: Quick Fire Mentoring (Brynley Gibson, Dave Ranyard, Fleur Marty, and panelists) Experienced producers offer one-on-one guidance and rapid feedback on your specific production challenges.
takeaway: Attendees will learn actionable strategies for tackling complex development problems. You will leave with new tools for your production kit and expert advice on your specific workplace hurdles.
intended audience: This workshop is designed for producers at all levels who are looking for practical tools to solve tough problems.
speakers: Fleur Marty(Gearbox), Dave Ranyard(Dream Reality Interactive), Brynley Gibson(Independent), Pamina Bou(Dream Reality Interactive)

session title: Secrets of the Tabletop Games Industry
description: The tabletop games industry is fragmented, confusing, and full of some the most exciting ideas in all of game design. In this talk Bryan Bornmueller will share advice from my multiple decades of experience and current insights gained both in my current role as Director of Sourcing at Asmodee (one of the largest players in the tabletop games space) and designer of the Fellowship of the Ring: Trick-Taking Game (winner of the Golden Geek for best co-op game 2024) for current and aspiring board, card, and tabletop game designers. He will discuss the shape of the tabletop landscape, what publishers are looking for, practical advice and resources for pitching, prototyping, and promotion of modern tabletop games.
takeaway: Learn practical advise on how to enter and thrive in the world of tabletop games from veteran Bryan Bornmueller. Get tips on pitching from Asmodee's Innovation and Sourcing Director, Tabletop Playtype, Learn how publishers approach development and product design from the designer of The Fellowship of the Ring: Trick-Taking Game.
intended audience: Perfect for anyone curious about the world of tabletop board and card game publishing who want practical advise on getting their ideas to see the light of day on the table. No prior knowledge or experience of the world of physical games is needed.

session title: Secrets to Hiring an Orchestra for Your Game
description: Are you eager to record live with an orchestra or chamber ensemble? This lecture breaks down the entire process—from budgeting and session planning to team roles, recording strategy, and post-production. Drawing on hundreds of hours in studios around the world, Mike will share real budgets, scheduling templates, and behind-the-scenes stories from sessions in London, Vienna, and beyond. Learn how to save thousands through smart planning, avoid costly pitfalls, and get the most out of your musicians and recording time. Whether you're a composer, audio director, or music producer, you'll walk away with practical tools to approach your first (or next) live session with confidence.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a clear roadmap for planning and executing a large scale professional recording session. They'll hear the powerful difference live players make, compare real-world studio costs, learn how to build the right team, and discover smart scheduling tactics to boost creativity and save money.
intended audience: This lecture is targeted to composers at any stage of their career, planning their first—or next—live recording session with a large ensemble. Developers, audio directors, and anyone managing a music budget will also gain insider strategies to maximize quality, minimize costs, and avoid common pitfalls in professional orchestral sessions.

session title: Supporting Thousands of NPCs in 'Kingdom Come: Deliverance' & 'Kingdom Come: Deliverance II'
description: One of Kingdom Come: Deliverance's most prominent features is the deeply simulated open world. All the NPCs are doing their daily routines and reacting to player actions even when the player is long gone. Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 not only quadruples the number of NPCs on the map to nearly 2400 but also concentrates around half of that into a single city. To keep the frame times and memory reasonably low, new techniques for the level of detail of the AI simulation had to be introduced, while making the scripting interface as oblivious to the LODs as possible. This talk describes the evolution of AI LOD solutions and optimizations through KCD and KCD2 from a technical point of view.
takeaway:
Attendees will takeaway that it is possible to simulate thousands of complex NPCs in an open world game and run 60 FPS.
It is critical to have a solid plan and limitations set early in the development.
Among helpful techniques belong thread usage, moving standardized logic to code, and knowing what can be stripped away in various use cases.
intended audience:
The presentation is intended mainly for AI programmers, but secondary also for scripters and other programmers.
No specific knowledge is required. Knowing what a modular behavior tree is may be helpful, but not necessary.

session title: Technical Artists Roundtable (20th Year)!!! Day 1
description: The role of a technical artist has grown from a clean-up artist and data wrangler to someone who is in charge of complex content creation pipelines and asset tracking systems. Increasingly, the lines between programmer and artist are blurring for technical artists. Tool creation, scripting, shader creation, working with both programmers and artists, and managing workflow are all skills required of a modern technical artist. This roundtable brings together technical artists from across the industry to share their knowledge, experiences, and tips for being the best at their jobs.
takeaway: Participants will learn what common skills effective technical artists share and how to apply those skills to their specific company. Participants will leave with a better understanding of tech art and the value it will bring to their projects.
intended audience: This roundtable is open to technical artists who strive daily to improve workflow, increase efficiency, and maintain art production pipelines. Artists and programmers are welcome as well, as their thoughts and expectations will help define the roles of all technical artists.

session title: The Process Process: Empower Your Team to Do Their Best Work
description: This session demystifies how to detect when a growing team needs process, how to roll out process changes, and how to evolve your team to do the detection and roll-outs on their own. Drawing on real examples from a rapidly scaling mobile game studio, Development Director Jonathan Rodgers and Engineering Manager Vicki Li demonstrate how teams can identify early signs of operational strain, translate those signals into practical process improvements, and avoid the common pitfalls of over-engineering. The talk contrasts organically evolving practices with large framework rollouts, such as SAFe Agile, outlining paths to success and how to adapt processes to fit your team's culture. Attendees leave with practical tools for building clarity, autonomy, and alignment.
takeaway: Attendees gain clear methods for identifying when their team needs more structure, designing lightweight processes that solve real problems, and adapting larger frameworks, such as SAFe Agile, to fit their culture. They leave with practical techniques for balancing autonomy and alignment, improving communication, and guiding teams through scalable change by building a culture of continuous improvement.
intended audience: This session is designed for entry and mid-level producers and anyone interested in how to effectively incorporate new processes or adapt existing ones to improve cross-team coordination. The talk assumes familiarity with collaborative development environments.

session title: Tool Design Roundtable Day 1
description:
For day 1:
This Tool Design roundtable is about the UX of in-house and external toolsets. Come and meet, discuss, and find out what everyone has been doing with Tool Design in the last year.
Examples of topics discussed in previous years are:
1. What user experience techniques have you seen best fit for your toolsets?
2. Is classical UX applicable in tools, or is it very different?
3. What differences, if any, have tool service companies seen with their tool designs?
This years' questions may be different, or similar! Feel free to join, and ask.

For day 2:
This Tool Design roundtable is about the UX of in-house and external toolsets. Come and meet, discuss, and find out what everyone has been doing with Tool Design in the last year.
Examples of topics discussed in previous years are:
1. What communication channels do you use to inform users about new tool features? E-mail? Meetings? Videos? Gifs?
2. What is a ticketing request pipeline that works really well for you?
3. In what size team did your tool request/ticket pipeline work best?
This years' questions may be different, or similar! Feel free to join, and ask.

For day 3:
This Tool Design roundtable is about the UX of in-house and external toolsets. Come and meet, discuss, and find out what everyone has been doing with Tool Design in the last year.
Examples of topics are:
1. What user research methods do you use?
2. Do you have a dedicated user researcher on your team?
3. What surprises has your research uncovered in the last year?
takeaway: Attendees will share their experiences, challenges, and successes with tool design. They can expect to leave with a list of actionable methodologies, and real world experiences from other teams, that your team can adopt and learn from to improve in-house tool design.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended for producers, tool programmers, tool designers, and technical artists who wish to discuss and improve the efficiency of in-house tool design.

session title: Turning a 'Dead' Genre into a Breakout Hit: The Story Behind 'Dispatch'
description: Creative Directors Nick Herman and Dennis Lenart share how their years at Telltale Games led to the founding of AdHoc Studio and the creation of its debut title, Dispatch. Using the game as a case study, they'll discuss how they approached building a story-driven experience that satisfies longtime fans of the genre while still welcoming players who never thought they'd play one. Along the way, they'll cover how they worked to evolve a beloved genre, keeping what worked, rethinking what didn't, and bringing in new ideas in structure, agency, and a visual-first production pipeline.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to assess and evolve an established game format, and gain practical insight into balancing innovation with production realities when building on a proven foundation.
intended audience: Game developers, creative leads, and studio founders. Familiarity with cinematic branching games is helpful but not required.
speakers: Nick Herman(AdHoc Studio), Dennis Lenart(AdHoc Studio)

session title: Use Your Game Developer Superpowers to Fight the Climate Crisis Workshop
description: This full-day interactive workshop returns for the fourth time, streamlined and improved to teach teams and individuals how to integrate climate change and climate resilience messaging into their games. Through expert lectures and hands-on design activities, participants will both better understand the climate crisis and unlock their own superpowers as game developers, immediately applying new theory and practice to develop an original game pitch by the end of the day. Join us and become equipped to impact gamers and ultimately change the world with actionable design approaches.
takeaway:
--In the morning, learn about specific dangers from climate impacts and the proven strategies that are already being used to intervene around the world, so you can integrate these into your games, with a breakdown from the producer of Fortnite.

--And then get concrete examples of how every discipline in game development can directly contribute to the creation of fun pro-environmental games.

--Kicking off the afternoon at 1:30 pm, hear from the Game Director of Monument Valley 3 about the team's approach to bringing climate topics to the latest installment of the award-winning series.

--Throughout the rest of the afternoon, dive deep into the latest theory and practice of transformational game design with talks from 2K's Director of Research & Insights, and Dire Lark's Director.

--Learn by doing throughout the day as you work with other attendees to develop a game pitch from scratch that integrates all of the workshop's lessons.
intended audience: Anyone making games in any role. Both individuals and teams are welcome to attend.
speakers: Grant Shonkwiler(Shonkventures), Trevin York(Dire Lark), Jennifer Estaris(Estaris Works), Paula Angela Escuadra(2K)

session title: Lunch and Entertainment in the Gardens
description:
Join us for lunch and entertainment in the GDC Gardens.

Stone Stage entertainment presented by MAGWEST (https://www.magwest.org/):



• 11:00 AM - 12:30 PM: Lacey Johnson
• 12:50 PM - 2:00 PM: Sixth Station Trio






Review the full week's schedule here (https://gdconf.com/lunch-and-entertainment-in-the-gardens/).
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Driving Innovation & RTX Advances with John Spitzer, Vice President at Nvidia
description: Discover the future of gaming and technology in this exclusive session with John Spitzer, Vice President at Nvidia. John will share the latest innovations in RTX hardware, neural rendering, and path tracing. See how Generative AI is transforming development workflows and unlocking player experiences once thought impossible.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA

session title: Agentverse: The Future of Games (Presented by NEXUS)
description: Games have always been experience machines — but for over a decade, we've been optimizing the same paradigm. Better specs, same loop between you and the screen. The next leap isn't coming from hardware. It's coming from a fundamental shift in who inhabits our worlds. Millions of autonomous AI agents are entering game worlds — not as NPCs, but as independent actors that scheme, create, and develop followings of their own. When NEXUS built the first games for them, agents formed alliances, betrayed each other, and broke our servers within hours. What emerged wasn't an incremental improvement to game design. It was a different medium. This session is a builder's account of that moment — and a vision for what comes next: that the people who design entire worlds — their rules, their economies, their stories — are about to become the most important creators alive.
takeaway: A clear picture of how autonomous AI agents change gaming world, firsthand lessons from building the first games for them, and a vision for why game developers are positioned to lead the next era of human experience.
intended audience: Anyone building games who wants to see what the next era of interactive entertainment looks like.

session title: Architecting DDoS-Resilient Multiplayer Game Servers on Amazon GameLift (Presented by Amazon Web Services)
description: Reliable player connectivity is foundational to modern multiplayer games, yet increasingly difficult to maintain in the face of sophisticated network-based attacks. This session focuses on how multiplayer games can defend player traffic - without adding latency, operational complexity, or forcing studios to redesign their networking stack. The Amazon GameLift team will walk through the multiplayer-specific DDoS threat model, common failure modes observed in live service games, and architectural patterns for protecting player connections in real time. We'll explore how protection can be integrated directly into the game server hosting layer, allowing studios to absorb large-scale attacks while preserving gameplay performance and developer velocity. Attendees will gain a practical understanding of how to approach player connectivity protection as a first-class part of their multiplayer architecture, including key design tradeoffs, adoption considerations, and lessons learned from real-world game workloads.
takeaway: Understand how Amazon GameLift Servers approaches player connectivity protection for multiplayer games, and learn architectural patterns for defending against network-based attacks without compromising latency or increasing operational burden.
intended audience: Designed for backend engineers, online services architects, technical directors, and live operations teams building or operating multiplayer games. Familiarity with real-time networking and game server architectures is helpful, but no prior experience with Amazon GameLift or AWS is required.
speakers: Michael Jackson(AWS), Mark Choi(GameLift), Michael Morris(AWS)

session title: Cross-Platform Isn't a Checkbox: Designing Games for 'Fortnite' (Presented by Epic Games)
description:
Fortnite is more than a game—it's an ecosystem where players come with different devices, inputs, and play styles, but share the same expectations for fun and quality.

In this session. we'll explore what it means to design cross-platform games using Unreal Editor for Fortnite (UEFN). We'll look at how cross-platform development has traditionally fragmented design and workflows, and how UEFN reframes cross-platform not as a checkbox, but as a design philosophy baked into creation and publishing. Using examples shown across PC, console, and mobile, we'll examine what stays consistent, what adapts, and why.

Dive deeper into UEFN tools—like profiling, world partitioning, modular design, and systemic gameplay—that support strong design judgment, faster iteration, and sustainable development.
takeaway: Learn how to approach cross-platform development in UEFN as a design discipline—using player-first thinking and practical tooling to build cohesive, scalable games that work across devices.
intended audience: Developers who have or are interested in UEFN development that want to ensure they reach the Fortnite mobile audience.

session title: IntelliScene: Multi-Agent for Reasoning-Driven Game Scene Layout (Presented by Tencent Games AI)
description: This presentation introduces IntelliScene, a multi-agent 3D scene placement system for game production. Its core enables AI to complete scene layouts via 'reasoning' instead of fragile handwritten rules. It encapsulates senior artist experience into data, allowing agents to conduct 'slow thinking'—a form of Deliberative Reasoning. Linguistic agents use Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to parse requirements, generate a scene graph, and auto-invoke geometric solvers.The speech will explain the evolution from the LLM-based 1.0 to 2.0, which incorporates visual guidance (style-aligned image models, VLM parsing, asset retrieval, and 6D pose estimation). It will cover method iterations, results, and implementing multi-agent AI into existing art pipelines.
takeaway: Attendees will learn to design multi-agent reasoning pipelines for 3D scene placement, integrating LLM deliberate thinking, visual guidance, geometric solving, and physical constraints. They will acquire data strategies, system models, and practical experience transferable to their own tools and environmental art workflows.
intended audience: Intended for practitioners (tech art leads, programmers, AI engineers, artists) applying multi-agent and vision-language models to 3D scene auto-placement. Attendees should be familiar with game engines and have a basic understanding of deep learning/AI concepts.

session title: Performance Tuning and Upscaling for Mobile Development in Unity (Presented by Arm)
description:
**Part 1 – John French (Arm)**

In this session, we explore three lesser-known mobile performance problems that might be affecting a Unity game, what can cause them, and how to avoid them. We use our in-house game to demonstrate what can go wrong, how to find where the problems are, and how to fix them. Learn how to ensure that your game runs efficiently on mobile devices and how hardware-level profiling tools such as Arm Performance Studio can reveal hidden performance issues with mobile 3D graphics. This talk gives mobile game developers insight into device-specific profiling tools and optimization techniques, helping you reach a wider audience with your games.



**Part 2 – Dominic de Graaf (The Naked Dev)**

High-fidelity visuals aren't just for desktop games. With the right upscaling techniques, mobile GPUs can deliver stunning graphics that feel next-gen, without tanking frame rates. In this session, Dominic de Graaf (The Naked Dev), creator of popular Unity upscaling solutions (DLSS 4 for Unity and FSR 3 for Unity) shares his experience integrating multiple upscalers, including Arm Accuracy Super Resolution (Arm ASR).
takeaway: Get practical guidance on upscaling in Unity projects, including key differences between popular approaches such as DLSS, FSR, SGSR, and Arm ASR. Learn why Arm ASR offers an effective solution for mobile combining high perceptual quality, stability, and hardware-friendly performance. Expect hands-on insights: pipeline integration tips, shader compatibility advice, testing methods, and how to balance visual quality with device power envelopes. We'll see where neural graphics and ML-powered upscaling are heading and what that means for the next generation of Unity developers pushing visual boundaries on mobile.
speakers: John French(Arm), Dominic de Graaf(The Naked Dev)

session title: Publishing Realities: Funding, Discoverability, and What Success Really Looks Like (Presented by Community Clubhouse)
description:
Bringing a game to market has never been more complex. Is it possible to get funding? Where best do you create community? Do wishlists matter? How do I cut through the noise? What is realistic success?

We'll aim to answer some of these questions and give better insights into most viable paths to market from early to late stage, setting expectations, and the current realities.
intended audience: Intended for All!
speakers: Ben Kvalo(Midwest Games), Jen MacLean(Dragon Snacks), Stephen MacDonald(Magic Potion Games)

session title: Turning Noise into Insight: Data Sifting and Sentiment Analysis for Live Games (Presented by Community Clubhouse)
description: Data has transformed the way we make games; using insight to drill down into what really matters for players. However, with the complexities that have arisen from an ever more complex market what are the best ways for us to separate the signal from the noise? What can we do to insulate our insights from the impact of seismic market events like the launch of GTA6? How can we leverage difference sources of qualitative and quantitative data to get more clarity on what Success Looks like from Attribution, Gameplay behaviour, Sentiment as well as Conversion from Ads, IAP and Subscription that will allow is to understand what we need to make next, able to predict the impact and ultimately get a clear ROI for our live operations?
takeaway:
How we can get the most from data capture across Attribution, Gameplay, Sentiment and Revenue data
How to leverage traditional data analysis alongside ML/AI to get deeper insights
How we can calculate and use 'Event Level Attribution'
What is the best way to understand your LiveOps ROI
intended audience: The intended audience for this talk is game industry professionals who are currently in a senior or decision making role in game development interested in learning more about how to make the most from data insight for live operated games.
speakers: Oscar Clark(Arcanix), Fred St-Amour(Frima Studio), Maxim Kirilenko(Happy Faces Games), Francis Hart(SEGA), Talia Schmuel(Scopely)

session title: UE5 Mobile, Unleashed on Snapdragon: How We Turbocharged 'Neverness to Everness' (Presented by Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.)
description:
This session presents a deep dive into optimizing the UE5 mobile game 'Neverness to Everness (NTE)' on devices with Snapdragon platforms. Featuring large-scale open scenes, dynamic lighting, and high-resolution rendering targets, NTE pushes the limits of mobile hardware. We will explore how UE5's mobile rendering pipeline was adapted to better align with the Adreno GPU architecture, with a focus on tile-based rendering efficiency and bandwidth reduction.

Key topics include integrating Adreno Tile Memory Heap into UE5, render pass optimization, and real-world performance results. The session also covers advanced Snapdragon technologies such as Adreno Frame Motion Engine 2.0 (AFME2), Snapdragon Game Super Resolution 2 (SGSR2), and Snapdragon Adaptive Game Configuration (SAGC), highlighting how system-level and GPU-aware strategies can significantly improve frame stability, performance scalability, and power efficiency across a wide range of mobile devices.
takeaway: Attendees will gain practical insights into optimizing UE5 mobile games for Snapdragon SoCs, including how to reduce GPU bandwidth pressure, improve frame pacing stability, and design tile-aware rendering pipelines. The session provides concrete techniques, integration strategies, and lessons learned that can be applied to other high-fidelity mobile UE5 projects targeting Adreno-based devices.
intended audience: This session is intended for UE5 rendering engineers, mobile graphics programmers, and technical leads working on high-end mobile games. It is especially relevant for developers optimizing Vulkan-based pipelines, targeting Snapdragon platforms, or dealing with performance scalability across diverse Android devices.

session title: VR 401: Accelerating Quest Development with Agentic Workflows (Presented by Meta)
description: Ready to take your game to the next level? Here we'll cover advanced tooling secrets to accelerate your development cycle, squeeze maximum performance out of Quest, and utilize VR native features to their fullest potential. Includes a deep dive into AI-assisted development environment workflows that are changing how we build and iterate.
speakers: Dilmer Valecillos(Meta), Joseph Carroll(Meta), Zac Bowling(Meta), Mike Geig(Unity Technologies)

session title: Breaking Sound Barriers: Navigating and Thriving as a Woman in Game Audio
description: This session explores the challenges and opportunities women face in the game audio industry. Drawing from extensive experience as a Senior Audio Designer and Composer at Zynga, the speaker shares personal insights and industry data on gender diversity and inclusion. Attendees learn practical strategies for women to advance their careers, build mentorship networks, and overcome common barriers. The talk also offers guidance for allies and leaders on creating supportive, inclusive work environments that foster creativity and innovation. By highlighting how diverse teams contribute to richer audio experiences, this session provides actionable advice for professionals at all levels seeking to promote equity and excellence in game audio.
takeaway: Attendees gain actionable strategies to support gender diversity in game audio, including mentorship approaches, overcoming workplace challenges, and building inclusive teams. The session highlights how diversity enhances creativity and game quality, offering insights valuable for audio professionals, producers, and leaders committed to fostering equitable, innovative work environments.
intended audience: This session targets audio professionals, producers, and studio leaders interested in fostering diversity and inclusion within game audio. No specialized prerequisite knowledge is required, making it accessible to all levels. Attendees seeking to create supportive, equitable work environments and improve team dynamics will benefit most from this presentation.

session title: Build Faster, Iterate More: AI-Powered Prototyping with the Model Context Protocol (MCP)
description: In this talk, Brent Vincent and Lynn Gong will showcase the power of leveraging Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers and clients that are poised to revolutionize game development. The session explores how these AI-enabled tools act as a universal middleware, bridging the gap between large language models and game engine functionality. Through practical, data-backed examples, the speakers demonstrate how MCP can be leveraged to automate tasks ranging from content creation and quality assurance to complex build pipelines. Attendees will leave with a clear understanding of the architectural principles behind MCP and its direct application in creating more efficient and agile development workflows.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to design and implement MCP servers for their game engines. They will be equipped with concrete examples for leveraging AI to automate content creation, rapidly prototype, and manage asset pipelines, enabling them to significantly accelerate their development cycles.
intended audience: This session is for programmers, technical artists, and technical designers at all experience levels. It is particularly relevant for those interested in tools programming, build engineering, and AI integration. No prior experience with the Model Context Protocol is required, though a basic understanding of AI-assisted development is helpful.

session title: 'Marvel Rivals': The Untold Launch and LiveOps Journey Behind Tens of Millions of Players
description:
Marvel Rivals achieved 10 million players signing up within 72 hours of its December 2024 launch. Within three months, the game had over 40 million users, peaking at 644,000 concurrent players on Steam.
The Publishing and Marketing Director of Marvel Rivals unveils the operational strategies behind this success, establishing Marvel Rivals as a premier live-service hero shooter.
Attendees gain actionable frameworks for building player-centric communities through real-time sentiment tracking and co-creation tools; executing effective publishing aligned with the IP lore; optimizing content cadence and transforming monetization through transparent systems; and enabling live-ops agility via cross-functional team synchronization.
These methodologies provide a replicable blueprint for launching and sustaining hit live-service games.
takeaway: Attendees will discover actionable frameworks for building hit live-service games through player-centric publishing, including the 48-hour feedback system, creator community structure, and player-led Discord model from Marvel Rivals.
intended audience: The intended audience for this presentation includes game marketing/publishing managers, community managers, game developers, and live-ops practitioners. It also welcomes those seeking behind-the-scenes insights into Marvel Rivals' operational framework.

session title: Praise the Architect and Pass the Ammunition: Health & Damage in 'The Outer Worlds 2'
description:
How does a game designer determine how many Hit Points an enemy should have? How does the designer determine how much damage a player should do? What are some of the fundamental structures of game design that affect this calculation? In short – how do you balance a game?

Obsidian's 'The Outer Worlds 2' is a hybrid FPS/RPG that contains both guns and leveling-up. This genre mashup posed some unique challenges to game balance and required multiple revisions to NPC Hit Points and player damage during its development. At each revision, some design philosophies became clearer and more focused, while others were discarded.

This talk will introduce a design theory of game balance, explain the reasoning behind it, complicate it with genre expectations, and ultimately determine how to make a 'balanced' game using Hit Points and damage. Throughout, this theory will be supported with concrete examples from 'The Outer Worlds 2.'
takeaway: A philosophical (and sort-of-mathematical) framework for how to design a balanced game. A language to better communicate with game designer colleagues. Ideas for how different implementations can deliver on (or frustrate!) genre expectations.
intended audience: Game designers who are not experienced in combat design, developers interested in design philosophy, or anyone who wants to turn fuzzy ideas into a concrete player experience.

session title: Putting the 'Friends' in Friendslop: The Story of 'PEAK'
description: In this talk, one of the developers of PEAK will tell the story of how the game came to be, from a wild month-long game jam in Korea to the astonishing launch that none of them could have predicted. The talk will share some of the insights learned from the experience, including what was successful about it but also some reasons why you'd want to proceed with caution before booking that AirBNB. Finally, the talk will discuss how making PEAK has shifted the perspectives of the leadership at Aggro Crab towards a stronger studio culture– one that can better prevent stress and burnout while also becoming more nimble in an ever-changing industry. Is friendslop the future? You'll have to watch the talk to find out. It'll be peak.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insight into PEAK's extremely rapid game development process that differs from the traditional production model we all know. The goal is to inspire them to explore these alternative methods that can enhance creativity, make the process more enjoyable, and help to prevent burnout.
intended audience: The intended audience is indie developers. Anyone from a solo developer to those who work at or lead a small-to-mid sized studio. Even if that doesn't describe you, I think the talk will still be interesting!

session title: Remaster, Reboot, Reinvent: Case Studies in 'Wizardry' IP Revival
description: This session presents a post-mortem on the successful revival of the legendary Wizardry RPG series. Initially launched in 1981, the dormant franchise was revitalized by Japanese publisher Drecom Co., Ltd., achieving a market scale of over 100 million USD. We will provide a data-driven look at how a decades-old IP not only reclaimed its legacy but also attracted a significant new demographic of 20-30 year old users and achieved unexpected global success, particularly in traditional Chinese-speaking markets. The talk will detail how the development team balanced historical reverence with modern game design, and how strategic cross-media collaborations expanded its reach. This is a must-attend session for any developer or publisher seeking practical, real-world lessons on how to resurrect, modernize, and sustain a cherished gaming IP for future generations.
takeaway: Attendees will discover how a dormant IP can transcend generations and gain new, younger fans. The session provides a data-driven post-mortem on the Wizardry IP's revival, detailing specific strategies for balancing legacy with modern design and leveraging organic, cross-border buzz to achieve global success.
intended audience: This session is ideal for developers, producers, and business leaders interested in IP revival. It offers valuable insights for anyone seeking to understand how to modernize a classic franchise, attract new audiences, and navigate the technical and strategic challenges of bringing a forgotten gem back to the market.

session title: Shader Like a Painter: Building Character Shaders with an Artistic Approach at Naughty Dog
description: What if building a shader felt more like painting a portrait than solving a math equation? In this session, Naughty Dog Technical Artist and illustrator Rosy Fu reveals how classical oil painting techniques can guide the development of artistic character shaders. Drawing on metaphors from Caravaggio's process—canvas stretching, grisaille, glazing, and varnish—matching real-time shading, from UV setup and depth masking to lighting models, color packing, and final polish. This talk reframes shader authoring as an artistic process, helping attendees visualize complex systems with greater clarity and creativity.
takeaway:
Learn to reinterpret traditional painting workflows (canvas prep, glazing, varnish) as shader stages in a PBR pipeline.
Understand how 'controlled errors' (intentional departures from photorealism) can define distinct art styles.
Discover practical examples of stylized rendering techniques used in the Last of Us.
Gain a new perspective on shaders as artistic tools for art direction, not just technical implementation.
intended audience:
This talk is for technical artists, shader developers, art directors, and students interested in bridging art and technology.
No deep graphics-programming experience is required—attendees will benefit whether they're artists seeking to understand rendering, or programmers looking to infuse artistic intent into their shaders.

session title: Why Good Games Fail: The Startup Audit Every Studio Needs
description:
This session gives game developers a practical framework for diagnosing the problems most likely to sink a studio, before an investor, publisher, or the market finds them first.

Drawing on 22 years of experience as a fractional COO, angel investor, and advisor to indie game studios, the speaker walks through seven categories of studio health that experienced investors and publishers use to evaluate teams, often without founders realizing it. The talk uses a simple information-quality lens, Signal, Proxy, and Placeholder, to help attendees assess whether their answers to hard questions are actually holding up, or just filling space.

Tailored for teams of 1 to 100, the session covers founder fit and role clarity, product and market alignment, production and operational readiness, go-to-market traction, business model realism, narrative consistency, and legal and platform risk. Each category includes a concrete red flag and a before-and-after example showing the difference between a weak answer and a strong one.

This is not a theory talk. It is a practical guide to what goes wrong, why it was usually visible in advance, and what you can do about it now.
takeaway: Attendees leave with two concrete tools: a Signal-Proxy-Placeholder framework for evaluating the quality of their own answers to investor and publisher questions, and a seven-category self-assessment checklist covering the areas where early-stage studios most reliably get into trouble.
intended audience: Founders, leads, and producers at early-stage indie studios who are building toward a funding round, a publishing deal, or simply want to make their studio harder to break. No finance background is required. Attendees will get the most out of this session if they have a working understanding of their game's goals, their team's structure, and where they are in development.

session title: You Owe It to Your Players: Creating Value with Effective Monetization in Mid-Core Free-to-Play
description: In this session, attendees will learn how free-to-play mid-core title 'Heroes of History' doubled per-player monetization performance compared to its predecessor, 'Rise of Cultures'. Based on this case study, Lukas will share tools and best practices for grounding games in solid business hypotheses, designing game systems that facilitate effective monetization and avoid cannibalization, and designing monetization initiatives that capture value effectively. Lukas argues that monetization is not a ploy to extract more money from players, but a win-win situation for both players and game developers: Failing to monetize effectively, UA-driven free-to-play games reach a smaller audience, receive fewer content updates, and ultimately die.
takeaway: This session will equip attendees with actionable tools to analyze, review, and design engaging and monetizable games and features that meet the high demands of UA-driven growth in today's competitive free-to-play market.
intended audience: Game designers, monetization experts, product managers, and producers who want to expand their understanding of what makes a commercially successful free-to-play game. While the session is particularly beneficial for those working on mid-core projects, many takeaways will also apply to other genres, including more casual games.

session title: Lunchtime Surgeries Day 1
description: An opportunity for deeper conversations with audio track speakers over lunch in a smaller scale setting. Grab some food and then come back to sit at tables in the round with the day's speakers on the topics that are top of mind for you about the industry, the day's topics, and the questions at the top of attendee minds.
takeaway: Get your specific questions answered, meet other attendees, and ask speakers about their experiences, perspectives, and opinions of audio for games today and in the future.
intended audience: All are welcome
speakers: Damian Kastbauer(Audiokinetic), Scott Selfon(Meta Reality Labs Research), Phillipe Bosher(Independent), Andy Martin, Guy Somberg(Blizzard), Jeffrey Brice(Independent), Edouard Brenneisen(EB Music LLC), Maclaine Diemer(First Name Maclaine, LLC)

session title: Community is Not a Feature: It's Your Growth Strategy (Presented by Community Clubhouse)
description:
Community is no longer a support function; it fuels growth.


As user acquisition costs rise, platform policies tighten, and player attention fragments, durable growth increasingly depends on the strength of your community. Studios that treat community as a marketing afterthought are leaving engagement, retention, and monetization on the table.


In this session, Chip and Cisco will explore how community strategy directly shapes product outcomes, business resilience, and long-term franchise value. Drawing from experience across publishing, live service, and cross- platform ecosystems, they will break down:
● The critical foundations of community design from day zero
● How community strategy impacts acquisition, retention, and lifetime value
● Why players disengage, and how to mitigate churn at the community layer
● Practical frameworks for scaling community from pre-launch through maturity
● The direct and indirect revenue impact of community-driven ecosystems
● Untapped opportunities studios are overlooking in 2026


This session moves beyond platitudes to provide strategic, actionable guidance for teams building games designed to not just survive but to thrive.
takeaway: This chat will enable attendees to understand why cultivating a strong community should be a high priority for every game, with best practices to do so.
