These questions and more will be answered by a group of developers composed of both seniors and juniors.
takeaway: Attendees will come away with a better understanding of the benefits of hiring and maintaining junior talent, what problems juniors are facing in the current climate, and how to best attract, support, and grow those who are new professionals within the gaming industry.
intended audience: This talk centers on the perspectives of juniors but is aimed at seniors, recruiters, and hiring managers. Even so, juniors are more than welcome to attend to gain further clarity on the issues they will face as they work toward success in the video games industry.
speakers: Natalie Checo(Black Tabby Games/Sunset Visitor), Sarah Helen Slovak(Lunacy Studios), Emma Kidwell(Independent), Ashley Poprik(Independent), Samantha Low(Neon Noroshi/Yotsuba Interactive)

session title: Leveling Up at Midlife Roundtable: Staying Relevant and Resilient After 40 in the Games Industry
description: This roundtable, led by career coach and former games executive Adam Hobbs, invites attendees to explore the realities of aging in the games industry. As layoffs, shifting technologies, and age bias disproportionately impact professionals over 40, many are questioning how to stay visible, relevant, and fulfilled in a youth-driven field. Through open dialogue and guided discussion prompts, participants share insights on navigating layoffs, reframing their experience, and designing sustainable mid-to-late career paths in games. Topics include consulting and fractional work, personal rebranding, and long-term career visioning. Whether currently employed, recently laid off, or considering a pivot, attendees walk away with new strategies, shared encouragement, and a deeper understanding of what's possible after 40.
takeaway: Attendees leave with new strategies for navigating their careers after 40, including insights on consulting, career reinvention, and visibility. The roundtable also provides an open space for shared stories and peer support, empowering participants to take informed next steps with clarity and community.
intended audience: This session is for mid-to-late career professionals, especially those 40+, as well as studio leaders and recruiters seeking to build age-diverse teams. No prerequisites are required—just an interest in career longevity, inclusive hiring, or navigating industry change with resilience and strategic insight.

session title: Local Game Community Organizers Forum
description: The Local Game Community Organizers roundtable is for people who are currently organizing events and communities in their areas for independent or professional game developers or people who are looking to start a game developer community. Organizing events and meetups for your local game development community can be a fun and rewarding experience that creates lasting friendships and memories for both organizers and participants. In building those communities, the organizers can often find themselves without a community of their own to lean on for knowledge and support. This roundtable invites games industry community organizers of all experience levels to come together, meet one another, learn from each other's experience in building and growing their respective communities and forming the basis for a support network for organizers around the world. In this roundtable, we will poll the group on topics that they are interested in discussing and then break into smaller groups based around those topics to have deeper discussions with the chance to switch groups to discuss different topics and make new connections.
takeaway: Attendees will have the chance to meet game dev community organizers from around the world, share their experiences with each other and start building a support network to lean on as they grow their local communities.
intended audience: The audience for this roundtable is current and aspiring game dev community organizers who are looking to meet and learn from other organizers around the world.
speakers: Marlena Abraham(Steel Wool Studios/Bit Bridge), Michael Lee(Another Axiom), Daniel Lin(Schell Games/Bit Bridge), Laia Bee(Pincer Games)

session title: The Invisible Layer: Redesigning Communication and Navigation in 'Candy Crush Soda Saga'
description:
In live games, it's often not the feature itself that fails - it's how players find it, understand it, and choose to engage with it.

This session shares how King's Candy Crush Soda Saga is strategically redesigning its Communication & Navigation systems after a decade of feature sprawl, UX debt, and scattered entry points. It's a candid look at system-level transformation on a live game played by millions: one that required cross-discipline alignment, buy-in to address invisible friction, and the willingness to challenge legacy assumptions.

The talk explores how Communication & Navigation is becoming a foundation for improvements in gameplay clarity, personalization, and scalability. For anyone evolving a live game without starting from scratch, this session offers a blueprint for redesigning the layer players rarely notice, but always feel.
takeaway: Attendees learn how a live game team built a system to modernize Communication & Navigation without starting over - by aligning teams across disciplines around a shared problem, challenging legacy assumptions, and investing in systemic redesign to enhance player clarity, unlock personalization and scale feature impact.
intended audience: This session is ideal for product managers, UX designers, game designers, and system designers. It offers practical frameworks for anyone tackling feature sprawl, discoverability issues, or alignment silos. Attendees should be familiar with basic game communication and navigation systems and practices, though no advanced technical expertise is required.

session title: Asian Representation in Games Salon: Where Do We Go from Here?
description:
This salon organizes and elevates the community of Asian gamer and gaming professionals across entertainment and media. After opening remarks and a short panel, we will dive deep in a group discussion.




The topics to discuss will range from:




* How we accelerate the growth and success of Asian talent and working professionals
* Improving representation in game art, design, stories, characters, worlds, and campaigns
* Building a vibrant community of gamers, designers, developers, artists, marketers, publishers, leaders and executives




Together, we can make a real difference and unlock the transformative power of games to improve how society views us.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: AI Bottlenecks & Solutions in Massive Game Engineering (Presented by Tencent Games AI)
description: This session explores the challenges of using AI programming tools in large-scale game projects and introduces a new differentiable AI solution. Attendees will learn how this solution addresses key pain points of traditional LLMs, including context limitations and hallucinations, enabling effective AI implementation in complex engineering environments. Practical case studies on automatic crash diagnosis and automated system development will be presented, offering actionable technical insights.
takeaway: Attendees gain a new agent development paradigm for AI implementation in large-scale game projects, with practical case studies and actionable technical solutions.
intended audience: Target audience includes AI agent developers and game programmers using AI Copilot tools. Basic AI and game development knowledge is recommended.
speakers: Yu Yu(Tencent Games), Qian Mu(Tencent Games)

session title: From Kernel to Community: Building a Modern, Multi-Layered Anti-Cheat Ecosystem for Frictionless Play (Presented by Denuvo by Irdeto)
description:
Maintaining game integrity is critical for revenue, community health, and long-term viability. This panel brings together experts from Amazon Games, Embark Studios, and Denuvo to explore the modern security landscape.

The discussion focuses on the strategic integration of robust, low-level protections—including kernel-level access—alongside sophisticated, data-driven detection methods. Beyond the technical 'arms race', panelists will address the industry's most delicate challenge: navigating community sentiment. The session will highlight the crucial balance of maximizing security efficacy while ensuring an invisible, frictionless experience for legitimate players. Attendees will learn how to maintain fair play without alienating their audience, gaining a comprehensive view of the strategic, technical, and operational efforts required to protect competitive environments while building—rather than breaking—player trust.
takeaway: Maintaining game integrity is a business-critical priority. A layered security approach—combining low-level protections with data-driven detection—is essential to counter evolving threats. Equally important is preserving player trust by delivering invisible, frictionless security that safeguards fair play without compromising the player experience.
intended audience: This session is for gaming industry professionals, including developers, security teams, producers, and business leaders. Attendees will gain insights into safeguarding game integrity, protecting revenue, and maintaining fair, trusted player experiences through effective, modern security strategies.
speakers: Michail Greshishchev(Amazon Game Studios), Tom van Dijck(Embark Studios), Andreas Ullmann(Denuvo by Irdeto), Niels Haverkorn(Denuvo by Irdeto)

session title: In the Era of AI, How Is Tencent Games' Technology Evolving and Innovating?
description: This session, presented by Nathan Chen, Head of Technology at Tencent Games, will delve into the profound reshaping of the gaming industry by AI, based on insights and practical applications from Tencent Games' technology. He will provide a multi-faceted analysis of how AI fundamentally enhances the efficiency of digital content production, enables intelligent and refined operations management, and propels the evolution of gaming experiences towards new paradigms characterized by emotionally responsive NPCs and natural interaction.
takeaway: Strategic insights and a future-ready framework to leverage AI for gaining competitive advantage, transforming game development, operations, and player experiences, as validated by Tencent Games' technical practices.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA

session title: 'Kingdom Come: Deliverance II', Optimizing Performance for Windows on Snapdragon (Presented by Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.)
description: Learn how to maximize your game's performance for the Windows on Snapdragon platform. This talk will highlight how the overhead of x64-emulation may leave free performance on the table, and how porting your game to Windows on Snapdragon captures the full benefits of a native runtime. Includes a real-world case study of the porting process of the award-winning Kingdom Come: Deliverance II.
takeaway: Attendees will walk away with a clear understanding of how they can benefit from shipping a dedicated Windows on Snapdragon variant of their game and will be equipped with the knowledge on how to do it.
intended audience: This talk is intended for C/C++ developers that are interested in achieving native runtime performance for their games running on Windows on Snapdragon devices.
speakers: Tomáš Barák(Warhorse Studios), John Parsaie(Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.), Carlos Dominguez Caballero(Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.)

session title: The Evolution of Real-Time Global Illumination in 'Love and Deepspace' (Presented by Arm)
description:
Love and Deepspace is a mobile game dedicated to providing players with a near-realistic romantic experience. Providing realistic rendering on mobile devices is our constant goal. Global illumination is one of the most challenging technical issues. Mobile platforms have significant constraints in terms of memory, bandwidth, and hardware performance, making traditional algorithms difficult to directly apply. This was especially true after we introduced user-generated content (UGC) gameplay. Players can customize scenes and lighting, making changes in real time. Traditional static baking algorithms are unable to cope with such flexibility.

We developed a real-time GI algorithm based on Surfel and 3D Radiance Cascade, which runs smoothly on mobile, supports dynamic transformations of objects and light sources, and offers performance comparable to mainstream PC algorithms. We precalculate surfel data during the model asset importing phase, avoiding any baking operations during game runtime or scene editing, and achieving near-fully dynamic global illumination.
takeaway:
How to pre-generate surfels on a per-asset basis, how to perform software ray tracing on them, and how to resolve gaps between surfels.

Optimize surfel ray tracing performance using 3D Radiance Cascade.

Significantly improve performance using surfel BVH and a hash cache for storing collision points.

session title: Turning Conversation into Gameplay: Lessons from 'Courtroom Chaos' with Snoop Dogg (Presented by Amazon Web Services)
description: This session will explain how the team behind Courtroom Chaos: Starring Snoop Dogg designed, built, and shipped a conversational AI game with AWS services on Amazon Luna. The talk will outline the design framework behind player and AI interaction, how the team structured goals and feedback around freeform spoken dialogue, and the prototyping process that shaped the final mechanics. The session will also cover the technical approach and backend services that ensured stability, personality, and speed.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with practical methods for creating replayable conversational gameplay and a clear understanding of the challenges in delivering AI-driven games to real players.

session title: VR LiveOps: Sustaining Player Engagement & Monetization (Presented by Meta)
Why We Still Believe: A Conversation with Amir Satvat (Presented by Community Clubhouse)
description: Join Amir Satvat, the founder of the Always Supporting Games Community (ASGC) and Business Development Director at Tencent Games, and Chris Melissinos, Games Industry advocate and the Principal Evangelist for Video Games at AWS, for a frank and illuminating conversation discussing the current state of the games industry and why they still believe it's the greatest time to be a game developer. Backed by data, experience, and historical context, this conversation will focus on the changing landscape of the games industry, changes to embrace, new opportunities that may be overlooked, and where the video game industry may be headed next.
takeaway:
Understanding the pressures that have informed the current environment that video games occupy.
Historical cycles the industry has gone through and what recovery looked like.
The shape of the current jobs market and what the next few years may bring.
Understanding the evolving audience of video games, predictions, and unexpected opportunities.
Celebrate game developers and the industry.
intended audience: The intended audience is EVERYONE! If you are a game developer for 30 years, a student looking to break into the industry, or anyone and everyone in between, this is a conversation for you.

session title: Art Leadership Roundtable Day 1
description: The art leadership roundtable is an open discussion forum for topics concerning the leadership of creative teams. All attendees are welcome to learn from industry veterans as we all seek to navigate the ubiquitous challenges of inspiring teams to their best work, while navigating both change and uncertainty. Come to listen. Come to share. Join us as we learn from each other and build better creative cultures.
takeaway: The roundtable is an open forum for discussion of topics related to creative leadership. This includes setting direction, managing expectations, building healthy teams, as well as developing resilience and adaptability. The moderator manages the flow of topics as they are surfaced with the possibility that notes will be shared with attendees after GDC.
intended audience: This roundtable is open to all attendees. Most takeaways are frequently targeted at experienced developers who are looking to level up their managerial skills or build stronger leadership networks. Veterans are strongly encouraged to attend to share their wisdom and experiences.

session title: Children and Safety Roundtable: An Ever-Changing Landscape (Presented by the IGDA)
description: In Children and Safety: An Ever Changing Landscape, we will discuss the rapidly shifting online social environment and protections that exist for children and vulnerable people of all ages. Participants share and discuss children's safety strategies in legal considerations to design/UX strategies to technical tools, moderation practices and more with real world examples and a closing exercise. Attendees are encouraged to share personal experiences or insights related to moderation and design with online safety in mind.
takeaway: At the end of the Children and Safety, An Ever Changing Landscape session, participants will feel more confident speaking up about tools or regulations supporting processes that include safety planning in the design process instead of an afterthought. Through community building and connection with other game developers invested in children's safety online we can make the virtual landscape safer for all who play there.
intended audience: Children and Safety, An Ever Changing Landscape is for anyone who is interested in the process, policy and community building around children's safety in online spaces. Anyone can work to make online spaces they are in safer and this discussion allows for sharing resources and networking to support that goal short and long term.

session title: 'despelote': Capturing the Feeling of 2001 Quito, Ecuador
description: When we think about media that captures the world around us, we tend to think of photography, film, or literature - but rarely video games. Through the process of making 'despelote', a slice-of-life soccer game set in 2001 Quito, Ecuador, Julián Cordero has come to believe that video games are a perfect vessel for capturing the real world. He talks about how using 3D scans, improvised dialogue, archival footage and ambient sound recordings create a world that feels lived-in. Drawing from personal memories and real places, the game becomes a collage of time, space, and feeling. It feels incredibly inspiring to conceive video games as an unexpected medium for documenting reality, to make games by stepping out of the computer and into the real world.
takeaway: People will learn about techniques for documenting the real world through games, using tools like 3D scanning, ambience field recording, and improvised dialogue. Attendees will engage with new perspectives on how games can capture time, place and memory.
intended audience: This talk is for game designers interested in grounding their work in the real world. It is also for anyone curious about using video games as a medium for memory and documentary.

session title: Game On: Why the Best is Yet to Come for Gaming's Future
description: Simon Zhu, an experienced game investor and advisor, delivers a grounded lecture on the gaming industry's resilient growth. Drawing from a decade of supporting nearly 100 studios worldwide, Zhu shares practical insights from his achievements and lessons learned. He explains how gaming thrives through bold innovation and authentic creator-driven stories, offering strategies to navigate industry challenges. Aimed at veteran developers, studio leads, investors, and aspiring creators, Zhu underscores gaming's enduring potential and motivates attendees to shape its promising future.
takeaway:
Takeaways:
      1     Understand why gaming's long-term growth is inevitable, driven by the 'Once a Gamer, Forever a Gamer' phenomenon rather than capital cycles.
      2     Discover why true innovation stems from 'Fantasy Fulfillment' and authentic creator expression rather than chasing market buzzwords.
      3     Learn how to navigate industry cycles by analyzing recent flops and refocusing on 'Common Sense' - respecting the player's time and money.
      4     Receive an insightful 'love letter' to the industry that inspires you to unleash your potential during this critical reset.
intended audience: Veteran developers, studio leads, investors, and dreamers hungry for hope and strategy. Perfect for anyone feeling the industry's weight or wanting to spark bold ideas.

session title: Machine Learning Roundtable Day 1: Runtime Techniques
description:
From classic ML to more recent generative approaches, we keep trying to push ML further into the runtime of our games and create delightful experiences for the players. Sometimes it works. Sometimes it barely works. Sometimes it really should not have worked at all.

This round table is about sharing what we actually managed to ship at runtime last year. What made it on screen. What survived real frame budgets, memory constraints, and certification. And what we had to kill along the way. Expect concrete examples, uncomfortable tradeoffs, small wins, and a few embarrassing failures.

This is not a theory discussion. It is a conversation about real systems running in real games.
takeaway:
Attendees leave with a clearer picture of what it really takes to ship modern ML at runtime in games. This includes concrete lessons on performance constraints, system design tradeoffs, debugging strategies, and when ML genuinely beats classical approaches and when it does not.

Rather than best practices or recipes, attendees gain practical intuition from peers about what worked, what failed, and why. The goal is to help you make better decisions the next time you consider putting ML directly in a game loop.
intended audience:
This round table is intended for ML practitioners and game developers who are already experimenting with or seriously considering runtime ML. This includes gameplay programmers, AI engineers, technical designers, rendering and animation engineers, and tech leads.

An intermediate understanding of ML is helpful, but curiosity and hands-on experience matter more than formal background. If you have tried to ship something ML-driven at runtime or are about to, this session is for you.

session title: Marketing Department of One: Staying Sane While Spinning Way Too Many Plates
description: When you're a one-person marketing department, the number of tasks you have to balance can feel like spinning WAY too many plates. Yacht Club Games' Marketing Director, Celia Schilling, handles PR, community, partner relations, campaigns, licensing (cameo & merchandising), copywriting, and more for the Shovel Knight franchise and Mina The Hollower. In this talk, she offers a practical and personal deep dive into how she manages multiple skillsets to create a sustainable workflow. Using Mina the Hollower's campaign as an example, she'll break down workflow strategies, reverse calendar planning, task estimation, and more to navigate time constraints and avoid burnout!
takeaway: In this talk, Celia shares how she runs a marketing department solo. She breaks down workflow strategies, reverse calendar planning, and task estimation. Attendees will walk away with real-world examples to use to build their own version of 'doing it all' without burning out.
intended audience: This session is for marketers, students, and folks at indie studios where wearing multiple hats is the norm. Attendees who are looking to improve workflow and manage marketing scope solo will benefit most from this talk! A basic understanding about indie game marketing is recommended but not required!

session title: Mastering Massive Worldbuilding: Production and Optimization of Ultra Large-Scale Maps
description:
Based on the successful massive worldbuilding practices of Mecha BREAK, this session will be talking about how Wenya confronted with significant environmental production challenges.

Wenya will provide an in-depth analysis of how her team achieved efficient production and optimization of ultra-large-scale maps.
takeaway:
Attendees will explore 2 key advancements :
1. How to split the ultra-large map construction and runtime scene reconstruction pipeline into three independently operable production lines: Art Scene Construction , Runtime Scene Generation and Light Baking.
2. Three Optimization Strategies of Runtime Scene Generation.
intended audience: Technical artists, senior environment artists, engine engineers

session title: Mid+ Stage Career Development for Marginalized Game Devs: Surviving the Post-DEI Climate
description:
In 2026, marginalized game developers will face a lot of new challenges due to changes in the global political climate and video game funding preferences. While there are still some resources for people who want to join the industry, the opportunities and resources who those who are beyond 'junior level' have been a lot slimmer in comparison.

Hear the stories of marginalized game developers about their unique challenges after a few years into their career, how they managed to survive in the industry against all odds, and shatter the industry glass ceiling to leadership roles. As well as how the industry can better support marginalized game developers under the current climate.
takeaway: The talk will give hope and tools for other mid and junior game developers who are experiencing similar difficulty in their game dev career. Help current and future leaders understand the unique challenges facing marginalized game developers and what they can do to help
intended audience: Game developers with marginalized identities who want to understand the cost and resources to maintain career longevity, game development managers and leaders who are interested in helping preserve the diversity of the game industry and its creative output
speakers: Monica Fan(Pipeworks Studio), Nina Kim(Rare Ltd.), Leyla Lacombe(Santa Monica Studios), Nahir Fajardo(Dreams of Heaven Games), Michael Jones(Independent), Lyn Dang(Studio Citrus)

session title: Player-Value First: Doubling a Seven-Year-Old Live Game
description: Forget the trendy sales, placement, event template gimmicks. In this case study, a seven-year-old live game doubles monthly revenue in a year by focusing on real game-play value—then monetizing the engagement that follows. No interstitials, no personalization rabbit holes; just systems and gameplay that matter, events that showcase them, and candid lessons from what didn't work.
takeaway: A step-by-step, proven recipe to grow mature live games: avoid distractions, establish a fair ad contract, add a progression system that improves game-play, build events on top, and monetize only after value is more than obvious.
intended audience: Game Directors, Producers, Product Managers, Live Ops Managers and Game Designers working on mature F2P titles on mobile or PC.

session title: So Your Game Got Cancelled Roundtable: Navigating Personal Development and Career Outcomes
description:
You spent years working on the biggest game of your career, only for it to be cancelled. Your studio ran out of money, your publisher wanted to go in another direction, or maybe it happened for seemingly no reason at all.

Okay, sad. But your next chapter has already begun. So let's get a bunch of us in a room and learn from each other how to write it!

For indies, how can you salvage your work and recover lost opportunities? For studio employees, how can you rebuild trust (or find a new job)? For everyone, can we find the positives? How do we mourn, and how do we move on? What can we take with us?

Share your perspective and hear from others across the industry. We'll take a moment to commiserate, but this isn't a place to discuss industry remedies, the focus is on what's next for you.
takeaway: Nothing cures tunnel vision like other perspectives. Attendees will come away with actionable strategies, unexpected lessons, and the perspective to see their own experience in a larger context.
intended audience: Anyone of any discipline, experience level, and/or industry sector who has worked on a game that was cancelled before release, and wants to learn how others have processed and built on that experience.

session title: Soft Skills for Devs of All Disciplines
description: Soft skills are a key part of working with a team in our highly-collaborative industry, but they're often taught indirectly and on-the-job. In this session, we demystify expectations and explore these skills in the context of a work product's lifecycle: from understanding the heart of the work, to managing stakeholder expectations, communicating with teammates, highlighting your contributions, and more. It's focused on context, communication, consideration, and industry-specific tips that will hopefully make it easier and more enjoyable to work together on what we're passionate about.
takeaway: Attendees will leave this session with actionable ideas for expanding and strengthening their ever-transferrable soft skills toolset to bolster their craft and careers.
intended audience: This session is for developers of all specialties and levels. Whether you're a junior engineer or senior artist, a hands-on maker or people manager, a social butterfly or the quiet type, this session has something for you.

session title: Spreadsheets Microtalks
description:
Spreadsheets! Spreadsheets! Spreadsheets! (Now you can't say you haven't been warned)

In these microtalks, spreadsheet gurus will showcase their creations, sharing insights on their origins, workflow, design choices, and the discoveries they made along the way.

Mike Rossmassler will share his process for creating paper prototypes informed by spreadsheets.

Tyler Coleman will discuss how to combine Google Forms & Sheets to create mini paper prototypes using just sheets and survey forms, a low-code solution to test gameplay and UI

Everest Pipkin will discuss using spreadsheets to update websites in realtime, and how this can be utilized both for responsive, flexible webgame development as well as community-sourced solidarity work and resource-building.

Guillaume Pierre will talk about the economy spreadsheet he created for Targon Studios' indie coop horror game Darkwater, simulating every loot drop, every room, every outpost and every district the game procedurally generates.

Ridima Ramesh will share how she drew on the versatility of spreadsheets in the process of developing an immersive strategy simulation; initially to alleviate complexity in early playtesting, and eventually to guide the creation of a companion app that facilitated player decision-making.

Tomo Moriwaki will talk about collaborating on google sheets and how Epic Tavern built directly from the google sheets into the game's data. The spreadsheets were set up to organize the data, but we needed to devise a way to work together on the story of the game in a way that worked for the data but didn't make thinking about the story impossible.

Cheryl Platz will teach you a localization and prototyping magic trick that transforms your Figma files into any language you plug into Google Sheets while demonstrating how spreadsheets can play a role in building a stronger UX design to implementation process.
takeaway: Attendees will feel inspired to view spreadsheets in new ways, having learned innovative techniques and novel methods for organizing and presenting data. They will gain fresh perspectives on leveraging spreadsheets for game design, enhancing their workflow, and discovering creative solutions to complex problems, ultimately boosting their productivity and creativity.
intended audience: System designers, technical designers, economy designers, narrative designers, advocates and anyone interested in diving deeper into the world of spreadsheets.
speakers: Guillaume Pierre(Magic Fuel Games/Playstack), Tyler Coleman(Ludo Systems Inc.), Ridima Ramesh(IDEO), Everest Pipkin(Pratt Institute), Mike Rossmassler(Turtle Rock Studios), Tomo Moriwaki(Hyperkinetic Studios), Cheryl Platz(Ideaplatz, LLC)

session title: Steam Annual Update: The Latest on Steam in 2026
description: In this talk, two members of the Steam Team outline the state of the Steam platform in 2026, walking through some of the recent feature additions and updates and the process we used to get us there. We'll share how the team approaches making improvements on Steam for both devs and players, while also touching on a few best practices for things you can do today to get the most out of Steam no matter what kind of game you're making.
takeaway: Learn what's new to the Steam platform in 2026, and how it helps developers and players. You'll also walk away with some tips for getting the most out of Steam.
intended audience: Developers and publishers releasing games on Steam.

session title: The Universal Language of Monsters: Designing Creature Vocals in 'Diablo Immortal'
description: Go behind-the-scenes with the Diablo Immortal audio team to explore how creature vocals are crafted from the ground up—without relying on human language. This session breaks down the creative and technical workflow of building monster voices, from planning and recording through processing and in-game implementation. Through detailed examples and live session materials, learn how Diablo Immortal's monsters speak the universal language of hell.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a comprehensive understanding of a AAA creature vocal pipeline, from early concept to final implementation. They'll walk away with actionable insight into how to direct non-verbal performances, process and design compelling monster voices, and integrate them effectively in-game—ultimately learning how to build expressive, universally impactful creatures through sound.
intended audience: This session is for sound designers, audio professionals, and anyone interested in the creative and technical process of designing non-verbal creature vocals. Attendees will gain insight into a voice-over pipeline that communicates narrative and gameplay without relying on spoken language.

session title: Educators Soapbox
description: This session consists of a series of enlightening, opinion based microtalks as given by veteran educators and game scholars. Experience different perspectives on topics including: student design of games about 'risky' topics, games and mental health, 5 things academics need to know about videogame law, the loss of international students in game education programs, and ethics in preparing students for the current job landscape.
takeaway: Attendees will get plenty of food for thought about opportunities and issues that are on the minds of their colleagues.
intended audience: Any game educator should find this session thought-provoking and worth attending for the conversations and connections it inspires.
speakers: Kelli Dunlap(Take This, Inc.), Sarah Hays(Take This), Laureline Chiapello(NAD-UQAC), Ashley Guajardo(NYU Game Center), Kyra Wills-Umdenstock(City College of New York/EGD Collective), Ross A. Hersemann(Loading Law)

session title: Peru is Key! - Meetup at GDC
description: Discover the creativity and innovation of Peruvian game developers at GDC Festival of Gaming. This showcase brings together unique projects from Peru's emerging gaming scene — from indie experiments to culturally inspired adventures. Join us to meet the teams, experience their games, and explore how Peruvian talent is shaping the future of interactive entertainment in Latam. Peru is Key!
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Boosting Game Retention with Voice, AI & Acceleration (Presented by Tencent Games AI)
description:
In this session, we will introduce GMES, a comprehensive multimedia solution designed to enhance modern gaming experiences. GMES brings together in-game voice chat, security acceleration, and AI-powered capabilities into a single, integrated platform.

You'll learn how these features work together to drive deeper player engagement, improve retention, and deliver a smoother, more immersive user experience throughout the player journey.

We'll also share GMES's developer-first philosophy—highlighting our commitment to early-stage teams through a permanently free version, seamless integration, and an exceptional developer experience that helps studios build, launch, and scale with confidence.
takeaway: Game developers will learn how Voice AI delivers an advanced feature set—combining in-game voice, security acceleration, and AI-powered capabilities within a unified platform. Attendees will gain insight into driving player engagement and retention, while leveraging a permanently free tier, robust APIs, and scalable architecture designed to support development from prototype to live operations.
intended audience: This session is designed for game developers building multiplayer or live-service games who are seeking advanced communication, security, and AI capabilities. It is especially relevant for engineers, technical leads, and indie teams looking to enhance player engagement, optimize performance, and adopt scalable solutions that support rapid development and long-term growth.

session title: Agentic Live Ops: Turning Player Data into Actionable Insights (Presented by Amazon Web Services)
description: Gaming companies generate terabytes of player telemetry daily, but traditional analytics can't keep pace—insights arrive weeks late while revenue opportunities vanish. This chalk talk demonstrates building an agentic live ops solution on AWS that transforms player data interaction at scale. Using Strands Agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore and Knowledge Bases, we'll show how teams can instantly surface behavior patterns, segment characteristics, and personalization opportunities
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to build agentic AI solutions using Amazon Bedrock that reduce live ops analysis cycles from weeks to days. They'll see a live demonstration of conversational analytics, understand the architecture patterns for scaling player segmentation from 100 to 1,000+ segments, and gain an actionable implementation strategy.
intended audience: This session targets data teams, analytics teams, live ops teams, and product managers at game studios and publishers struggling with slow analytics workflows and manual player segmentation. Basic familiarity with live operations concepts and AWS services is helpful but not required—we'll explain core concepts before diving into technical implementation.

session title: Mobile Development Best Practices, 'Delta Force' Mobile (Presented by Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.)
description:
With the rise of integrated PC and mobile development, maintaining high-quality visuals on mobile while managing complex PC assets is a critical balancing act. This talk reveals how the Delta Force team tackles the trade-offs between performance, visual quality, and R&D efficiency.

We will share our journey in optimizing high-complexity scenes for mobile platforms, covering key areas such as asset pipeline management, memory usage, and solutions of CPU/GPU/Memory/Bandwidth bottlenecks.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a comprehensive understanding of the challenges and detailed solutions for resource recomposition pipeline for complex scenes, CPU and memory management, GPU strategies for graphics-intensive titles, and methods for achieving extreme bandwidth efficiency on mobile devices.
intended audience: This session is designed for Engine Architects, Graphics Programmers, Technical Artists, and Level Artists. The talk will cover the technical details of scaling high-fidelity PC content down to mobile platforms, focusing on how to achieve strict performance targets across a wide range of devices.
speakers: Guihong Xu(TiMi Studios), Walker Wang(TiMi Studios), Jane Tian(Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.)

session title: Staying Ahead of the Game: The Power of Early Access to Emerging Technology (Presented by Arm)
description:
In the fast-evolving world of game development, timing is everything. Early access to new technology like SDKs, plugins, engine integrations, and all-new technologies is the difference between following trends and setting them. In this session, Enduring Games shares real-world benefits from working closely with technology partners, including Arm and Unreal Engine, to integrate the latest hardware features before launching games.

We walk through the typical lifecycle of a game (concept, mid-development, and release, variations like ports and remasters) and show how factoring in early access to technology enables smarter planning, smoother integration, and better performance.

Through candid case studies and practical examples, we explore the opportunities and challenges that come with early access programs, including managing third-party dependencies and providing actionable feedback to hardware partners. We also explore forward-looking technologies such as AI/ML integration, ray-tracing, and next-generation rendering techniques, illustrating how these innovations should transform production pipelines and in-game quality.
takeaway:
Learn how early access accelerates innovation and technical readiness for next-gen experiences.

Understand the practicalities of early integration, from asset and pipeline changes to collaborative workflows with hardware partners.

See practical examples of how AI, ray tracing, and other advanced features can be successfully adopted in live Unreal Engine projects.
speakers: Adam Creighton(Enduring Games), Kevin Nappoly(Enduring Games)

session title: The AI Design Stack: Agents, 3D Generation, and Beyond (Presented by Tencent Games AI)
description: This session explores how agentic AI and 3D generation reshape game design workflows. The speaker presents an end-to-end demo where a 'design agent' helps define a feature (e.g., a wandering merchant), grounds it in game lore and constraints, and orchestrates multi-step workflows for quests, economy, and content briefs. In parallel, a 3D generation pipeline rapidly produces and optimizes props—from rough exploration meshes to topology- and texture-tuned assets ready for tech-art review.
takeaway: Attendees learn where AI agents already work well in game development, where they still fail or require heavy human oversight, and concrete patterns for using agentic tools and 3D generation to expand design exploration without surrendering creative control.
intended audience: Designers, system designers, and design-minded producers who are exploring practical ways to integrate AI agents and 3D generation into their workflows, plus technically curious artists and AI engineers interested in agentic patterns that support, rather than replace, human-driven game design.

session title: 3rd Annual Game Developer's Concert
description:
The Developer's Concert returns for its 3rd edition at the GDC Festival of Gaming 2026!

Now taking place after conference hours as an official GDC Nights event, come experience this unique show at the Main Stage on Tuesday evening of GDC week, for an awe-inspiring night of music led by the Grammy-nominated and BAFTA-winning composer Austin Wintory. Known for his groundbreaking scores for Journey, Abzû, The Banner Saga, Sword of the Sea, Stray Gods, and many more, Austin has redefined the role of music in games, earning global acclaim for his innovative and emotionally resonant compositions.

Past editions have featured music from iconic games past and present, honored industry legends, and showcased original medleys of the most influential game scores. This year, the live orchestra returns with a new set of surprise special guests to bring even more unforgettable moments to life.

Don't miss this celebration of the artistry and legacy of video games on Tuesday, March 10th at 6:30-8pm.

Early access to concert seating for Game Changer passholders begins at 5:45pm. Doors open to all festival passholders at 6pm. We recommend getting there early to secure seats for you and your GDC crew. Your buddies will thank you later!

session title: Latinx Game Awards 2026, Presented by Latinx Games Festival
description: The Latinx Game Awards celebrates excellence in games created by Latino/a/x/e and Latin American developers and studios worldwide. Presented by Latinx Games Festival in partnership with Informa, the 2026 ceremony marks the first-ever in-person Latinx Game Awards at GDC Festival of Gaming. The evening will honor outstanding achievements across categories including Game of the Year, Excellence in Visual Art, Excellence in Audio, Excellence in Narrative, and more, recognizing creativity, storytelling, community empowerment, and industry impact. Join us as we amplify Latino/a/x/e and Latin American voices in the global games industry and celebrate the next generation of creators.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: GDC Festival Hall Open!
description:
The GDC Festival of Gaming 2026 marks a historic milestone as it celebrates its 40th edition by bringing together the global game industry in a celebration of innovation, creativity, and collaboration across 5 days.

The opening of the Festival Hall in South Moscone Center on Wednesday, March 11th unlocks more engaging content across 5 stages, interactive spaces, and community networking to further connect with industry professionals and and learn in a myriad of ways!
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Base Camp: Day 1
description: **Formerly known as GDC Connect, this dedicated lounge in the Festival Hall offers space for both casual mingling and pre-arranged meetings scheduled through the event app.**
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Boss Rush Live Podcast Recording
description: Join us for a live recording of the Boss Rush podcast with game industry veterans Dean Takahashi, Mark DeLoura, Susan Cummings, and Chris Melissinos.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Tabletop Free Play & Playtesting Lounge
description:
Grab a board game and let the games begin! Stop by the lounge and check out a game from our Free Play board game library or take a moment to playtest something brand new. Enjoy a little friendly competition and share feedback directly with the designers!

Check out a gallery of the schedule of featured games here (https://unpub.softr.app/gdc-2026).

Brought to you in partnership with Gamescape (https://www.gamescapesf.com/), Golden Gate Gamemakers (https://goldengategamemakers.com/), and Unpub (https://www.unpub.org/)!
location: Tabletop Free Play & Playtesting Lounge, West Hall Level 3
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Building a Co-Playable Character: 'PUBG' Ally, an AI Teammate Powered by NVIDIA ACE (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: Krafton Deep Learning engineers and NVIDIA experts deep dive into PUBG: Ally, a real-time AI companion that talks, jokes, reacts to player commands and brings extra fun and personality to every match. This session details the implementation of a complete conversational pipeline—spanning speech-to-text, small language model, and text-to-speech —powered by NVIDIA ACE. Attendees will learn how cutting-edge models were integrated and explore the latency reduction techniques required for seamless combat interaction.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a comprehensive understanding of the PUBG: Ally architecture, examining how the full ASR, SLM, and TTS pipeline functions in a live environment. The session will provide technical details on optimizing inference performance using NVIDIA Nemotron, while demonstrating practical methodologies for calculating and reducing latency metrics to ensure seamless player interaction.
intended audience:
AI and Gameplay Programmers interested in integrating Generative AI into real-time game loops.

Technical Directors evaluating architectures for on-device inference.

ML Engineers looking to optimize ASR, SLM, and TTS pipelines for low-latency interactive applications.

session title: Building for the Future with Xbox (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description: 2026 marks the 25th anniversary of Xbox, a milestone that reflects a legacy of innovation, community, and the joy of play. From our first Xbox console to Xbox Series X|S, from Xbox Live, to Xbox Game Pass, and Xbox Cloud Gaming, and the recent launch of the ROG Xbox Ally, Xbox has continually evolved to meet players where they are. As we look ahead, we remain focused on empowering game creators and enabling seamless play across devices. Join us for a conversation about the vision shaping the future of Xbox and how we're building a more flexible, connected future for game creators and players everywhere.
takeaway:
Developers will learn:
- How Xbox's 25-year evolution—from console to cloud—shapes its future platform vision
- How innovations like Game Pass, Cloud Gaming, and ASUS ROG Xbox Ally enable play across devices
- How Xbox is empowering creators with a more flexible, connected ecosystem.
intended audience: Game developers, technical leaders, and studio decision‑makers building for console, PC, cloud, or emerging devices who want strategic insight into Xbox's platform direction, tools, and long‑term vision—especially those interested in creating seamless, connected player experiences and scaling games across a flexible, evolving ecosystem.

session title: Built for Games: Explore Apple Hardware and Software for Game Developers (Presented by Apple)
description: Discover how you can create incredible gaming experiences across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS with Apple tools and technologies. Get a comprehensive technical overview of the unified architecture, tools, and system features used to build, scale, and deliver high-performance games across Apple devices. Find out how to boost performance, streamline launches, and create seamless gameplay. And explore how to port PC and console titles, craft mobile masterpieces, and create innovative spatial computing experiences.
speakers: Allan Schaffer(Apple), Alexey Vinogradov(Apple), Rich Forster(Apple), Charlyn Keating(Apple)

session title: Maximize Performance: Porting, Profiling, and 16KB Page Size Innovations for Android Games (Presented by Google Play)
description:
Ready to bring your PC game to Android? Need to optimize a demanding game for Android? In this session, Google's Android team and Emil Kjæhr from Funday Games (creator of Deep Rock Galactic: Survivor) share real-world lessons on porting, profiling, and performance optimization. From navigating common pitfalls to leveraging the latest Android features and tools, this session offers practical strategies for developers looking to optimize their games to run well on Android.

Topics include:


• State of Vulkan on Android:** **A quick recap of how Vulkan is gaining adoption by high performance games, and what to expect for future devices.** **
• Porting PC Games: Case studies, essential steps, and how to avoid the mistakes that slow down your launch.
• New Game Profiler: An exclusive first look at Google's upcoming system and GPU profiler for improving performance across diverse architectures.
• 16 KB Page Size: How Android's memory page size support boosts performance, now enabled through Unity (since 2021) and Unreal (since 5.6).
takeaway: Attendees will leave with insights on porting strategies, new tools to diagnose performance issues, and a clear understanding of how to optimize for Android's latest features.
intended audience: This session is intended for game developers, engineers, and technical leads working on or considering high performance graphically demanding games like PC to mobile ports. Familiarity with game engines like Unity or Unreal is helpful but not required.
speakers: Val O'Donnell(Google Play), Simon Cooke(Google Play), Steven Moreland(Google Play), Mark Dochtermann(Google), Emil Kjæhr(Funday Games)

session title: Test Like the Top Studios: Actionable Player Insights in 60 Minutes (Presented by PickFu, Inc.)
description: Bring your laptop and get real player feedback on your game elements in this interactive workshop led by Heather De Bernardi, Director of Product, Growth at Scopely. Learn how leading studios use PickFu to validate game mechanics, app store assets, art styles, new markets, and more. You'll walk away with practical workflows, fresh insights, and a clear framework for incorporating fast player feedback into your own development process.
takeaway: Attendees will run free PickFu consumer research tests during the session and walk away with real audience insights on their game concepts, designs, marketing, and more. They'll also gain practical workflows, tips, and best-practice testing methods they can immediately bring back to their teams.
intended audience: Anyone can benefit from data-driven game insights – but this session will be especially helpful for product managers, design teams, live ops, and marketing.

session title: The State of the VR Ecosystem: Building a Sustainable Future (Presented by Meta)
description: In this session, Chris Pruett (Director of Games, Meta) shares a candid look at how VR is evolving and what's next on Meta's roadmap. He'll cover changing player behavior, improvements to the Quest store, and details on the size of the ecosystem overall. This session will also cover the common characteristics of recent hits, platform trends, an update on Oculus Publishing, and Meta's plans as it continues to focus on building a sustainable business for game developers.

session title: Bridging East and West Roundtable: Opportunities for Global Game Collaboration (Presented by the IGDA)
description: In today's global game industry, East meets West more than ever, making cross-regional collaboration increasingly essential. This roundtable, hosted by the IGDA Chinese in Games SIG, is designed as a discussion-first session where attendees actively exchange perspectives on how Eastern and Western segments of the industry can collaborate more effectively. Guided by discussion prompts from two co-moderators, the session focuses on three high-impact areas: co-development, publishing and market entry, and investment and partnerships.
takeaway: By the end of this session, participants will have gained a broader understanding of how to navigate and leverage East-West collaborations in game development. They will come away with concrete ideas and best practices for working across cultures, whether in co-production, publishing, live ops, or team management, and a clearer sense of the opportunities (and common pitfalls to avoid) when bridging the Eastern and Western game markets. Attendees will also leave feeling inspired and better equipped to engage in cross-border projects, armed with insights from industry peers who have first-hand experience in making global collaborations work.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended for a general GDC audience – game developers, publishers, investors, and students – who are interested in the intersection of Eastern and Western game industries. Whether you have direct experience in cross-border game projects or are simply curious about how to collaborate across cultures, you're welcome to join. No specialized knowledge of the Chinese or Western markets is required; attendees just need an openness to discuss global opportunities, share experiences, and learn about best practices for bridging cultural and market divides in game development.
speakers: Bo Mei(GoodDay Solution), Chase Shi(Blizzard Entertainment)

session title: Constructive Destruction: Fusing Voxel Tech and 3D Action Platforming in 'Donkey Kong Bananza'
description: Oh, Banana! Developers Kenta Motokura and Tatsuya Kurihara discuss how they brought Donkey Kong gameplay into 3D with the Donkey Kong Bananza game on the Nintendo Switch 2 system. Stemming from their experience developing Super Mario Odyssey, the developers will peel back the layers of their game design approach for Donkey Kong Bananza and dig into how DK's action-platforming world incorporates voxel technology and destruction-based gameplay where virtually everything in sight can be destroyed.
takeaway: Attendees will discover how the development team created a 3D action-platforming Donkey Kong game that fuses voxel technology with destruction-based gameplay for Nintendo Switch 2. They'll also gain valuable insights into designing a game world where virtually everything in sight can be destroyed.
intended audience: Whether you're a game developer or want to become one, this talk will provide you with insights from the development team behind the Donkey Kong Bananza game and their approach to creating a 3D action-platforming Donkey Kong game on Nintendo Switch 2.
speakers: Kenta Motokura(Nintendo Co., Ltd.), Tatsuya Kurihara(Nintendo Co., Ltd.)

session title: Developing Ethical Games: Why and How
description:
Building a sustainable and ethical gaming industry is not just the right thing to do, it is a solid business decision. Research reveals compelling financial incentives for implementing ethical workplace standards in game development. For example, employee burnout in the U.S. is estimated to cost somewhere between $4,000 and $21,000 per worker, per year, while the game industry faces particularly acute burnout challenges given crunch culture. Studios have also been facing fines from the FTC or European Union regulators that have reached hundreds of thousand dollars for exploiting players (e.g. dark patterns) or not protecting them enough in online spaces.


The game industry can experience unsustainable development cycles, legal challenges, and bad retention metrics (e.g. toxicity in online games). This panel will share recent progress in the development of a code of ethics crafted by the Ethical Games initiative that aims to improve workplace, design, and monetization standards that will benefit players, workers, and businesses alike. This initiative is a partnership between Thriving in Games Group (formerly The Fair Play Alliance), KU Leuven's Digital Society Institute (DigiSoc), and includes game UX strategist Celia Hodent, Dr. Rachel Kowert, and developmental psychologist Fran Blumberg as active members. To date, members of the initiative have been refining a code of ethics for the game industry through consultation with game industry leaders, educators, researchers, and regulatory bodies. This panel will share the finalized code of ethics and current efforts compiling a 'do's and don'ts' to guide game developers in crafting more ethical and long-term thriving games.
takeaway: The finalized code of ethics from the Ethical Games initiative. Some guidelines to create more ethical games, for players and for industry workers. Business case for better ethics in our industry.
intended audience: This panel is for everyone but industry decision makers will make the most out of it.
speakers: Celia Hodent(Independent), Rachel Kowert(University of Cambridge)

session title: Developing Supports for Queer Game Developers (Presented by the IGDA)
description: In our session, 'Developing Supports for Queer Game Developers', the LGBTQ+ SIG will discuss the current issues facing queer game developers in the USA and internationally, offering strategies for how networks of support can be built and supported to push against and overcome them. The session will be divided between a presentation and open discussion, providing opportunities for attendees to learn about various advocacy organisations and effective strategies, along with a space to voice their own personal experiences and opinions on best practice. It is requested that no photos or videos are taken during the session.
takeaway: Attendees will come away from our session with an understanding of issues facing queer game developers both nationally and internationally, and a developed knowledge of strategies for community building in the game industry.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended primarily for those who identify as queer and are involved in the games industry.

session title: Economics of a $1B Cosmetic Economy
description: Phillip Black explores the core economics and strategies behind billion-dollar cosmetic economies in his session, 'Finding The $1 Billion Cosmetic Economy.' Leveraging extensive experience from Electronic Arts (Battlefield, Knockout City) and numerous PC and console titles, Phillip introduces the economic fundamentals underpinning successful cosmetic monetization, such as Veblen goods, social hierarchy, and inflation dynamics. He provides detailed, practical examples illustrating how cosmetics drive sustained revenue through rarity systems, mirrors and stages, strategic bundling, and expanded cosmetic offerings like player passports and titles. Phillip also evaluates the impacts of distribution methods (battle pass, direct store, loot boxes), supported by unique data from titles such as Fortnite, Valorant, and CS:GO.
takeaway: Attendees leave equipped with actionable strategies to improve cosmetic economies not only for monetization but also player engagement. This includes fundamental cosmetic design and distribution strategy. Attendees will also leave with a foundational model to examine future cosmetic games economies.
intended audience: This session targets product managers, game designers, live-service game economies, and building cosmetic progression systems across both PC/Console and mobile titles. Attendees should be interested in live service mechanics. Those managing or launching games leveraging cosmetic revenue will particularly benefit from insights into economic modeling, rarity management, and effective distribution strategies.

session title: G.A.N.G. Demo Derby: Voice Over
description:
Making a GDC 2026 Debut is the G.A.N.G. Voice Over Demo Derby! Attendees can submit 90 seconds of their best work for a detailed critique and feedback from a team of leading audio directors and professionals. They can also participate in an active discussion with fellow panelists and audience members. The Derby is open to GDC attendees across all disciplines




Attendees can submit their demos using the following link: https://www.audiogang.org/demo-derby-submission/ (https://www.audiogang.org/demo-derby-submission/)
takeaway: Attendees will hear a variety of voice over demos, and see how professionals involved in casting and hiring voice actors react to and evaluate the demos.
intended audience: Any one with an interest in voice acting, casting, directing or in creating voice over demos.
speakers: Nick Hartman(Sound Lab), Morla Gorrondona(Independent), Tom Keegan(Independent), Alyssa Gentile(Riot Games)

session title: Game Design Documentation Bootcamp​
description:
This workshop develops an overlooked skill in game design: Communicating through clear and effective internal documentation. Attendees explore how well crafted documents support collaboration, align teams, and articulate intent and vision across disciplines and development phases.

Through a mix of individual and group exercises, attendees draft, structure, revise, and analyze different types of documentation, featuring a selection of industry samples, guided evaluation with rubrics, and discussion of documentation best practices.
takeaway: Attendees are given hands-on experience with key elements of specific documentation types, with a clear understanding of best practices. They take away a framework for deciding what document to use for what purpose, rubrics for each type of documentation that break down their goals and uses, and are given worksheets and examples that they can reference.
intended audience: This workshop is intended for entry-level designers to gain literacy and understanding of documentation, as well as mid-level designers who have worked with documentation before but are now starting to create their own.
speakers: Nina Kim(Rare Ltd), Ziz Simoens(Warcradle Studios), Ben Sabin(Unskippable Dialogue), Jefferey Sparks(Mythwright)

session title: Game Narrative Review Competition Winners: Poster Microtalks (Session A)
description:
Every year, students at leading game design programs conduct a detailed analysis of the narrative elements of a game title of their choice as part of the Game Narrative Review Competition. The resulting papers are submitted to the GDC narrative board for review, and the submitters with the most promising entries are then invited to create a digital poster as a visual synopsis of their review.




Successful digital poster submissions are declared winners and exhibited at the GDC. Each winner will be given 5 minutes on stage to discuss their project in front of a live GDC audience.




This year's winners have explored everything from big-budget blockbusters to smaller, critically acclaimed indie titles—presenting complex and detailed analyses that foreshadow the contributions they will likely make to the field of game narrative over the course of their careers.




We're proud to announce that this year's winners are:





**Platinum Winners**

Anna Green, Kennesaw State University (Bayonetta)*

Basil Tutza, Drexel University (IMMORTALITY)*

Qi Ren Soo, DigiPen Institute of Technology (Flower)


**Gold Winners**

Aleksandra Tretiakova, Sogang University (The Last of Us Part II)

Allison Lee, University of Southern California (Consume Me)*

Ben Yu, University of Southern California (Klonoa 2: Lunatea's Veil)

Blythe Watson, Sheridan College (Minecraft)

Cary Shin, DigiPen Institute of Technology (Pokémon Black & White)*

Hannah Geissler, Sogang University (Planet of Lana)

Isaiah M. Singleton, Drexel University (Metal Garden)

Jeff Chen, Rochester Institute of Technology (In Stars and Time)*

Sean Erren, Sheridan College (There Is No Game: Wrong Dimension)

Theodoros-Thirimachos Davarakis, Northeastern University (Slay the Princess)

Tyler Majewski, Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute (Fran Bow)*

Violet Li, Sheridan College (Stray Gods: The Roleplaying Musical)*

Xinyu (Ella) Zhang, Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute (Cyberpunk 2077)

Zac Millman, Drexel University (PEAK)*
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Improv Games for Game Developers
description:
Step away from your computer screens and into a circle of creativity! This hands-on session uses improv and actor games to build connection, spark creative thinking, and strengthen your storytelling muscles. Led by an experienced improv instructor, actor, and magician, attendees will explore how spontaneity and collaborative play can unlock new perspectives in their work.

The session will include spontaneity games, brain fry games, narrative exercises, and some optional scene-focused exercises. Attendees should come with an open mind, ready to be silly, think differently, and discover how the principles of improv can enhance brainstorming and team dynamics in their daily work.
takeaway: Attendees will come away with a set of improv games they can take back to their studios as well as a greater appreciation of playing together.
intended audience: Anyone and everyone is welcome to attend this session.

session title: Inside Indie (Self) Publishing
description: How many times have you posed a question like this: 'What do indie publishers think about?' 'What do the big platforms or storefronts they work with think about?' And most importantly, what does that mean for people who make games? Rebekah Saltsman, who has been self publishing Finji internal development and published titles for 15 years alongside storefronts and platforms big and small, will set a foundation for what dev teams should know about our volatile industry which spans publishing access, available development funds, decreased access to co-marketing agreements and their partners in any business development relationship. She will also describe the how it is necessary that small to midsized developers should understand the skills and principles required to self-publish. This talk stresses the importance that both new devs and experienced devs understand and build these skills to support either their own team with self publishing or their business partner who is bringing these games to the market.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about how these ecosystems work together (indie publishers, big platforms, and development teams.) They will learn about the goals of platforms and indie publishers and how these provide a framework for developers to align their individual project to meet the external goals of business development partners. They will also learn that the skills required for self publishing are the skills required to support any game release.
intended audience:
Are you making a small game? Do you want to make a small game? Do you want to publish small games?
If yes, lets talk about what indie publishers are constantly thinking about.

session title: Invisible Cameras: Reimagining Cinematography in Real-Time Game Engines
description:
As digital storytelling becomes increasingly cinematic, the role of virtual cameras is rapidly transforming the landscape of games and entertainment.

Presented by the Society for Game Cinematics, this session will explore how cameras (tools once constrained by physical limitations) are now virtual, dynamic, and shaped by gameplay, procedural systems, and user interactivity. Creators from AAA, indie, and solo backgrounds will present practical approaches to procedural cameras, seamless narrative-driven camera choices, scalable camera architectures for small teams, and virtual production workflows that empower individual creators.

Through case studies and real-world examples, presenters will examine how traditional cinematography principles are upheld, challenged, or completely redefined when 'shooting' in a digital world, and how modern camera systems are reshaping storytelling across diverse productions. Attendees will gain insight into how cinematic cameras are crafted for interactive experiences, how aesthetics and narrative intentions are balanced with technical constraints, and where virtual cinematography is headed next.
takeaway: Attendees will walk away with a deeper understanding of how cinematic cameras are being designed and used in real-time pipelines. They'll gain insight into the unique challenges and opportunities of interactive cinematography, explore practical tools and approaches for integrating camera design into game development, and learn how other industries are influencing the future of camera direction in games and vice versa.
intended audience: Anyone interested in the intersection of narrative and game design. Especially valuable to those working with or building tools for virtual cameras, cinematic pipelines, or real-time workflows, but a basic understanding of camera lenses and visual storytelling principles will be sufficient.
speakers: Adam Myhill(Black Eye Technologies), Erin Olorenshaw(Pull Focus Consulting), Cory Williams(Daydream Studios), Nick Romick(RetroPixel Digital)

session title: It Takes a Village: Live at GDC Day 1
description:
'It Takes A Village' is a chat show now in its second season (hosted on YouTube) where we shine a light on the people who make our games, on themes of collaboration, community and co-advocacy. It's a love letter to our unsung heroes, a thank you to those who uplift others, inspiration to those interested in starting a career in games, and provides unique industry insight. Host Samantha Béart will chat with guests about how they got into games and the details of their craft, then moderate an informal audience Q&A. It is a wholesome fireside chat where developers can recognise and learn from those who've helped the industry, as well as share advice on navigating what can often be a challenging industry to work in.

Hosted by Samantha Béart, a classically trained and BAFTA-nominated actor, best known for their role as Karlach in 'Baldur's Gate 3'.
takeaway: Attendees will gain deep industry knowledge in not only the specialisation of each guest, but also their perspective of the games industry as a whole. They'll feel inspired to nurture their own communities in whatever form those communities might take.
intended audience: This is for anyone who's interested in intimate, behind-the-scenes chats with games industry professionals.

session title: Lasers, Mimic Masks, and Squid Battles: The Technology Evolution of 'I Expect You to Die'
description: I Expect You To Die is a spy themed VR puzzle game that launched as a free demo in 2015 and has since grown into an award winning trilogy. This talk will focus on the content creation pipeline and how it evolved across these games. From using monolithic classes, rigid finite state machines and singleton patterns to adopting a more modular, event driven architecture that fundamentally changed how the game was developed. Using in-engine footage and code samples, this presentation will demonstrate how improvements to the content pipeline helped the team craft bigger and more ambitious levels to fulfill the super spy fantasy.
takeaway: The attendees will learn about the unique challenges and trade-offs the team faced when creating content for I Expect You To Die and how improvements to its pipeline made level creation accessible across disciplines and improved efficiency.
intended audience: The target audience for this talk is anyone interested in a game's content creation pipeline and its evolution from an engineering perspective. A general understanding of game architecture and coding patterns is recommended for this talk.

session title: Mics in Spaces: The Importance of Field and Foley Recording
description: This session will cover the techniques, creative philosophy, and methodology of Field Recording & Foley Recording in the context of sound design for games. It will touch on the justification of these techniques over, and in addition to, using stock sound library material or generative tools. There will also be discussion on how these original recordings increase the variety and inherent originality of your game's sound vision as well as a discussion on building out a bespoke library of source material for you and your team. This lecture will emphasize how a strong and defined sound vision relies on having quality source material and how that material can be acquired from recording unique sounds.
takeaway: Attendees will leave this talk with an understanding and appreciation for field and foley recording as practices. Attendees will gain knowledge in the skills and methodology of recording, in live spaces, and how to best leverage them in their games.
intended audience: This talk is intended for Sound Designers and Audio professionals of all experience levels. Attendees should have a foundational understanding of recording.
speakers: Jeffrey Brice(Independent), Joanna Fang(Sony Interactive Entertainment)

session title: Play with the Designers
description: This unique session will allow you to play a board or card game with the designer present, who will discuss design decisions and options during play and their development process. This session will feature Tom Lehmann (Race for the Galaxy, Res Arcana), Cole Wehrle (Roots, Arc), Bryan Bornmuller (Lord of the Rings Trick Taking Game), Jon Perry (Hot Streak, Air Land and Sea), and Geoff Engelstein (Super Skill Pinball, The Expanse). Participants will be able to play in or observe one or two games.
takeaway: Designers who like a hands-on approach to learning about the design process.
intended audience: How different designers approach mechanics, theme, and balance; what options are considered and discarded during development; What subtle features of designs are often overlooked.
speakers: Cole Wehrle(Leder Games/Wehrlegig Games), Tom Lehmann(Independent), Jon Perry(Quibble Media, LLC), Bryan Bornmueller(Asmodee), Geoff Engelstein(Mind Bullet Games)

session title: Production Roundtable Day 2: People
description:
There are countless ways to produce a game. The Game Production Roundtable aims to move the industry forward by creating space for producers of all levels to come together to share their experiences, discuss challenges they're facing, and build relationships with their peers.

Each day, we'll cover topics across the spectrum of production, loosely following the themes of Process, People, and Career. Potential topics include: Supporting team motivation and fulfillment through production, identifying when a process isn't working, using scope and schedule as tools to make a better product, navigating changes to scope, cultivating team buy-in and ownership of processes, crunch, empowering the team, scheduling, advice for first-time producers, and how to continue developing your production skills.

Another component of the roundtable will focus on supporting the next generation of producers by allowing newcomers to ask questions that help prepare them for their production careers.
takeaway: Attendees will walk away with expanded knowledge and ideas on the ways that producers across the industry grapple with various challenges, and will build relationships with other producers to further inform and support their craft.
intended audience: All are welcome. Producers of all levels of experience who are interested in contributing to the craft's pool of knowledge, and in building relationships with others in the craft will benefit the most. Other disciplines who want to cultivate a better understanding of production are also invited to attend.
speakers: Nico Williams(Unknown Worlds), Shayna Moon(CD Projekt RED)

session title: Rules of the Game 2026:  Revealing Techniques from Resourceful Designers
description: The Rules of the Game session returns for GDC 2026 to introduce you to new techniques for game design shared by a stellar lineup of successful designers. In this long running microtalks-style session, each designer gets 10 minutes to share a 'rule' they use in designing their games, diving into detail about how and when the rule is best deployed. Each of these rules is unique to that designer's own sensibilities, presenting you with a new way to think about making games. Even though game design is a creative field where sometimes you might have to break a rule or two, this fast-paced and energizing session will leave you with plenty to think about and a new toolkit of rules you will want to apply to your own project.
takeaway: Audience members will hear very specific, practical, unique, and personal game design rules from veteran, respected game designers. Expect to leave with an interesting new set of design principles to try out on your own projects.
intended audience: This session is intended for intermediate to advanced game designers who are looking to expand their knowledge of game design craft and learn new ways to tackle challenges from veteran designers.
speakers: Richard Rouse III(FarBridge), Xalavier Nelson Jr(Strange Scaffold), Ashley Ruhl(Broadsword Online Games), Theresa Duringer(Temple Gates), Joel Burgess(Soft Rains), Steve Meretzky(PeopleFun)

session title: The Digital Red Pen: From Draft to Edit to Triage
description:
Good game writing doesn't appear fully formed.
