It's carved, reshaped, and sometimes duct-taped together at 2 a.m. In this series of microtalks, three veteran narrative professionals share their best practices for editing, patching, evolving, and polishing your game story while in the trenches of development.




Diandra Lasrado will demonstrate that in a constantly changing world where writers are seemingly disappearing, the discipline of editing is more crucial than ever in AAA. Marissa Koors will bring the indie perspective, revealing how an editor's command of narrative magic optimizes limited resources and relieves pressure on small teams. Finally, Joshua Rubin shares hard-won triage techniques for rescuing broken stories when it's too late for gameplay to change.




Together, these talks give writers, narrative designers, and studio leads field-tested toolkits for strengthening story, as well as new perspective into the narrative discipline. Because great game writing isn't born perfect—it's edited and refined through collaboration, and sometimes saved in the nick of time.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how editing enhances game narrative, and come away with practical editorial techniques and best practices for managing feedback workflows, anticipating interdepartmental needs, and refining narrative at every stage of development.
intended audience: This talk is intended for writers, narrative designers, studio leads seeking scalable narrative quality, and anyone who works with, or aspires to learn more about game stories and how they come together.
speakers: Joshua Rubin(Subverse Interactive Narrative), Marissa Koors(Ape Law LLC), Diandra Anne Lasrado(iwot games)

session title: The Next Wave of Game Development Is Small, Agile, Social, and Live
description:
As budgets rise and consolidation accelerates, the traditional model—large teams spending years building a single boxed release—is giving way to something more resilient: small, agile teams building social, live experiences that grow alongside their communities.

In this session, Roblox's Head of Developer Community Justin Sousa deconstructs why developers are already pivoting away from isolated platforms to build on ecosystems where distribution is baked into the social fabric. Utilizing the data of Roblox's 141M+ daily active users, we will step into the cultural zeitgeist of Gen Z and Alpha to explore the generation shaping the future of gaming. We'll examine the 'Small Team, Big Impact' blueprint and how teams of fewer than 10 are capturing 25M+ concurrent users by prioritizing social loops and modern technology over static content.

Walk away armed with insights on how to use real-time behavioral data to iterate faster, deliver a top-charting, high-retention title without the traditional $10M+ user acquisition budget, and understand the business programs available for professional teams moving into social-live development.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA

session title: Tools Roundtable Day 2: Assets
description:
The Tools Technology Roundtables focus on discussion of best practices, novel techniques, and technical trade-offs for building game development tools and services. Game development tools are constantly evolving, so come to share what works (and what doesn't) at your studio, and listen to others' take on how to keep their creative teams producing awesome games.

Day 2: 'Assets' focuses on export/import, interchange, persistence, collaboration, search and organization, development models (branching and/or trunking), revision control, and outsourcing.
takeaway:
A better understanding of current techniques and trade-offs for building game development tools, plug-ins, pipelines, and services.
Advice in selection between various techniques, languages, and frameworks.
Advice in handling increasingly complex teams, asset pipelines, and requirements.
intended audience: Engineers, technical artists, and technical producers.

session title: VFX Roundtable Day 2
description:
On Day 1, Ellie and Jason will facilitate the roundtable covering general topics and revisit ground broken in the VFX Summit earlier in the week. Be sure to also check out the Thursday session if you'd like to discuss the artistic aspects of VFX and in the Friday session the discussion will go deep on more technical aspects of VFX. See you there!

The roundtable on Day 2 will focus on VFX through an ARTISTIC LENS. Jason will focus the discussion on your questions concerning the artistic aspects of VFX. This may include art education (as students or veterans), art direction, or whatever else comes up! See you there!

On Day 3, the roundtable will give a TECHNICAL LENS for creating VFX. Ellie will guide the conversation through such as graph based effect authoring, effect lighting and optimization, new mesh based techniques, VR/AR/XR and/or whatever attendees want to go deep on.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with an increased awareness of visual effects techniques, approaches and technology, as well as a greater insight into how different artists and studios approach visual effects challenges.
intended audience: This is for visual effects artists, technical artists, students, graphics programmers and anyone else with an interest in the art and creation of visual effects.
speakers: Jason Keyser(VFX Apprentice), Ellie Koorlander(Creative Assembly)

session title: When East Meets Western: Art Direction of 'Ghost of Yōtei'
description:
Ghost of Yōtei blends the vibe of classic Westerns with that of Japanese samurai movies. That means long sightlines over vast, empty areas. Authentic Japanese visuals, but recast from the close quarters of Tsushima to the openness and incredible scale of Hokkaido, the frontier of Japan when the game takes place. Sometimes even a feeling of loneliness, alone with a sword and a horse with nobody in sight for miles in any direction.

Art Director Joanna Wang walks through how the team went from this statement of purpose to the beautiful, fully realized world of Ghost of Yōtei. She covers the definition and discovery of the key focus areas that created our target vibe and follows up with the production process the team used to deliver on the emotional promise of the game.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to define and maintain a clear stylized art direction, translate cultural and environmental inspirations into playable spaces, and streamline the production process for foliage, color palettes and worldbuilding, balancing artistic ambition with production realities to deliver a visually cohesive, deeply immersive world.
intended audience: This session is designed for game developers involved in art direction, environment art, concept design, level design and technical art who want to understand how to translate stylized visual vison into a shippable game. Especially for anyone interested in cross-culture art production work.

session title: Moms In the Industry: Balancing Caregiving and Dreams
description:
Join us for a curated panel discussion featuring four mothers from different sectors of the gaming industry, moderated by Nahir Fajardo (Social Worker and CEO of Dreams of Heaven Games). This session will explore the profound sacrifice and unwavering love behind balancing a professional career with motherhood. Through a structured conversation on specific topics—ranging from professional growth to community support—our panelists will share their personal journeys and insights. The event aims to bring visibility to the challenges we face while fostering a supportive network for all attendees. Following the discussion, we will open the floor for community connection and mutual support.

Panelists:

- Ashley Conley works in Business Development and Account Management at UX Is Fine (UXIF), helping game studios access world-class UX/UI design and implementation support. She guides clients from first conversation through delivery, building partnerships that last beyond a single project.

- Christina-Marie Drake is the founder of Moms in Gaming and a senior recruiting and talent operations leader in the video game industry. As both a working mom and a hiring decision-maker, she brings a dual lens: lived experience and behind-the-scenes insight into how studios actually evaluate, promote and retain talent.

- Jesenia Vargas: Currently a Producer on the popular card-guessing 'Heads Up!' mobile game, Jesenia brings more than a decade of experience leading projects and teams across tech and games. Outside of globally recognized AAA franchises (Madden NFL, NBA Live, UFC), mobile, and indie games, Jesenia has also worked on Princess Cruise Line's Medallion Class IoT project and played a key role in helping scale Orlando's first Fintech Unicorn startup.

- Shaina Grade is a game production leader based in California. She has spent her career doing everything from AAA PC/Console games at Blizzard to educational games at Filament Games, where she led studio operations/production leadership. She currently works at Obsidian Entertainment as a Senior Producer shipping The Outer Worlds 2.


Moderator:

Nahir Fajardo Hernández: CEO and Creative Producer at Dreams of Heaven Games. A professional social worker, rural woman, mother, and autistic person. She has participated in international game events such as GDC, Gamescom, and Tokyo Game Show, both in person and virtually. Currently organizer of the Chile Game Showcase, she advocates for inclusion, interactive storytelling, and game development from non-central regions.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: GDC Side Quest Podcast Live: Sam Carlisle
description: In this live podcast session, GDC's Beth Elderkin chats with Sam Carlisle, a veteran of external development and co-founder of XDS Spark. Sam discusses the evolving landscape of co-development and outsourcing, highlighting the opportunities available for indie studios and developers alike.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with a better understanding of how to leverage co-development and outsourcing to create opportunities, build partnerships, and navigate the challenges of the modern game industry.
intended audience: Ideal for indie developers, studio managers, and anyone interested in external development and collaborative opportunities.
speakers: Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming), Sam Carlisle(XDS)

session title: Lunch and Entertainment in the Gardens
description:
Join us for lunch and entertainment in the GDC Gardens.

Stone Stage entertainment presented by MAGWEST (https://www.magwest.org/):



• 11:00 AM - 12:10 PM: Anton Corazza
• 12:30 PM -1:00 PM: Parabellum Raps
• 1:20 PM - 2:00 PM: Cartridj






Review the full week's schedule here (https://gdconf.com/lunch-and-entertainment-in-the-gardens/).
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Orchestrating Speed with AI: Using llm-d, vLLM, and RL for Speedrun Planning (Presented by Red Hat)
description: This session shows how we turn a suboptimal reinforcement learning exploration agent into a route seeking speedrunner by pairing PPO with a panel of model judges orchestrated by llm-d and served with vLLM. The agent plays normally while a narrow planner handles dialog and puzzle moments, then llm-d routes short clips to a vision judge, a state checker, and a rule judge to produce preferences that train a small reward model. The next PPO burst learns with shaped rewards, improving coverage, reliable dialog completion, and key speedrun metrics. We keep it practical for game developers with YAML first deployment on OpenShift AI using KServe and vLLM, plus a reusable kit of prompts, rubrics, and metrics. We will showcase Double Dragon and Zelda: Oracle of Seasons on the original Nintendo Gameboy, but the same pattern applies to any game for automated testing or optimal route discovery for speed running.

session title: Redefining Quality: The Critical Role of QA in a Changing Games Industry (Presented by Testronic)
description:
The games industry in 2026 and beyond is evolving under mounting creative, technical, and commercial pressure. QA stands at the centre of this, but must move at the same pace – and, crucially, speak for the players.

This session will explore how FQA is being reimagined by Testronic to deliver a service that is Player-Focussed, Partner-First and Outcomes-Led – and why the industry is ripe for disruption in this space.

With studios under increasing strain to monetise, release faster, and maintain global reach, QA must now drive insight, efficiency, and trust, not just report bugs. Testronic's global testing floor and Centres of Excellence combine world-class, enthusiastic testing talent with leading edge technology, delivering the best possible experiences that matter most to players and partners.

Attendees will gain a deeper understanding of QA's expanding role in shaping the future of excellence in games.
takeaway:
- Why QA is central to delivering world-class player experiences, monetization and ultimately ROI
- How FQA adds value beyond bug detection
- Industry pressures driving QA innovation and disruption
- The benefits of a global, outcomes-led testing approach
- How Testronic's Centres of Excellence and specialist talent help studios prioritize what truly matters
intended audience: This talk is for studio leaders, product owners, and QA decision-makers redefining quality standards. It will deliver insights for teams facing accelerated release cycles, monetization demands, and global scalability challenges who want QA to become a strategic driver of trust, insight, and value.

session title: Why Build for Fortnite: $900M Paid to Devs—and What You Can Build Next (Presented by Epic Games)
description:
Developers have made over $900M in Fortnite since UEFN launched at GDC 2023. In this session, we'll explore how the Fortnite ecosystem enables devs to build scalable businesses through two complementary revenue streams: In-Island Transactions and Engagement Payouts.
Learn how direct in-island transactions are enabling more advanced game economies, progression systems, and premium experiences at Fortnite's massive global scale — with dev-friendly economics. We'll spotlight real examples of developers building sustainable, high-quality experiences, walk through new Creator Portal features to grow your business, and share a high-level look at how the Fortnite economy will continue to reward innovation in 2026 and beyond.
takeaway: Understand how to build, monetize, and grow a scalable game business in Fortnite: leveraging In-Island Transactions, Engagement Payouts, actionable analytics, and the evolving Fortnite economy.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall
speakers: Andre Balta(Epic Games), Louis Denoly(Epic Games)

session title: Behind the Scenes of Game Funding
description:
In today's volatile funding landscape, building a studio independently demands more than creativity -- it requires strategic capital partnerships. Ed Fries, Founder of 1Up Ventures and former Xbox executive, and Emily Greer, Venture Partner at 1Up Ventures and former CEO and Co-Founder of Kongregate -- bring deep experience backing and scaling game studios.

In this candid discussion, they share what it's truly like to fund a game - how investment decisions are made and how risk is evaluated. From traditional avenues like venture capital, publishing deals, and crowdsourcing to innovative methods such as UA financing, revenue-based financing, and IP licensing, the opportunities are vast but require deliberate planning. Join them to hear their stories, insights, and lessons learned from years of shaping the gaming industry.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA
speakers: Emily Greer(1Up Ventures), Ed Fries(1Up Ventures)

session title: AI Executive Panel: Using Agentic AI to Understand Your Players and Provide Hyper-Personalized Experiences (Presented by EPAM)
description: At GDC 2025, EPAM brought together a diverse group of industry experts to explore the rapidly evolving role of AI within the gaming industry. In this executive panel, experts will discuss how to leverage agentic AI and massive real-time player data to gain deeper insights into player behavior and preferences, enabling hyper-personalized gaming experiences and marketing that drives engagement and loyalty. Learn how cutting-edge AI technologies are reshaping player experience, from dynamic in-game content tailored to individual players to personalized support and adaptive storytelling. Hear from industry leaders as they share exclusive insights, real-world case studies and actionable strategies to implement AI-driven player 360 and personalization in games. This session offers a glimpse into the future of game development and the real-world impact it will have in 2026, empowering you to stay ahead in the rapidly evolving gaming landscape.
takeaway: Attendees will gain actionable insights into leveraging agentic AI to analyze player behavior, enabling hyper-personalized experiences like dynamic in-game content and marketing. Learn from real-world case studies and industry leaders how AI-driven personalization boosts engagement, retention and monetization—empowering you to stay competitive in the rapidly evolving gaming landscape.
intended audience: This session targets high-level decision-makers, including studio executives, creative directors, product managers, business development leaders, marketing strategists, tech innovators, development directors and AI leaders. It is designed for professionals shaping the future of gaming platforms, offering insights into leveraging AI for player understanding and hyper-personalized gaming experiences.
speakers: Vitalii Vashchuk(EPAM), Francis Hart(SEGA), Ryan Valenza(PlayStation Studios), Haiyan Zhang(Microsoft Xbox)

session title: 'Angry Birds' Meets GenAI: Transforming Rovio's Asset Creation with AWS (Presented by Amazon Web Services)
description: Explore how Rovio, the creator of Angry Birds, is reimagining game asset creation using AWS's generative AI services. Using Angry Birds Dream Blast as a real example, this session explores how GenAI—powered by Amazon SageMaker and Amazon Bedrock—fits into live game production, enabling faster content creation while maintaining art direction and brand consistency.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how Rovio used AWS services to integrate generative AI into a live game production pipeline, with practical lessons on scaling asset creation while preserving art direction and creative control.
intended audience: This session is for game developers, technical artists, art directors, and production leaders interested in applying generative AI to game development. No deep AI expertise is required; familiarity with game production pipelines and live game workflows is helpful.
speakers: Ignacio Amaya de la Pena(Rovio), Ruphar Dhawan(AWS)

session title: Art of the Possible: Experimenting with AI in Art (Presented by Keywords Studios)
description: This session demystifies how AI can reliably produce high‑fidelity art, animation and video assets for live game production. It begins with 'brand‑trained' pipelines that ingest existing assets and learn a studio's visual language; these pipelines handle data curation, model training, evaluation, inference and operations. Examples include stylized avatar creation, novel character and IP generation and synthetic dataset production. The talk then explores a Swiss‑army‑knife toolkit: LLM‑driven tools, multi‑view 3D reconstruction, multi‑modal character generation using segment prompts and image‑likeness matching, and gameplay‑footage analysis that automatically selects segments, composes trailers and adds AI‑generated audio. It covers personalized video flows, metahuman localization and motion capture via text prompts. Throughout, the emphasis is on responsible adoption, knowledge‑sharing and staying ahead of rapid technological change.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to build production‑ready AI pipelines—combining open‑ and closed‑source models—to create on‑brand 2D/3D/video assets, synthesize data, automate asset creation and personalize content. They'll also understand governance and workflow integration, ensuring AI enhances rather than disrupts game development.
intended audience: This talk targets studio leaders, producers, art/technical directors, pipeline engineers and developers who want to integrate AI into active production. It assumes familiarity with modern game development pipelines but requires no machine‑learning expertise, focusing instead on practical workflows, cross‑disciplinary collaboration and management considerations.
speakers: Sergei Gourski(Keywords Studios), Nicolas Liorzou(Keywords Studios)

session title: Bringing Cyberpunk 2077 to Mac (Presented by Apple)
description: Go behind the scenes on how Cyberpunk 2077 came to Mac — and set a new standard for AAA gaming on macOS in the process. Find out how CD PROJEKT RED leveraged Apple's robust hardware, software, and development tools to bring this high-fidelity experience, and explore the innovative 'For this Mac' preset, which automatically optimizes graphical settings and ensures a consistently smooth frame rate across the Mac lineup.
speakers: Oleg Shatulo(CD PROJEKT RED), Pawel Sasko(CD PROJEKT RED), Charlyn Keating(Apple)

session title: DirectX State of the Union 2026: DirectStorage and Beyond (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description:
Over the past year, we've tackled some of the toughest challenges facing developers on DirectX 12. One major investment is the next chapter in game asset streaming where we're introducing Zstandard compression support in DirectStorage. Alongside this we'll release the Game Asset Conditioning Library (GACL) and talk about asset pipelines optimizations that will unlock the full potential of NVMe storage. These innovations reduce I/O latency and enable smoother streaming, paving the way for richer, more responsive worlds.

In this session we'll also explore how we're addressing other critical developer needs, including advancing raytracing features and shader pre-compilation. We're evolving ML integration in DirectX and we're delivering tooling improvements that converge our PC and Xbox developer experience. Join us to learn more about how we're building the future of graphics.
takeaway: This session gives developers an overview of recent DirectX 12 improvements focused on real‑world challenges, from faster, more efficient asset streaming using Zstandard and the Game Asset Conditioning Library to ongoing work in ray tracing, shader pre‑compilation, ML integration, and major tooling improvements.
intended audience: This session is designed for anyone who wants to learn the latest about DirectX including engine and graphics programmers, technical directors, and performance‑focused developers. Attendees benefit most with prior knowledge of DirectX 12, GPU pipelines, and asset streaming, though anyone shaping or working on modern games will benefit.

session title: Good Game Club Live Podcast Recording
description: Join us for a live recording of the Good Game Club podcast - where conversations about good gaming happen. Hosted by Jude Ower MBE and Mathias Gredal Nørvig, and in strategic partnership with Tencent, they shine a light on gaming's hidden and unexpected benefits, and the positive impact digital play can have on people and the planet.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Play by the Rules: Investigating and Enforcing IP and Contractual Rights in Games (Presented by Perkins Coie LLP)
description: This panel would dive into ways in-house legal teams at video game companies can successfully investigate and enforce their intellectual property rights and contractual terms against a variety of harmful misuse in their games (e.g., intentional and accidental infringement, fraud, harassment and abuse of other players, cheaters, etc.). The panel will discuss ways to successfully utilize cross-company teams/departments (legal, communications, security) to identify and achieve specific enforcement goals, such as game security, player protection, IP enforcement. The panel will also discuss how to address the challenges of enforcement against anonymous online individuals and targets in foreign jurisdictions. This would be a topic focusing largely on copyright, trademark, and breach of contract (e.g., breach of EULA or Terms of Service).
takeaway: Objectives: • Build effective cross functional programs: Define roles for legal, security, trust & safety, and communications to set clear enforcement goals (game integrity, player protection, brand/IP protection). • Strengthen copyright and trademark enforcement: Apply takedowns, notices, and litigation triage to mods, asset rip offs, counterfeit merchandise, and brand misuse on platforms and marketplaces. • Operationalize contract remedies: Enforce EULAs and Terms of Service against cheaters, botters, fraudsters, and harassers using suspensions, bans, clawbacks, and arbitration/venue clauses. • Investigate and attribute misuse: Develop lawful and practical evidence collection, logging, and chain of custody practices for online misconduct and third party tool ecosystems. • Overcome anonymity and borders: Use subpoenas, platform workflows, and international cooperation to identify anonymous actors and pursue targets in foreign jurisdictions.
intended audience: • Startup founders, studio executives, and operational leaders (product, engineering, live ops, growth) • Investors and advisors evaluating IP risk and operational resilience • In house counsel and legal professionals at game publishers, platforms, and studios • Trust & safety, security, and fraud operations leaders • Brand protection and IP enforcement teams • Community management and communications professionals • Outside counsel and investigators supporting games enforcement.
speakers: Jacob Dini(Perkins Coie LLP), Steven Guris(risk3sixty), Joel Emans(Wizards of the Coast and Hasbro Digital Games), Eugene Marder(Pocket Entertainment)

session title: We're Speaking Your Slang-uage Here: A Proof-of-Concept Console Backend for the Slang Shading Language (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: Tired of maintaining separate shader stacks for every platform? Slang modernizes graphics development with features like generics, interfaces, and first-class support for differentiable programming. However, leveraging these advanced capabilities on closed platforms requires the ability to target your specific hardware. In this session, Enduring Games presents a technical proof-of-concept demonstrating Slang's extensibility, featuring the first public demo of Slang-compiled shaders executing on current-gen console devkits. Presenters will deconstruct the backend implementation, showing how to map Slang's intermediate representation to bespoke APIs. This case study serves as a blueprint for any developer needing to bridge modern shading languages with proprietary systems. Whether you are targeting next-gen consoles or specialized embedded devices, join us to learn how to extend the compiler and achieve a truly single-source graphics pipeline.
takeaway: Attendees will witness the first public demonstration of Slang shaders executing on console devkits. They will gain the blueprint for implementing custom compiler backends for any bespoke hardware and understand the path to unifying shader pipelines across open and closed platforms.
intended audience: Engine Programmers, Graphics & Rendering Engineers
speakers: Adam Creighton(Enduring Games), Kevin Nappoly(Enduring Games)

session title: Player Safety Strategy in Action: Real-World Moderation Strategies from Two Live Multiplayer Games (Presented by Modulate)
description:
As multiplayer games grow in scale and social complexity, player safety has become a core design challenge, and one that directly impacts retention, engagement, and community trust. In this session, Mark Frumkin, Director of Customer Success at Modulate, presents a comparative look at two live, commercially successful multiplayer games that implemented distinct approaches to moderating player behavior at scale. The talk explores:

A large, globally distributed competitive multiplayer title, where proactive moderation, player reporting, and positive-play systems are used to address harmful voice behavior across millions of players.

A social deduction multiplayer game built around proximity voice chat, where a shift from immediate bans to a tiered escalation system (warnings → mutes → bans) dramatically reduced negative player impact while maintaining moderation effectiveness.

Using real production data and lessons learned, this talk demonstrates how modern moderation strategies can change player behavior without sacrificing playtime or social connection. Attendees will leave with practical guidance for designing escalation pathways, choosing enforcement severity, and measuring success beyond simple ban counts.
takeaway:
Attendees will leave understanding why:
1. Tiered Escalation Pathways Outperform Immediate Punishment
2. Less Disruption Contributes to Strong Engagement
3. Wide Coverage, Transparency, and Community Feedback Matter
4. Moderation Should be Core to Game Design
intended audience:
This session is designed for:

- Game designers building multiplayer features who want to bake player safety into game systems
- Live service operators and community managers seeking measurable safety strategy insights
- Engineering and data teams tackling real-time moderation and escalation logic
- Player experience leads / UX researchers exploring how feedback systems and player control influence community health

session title: AAA Strategy, Indie Budget: Building a Smarter, Long-Term Game Launch
description:
This session is built for indie developers, small studio teams, and producer-led game projects looking to launch with the strategic precision of a AAA studio, without the AAA budget. If you're building your first go-to-market plan, trying to stretch limited resources across marketing channels, or wondering how to make data-driven decisions without a full analytics team, this panel will walk you through a proven, stage-gated approach to bringing your game to market.

Indie games are competing in an increasingly crowded marketplace where discoverability is harder than ever and marketing budgets can't keep pace with player expectations. The difference between a successful launch and a quiet release often comes down to planning: knowing when to invest, what to prioritize, and how to adapt as you learn more about your audience.

Led by **Kerri Norton of Open World**, alongside **Brynjar Eli Gigja, CEO & Game Director of Cygnus Cross International Ltd**, this conversation bridges research-backed strategy with on-the-ground indie execution. Together, they'll break down real gameplay behaviors, use research to inform platform and creator strategies, and build content, paid media, and activation plans that evolve as your understanding of your players deepens. The goal is a go-to-market approach that stays dynamic, keeps data fresh, and ensures every dollar is spent intelligently.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall
speakers: Kerri Norton(Open World), Brynjar Gigja(Cygnus Cross International Ltd)

session title: Beyond the Logo: Crafting Comprehensive Brand Systems for Games
description:
Game branding extends beyond merely combining a logo with key art; it's the essential connection between the immersive game world you've created and the players you aim to captivate. In our discussion, we'll explore the balance between brand consistency and flexibility, and examine how branding strategies differ in live service games versus non-live service games.

Leveraging our experience in crafting visual identities for major titles from PlayStation and Bungie, we'll reveal the strategies, creative principles, and player engagement and marketing insights that drive our approaches.
takeaway: Explore actionable tips and best practices for extending game branding beyond the confines of the game itself. Learn how to effectively collaborate with diverse teams and stakeholders to craft a visual identity that not only stands out but also endures in a fiercely competitive market. Ensure your brand and art resonates across all platforms, maximizing its usability, impact, and longevity.
intended audience: This lecture welcomes everyone interested in elevating their skills in brand and visual design within the gaming industry. Whether you are a seasoned designer, a creative professional in marketing and publishing, or someone overseeing a logo or branding project for your game, this session is for you.
speakers: Alanna Cervenak(PlayStation Studios Creative Arts), Matt Redway(PlayStation Studios Creative Arts)

session title: Decolonizing Play and the Rise of African Game Development (2013-2026)
description: Eyram Tawia returns to GDC with a powerful update on the African games industry's growth and transformation since his landmark 2013 talk. Drawing on over a decade of studio leadership, teaching, and research, Tawia explores how African creators are redefining game narratives, partnerships, and education—fulfilling and challenging earlier predictions. This session dives into concrete successes, ongoing challenges, and the meaning of 'decolonizing play' in a global industry. Attendees will gain practical strategies from African studios' experiences, insights into new talent pipelines, and a candid look at the future of collaboration between the continent and the world. Essential for anyone interested in global markets, diversity, and new creative frontiers.
takeaway: Attendees will gain real-world strategies for fostering meaningful partnerships, developing locally relevant content, and navigating the complex realities of IP, ownership, and representation in emerging markets—plus fresh insight into how 'decolonizing play' can drive innovation, new business, and talent growth for the global games industry.
intended audience: This session benefits game developers, producers, publishers, educators, and industry leaders interested in global expansion, diversity, or working with emerging markets. No specialized knowledge is required—just curiosity and openness to new ways of making, teaching, and thinking about games. The talk offers value across disciplines and company sizes.

session title: EA Sports 'F1 25': Path Tracing at 200mph
description:
How do you deliver the pinnacle of lighting technologies to a racing game engine and ship in a yearly cycle? In this session, Tom Hammersley, Principal Engineer at EA Codemasters, shares how two senior engineers built a path tracer on EGO, the bespoke racing game engine that powers EA SPORTS F1® titles without having to change a decade of art… and shipped it in 12 months.

Tom shares insights from throughout the journey of shipping EA SPORTS F1® 25's 'Ultra Max' quality mode, including real challenges and real solutions.

With practical tips on shortcuts, optimizations and lessons learned, this session will show you what it took to get to the finish line, and how you can do it too.
takeaway:
We aim to give you an understanding of what a path tracer is and how to build one without needing to redevelop your art pipelines.

Sharing our experience of issues during adoption, and the solutions we developed so that you can get a head-start on your journey.

There's discussion on ML techniques available across different GPUs that make path tracing a viable option, even when you move through the world at 200mph. Plus insights into how we applied the latest research to overcome challenges like solving for 300,000 lights in real time.

You should come away with practical tips on how to use the code and art you already have to make a huge leap forward in image lighting - and ship!
intended audience: The talk is aimed at graphics engineers, technical artists and technical directors who are interested in path tracing and adopting it for their engine or title, and want to see how that actually goes when you try.

session title: Extreme Recording: Rockets, Wildlife, and Beyond - Capturing Sound in High-Stakes Environments
description: Join sound designers Howard Mostrom (Kerbal Space Program 2, Planetary Annihilation) and Andy Martin (Star Wars: Tales from the Galaxy's Edge, inFamous) as they explore the technical and creative challenges of extreme field recording. From the raw power of live rocket launches at Cape Canaveral to the remote, hostile stillness of deep wilderness environments, this session dives into how high-risk conditions shape the art of capturing authentic sound. Attendees gain practical insights into gear selection, safety considerations, mic techniques, and workflow strategies for both urban chaos and natural isolation. The session also reveals how these rare recordings are shaped into compelling, emotionally resonant audio experiences for games and interactive media. Whether you're braving a blast zone or waiting hours in a swamp, this talk offers the tools and inspiration to elevate your field recording craft.
takeaway: Attendees learn field-proven techniques for capturing powerful, high-risk recordings—from rocket launches to remote nature ambiences. They will gain practical insights into gear selection, safety planning, mic placement, and transforming raw recordings into polished assets for interactive media, all from two veterans of extreme and environmental sound design.
intended audience: This session is for sound designers, field recordists, and audio directors interested in high-stakes or location-based recording. Attendees should have a working knowledge of audio fundamentals, as the talk explores advanced techniques, logistical planning, and creative application of extreme field recordings in game and interactive media production.
speakers: Howard Mostrom(Injected Senses LLC), Andy Martin

session title: Games From Spain: Diverse Voices & Bold Visions (Presented by Spanish Pavilion)
description:
Spain continues to strengthen its position as a dynamic European hub for game development, combining distinctive creative identity with a growing international reach. The session opens with a brief overview of the Spanish development ecosystem, outlining the structural, institutional, and industry factors supporting its expansion.

It then presents four Spanish titles—Crisol: Theater of Idols and BodyCombat, already released, alongside The Occultist and Bogdan's Cross, both scheduled for release in the near future. Together, they reflect the country's genre diversity, from atmospheric horror to action-driven and performance-based experiences.

Each studio shares concise insight into its creative direction, core design pillars, and market positioning, highlighting both launch experience and upcoming release strategies. The session offers a focused industry snapshot of how Spanish studios translate creative ambition into globally competitive projects.
takeaway: Attendees gain insight into how Spanish studios position, launch, and prepare new IP for international markets, including practical examples of genre differentiation, creative direction, and ecosystem support mechanisms that strengthen global competitiveness.
intended audience: This session targets developers, producers, and publishing professionals interested in international collaboration and emerging development ecosystems. It benefits industry attendees seeking concise case studies on creative positioning and release strategy. No advanced technical prerequisites are required.
speakers: David Lorenzo(DALOAR Studios), Juan Manuel Freire(1st Level), Jaime Pichardo(Odders Lab), David Carrasco(Vermila Studios), Pablo Barea(Embassy of Spain - Trade Commission in Los Angeles)

session title: GenAI at Studios: Leadership, Tools, and Trust
description:
The use of GenAI tools and workflows is an undoubtedly hot but often thorny topic: studio leads are often forced to navigate polarized narratives: hype-driven promises on one side, and fear-driven concerns about harm, authorship, and team trust on the other.

This 30-minute session brings together leaders from small studios who have already navigated introducing GenAI into real production workflows. Through two concise microtalks and a short moderated discussion, the session focuses on lived experience and the challenges and opportunities of introducing these tools into existing workflows: and goes beyond the speculation.

Speakers will share their stories about where GenAI tools or processes added value, where it created friction, and how they communicated tradeoffs to their teams. Rather than advocating for or against GenAI, the session offers grounded insight into how thoughtful leadership, clear intent, and human judgment shape whether GenAI strengthens or destabilizes a studio pipeline.
intended audience: This session is for studio leaders and developers considering how GenAI might fit into real production workflows, including those who are curious, cautious, or skeptical. It is also relevant for attendees not currently working in studios using GenAI who want a clearer picture of how these tools are being introduced and evaluated in practice.
speakers: Mitu Khandaker(NYU Game Center), Phil Stuart(PRELOADED), Irena Pereira(Infinite Realms/Unleashed Games)

session title: 'Marvel Rivals': Putting the Super into Hero Shooter
description:
In this session, we dive into the intricate process of transforming iconic Marvel characters into compelling gameplay experiences. This session explores our unique design philosophy, emphasizing how we distill 85 years of Marvel lore into engaging game mechanics without sacrificing the essence of beloved characters like Spider-Man, Hulk, and Iron Man.

Participants will learn about our approach to integrating superpowers as core gameplay elements, ensuring these powers enhance the hero shooter genre rather than confine it.

The talk aims to reveal the long-term balance methodology in Marvel Rivals, allows for the creation of thrilling, strategy-rich encounters that respect and expand upon the Marvel universe.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insight into integrating complex character core essence into gameplay, and discover the long-term balance methodology to create fun, engaging, and strategically rich superhero showdowns in Marvel Rivals.
intended audience: This session is ideal for anyone interested in hero design, innovative game mechanism and long-term balance methodology in the hero shooter genre.

session title: 'Neopets': From Web-Browser to Everywhere
description: Dominic Law, CEO of Neopets, shares the transformation of one of the internet's longest-running virtual worlds into a modern, multi-platform gaming and entertainment ecosystem. For over 26 years, Neopets has captured the imagination of multiple generations – but aging technology and limited platform reach threatened its survival. Dominic details how the team rebuilt the game's core infrastructure, coordinated physical and digital product launches, and reignited fan engagement with 'The Void Within,' Neopets' first major plotline in over a decade. He also explores the forward-looking strategy behind expanding onto new platforms beyond the browser, designed to meet players where they are and attract new audiences. This session offers a rare look at revitalizing a nostalgic brand while preserving its heart – showcasing lessons in infrastructure modernization, transmedia storytelling, and community-driven design.
takeaway: Attendees gain a practical blueprint for modernizing a legacy IP, integrating community feedback into product evolution, and creating synergy between digital and physical worlds. Dominic demonstrates how strategic platform diversification and coordinated brand storytelling can transform nostalgia into sustainable, multi-platform relevance.
intended audience: This session is for game developers, producers, brand managers, and transmedia storytellers interested in revitalizing legacy IPs or building cross-platform ecosystems. Prerequisite knowledge is not necessary.

session title: No More Walled Gardens! D2C Playbook for Every Developer
description: Epic's legal victories against Apple and Google reshape the direct-to-consumer (D2C) landscape, allowing game publishers to bypass app store fees. Yet, even among games that have webstores, fewer than 20% of games with webstores currently leverage external payment links from within game. This 30-minute talk equips developers and publishers with a tactical playbook for accelerating their D2C shift. It covers key metrics, high-impact revenue strategies, in-game messaging, link-out methods, and SEO. The session also tailors insights by genre, highlights common pitfalls, and explores D2C opportunities beyond mobile, extending to PC and console. Attendees gain clear, actionable guidance to unlock D2C growth and reduce dependence on traditional app stores.
takeaway: Attendees leave understanding what metrics to track, how to pick payment partners, and an which proven tactics they can pursue to drive D2C revenue and profit.
intended audience: This talk is designed for GMs, product managers, and marketers working on mobile games who are responsible for growth, monetization, or go-to-market strategy. It is relevant for teams that already have D2C stores as well as those considering launching one, offering guidance on how to maximize impact and avoid missteps.

session title: QA is Your Strongest Design Ally
description: In modern video game dev, having QA as a design ally can provide you with more than a feeling of safety. This talk will explore how empowering QA teams as subject matter experts (SMEs) can elevate game design through intentional collaboration, visibility, and qualitative feedback. Drawing from experience as a QA Lead on World of Warcraft, Carey Littlefield will walk through real examples of how embedded QA improved features before they reached players. Attendees will learn how to integrate and align with QA to bring them into the fold early; to build trust between disciplines and find feedback congruency with design timelines. This session will challenge older, outdated models and offer actionable strategies to make QA an invaluable inclusion in your development pipeline.
takeaway: Learn how embedded QA subject matter experts helped shape features in World of Warcraft through early playtests, sprint-aligned feedback, and design visibility; additionally take home actionable strategies to integrate QA into your own development and design cycles for impactful iteration, polish, and cross-discipline collaboration.
intended audience: QA professionals, designers, and producers who want to improve development outcomes through early QA involvement. This talk benefits anyone managing features, facilitating playtests, or cross-discipline workflows, offering real examples and actionable strategies to embed QA as a design ally.

session title: Reddit Has Games: Build, Distribute, and Monetize on a Global Platform (Presented by Reddit)
description: Discover how Reddit is becoming the next frontier for game developers. In this session, we'll explore how to build interactive web games with Devvit (Reddit's developer platform), reach millions of cross-platform players across thousands of communities, and unlock new monetization opportunities—all within the ecosystem of Reddit. Learn how to bring your game to life where conversations, culture, and creativity collide: on Reddit.
takeaway: Learn more about Reddit's Developer Platform and how your game can earn you money on the heart of the internet.
intended audience: All game developers.

session title: The Goose that Laid the Golden Egg: What to Do if You Have a Hit Game
description: Did you release a hit game on Steam? Congrats! You actually found the goose that lays the golden egg! Now what? In this practical talk you will learn exactly what steps you need to take with your goose - I mean game - now that it's a hit. How do you get the most out of this much sought after prize?
takeaway: Attendees will learn what defines a 'golden goose game.' If you potentially have one, you will see what steps you should take before launch, after launch, and when you are ready to launch your next game. You will learn about how to work with Valve to get the most visibility, and what to do so that you don't miss out on more exposure and more money after launch.
intended audience: This talk is for those fortunate developers who have a hit game. This talk will not tell you how to create a hit game (see my other talks on the GDC vault for that). However, if you have a hit game on your hands, or are working on a game that has early signs of being a hit, this talk will give you step by step instructions on making the most of it.

session title: Why Virtualization Is Reshaping Enterprise Game Development (Presented by Dell Technologies)
description:
As game development teams scale across larger studios, higher-fidelity content, and globally distributed workflows, purely workstation-bound development models increasingly strain productivity at scale, cost, and iteration speed. This session examines how GPU virtualization is reshaping enterprise game development by decoupling developers from fixed hardware and enabling more flexible, scalable pipelines.

Through real-world studio patterns, the talk explores how virtualized infrastructure supports key workflows such as content creation, build and test automation, quality assurance, and live service operations. Rather than focusing on specific implementations, the session frames virtualization as a systems-level shift, highlighting the tradeoffs, organizational impacts, and practical benefits studios are seeing as they modernize their development environments. Attendees gain a clear understanding of why virtualization is becoming a foundational capability for large-scale game production and how it fits into the future of studio technology stacks.
takeaway: Attendees gain a practical mental model for how GPU virtualization enables scalable game development, with concrete examples spanning development, testing, and live operations. They leave understanding when virtualization adds value, what problems it solves, and how studios are using it to improve utilization and iteration speed.
intended audience: This session is intended for technical directors, tools programmers, production engineers, and studio IT leaders working on mid-to-large game productions. No prior knowledge of enterprise infrastructure or virtualization is required; the talk focuses on concepts, tradeoffs, and studio-level patterns rather than deep technical details.

session title: C Programmers Meetup
description: Do you prefer your language with a side of undefined behavior? Are you a memory management enthusiasts, who like natural free range pointers? Do you think the youth of today is too addicted to syntax sugar? Then you will be among friends at this C programmers meet-up. Hosted by a ISO Wg14 member.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Students @ GDC Meetup & Networking
description: Looking to make new friends and build your network with peers? Join the EGD Collective for a casual gathering for students, recent graduates, early-career professionals, and anyone who cares about them. This meetup offers a space to connect, swap notes on sessions, talk about portfolios and career paths, or simply take a breather from the conference rush. We'll have resources to support your career journey, as well as games and prizes!
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: GDC Pitch: Day 1
description: Five GDC Play studios have been selected to pitch their games to an opinionated panel of investors and publishers, in front of a live audience (no pressure!) followed by questions, advice, and feedback before the judges declare the day's 'Best Pitch' and award a free GDC 2027 Game Changer Pass. Each year, the theater fills to capacity, as the developers compete for the judges' praise.
speakers: Rebekah Saltsman(Finji), Jason Della Rocca(Execution Labs), Justin Woodward(Media Indie Exchange), Sylvain Sechi(New Tales)

session title: Lunchtime Surgeries Day 2
description: An opportunity for deeper conversations with audio track speakers over lunch in a smaller scale setting. Grab some food and then come back to sit at tables in the round with the day's speakers on the topics that are top of mind for you about the industry, the day's topics, and the questions at the top of attendee minds.
intended audience: All are welcome
speakers: Damian Kastbauer(Audiokinetic), Scott Selfon(Meta Reality Labs Research), Mason Lieberman(Interleave Inc.), Kareem Shuman(Insomniac Games), Olivier Penchenier(Sandfall Interactive), Paul Lipson(Arrival), Akash Thakkar(Independent), Charles Pateman(Independent), Jose Angulo(Teravision Games), Mike Ladouceur

session title: Maximize Your Game's Potential on the App Store (Presented by Apple)
description: Discover best practices for bringing your premium, indie, or free-to-play games to millions of players worldwide. Get to know the features that can help you elevate your game on the App Store and Apple Games app, and explore opportunities for editorial discovery. Plus, learn from the developers at Black Salt Games about how they successfully brought their Apple Design Award-winning game, DREDGE, to Apple platforms.
speakers: Abhishek Radhakrishnan(Apple), Kelli King(Apple), Nadia Thorne(Black Salt Games)

session title: Press Start: Get Your PC Game Ready for Xbox in One Day (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description: This session shows how developers can quickly begin working on Xbox through a simplified, updated onboarding flow. Explore how recent improvements in Partner Center and the GDK — now publicly available — make it straightforward to bring up identity, data, and cross-platform services — including Xbox Network and PlayFab — enabling teams to start iterating almost immediately. Walk through setup, integration, and delivering a working development build your team can install and playtest through the Xbox app. Attendees gain a clear, practical view of how Xbox reduces early friction and helps creators build momentum quickly.
takeaway: Attendees learn that it has never been easier to bring a game to Xbox. They see how updated onboarding, simplified GDK setup, and clear integration patterns reduce early friction, helping developers reach a working build quickly and begin meaningful iteration with confidence.
intended audience: This session is designed for developers, engineers, technical decision-makers, and production leaders who want a clear understanding of how to get started with Xbox development. It benefits anyone evaluating the platform or beginning their first integration, and no deep prior knowledge of Xbox tooling or services is required.

session title: Supercharging Godot Development: Rapid Path Tracing Integration with Cursor (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: Learn how AI coding assistants can accelerate complex engine work—not the theory of path tracing, but the process of adding it. We'll show how iterative design docs, AI-guided refactors, and custom helper tools (Jira/Git integrations, debugging utilities) enabled one part-time engineer to deliver a major rendering feature quickly. You'll leave with practical workflows, prompt patterns, and realistic expectations of AI's strengths and limits.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how AI coding assistants can accelerate complex engine work. You'll leave with practical workflows, prompt patterns, and realistic expectations of AI's strengths and limits.
intended audience: Engine Programmers

session title: VR 201: Essential Tools to Power Your Quest Development (Presented by Meta)
description: The session covers top core developer tools to make great virtual reality and passthrough experiences, implementing LLMs into your games, and best practices learned from exciting new launches who are currently pushing the boundaries of what is possible on Quest.
speakers: Kirk Barker(Meta), Mike Lamprinos(Meta), Alexandre Thivierge(Meta)

session title: The Attention Equation: How Gaming Wins Consumer Focus and What Comes Next
description:
Join gaming industry experts across the ecosystem for a panel discussion moderated by McKinsey on Consumer Focus and how gaming wins. Explore the attention equation and how the gaming industry can capitalize on shifting trends in customer engagement. Key topics include:


• Consumption Trends: Understanding consumption trends across regions and demographics
• Consumer Engagement: Explore opportunities to leverage video game's unique engagement model with customers to increase stickiness
• Marketing Strategies: Social connections drive game discovery. Marketers can learn from current trends across the industry to increase success in subsequent campaigns
• Ad Monetization: Gamers' increasing openness to in-game ads offer new revenue potential revenue options

	* Evolving Technology: Generative AI and other emerging technologies can enhance game quality and efficiency, enabling studios to produce quality games better and faster than ever
This session will provide actionable insights for developers, publishers, and marketers to stay ahead in the battle for customer attention.
takeaway: Attendees will come away with a greater understanding of key trends in customer engagement within the gaming industry. Also included are tactical strategies to capitalize on those trends that can translate directly to topline growth.
intended audience: Our session will target developers, publishers, and business leaders within the gaming industry, but is also open to all interested parties (i.e., artists, other industry experts). There are no knowledge prerequisites above and beyond a baseline understanding of the industry.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA
speakers: Kabir Ahuja(McKinsey & Co.), Ichiro Otobe(McKinsey & Co.), Itamar Benedy(Anzu.io), Moritz Baier-Lentz(Lightspeed Venture Partners)

session title: GLITCHED: Women in Game Dev Networking Jamboree
description:
Squad up with a team and complete quests for prizes! In this session, form a team to win points by networking, playing team games, answering gaming themed trivia questions, taking part in the scavenger hunt, and getting to know your fellow developers!




This session is a friendly competition. Work together for top places on the leaderboard or play it casual by networking and playing games!




This session is hosted by GLITCHED - Women in Game Dev, a community that organizes bi-monthly women in game development meetups in the San Francisco Bay Area.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Global Game Jam (GGJ) Roundtable
description: Calling all Global Game Jammers! Come connect with others in the GGJ community, including organizers, staff, board members and jammers from around the world. Hear about all we've got going on in 2026, learn about other ways you can get involved, share your feedback and/or just generally discuss all things GGJ. Your insights help us shape the future of the jam.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: The Narrative Department Meetup
description: The Narrative Department's annual 'we survived another year in games' gathering. Alumni, friends, and anyone who's ever stressed about branching dialogue—come hang out.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: #1ReasonToBe
description:
This celebrated panel carries forward the legacy of one of GDC's most impactful diversity panels, originally initiated by the legendary Brenda Romero and Leigh Alexander, and later continued by Rami Ismail. #1ReasonToBe showcases women from underrepresented sectors, engaging in candid conversations about game development from their distinct and unique perspectives.


Through 8-minute presentations, each speaker will unveil their individual #1ReasonToBe in the game industry. These presentations will not only highlight personal motivations but also share their unique journeys in game development, touching on themes of community, culture, and opportunity.


Featuring speakers from diverse backgrounds, #1ReasonToBe offers a revealing glimpse into the industry's true global reach—demonstrating that its impact extends far beyond what may initially meet the eye.
takeaway: #1ReasonToBe holds the power to broaden the horizons of our industry and the global challenges we confront. By providing insights into the often-overlooked realities and cultures, the panel sheds light on dimensions that are not conventionally visible to game developers.
intended audience: #1ReasonToBe resonates with the game developers' community hungry for inspiration. It's a symphony of diverse voices, particularly spotlighting women's experiences across cultures. For those eager to infuse their creativity with global insights, this panel offers a harmonious blend of perspectives that redefine game development possibilities.
speakers: Laia Bee(Pincer Games), Jay Justice(Independent), Samantha Halloran(Unseen Inc.), Irene Cortes(Gata Games), Briana White(Strange Rebel Gaming), Elaine Gómez(Midnight Hour Games)

session title: Audio Optimization Microtalks
description:
In this microtalks session, several practical techniques taken from real-life game audio optimization are presented with a focus on step-by-step application.

Colin Walder: Optimizing Cyberpunk 2077 Audio for Switch 2
Attendees will learn how to use the Wwise CPU profiler to investigate audio performance, using real life cases taken from the port of Cyberpunk 2077 to Nintendo Switch 2.

David Su: Managing Object Counts
Attendees will learn about different audio performance management techniques and tradeoffs, including reducing audio object counts and processing, using a scenario from Wu-Tang: Rise of the Deceiver as a case study.

Guy Someberg: Applied SIMD
Attendees will learn a relatively simple recipe they can apply to problems to evaluate whether SIMD will help, then apply SIMD instructions to get 4x, 8x, or 16x performance improvements on applicable code.
takeaway: Attendees will learn step-by-step approaches to practical audio optimization techniques taken from real-life scenarios, including applying SIMD, using the Wwise profiler, and managing audio object counts.
intended audience: This talk is ideal for audio programmers of any level who wish to deepen their knowledge of audio optimization tools and techniques, and also to programmers who do not specialize in the discipline but who would like to take an active role in optimizing audio of their games.
speakers: Colin Walder(CD PROJEKT RED), Guy Somberg(Blizzard), David Su(Naughty Dog)

session title: Automated Testing Roundtable Day 2
description:
Day 2: Legacy
Automated testing provides a team with stability across development, allowing early feedback, fast iteration and empowering QA. Yet investing in automation has a bad reputation in the industry, where just-ship-it is the mantra. Maybe, as the market becomes increasingly competitive, its time has finally come.

On Day 2 we prefer to talk about introducing automated testing to an organization. Do you have a culture or a codebase that doesn't currently embrace automated testing? Are you having trouble getting people on-board, or maybe it was very easy and you can share why? Grand failures are just as interesting as successes!

These roundtables bring together people of all experience levels and budgets to discuss how they currently, or would like to, use automated testing of any form. See atig.dev/gdc (https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/QZfJCKrY6nHBYOW65HnFRI5JWxt?domain=atig.dev) for more details and to join our discord!
takeaway: Attendees will discover common definitions around automated testing, how other studios are currently using it, what works well, and what doesn't. They will return to their studios and extol the benefits of automated testing, and that it doesn't have to be hard or time consuming to see returns on investment.
intended audience: Programmers and technical QA with experience or interest in automated testing. It's ok to not be an expert - all ability levels encouraged.

session title: Building Games, Building Teams: A 5-Year Student Studio Playbook (Presented by Silverjay Studio)
description: Drawing from five years running a student-led game studio, this Power Talk breaks down how to start making games in college and leave something that outlasts graduation. It focuses on the unglamorous mechanics: recruiting and onboarding across semesters, building a ship-first culture, scoping for part-time teams, and setting up lightweight production systems (docs, sprints, playtests, and decision logs). Attendees see how a small group turns into a reliable pipeline: from prototype to vertical slice to release, while protecting morale and creative ambition. The talk shares hard-earned failures, the specific rituals that fixed them, and the metrics that signal whether a student studio is growing into a sustainable community or just sprinting toward burnout.
takeaway: Attendees leave with a concrete starter kit: a semester-based milestone map, a recruiting/onboarding funnel, a minimal documentation stack that survives turnover, and a playtest loop that converts campus curiosity into shipped builds and repeat contributors.
intended audience: Ideal for student developers, indie producers, and educators building campus programs. Helpful for anyone managing part-time, high-turnover teams who still want to ship.

session title: Designing POIs (Points of Interest) for 'The Outer Worlds 2'
description: In a role-playing game like , Points of Interest (POIs) are where worldbuilding, gameplay systems, and player progression intersect. This session breaks POI design down into four core aspects: worldbuilding, progression, spatial design, and navigation. It focuses on how aligning these elements creates readable, engaging spaces that evolve with the player, exploring each aspect through concrete production examples, from early ideas and iteration to the solutions that made them work.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insight into the design considerations and iteration behind crafting Points of Interest that support narrative, gameplay, and player progression, ensuring each location meaningfully contributes to the player's experience at different moments of play.
intended audience: This session is intended for level designers and game designers working on exploration-driven role-playing games. It may also be of interest to narrative and world designers collaborating on authored locations. No specialized knowledge is required, though a basic understanding of 3D space and player behavior will help attendees fully engage with the content.

session title: Getting the Sequel Right with 'Kingdom Come Deliverance II'
description: Kingdom Come: Deliverance proves that a rough but ambitious game can find a passionate audience. Its sequel, Kingdom Come: Deliverance II, shows what happens when that same studio learns how to turn passion into a more professional craft. In this session, Warhorse Studios' Lead Designer reflects on how the team evolves between the two games, focusing on the changes in both design and production that shape the sequel. The talk offers a practical look at what it takes for a studio to grow after its first major success without losing the identity that made it special.
takeaway: Attendees gain insight into how a studio evolves after its first major success, with real examples of how changes in design and production shape a better sequel. The session offers practical lessons for teams navigating growth while trying to keep what made their first game work.
intended audience: The session benefits developers interested in improving how their teams work, whether they are scaling a studio or rethinking their current organisation. It speaks to anyone curious about how design and production change as projects grow. No specific prerequisite knowledge is required beyond general experience in game development.

session title: Growing Leaders from Within: Leadership Development for Video Game Teams
description:
What are you doing to ensure your organization has a steady supply of future leaders? Being intentional about growing your current team so they are ready to step into future leadership positions is one of the defining characteristics of great video game studios. Framing the challenge of leadership development as a game design problem helps anyone in video games begin to demystify the art of developing leaders.

By understanding the fundamental expectations of leadership, how our mindset impacts our leadership journey, and the essential tools one can use to have leadership impact, you will be better able to grow leaders from within your current team.

Whether you are in a leadership position today and want to ensure your team's success into the future, or you are eager to grow into a leadership position yourself, this session is meant for you.
takeaway:
A concise set of expectations for leadership
How one's mindset affects the leadership journey
Essential tools allowing anyone to have leadership impact
Guidance on how to teach the tools presented
The role of mentorship in leadership development
The importance of succession planning
All combined into a comprehensive leadership development program
intended audience: This session is intended primarily for those currently managing teams or organizations but is also useful for those still early in their own leadership development journey, eager for practical lessons to support their own leadership development. Anyone interested in being intentional about leadership can benefit.

session title: How 'Infinity Nikki' Turns Outfits into Fantasy (And Why It Works)
description: Infinity Nikki redefines dress-up gaming by weaving deep emotional storytelling, rich open-world lore, and Unreal Engine 5 craftsmanship into every outfit. This session explores how the team creates emotionally resonant, lore-inspired costumes at scale, unlocking immersive narrative experiences that spark strong player-avatar bonds and drive lasting engagement.
takeaway: Attendees will discover how Infinity Nikki transforms outfits into storytelling vehicles—melding emotional design, fantasy worldbuilding, and advanced textile simulation—to deliver a wardrobe system that boosts immersion, authenticity, and enduring player attachment.
intended audience: Developers of all experience levels are welcome.

session title: IGDA Game Writing SIG Coffee Chat (Presented by the IGDA)
description: The IGDA Game Writing SIG leadership would like to chat and have coffee with those involved in narrative for games. We're actively recruiting volunteers to help pursue the bold mission set out in our constitution: https://www.game-writing.com/constitution. Several SIG committee members and active volunteers will be on hand to talk about the future of the profession, the next steps for our SIG, and how we can make it better. We'll present details about our past year, talk about our growing initiatives, and field questions by attendees. Those interested in volunteering will learn about our community and find out if there's a good opportunity to get involved!
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Learning to Move: Physics-Based Enemy Locomotion in 'ARC Raiders'
description: What does it take to make a machine feel alive? In ARC Raiders, we set out to reinvent how enemies move — not through animation clips or hand-tuned logic, but through learning. Using state of the art methods that combine animation, reinforcement learning and physics-based control, we taught our AI to walk, run, stumble, and fight with intent. Each motion emerges from experience, not scripts. Combined with a perception system built on point clouds, these agents see and react to the world around them with surprising fluidity and realism. This talk covers how we trained and deployed these agents in Unreal Engine, the challenges of achieving responsiveness and stability in a high-fidelity action setting, and the lessons learned integrating learned locomotion into a large-scale production game.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insights into bridging cutting-edge reinforcement learning research with practical, performant character animation in a shipped title.
intended audience: The technical talk is aimed at game developers interested in the intersection of movement, physics and emergent gameplay. It should be interesting for animators and designers interested in new tech as well as physics and gameplay programmers. Some high school math will help but the necessary background will be covered.

session title: Making Player Data Work Roundtable Day 2: Production
description:
The role of player feedback in game development can be divisive and complex. Some studios totally embrace players' engagement in design through early access, open development, routine user research, and community engagement. Other studios limit player feedback to just post-launch, or perhaps dismiss feedback completely.

How can teams use the 'voice of the player' rationally, effectively and deliberately?

Are there challenges you face that player feedback could overcome?

Can you make a greater success of the feedback and data you already have?

How does the 'voice' of player data change through development?

These three roundtables present a valuable opportunity to surface your challenges, viewpoints and experience on the use of player feedback to inform game design. Learn from the experiences of fellow designers, researchers, data scientists, community and player champions, and take away new perspectives on the value of the players' voice in decision-making.




This roundtable covers Production. What routines and habits are worthwhile forming to support iterative and ongoing development? How can teams ensure secrecy, pace, and insight quality, sprint-to-sprint? Bring your challenges, questions and experiences to this roundtable focusing on player data strategy for mid-production and pre-launch.
takeaway:
An open discussion on approaches, lessons, challenges and opportunities in the use of player feedback. Bring your questions on how to capture, comprehend, action and measure the impact of insights from real players.

Each of three roundtables covers a differing phase of game development.
intended audience: Game designers, producers and quality-owners with advice and opportunities. Researchers, data scientists, accessibility and community advocates with practical experience of player research, seeking broader horizons and a chance to learn from the successes of others.

session title: Melting Hearts and Quenching Thirst Roundtable Day 1: Romance
description:
Making a game with romance or sex in it? Want to talk about love, lust, and intimacy in game design? Then come join fellow developers for a two-day roundtable discussion on romantic and sexual game content, touching on everything from the state of the industry to the potential future of intimacy in games! Attendees can share their thoughts on our prepared discussion topics, or bring their own questions to put to the group!

Roundtable Day 1: Romance in Game

This session focuses on game romance and relationships on a variety of levels such as narrative, design, and even marketing.
takeaway: Attendees to one or both sessions will take away a better understanding of current and future trends regarding romantic and/or sexual content in games and collaborative best practices for creating compelling games and ensuring they find their market. Attendees will also connect with like-minded developers, academics, and critics in the community.
intended audience: Developers of romance games, adult games, or games with romantic or sexual content, as well as academics, critics, and fans of these games and themes.

session title: 'Metro: Awakening': Development Postmortem
description:
With Metro: Awakening VR, Vertigo Games brought the atmospheric, claustrophobic tension of the best-selling Metro franchise to VR. The project required rethinking of vintage Metro gameplay for VR, but also brought about many interesting challenges associated with general game development. The game also had numerous stakeholders each with their slightly differing set of objectives and expectations. All this against the backdrop of some impactful real-world events, setting the stage for a classic, but fun roller coaster ride through the world of game development.
This post-mortem examines the creative, organizational and technical challenges Vertigo Games faced and the solutions that emerged and/or the lessons learned. It explores not just the specific VR ones, but rather, the general challenges associated with building a game of this magnitude subject to numerous constraints and conditions (VR being one of them) and how the studio dealt with them.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with concrete advice and specific examples of bringing a successful FPS property to VR, while managing a complex project on a short timeline.
intended audience: This session is for studio managers, producers, game directors and any discipline development team members interested in the general challenges of game development.

session title: Serious Games Roundtable (Presented by the IGDA)
description: The Serious Games Roundtable provides a forum for professional networking and discussion of critical topics in the serious games space. These topics include evergreen favorites such as funding, best practices for addressing serious topics (i.e. politics, mental health, etc), and scaling the impact of serious games, as well as emergent issues such as the continuing impact of AI (and other novel technologies), and the challenges and opportunities afforded by the economic and political landscape. Attendees are encouraged to bring up additional relevant topics for group discussion as a room and in smaller breakout discussions. The Serious Games Roundtable is hosted by the IGDA Serious Games SIG.
takeaway: Attendees will come away with an awareness of the current state of the serious games industry through a discussion with others in the field regarding its challenges, opportunities for growth, and what resources exist for current and aspiring practitioners.
intended audience: This session is aimed at those working in and around the field of serious games, or who are developers or academics looking to get involved and have input to provide.

session title: Soft Power Is Real Power: Reframing Brazil's Global Narrative through Games (Presented by Brazil Games)
description: Any country can be reduced to stereotypes once its culture crosses borders. Emerging markets, in particular, often struggle to define themselves beyond simplified global narratives. This session examines why cultural exports matter, and how games have become one of the most powerful instruments for shaping national identity abroad. Using Brazil as a case study, the session presents over two decades of coordinated advocacy and internationalization efforts led by Abragames, together with the Brazil Games Export Program, developed in strategic partnership with ApexBrasil. From regulatory advances such as the Games Legal Framework to the creation of structured acceleration programs and cultural recognition initiatives, the talk demonstrates how games operate not only as commercial products, but as strategic cultural assets with tangible impact.
