The result is measurable ecosystem growth, increased exports, and a stronger, more nuanced global presence for emerging creative economies.
takeaway: Attendees gain insight into how games can function as instruments of cultural influence and international positioning, drawing lessons from a long-term national case study that connects advocacy, public policy, and industry coordination to measurable ecosystem growth.
intended audience: This session is designed for policymakers, ecosystem builders, trade agencies, and studio executives of all sizes interested in international growth, cultural positioning, and emerging markets. No prerequisite knowledge is required, the conversation is relevant to anyone who sees games as cultural expression and global opportunity.
speakers: Rodrigo Terra(Abragames), Patrícia Sato(Brazil Games Export Program)

session title: Stop, Collaborate, and Listen: How to be an Actor's Favorite Director
description: As graphical and audio fidelity in games increases, players demand ever greater performances from their video game actors. In this panel chaired by Phillipe Bosher (Baldur's Gate 3), actors Yuri Lowenthal (Marvel's Spider-Man), Cissy Jones (Starfield), Elias Toufexis (Deus Ex: Mankind Divided), and Courtenay Taylor (Resident Evil) use their experience across stage, screen, audio, and video game to share how they achieve their best work, and how you can ensure your game utilizes its actors to their fullest potential. With a combined total of over 1,000 projects between them, the panel will discuss tips and techniques they've learned across their careers, covering topics like ensemble recording, actionable direction, the pitfalls of splitting voice and body performance, and trust and collaboration. They will also share concrete tools for how to save a session that is not going how you intended, before opening the floor to questions.
takeaway:
Learn what 'actioning' is, and how to use different acting methods to direct great performances.

Discover scientifically-backed methods for how to save a sinking session.

Explore how to use your actor's best assets to create rich characters.

Develop an approach to ensure your game's performances are consistently excellent.
intended audience: Developers working on projects that rely on strong, consistent actor performances to meet their narrative goals: especially directors, designers, writers, and producers who are involved in the recording pipeline. It is also for anyone interested in learning how to incorporate techniques from other media into video game development.
speakers: Phillipe Bosher(Independent), Yuri Lowenthal(Independent), Cissy Jones(Ethovox), Elias Toufexis(Independent), Courtenay Taylor(Independent)

session title: Teaching While Gamedev
description:
This session explores how teaching and game development can work together to build sustainable, fulfilling careers. A panel of developers who also teach will share how this path supports both their creative work and their students' growth.
Topics include how to start teaching, how to build a strong portfolio or CV, and how to find or propose the right courses or workshops to offer. The panel will also discuss hiring students, avoiding burnout, staying current with industry trends, and working on research projects.
Whether you want a steady income, a way to give back, or a new view on your own work, this session offers clear, practical advice for combining teaching and game development. No teaching background required.
takeaway: Understand the skills and mindset for a dual role in development and academia while avoiding burnout; know more about career options that are game industry adjacent, while also staying at the front lines.
intended audience: Game developers who want to share their skills through teaching. Developers seeking alternative, game-adjacent career paths that keep their skills sharp and support long-term sustainability.
speakers: Tyler Coleman(Ludo Systems Inc.), MJ Johns(University of California Santa Cruz), Colleen Macklin(Parsons School of Design), Jo Cronk(DigiPen Institute of Technology), Graeme Devine(GRL Games), Ian Schreiber(Independent)

session title: Technical Artists Roundtable (20th Year)!!! Day 2
description: The role of a technical artists has grown from a clean-up artist and data wrangler to someone who is in charge of complex content creation pipelines and asset tracking systems. Increasingly, the lines between programmer and artist are blurring for technical artists. Tool creation, scripting, shader creation, working with both programmers and artists, and managing workflow are all skills required of a modern technical artist. This roundtable brings together technical artists from across the industry to share their knowledge, experiences, and tips for being the best at their jobs.
takeaway: Participants will learn what common skills effective technical artists share and how to apply those skills to their specific company. Participants will leave with a better understanding of tech art and the value it will bring to their projects.
intended audience: This roundtable is open to technical artists who strive daily to improve workflow, increase efficiency, and maintain art production pipelines. Artists and programmers are welcome too, as their thoughts and expectations will help define the roles of all technical artists.

session title: The Growth Gap: Where App Advertisers Are Leaving Impact on the Table (Presented by Snap)
description:
Reaching new audiences is harder than ever in 2026. With more competition, more channels, and more noise, marketers must cut through the clutter to drive real, incremental business growth. Yet many may be leaving revenue on the table by measuring performance in silos instead of through a unified, cross-channel lens. Without a holistic view, the channels truly driving lift can go undercredited–and underfunded.
This session hosted by Snapchat explores how app advertisers can both optimize their campaigns and rethink how to measure them to unlock smarter growth. Guests will learn how to identify where they're seeing the greatest impact, take strategic risks with emerging ad formats, and ensure foundational elements– from pixels to attribution setups–are optimized for success. The result: clearer insight, sharper investment decisions, and sustainable growth that actually moves the business.
takeaway: Guests will learn how to identify where they're seeing the greatest impact, take strategic risks with emerging ad formats, and ensure foundational elements– from pixels to attribution setups–are optimized for success. The result: clearer insight, sharper investment decisions, and sustainable growth that actually moves the business.
intended audience: Full-funnel app marketers, agencies supporting app businesses, CMOs, growth/performance marketers, brand marketers

session title: Future‑Proof Your Game: Streamlined Workflows for a Multi‑Device World (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description: This talk explores how developers can efficiently build and optimize games for a broad range of Windows devices to reach more players. When you ship with Xbox, build for PC and we'll give you the tools to scale across devices. Learn how to leverage remote iteration workflows, PIX, and engine integration (Unity and Unreal) to streamline Windows multi-device development. Attendees will learn practical strategies for scaling games across different form factors, ensuring compatibility and performance fit, and ultimately future proofing their titles for an evolving ecosystem.
takeaway: When you ship with Xbox, build for PC and we'll give you the tools to scale across devices!
intended audience: Developers, engineering leads and technical evaluators, and directors whose focus is doing evaluations of platforms/stores. Developers who would like to get an overview of how to expand their reach when building for PC.
speakers: Karla Larriva(Microsoft Xbox), Zach Hooper(Microsoft Xbox), Jon Martin(Microsoft Xbox)

session title: Game Developer Podcast Live Recording - Owlchemy Labs CEO Andrew Eiche
description: Game Developer's Bryant Francis interviews Owlchemy Labs CEO Andrew Eiche live in the GDC Podcast Studio.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: How to Build or Use Generative AI That Is Legally Compliant, Safe, and Ethically Sustainable (Presented by Lingotion)
description:
Generative AI challenges how to interpret intellectual property rights, copyright, personal data, and artists' rights. Many companies developing generative AI use this legal uncertainty to train AI models on data without procuring the lawful rights or respecting artists rights. Generative AI has thereby become a highly contested legal area where ethics is hotly debated.

Lingotion offers a solution of generative AI actor cloning that clones an actor with full emotional expression and generates acting in real-time on device. In this session Lingotion shares the legal research results and experience in how to navigate and mitigate these legal risks, and how to safeguard artists rights.

The legal risks are outlined along with their underlying causes and the ways to mitigate them, helping you build a legally compliant and ethical generative AI solution.

The knowledge is applicable for all types of generative AI for developing internally or using third-party suppliers.
takeaway: The session describes how to create generative AI solutions or select third-party solutions that are legally safe from the ongoing legal challenges and are ethically sustainable. Additionally, it provides a deeper understanding of the underlying complexity of the rights for training data used in generative AI.
intended audience: The primary audience is management, developers, publishers, or game directors that want to use or develop generative AI in any form for game development.

session title: Live Podcast Interview: Scaling Up a Project with the Creators of 'Clair Obscur: Expedition 33' (Presented by Perforce)
description:
Join Jase Lindgren and Tom Guillermin, Co-founder and CTO of Sandfall Interactive, for an inside look at how a small indie team turned a passion project into a fully funded AAA-quality game. What began in 2019 with a single developer grew into a full 30-person studio—overcoming funding hurdles, expanding their tech stack, and building processes to stay organized along the way.


In this lively discussion, Tom will share lessons, missteps, and breakthroughs that shaped their journey – from the initial prototypes to a vertical slice, onto signing with Kepler Interactive in 2021 and scaling up to a full team with dedicated animators, artists, and QA engineers.


Stories like this are why we created In Development (https://www.perforce.com/podcasts/p4/in-development), Perforce Software's podcast spotlighting the technical minds behind today's most ambitious projects – from game development pipelines at CD PROJEKT RED to animation workflows at Nickelodeon. For our season two premiere, we'll be recording this conversation live at GDC 2026.
takeaway: Attendees will walk away with a real picture of what it looks like to build a game targeting a high production value as an indie, and learn how Sandfall navigated the challenges of building a studio from the ground up and secured funding without compromising their initial creative vision.
intended audience: This talk is for industry veterans, indie developers, and anyone curious about the realities of creating a high-production-value game.
speakers: Jase Lindgren(Perforce Software), Tom Guillermin(Sandfall Interactive)

session title: Play with the Designers
description: This unique session will allow you to play a tabletop roleplaying game with the designer present, who will discuss design decisions and options during play and their development process. This session will feature Jonaya Kemper, Sean Nittner (Agon), and Banana Chan (Jiangshi: Blood in the Banquet Hall). Participants will be able to play in one of the three games that the designers will be running.
takeaway: Designers who like a hands-on approach to learning about the design process.
intended audience: How different designers approach mechanics, theme, and balance; what is considered when writing for campaigns and characters for open-ended storytelling.
speakers: Banana Chan(Game and a Curry), Jonaya Kemper(UCSC), Sean Nittner(Evil Hat Productions), Tom Lehmann(Independent)

session title: Real-Time Generative Video Re-Styling for Gaming in the Next-Generation Datacenter (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: Decart's Lucy Restyle is a World Transformation Model that reimagines any video, game, or camera feed into a new digital world in real time. We will dive into how Decart and NVIDIA optimized Lucy Restyle inference for low-latency, cost-efficient cloud deployment on NVIDIA B40 GPUs through GeForce NOW (GFN). Finally, we'll demonstrate how to harness Lucy Restyle to completely restyle titles via the ultimate modding platform: RTX Remix.
takeaway:
Learn how the latest generative models can transform video, with fine grain control over the output style & look
Discover how GPU experts at Decart and NVIDIA optimized the Lucy Restyle model to run in the cloud on GDN datacenters, precision optimized for performance and ultra-low latency.


Learn how GDN SDK integration enabled Lucy Restyle to stream from the cloud to reach a range of consumer devices
See how Video to Video models can be plumbed into a game rendering pipeline for real-time frame buffer modification
intended audience:
Artists (Tech)
Engineers (Graphics, ML, Software)
Researchers (Graphics, AI)
speakers: Alex Dunn(NVIDIA), Bhavani Gopalakrishna Rao(NVIDIA), Ethan Peterson(Decart)

session title: Skillz X Beamable: Powering the Next Generation of Competitive Creators (Presented by Skillz)
description: The future of competitive gaming is open, flexible, and creator-driven, and it is here. In this session, Skillz unveils the next evolution of its SDK platform, an upgrade that builds on our foundation to empower developers to monetize their games through skill-based competition. The Skillz platform powers real-money gameplay through head-to-head matchups, live events, and leaderboards that engage millions of players worldwide. The new Skillz Pro SDK builds on this foundation, increasing flexibility, expanding creative possibilities, and unlocking additional monetization opportunities. It enhances how competition, engagement tools, and rewards come together in a more advanced development flow that delivers faster gameplay, richer progression, and deeper personalization. Learn about the vision behind the Skillz Pro SDK and why you should apply for the $75 Million Skillz Developer Accelerator program, designed to fund and support the next generation of competitive game ideas, while exploring titles that showcase how the Skillz Pro SDK empowers creators to build more seamless and engaging experiences.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to leverage the new Skillz Pro SDK platform improvements to create competitive experiences, scale faster, and access funding through the Developer Accelerator.
intended audience: Developers, product managers, and studios interested in building competitive mobile games or applying for the Skillz Developer Accelerator.
speakers: Gaurav Verma(Skillz), Jon Radoff(Beamable), Robert Burnett(Skillz)

session title: Stopping Insider Leaks Through the Use of AI-powered Steganography (Presented by EchoMark)
description:
Insiders leaking gaming artwork, plots, characters, trailers, launch information, pricing, and more is a chronic, pervasive, and highly damaging problem throughout the industry.

EchoMark, through its use of steganography, has developed an exciting new way to combat these leaks. See a live demo of content leaked through a digital copy/fragment, a camera photo of a computer screen, or even manually re-typed into another computer and how the source can be immediately identified.
takeaway: Learn how EchoMark is changing the game with AI-powered steganography. Now, publishers can easily and silently protect their intellectual property against insider leaks without impeding productivity.
intended audience: Game developers, marketers, PR, legal, or anyone else affected by insiders leaking information will benefit from learning how steganography is protecting intellectual property.

session title: The Uncomfortable Truth About AI in Game Production (Presented by Keywords Studios)
description:
AI is everywhere in game development conversations, but far less visible in real, shipped production. Most studios don't fail because the tools aren't powerful enough. They fail because the surrounding realities are ignored.
This talk cuts through the hype to examine the unsexy truth of adopting AI in day-to-day game development: how it fits (or doesn't) into existing workflows, who owns it once the experiment ends, how context and compliance shape outcomes, and why culture often matters more than capability. Drawing on real production experience across live projects and distributed teams, the session explores where AI initiatives typically stall, the hidden costs studios underestimate, and what actually enables AI to deliver sustained value without disrupting creative flow or production stability. This is a practical, experience-led look at AI as it really exists inside studios today and not as it's pitched.
takeaway: Attendees learn why AI pilots rarely survive contact with production, how to identify friction before it derails teams, and what practical conditions allow AI to support real development work without increasing risk, overhead, or creative disruption.
intended audience: This session is for studio leaders, producers, technical leads, and developers navigating AI adoption in active production environments. It assumes experience with modern game development pipelines but does not require machine-learning expertise.
speakers: Jon Gibson(Keywords Studios), Tara Phillips(Keywords Studios)

session title: We Built the Wrong Games First: Failing Forward to 'UG', VR's Fastest-Growing Game (Presented by Meta)
description: Successful games don't always start as hits. In this session, we share how early experimentation and failed assumptions ultimately led to the creation of UG, one of VR's fastest-growing games. We'll explore how analytics, player feedback, and strategic pivots reshaped our approach to game design, retention, and live operations. Attendees will leave with practical tools for recognizing early warning signals, knowing when to pivot, and building momentum in rapidly evolving platforms.
speakers: Spencer Cook(Continuum), Michael Murdock(Continuum), Kyle Sills(Continuum)

session title: GamePlan: Day 1
description: **GamePlan brings together qualified executives, decision makers, and innovators for pre-scheduled 1:1 business meetings that drive results. Build new customer and partner relationships, reconnect with familiar faces, generate leads, pitch ideas, explore funding opportunities, discover emerging technologies, and engage with media.**
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: GDC Side Quest Podcast Live: Samantha Béart
description: Samantha Béart, a BAFTA-nominated actor and celebrated performer in games like and , joins GDC for a live podcast recording on the Festival Hall floor. This session explores the growing role of actors in the games industry and why it's essential to include talent in narrative and development conversations.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a deeper appreciation for the role of actors in game development and learn how to create meaningful collaborations that enhance storytelling and player experiences.
intended audience: Perfect for developers, narrative designers, performers, and anyone interested in the intersection of performance and game development.
speakers: Samantha Beart(Independent), Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming)

session title: How Education Programs Seed the Next Generation of Game Creators (Presented by Epic Games)
description:
Indie creativity starts with opportunity. In this talk, we'll spotlight real student success stories, projects born in classrooms, community spaces, and university labs that grew into meaningful indie endeavours.

Through these examples, we'll explore how Unreal Engine and Epic's ecosystem help young creators turn early prototypes into promising careers—and how educational programs can ignite the next wave of indie innovation.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how real student projects evolve into indie games, how education programs enable early creative success, and how Unreal Engine and Epic's ecosystem support emerging developers from prototype to public release.
intended audience: Students, educators, and industry professionals interested in supporting student creators, early-stage indie teams, and the growth of local and global indie game scenes.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall

session title: Next-Gen Game Creation: Multiplayer Economies & AI World Models (Presented by German Pavilion)
speakers: Ferhan Ozkan(XR Bootcamp), Mark Val(Photon Engine by Exit Games GmbH)

session title: Responsible AI (Presented by Villain Studios)
description:
AI is an incredible accelerant for early-stage creativity. It excels at rapid prototyping, sketching concepts, exploring level layouts, generating quest variants, and helping engineers debug. But when it comes to a game's final creative expression—the characters players bond with, the stories that move them, the art that defines a world, and the music that anchors emotion—the work must remain unmistakably human. Anything less flirts with plagiarism.

Mark Long and Don Norbury outline how VILLAIN Studio applies 'responsible AI' on their upcoming title FRONTIER, using automation as a force multiplier while preserving the integrity of human authorship. Their framework blends ethical guardrails, transparent pipelines, and creative oversight to ensure AI supports developers without replacing them. The result is a production model that protects originality, empowers artists, and uses AI where it's strongest: speeding iteration so teams can create better, faster, and more boldly.
takeaway: Learn how VILLAIN uses AI for rapid prototyping while keeping final art, story, music, and characters fully human-made; how to avoid plagiarism risks; how to build ethical guardrails around AI tools; and how a responsible AI pipeline can accelerate development without sacrificing originality or creative authorship.
intended audience: This session targets game developers, technical artists, designers, and studio leads exploring AI-assisted pipelines. It benefits teams seeking faster prototyping without sacrificing originality. No prerequisites are required beyond basic familiarity with game development. The talk remains accessible to anyone interested in responsible, human-centered use of AI in production.
speakers: Mark Long(VILLAIN Studios), Don Norbury(VILLAIN Studios)

session title: Mastering Steam: Why Discovery is Hard and How to Break Through the Noise
description: The discovery ecosystem is chaotic, misunderstood, and unforgiving. Most games sink without a trace; not because they're bad, but because developers are still using a 2010s playbook on how platforms and media actually work. Ichiro Lambe is a 30 year industry veteran who founded one of the longest-running indie studios, Dejobaan Games, and was recruited by Valve to help improve discovery on Steam. He'll deconstruct the sales funnel, reframe the roles of media and platforms, and provide a concrete, iterative framework for making your game seen, understood, and desired. You will leave knowing how to test your messaging, align with the real goals of games media, and engineer every part of your store page—from its name to its trailer—to command attention in a marketplace of 130,000+ games.
takeaway: You'll gain an understanding of the 2020s discovery ecosystem and a framework for improving your game's visibility in a saturated market, including how to position your game for top-of-funnel traction, align with games media incentives, and iterate on your store page to convert interest into wishlists, purchases, and long-term engagement.
intended audience: This session is for developers, producers, and marketers working on PC games, who want to navigate the modern discovery ecosystem. It's especially relevant to small and mid-sized studios without dedicated marketing teams, but who still need their games to compete for visibility across storefronts, media, and social platforms.

session title: Case Studies in RTX Ray Tracing Optimization with NVIDIA Nsight Graphics (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: At NVIDIA, Nsight developer tools power daily validation systems that analyze many game titles across a wide matrix of development environments, catching performance regressions and identifying optimization opportunities at scale. This session explores the workflows that make this possible, then zooms in closer to review concrete examples of game optimization problems that required detective work to solve. You'll walk away with techniques to improve your own profiling and optimization workflows, and a deeper insight into the NVIDIA hardware capabilities behind ray tracing and other RTX features.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about the value provided by the NVIDIA graphics developer ecosystem, and that NVIDIA hardware brings with it a large network of support for shipping games with production needs in mind. This session will provide direct examples for how to accelerate the graphics development process at both the macro and micro level.
intended audience: Graphics Programmers, Engine Programmers, Technical Artists, QA Engineers.

session title: Discovery and Distribution in a Multi-Platform, Multi-Store World
description:
Developers today face unprecedented choice when it comes to distribution. Multiple storefronts, platforms, and payment models create new opportunities, but also new challenges: How do you cut through the noise? Which platforms are worth your time? What payment and revenue models lead to real success?


Join **Matthew Ball **(CEO and Managing Partner, Epyllion) and** Steven Allison** (VP and GM, Epic Games Store) for an essential conversation about distribution strategies that drive success in today's fragmented market.


Matthew Ball will share key insights from his newly released '*The State of Video Gaming in 2026'* report, revealing the trends and data reshaping how games reach players. Steven Allison will discuss proven strategies from the Epic Games Store for maximizing reach, connectivity, and revenue for indie studio AAA publishers alike.


Whether you're launching your first title or your fiftieth, this session will equip you with actionable intelligence to thrive in today's multi-platform, multi-store era.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA

session title: Expanding Globally: How Alternative Payment Methods Unlock International Player Growth (Presented by Antom)
description:
This session explores how payment strategy directly impacts global player acquisition and monetization for game publishers. As games scale beyond their home markets, understanding regional payment preferences becomes critical to reaching new audiences and reducing conversion friction.


The session also briefly introduces Antom, a leading global payment provider connecting businesses to over 2.2 billion users worldwide. It highlights key payment trends across major gaming markets, including the rise of digital wallets and alternative payment methods preferred by players in emerging regions.


Through real-world case studies, attendees will learn how game publishers leverage localized payment experiences to improve checkout conversion, expand into new markets, and accelerate international growth. The session provides practical insights into building a payment strategy that supports long-term global expansion.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insights into emerging payment trends across key gaming markets and learn how localized payment methods, especially digital wallets, can improve player acquisition, conversion, and global expansion strategies.
intended audience: Game publishers, monetization and growth leaders, product managers, payments and commerce teams, and business leaders responsible for global expansion and player acquisition.

session title: From Win to Wallet—Rethinking Player Experience in Skill-Based Gaming (Presented by AviaGames)
description: AviaGames and PayPal explore how payments and payouts shape the player experience in skill-based gaming, where speed, reliability, and transparency directly impact trust and retention. The panel examines how closing the 'win to wallet' gap turns winning moments into long-term engagement.
takeaway: Attendees hear how PayPal and AviaGames work together to improve player experience through Express Checkout, fast payouts, and reduced friction. They learn why payouts matter in real-money gaming and how pay-in/pay-out risk controls, anti-fraud, and chargeback management help support safer transactions and higher payment success.
intended audience: This session is for anyone interested in player experience and payments in competitive gaming, including developers, designers, marketers, operators, and partner teams. It is useful for those curious about checkout and payout journeys and how trust, risk controls, and anti-fraud practices support smoother, safer experiences.
speakers: Yanjuan Chen(AviaGames), Justin Butler(PayPal), Michael Vella(AviaGames), Qi Zhao(AviaGames)

session title: Policy to Profit: What the New App Rules Unlock for Game Publishers Globally (Presented by Coda)
description: The app ecosystem is changing fast. Between the EU DMA, Japan's MSCA, and the Epic rulings in the US, publishers are hearing a lot of noise about what is now possible. The reality is more complicated. Implementation differs by market, and the opportunity is real but uneven. This session gives publishers a clear and practical cheatsheet of what has changed, what it means in the real world, and what to watch next. We focus on the business impact for game publishers, not legal details, so publishers leave with clarity, not confusion.
takeaway: Cut through the regulatory noise to understand what the EU DMA, Japan's MSCA, and Epic rulings actually change for game publishers. See the real business impact on distribution and monetization, what remains restricted, and get a practical 12–24 month strategy to capture upside while managing risk.
intended audience: This session targets game publishers, commercial and strategy leaders, legal and policy teams, and product owners navigating the EU DMA, Japan's MSCA, the Epic rulings, and other global policy developments. It benefits teams in reassessing monetization and distribution strategy. No legal expertise is required; a working knowledge of app stores, publishing models, and monetization fundamentals is sufficient.
speakers: Shane Happach(Coda), Liz Adam(Coda), Lisa Hanson(Niko Partners)

session title: Unlocking the Next Billion Gamers: Where the Industry Is Growing and Why (Presented by Testronic)
description:
Player growth is accelerating across regions once considered secondary, reshaping the global landscape of game development, publishing, live operations and associated industry services. This session will explore where the next billion players are emerging – and the market, cultural, and technological forces driving that expansion.

We'll examine fast-rising audiences across MENA, Southeast Asia, LATAM and India: how mobile-first ecosystems, localised business models, and shifting demographics are redefining player expectations; and why cultural relevance, affordability, and accessibility are now essential to global success.

As studios scale into these regions, quality and trust matter more than ever. Testronic's global Centres of Excellence – including its state-of-the-art Manila studio in the heart of SEA – deliver player-focussed, outcomes-led QA services that ensure cultural alignment, stability, and polished day-one experiences.

Attendees will leave with actionable insight into the opportunities, and operational realities, of building for tomorrow's global audience.
takeaway:
- Where and why the next billion players are emerging
- Key growth drivers across SEA, LATAM, MENA, and India
- Why cultural relevance, accessibility, and trust determine success
- How Testronic's global Centres of Excellence support high-quality launches
- Practical considerations for scaling into new markets
intended audience: This talk is for studio leaders, business strategists, and product owners exploring global expansion. It will deliver insights for teams evaluating new markets, evolving player behaviors, and the operational demands of launching culturally aligned, high-quality experiences across emerging regions.

session title: GDC Side Quest Podcast Live: State of the Game Industry Panel
description:
Join Laine Nooney, Ben Porter, and Liam Deane for an insightful discussion on the 2026 report from GDC Festival of Gaming. Moderated by Beth Elderkin, this panel explores key findings from the report, alongside research from Newzoo and Omdia—offering a comprehensive look at the trends shaping the future of the games industry.

Topics will include the rise of generative AI, the impact of layoffs on the industry, the growing importance of cross-platform games, the shift toward direct-to-consumer (DTC) payment methods, and more. This session provides a unique opportunity to understand how these trends are influencing the industry and what they mean for developers, publishers, and players alike.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a deeper understanding of the most pressing trends in the games industry, how they intersect with broader research, and actionable insights to navigate the evolving landscape.
intended audience: Perfect for developers, publishers, industry analysts, and anyone interested in the current and future state of the games industry.
speakers: Liam Deane(Omdia), Laine Nooney(New York University), Ben Porter(Newzoo), Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming)

session title: Art Leadership Roundtable Day 2
description: The art leadership roundtable is an open discussion forum for topics concerning the leadership of creative teams. All attendees are welcome to learn from industry veterans as we all seek to navigate the ubiquitous challenges of inspiring teams to their best work, while navigating both change and uncertainty. Come to listen. Come to share. Join us as we learn from each other and build better creative cultures.
takeaway: The roundtable is an open forum for discussion of topics related to creative leadership. This includes setting direction, managing expectations, building healthy teams, as well as developing resilience and adaptability. The moderator manages the flow of topics as they are surfaced with the possibility that notes will be shared with attendees after GDC.
intended audience: This roundtable is open to all attendees. Most takeaways are frequently targeted at experienced developers who are looking to level up their managerial skills or build stronger leadership networks. Veterans are strongly encouraged to attend to share their wisdom and experiences.

session title: Content Creation in Game Development
description: Many developers discover game development content through their favorite creators, whether via devlogs, shortform video, or social media clips that pull back the curtain on how games are made. This panel brings those creators to the stage -- Juniper Dev, SonderingEmily, Nick Kaman (studio head of Aggro Crab), and Jordan from O. And Co. Games -- to examine how content creation and game development increasingly intersect, and how to effectively use social media when marketing and making games.
takeaway: Through candid, conversational discussion, panelists will share real examples of what elements of content creation have worked, what hasn't, and the tradeoffs of building games publicly. Expect to leave with a clearer, specific understanding of how to leverage social media intentionally within game development, as well as a thoughtful new conversation on the broader role of social media in making games!
intended audience: This panel is for anyone who wants to hear from their favorite creators about the intersection of social media and game development, as well as developers hoping to gain insight as to how to effectively utilize content when making games.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall
speakers: Juniper Dev(Independent), Nick Kaman(Aggro Crab), Jordan Ottesen(O. and Co. Games), Emily Pitcher(SonderingEmily)

session title: Crafting the Core Sonic Identity of 'Battlefield 6': From Concept to Execution
description:
This talk outlines the process of defining the audio vision for Battlefield 6 by learning from the franchise's past, refining its identity, and evolving it for the game's sound. We'll showcase our progress and the challenges faced during production, highlighting lessons learned for future projects. Gameplay examples will demonstrate enhancements in core audio features, like believable acoustics and a gritty soundscape, balancing immersion with gameplay readability. Additionally, we'll introduce new tools and features supporting this vision.
The talk also highlights challenges in building a new audio team, focusing on nurturing team culture while creating a new title intended to be a return to form. Examples will show how simple yet impactful implementations were combined with complex new tech, deciding between using smoke and mirrors or simulation methods, and employing dynamic mix techniques for readability amidst the chaos of the battlefield.
takeaway: The talk covers building Battlefield 6's audio identity across global teams, creating an immersive soundscape, refining audio direction and examples of how to execute on a raw, gritty and believable war soundscape in a modern, competitive shooter. It touches on sound design and recording techniques, team collaboration and overall mix.
intended audience: This talk is aimed at game audio developers, junior to senior with an interest in how to craft loud and complex soundscapes. A basic knowledge in video game audio is beneficial.
speakers: Mari Saastamoinen Minto(EA DICE), David Jegutidse(EA DICE)

session title: Educator Game Research Download
description: Too busy to read academic papers, but wondering if game researchers in the ivory tower have come up with some useful findings for you? Curious about how games are used by academics to ask basic questions about how people behave? We've got you covered with this panel–experts from game research conferences bring the latest findings from the top academic conferences to GDC with rapid-fire summaries and a chance for you to pose your own questions.
takeaway: Attendees will get a quick sense of some key recent game research findings, and have a chance to engage with game researchers to help shape future research questions, as well.
intended audience: Anyone who wants to get a quick sense of current game research that might apply to their work as a game designer/developer.
speakers: Julian Frommel(Utrecht University), Erik Harpstead(Carnegie Mellon University), Regan Mandryk(University of Victoria)

session title: Ethics in AI Tools for UX Roundtable
description: With AI tools like ChatGPT and Gemini being enforced tools within studios, how are UX teams ethically integrating them into their frameworks and processes? What strategies are working and what ethical boundaries have teams encountered? For those required to use AI tools by their employer yet ethically opposed, how have you raised awareness around its issues, protested its use, and impacted change in development processes? This roundtable aims to create a shared discussion space around AI, UX, and ethics as this topic remains at the forefront of corporate leadership.____

session title: Games from Wales / Gêmau o Gymru (Presented by Wales Pavilion)
description:
In this session, Osian Williams explores the rise of the Welsh games industry through the lens of COPA Gaming, an independent studio developing The Department, a single-player detective game set in Wales.

Drawing on over a decade of experience in UK film and television, Osian shares how cinematic craft, cultural identity, and government-backed support are converging to shape a new generation of Welsh-made interactive experiences.

He discusses the impact of Creative Wales' Scale Up funding, the importance of building sustainable studios outside traditional industry hubs, and how diversified revenue including Unreal Engine education and marketplace assets enables creative independence.

This talk offers practical insight into building globally competitive IP from a small nation, and demonstrates how embracing local culture can become a powerful strategic advantage in the international games market
takeaway: Attendees gain practical insight into building a first original IP from the ground up, including securing public funding through Creative Wales, structuring a vertical slice for publisher conversations, and using Unreal Engine education and asset revenue to de-risk development while remaining creatively independent.
intended audience: This session targets independent studio founders, producers, and creative directors building their first original IP, particularly those outside major industry hubs. It benefits developers seeking sustainable funding and revenue strategies. No prerequisites are required, though familiarity with game production pipelines and publisher pitching is helpful.

session title: Hybrid and Remote Work Roundtable Day 1: Hybrid
description:
What does it take to build and nurture a hybrid working environment? What are the challenges of working with and coordinating teams between on-site and remote team members? How can we foster a sense of belonging and inclusion? What are the challenges to hiring for and leading a hybrid team?

Many companies are beginning to consciously offer hybrid work environments. With globalization leading to faster and more reliable access to internet, these development environments are becoming more feasible with each passing year. Yet they present their own unique challenges.

Engage with other industry professionals on the topic of hybrid working arrangements—including hiring, team leadership, accountability, company events, IT solutions, and more. Challenge the status quo of working a 9-to-5 office job with this collaborative and innovative approach development.
takeaway: Attendees will expand their knowledge on hybrid work solutions through a collaborative discussion. Join veterans and new team leaders alike to contemplate the challenges and celebrate the successes of this powerful workplace solution.
intended audience: This is for team leaders (of large or small teams) interested in dissecting how to create and support hybrid working environments. Whether the team is already hybrid or considering the change, this roundtable is open to all. Regardless of roundtable topic, both hybrid and remote teams welcome as both types share many topics.
speakers: Jessica Damerst(Unknown Worlds), Trevin York(Dire Lark)

session title: Involving Kids in Game Development Roundtable: Possibilities, Boundaries, and Responsibility (Presented by the IGDA)
description:
This roundtable is hosted by the Devs with Kids SIG.

Last year's round table discussed ways to involve children in more game jams and how we, as parents, can create kid friendly game jams. This year, we want to focus on the influx of children's games that allow for kids to make their own game modes and encourage children to participate in the game's development through creative modes. We'll take a look at clubs and schools that are hosting game jams for students and we'll discuss the possibilities for children interested in game development. We'll also discuss our responsibilities as parents and reasonable boundaries to set for game jams and the children involved.

The goal is for attendees to be able to collaboratively discuss concerns that we have as parents about the nature of children's games capitalizing on them as developers, game jams, and how we can be proactive in helping them navigate game development as a creative hobby.
takeaway: Participants in this roundtable session will gain a better understanding of potentially predatory practices by large game companies, ways to work around those, and to encourage their children. Ideally, everyone will leave the roundtable with a toolkit for responsibly supporting their children's game development hobby.
intended audience: All are welcome but the topic and subject matter will be most interesting to parents of children that are interested in or actively participating in game development as a hobby.

session title: Let's Play Economics: A Live Trading Game on Asymmetry and Incentives (Presented by the IGDA)
description: The Game Economics SIG is hosting an interactive session on trading! We'll explore the purposes trading serves in games, key factors that drive players to trade, and market tools to streamline or add friction as needed. Bring your game face, because we'll demonstrate these principles with a live trading game for everyone to participate in!
takeaway: At the end of Let's Play Economics session, participants will gain firsthand insight into how asymmetric information, shifting incentives, and trading tools shape player behavior and market dynamics in games. Attendees will leave with a stronger understanding of how to design, observe, and balance in-game economies that evolve dynamically and respond to player strategies.
intended audience: Let's Play Economics is intended for game designers, economists, producers, and anyone interested in player-driven economies and live-service balancing. No prior economics knowledge is required—this session welcomes both designers seeking new ways to simulate market systems and players curious about the hidden forces behind in-game trading and value creation.
speakers: Dario Gentiletti(Google), Eric Guan(Second Dinner), Christopher Kaczmarczyk-Smith(Star Atlas)

session title: Marketing is Art: Working with Your Team to Get What You Need
description: In the current landscape of game development, Marketing teams need to make countless numbers of assets to reach audiences online. This talk explores how the Marketing and Art teams behind Astroneer collaborate to build content creation pipelines and outline best practices, enabling non-technical team members to produce high-quality marketing materials over 8+ years of development.
takeaway: This talk aims to help marketers ask for tools to enable themselves to do their best work. Attendees should be able to identify their needs and subsequently work with their teams to build documents, tools, and pipelines that will pay off in the long run.
intended audience: The main audience for this presentation are marketing team members (community, social, etc) but also artists, engineers, and other development team members who create tools or pipelines for others.
speakers: Joe Tirado(System Era Softworks/Latinos In Gaming), Spencer Kern(System Era Softworks)

session title: Meaningful Reasons to Wander: Creating Open World Engagement in 'Ghost of Yōtei'
description:
After Ghost of Yotei's release, reactions online have made something very clear: players around the world find themselves so engaged with our open world content that they continue to delay their main story playthrough, sometimes for countless hours, as they instead choose to bask in the wandering ronin fantasy of our game.




However, during development, this was not always how playtesters felt. In fact, there was a time when playtesters chose to skip most of our open world content while rushing into the main story instead, causing onboarding, comprehension and progression issues. So how did we manage to turn things around?




Join Senior Writer and Narrative Designer, Ariadna Martinez, for a deep dive into how narrative design was instrumental in solving the challenges of engaging and retaining the player's investment in Ghost of Yōtei's open world. Discover how effective narrative design was able to identify and tackle the underlying issues that were causing players to lose interest in our open world content, allowing us to increase player retention, and ensure full investment in our core gameplay loops, progression systems, and story during those first crucial hours (and beyond) primarily through non-linear content.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insight into specific narrative design processes and strategies used to tackle issues such as lack of player engagement and retention in our open world, poor comprehension and usability, and narrative dissonance within the first foundational hours of a modern open world game like Ghost of Yōtei.
intended audience:
This lecture is intended for those looking to add tools and strategies to their own design process when tackling open world development, from narrative designers and writers to level and world/systemic designers.

It is also meant for any Ghost of Yōtei fans looking to get a behind the scenes look at the game's development process.

session title: Outsourcing Without the Overhead: Practical Tips for Smarter Collaboration
description: Whether it's audio, codev, art, or QA, working with external contributors is the norm in today's game development cycles. But misalignment costs time, money, and morale. In this conversation between outsourcers and outsource managers from different disciplines, key insights will be shared to help developers build better workflows with outsourced teams—with a focus on clear ownership, defining goals, adapting schedules, and planning around a budget. Whether you're commissioning work for the first time or refining long-term vendor relationships, this session offers real-world advice on managing external collaborations with less friction and better results.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with actionable strategies for working more effectively with outsourced teams—whether you're a producer, artist, or engineer. Learn how to set clear expectations, integrate deliverables into your workflow, and avoid common traps that cost time and money.
intended audience: This panel is designed for mid-level to senior developers who interact with external teams—particularly in roles such as producers, project managers, technical and art leads, audio and QA coordinators, external development managers or outsourcing managers, as well as indie developers scaling up with remote contributors.
speakers: Mathieu Lavoie(Side), Sam Carlisle(XDS), Matthew Schulz(Virtuos), Maciej Glomb(Bloober Team), Amelie Meunier(Behaviour Interactive)

session title: Performance and Direction Deep Dive
description:
In this panel, actors Jane Perry and Debra Wilson with directors Damien Goodwin and Tom Keegan take a deep dive into the craft and art of performance in games.
Jane and Damien have worked together on several projects, most notably 'Returnal' and 'As Dusk Falls'. Together, Tom and Debra have contributed to major titles like 'Wolfenstein II: The New Colossus' and 'Jedi: Fallen Order', along with numerous other projects.
The panel will explore the relationship between actor and director, examining how their roles interact and evolve. It will also delve into the distinct skills required for voice sessions versus performance capture, along with the creative decision-making and techniques involved in shaping a compelling performance.
They will also examine effective collaboration with the wider development team.
Fellow Audio and Voice Director Adele Cutting will chair this discussion.
takeaway:
A clearer understanding of the actor-director dynamic.
Insight into the practical differences between voice acting and performance capture.
Knowledge of how creative decisions are made during recording and capture sessions and how choices evolve and how actors and directors collaborate to refine them.
Real examples from acclaimed titles.
intended audience:
This panel will be interesting to anyone involved in games Narrative.
Actors, Directors, Audio Directors, Dialogue Editors and Designers, Narrative Directors and Designers, Game Designers and Directors.
Game Devs of all levels.
speakers: Jane Perry(Independent), Debra Wilson(Independent), Tom Keegan(Independent), Damien Goodwin(Independent), Adele Cutting(Soundcuts Ltd)

session title: 'Pianomania': Piano Rhythm Game Debut & Live Tournament
description:
Pianomania, a rhythm game in the style of Guitar Hero and Dance Dance Revolution, lets players hone their piano skills and compete against players around the world. In the game players can master their favorite songs in practice mode, as well as personalize their very own studio.

Pianomania is founded on the idea that learning to play the piano is one of the most enriching activities for the human soul. Join this session with Pianomania founders, former professional concert pianist, Will Sievern and Google software engineer, Matt Takao to experience their creation yourself - DDR meets playing the piano!

This hybrid session begins with a short presentation by Will and Matt, followed by Q&A and in-person tournament!
intended audience: All are welcome to learn and play! In particular, game creators, rhythm game enthusiasts, piano players of all levels, and attendees looking to discover something new will find this session engaging. Also, art directors seeking a new professional venture are encouraged to check it out and meet the team!

session title: Preservation Is Profit: How Retro Games Unlock Long-Term Revenue
description: Once seen mainly as a cultural responsibility, game preservation is now proving to be a smart business move. In this session, three experienced professionals working in remastering, platform strategy, and legacy content will each deliver focused micro-talks. Together, they'll explore how retro games can generate long-term revenue, extend the life of intellectual property, and reduce risk for both publishers and platforms.
takeaway: Attendees gain strategic insights into how retro games can be used for long-term monetization, IP risk reduction, and brand revitalization. This session will cover everything from how remastered titles are chosen and marketed, to how digital storefronts meet rising demand for older games, to what companies can do now to preserve source materials and licensing rights before they become roadblocks.
intended audience: Developers, publishers, and IP rights holders will all benefit from this session.
speakers: Marcin Paczynski(GOG), Larry Kuperman(Nightdive Studios), Garrett Fredley(PlayStation)

session title: 'SILENT HILL f': The Challenges of Creating a Melee-Only Horror Game
description:
Breaking the mold of traditional horror game combat, SILENT HILL f was intentionally designed as the first melee only entry title in the series. During this talk we will examine and break down the critical tempo regulating design of traditional ranged horror game combat and how we adapted or created new systems in SILENT HILL f in order to preserve combat pacing.

Examples will be given using direct system and mechanical comparisons and explaining the design process and decision making steps of: key combat systems, progression, enemy A.I., all the way to the visual and mechanical design of our main character, Hinako.
takeaway: Attendees will learn the decisions behind why SILENT HILL f became a melee only horror game and gain a deeper understanding of how gameplay systems in traditional horror games that we might take for granted are critical to the genre's combat tempo. This will be shown by development examples that show how we analyzed, replicated, and created variations of these regulatory systems for SILENT HILL f.
intended audience:
Developers curious about how SILENT HILL f's design chose to go fully melee combat and our analytical approach to methodically breaking apart historical horror game combat systems and rebuilding them for SILENT HILL f may find this talk interesting.

This content will be useful in general for developers working with combat systems, especially those that are working towards significantly different directions in genres that traditionally have strong and more rigid player expectations.

session title: SoftMax: Neural Simulation of Skeletal Physics
description: This session presents a production-proven Neural Simulation system for skeletal physics, as seen on dozens of characters in the action game FateTrigger. The presenters explain how they overcame the notorious performance bottleneck of real-time secondary physics simulations (hair, cloth, accessories) for a large cast of characters. Their deep learning solution delivers high-fidelity, stable physics for a fraction of the cost—just 0.02-0.13ms on a single CPU core—making complex simulations viable while maintaining smooth, high-framerate gameplay. The talk explores the entire production pipeline, from its meticulously designed network architecture with quantization-aware training, to its high-performance custom C++ inference engine and a dynamic, load-balanced LOD system. It demonstrates that neural physics is no longer a research concept but a powerful solution for achieving superior performance while scaling to a higher volume of dynamic character simulations.
takeaway: Attendees gain a practical blueprint for building their own high-performance neural physics system. This includes specific techniques for creating an automated data pipeline, designing a versatile network architecture for temporal dynamics, and deploying a highly optimized C++ inference engine for real-time performance in a production environment.
intended audience: This session is for graphics programmers, engine developers, AI programmers, and technical artists. A solid understanding of 3D math and game engine architecture is recommended. Basic familiarity with machine learning concepts and traditional character physics systems will be beneficial for a deeper understanding of the material.
speakers: Xin Qiao(LIGHTSPEED STUDIOS), Xiaolin Zhang(LIGHTSPEED STUDIOS)

session title: Spatial Content Creation with Sony | XYN (Presented by Sony | XYN)
description:
Introducing XYN™ (/zin/) - an integrated software and hardware solution that supports the creation of spatial content. By using Sony's unique technology, you can accurately capture the real-world data and combine the content with a wide range of third-party tools to enable flexible workflow construction.

We will be going over available tools and talk beta access to studios interested in 'Spatial Capture Solution' for object scanning.
takeaway: Information on beta programs at Sony | XYN for Spatial content creation tools.
intended audience: Game Developers, Visual Effects, Graphic Artists, Animation Artists, Environmental Artist
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Stories from the Original Game Art Scene
description: A panel of legendary game artists reminisce about the birth of the western game-art scene, and learning how to make art in days before anyone knew how and the communities we formed to push ourselves with challenges, contests, rivalries, and more. We're talking about the old Polycount vs cgchat days, about manually obsessing over every every pixel on our character textures, and about getting our minds blown (and butts kicked) by the arrival of normal maps. We'll talk about companies poaching talent, about lifting each other up, about scandalous internet drama, mysterious art-gods and tech wizards and engine-gossip and more--all brought to you by the people who helped form and run these communities.
takeaway: Attendees will come away from this panel with some insight into the history of our trade, as well as some inspirational anecdotes about how scrappy (and barely functional) development was in those days, how small the industry once was, and how much we all had to lean on each other in order to grow as artists.
intended audience: This panel is for any game artist, but particular 3d artists who want to see where we've come from, hear from early pioneers of the craft.
speakers: Joe Mirabello(Terrible Posture Games, Inc.), Peet Cooper(Millions of Monsters), Josh Singh(Epic Games), Kenny Carvalho(Dardo Studios), Grace Liu(Netflix Games)

session title: Supporting Junior Talent and Why the Industry Needs Them
description: How do you support juniors in the current industry environment? The short answer is: be brave and take the risk. The long answer, we'll share. The current state of the games industry has resulted in a landscape devoid of work for junior and entry-level developers. In a time where more universities and programs are offering game development degrees and classes, these hopefuls are graduating into a world where their field actively makes little room for them. Furthermore, those hopeful to break into the games industry have no point of entry. This panel will examine why this trend is dangerous to the overall health of the industry and why we need junior-level talent in our studios. We will also share best practices for hiring, managing, and developing interns and entry-level talent, with real-world examples of how to build effective support structures and set them, and their leads, up for success.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with a clear view of why studios need to create roles for entry and junior talent and how they will benefit from hiring them in the long term. They will also leave with examples of how this process has worked at other studios and best practices for advocating to leadership for these roles as well as methodologies for supporting them.
intended audience: Developers at all levels, especially those in leadership and positions to hire, will benefit from this talk.
speakers: Alexa Ray Corriea(Gearbox), Anna C. Webster(Independent), Jordan DeVries(Respawn), Kevin Johnson(Double Fine Productions), Brie Chin-Deyerle(Independent)

session title: The OpenUSD Roundtable Day 2
description:
USD stands for 'Universal Scene Description', and it is a 3D file format Pixar created over a span of many years and movies, and was released open source to the public in 2016. Although technically it is more of an API than a file format, which is an ongoing discussion into the terminology and use of USD.

In this session, attendees can gather to talk about USD. Come and meet, discuss, and find out what everyone has been doing with USD.

Questions can be submitted during the roundtable. For example, these may include topics such as:
1. What are issues you have bumped into when implementing USD into your pipeline?
2. What would be required for USD to be more widely adopted?
3. How can studios, and vendors, best share Schemas?

Questions can be oriented towards technical issues, design, and artistic workflows, with day 1 having a larger focus on artistic workflows, and day 2 having a larger focus on technical issues.
takeaway: Attendees will share their experiences, challenges, and successes with USD. They can expect to leave with information shared by other studios who have used USD, and real world experiences from other teams, that they can then apply to their own thought process around USD, as well as in their own implementations of USD.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended for anyone with an interest in USD. This could be programmers, technical artists, designers, but also producers and technical directors who are looking into how USD is used, and what the pros and cons are that those in the industry have experienced with USD.

session title: The Ripple Is Real: Influence in 'World of Tanks' Campaigns and Social Value
description: This session will explain how Wargaming has used a powerful new algorithm called Social Value to first track all of the human influence in its game World of Tanks, and then test against that influence in a series of LiveOps campaigns. High-level, the results show that a large portion of revenue is driven by social interactions. Specific campaigns have proven out the idea that getting the right people to get an offer impacts their receptive friends like the proverbial ripples on a pond. This session will share the results, explain how it's done, and then offer a workshop portion in which attendees can explore how the approach might work within their specific games and communities.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how Social Value works, and see its use in successful LiveOps campaigns from within World of Tanks. A workshop portion will allow attendees to brainstorm and explore the possibilities, including how it might apply in their own titles.
intended audience: Attendees should have a basic knowledge of LiveOps campaigns and some familiarity with their in-game communities and game mechanics. Experience with AB testing would be helpful. Although data scientists will have extra insights and questions, no background is required to understand the concepts.
speakers: Dmitri Williams(USC & Griffin Scientific), Michal Reszka(Wargaming, Inc.)

session title: BIPOC Game Studies Conference
description:
The annual BIPOC Games Studies Conference is gearing up for its next release, and we're looking for collaborators, input and leaders. BIPOC Game Studies is a unique space where peer-reviewed research meets raw industry experience, tackling everything from decolonizing game design and Afrofuturism to digital heritage and social activism. We're moving beyond representation as a buzzword and into representation as a mechanic, bringing together leaders from academia and the studio floor to explore the intersection of culture, tech, and the BIPOC experience.

To make the next conference our Gold Master version, we invite your input to map the quest ahead. This interactive planning session will focus on bridging the gap between academic research and industry application, brainstorming the next Game Showcase, and expanding our community network. Whether you're an academic pushing the boundaries of game studies, a student looking to break in, or a veteran dev interested in social impact, join us to help shape a roadmap that fosters visibility, education, and—most importantly—play. Let's build something that matters.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: From Margins to Markets: Funding Women & the Underdog in Gaming
description:
Despite driving culture, innovation, and emerging player communities, women, minorities, and underrepresented founders continue to receive a disproportionately small share of gaming and tech capital. Yet history shows that the so-called 'underdog' often builds the next breakthrough market.

From Margins to Markets is a bold conversation at GDC, powered by NAMWMT, focused on transforming overlooked talent into funded, scalable companies. This session brings together founders, investors, publishers, and ecosystem leaders to address the capital gap — and more importantly, to design solutions that move real dollars.

We will explore:

The structural barriers limiting access to funding in gaming

Why diverse teams outperform and unlock new player audiences

How investors can reduce bias in sourcing and diligence

What bridge funding, partnerships, and mentorship models actually work

Concrete commitments to increase capital flow over the next 12 months

This is not a diversity discussion for optics.
This is about market expansion, competitive advantage, and building the future of gaming with those who have historically been left out of the room.

Because when capital reaches the margins, it creates entirely new markets.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Fireside Chat with Amir Rao of Supergiant Games
description:
Join host of the AIAS Game Makers Notebook podcast, Trent Kusters (League of Geeks), as he chats with Amir Rao, co-founder and studio director of Supergiant Games, developer of indie classics Bastion, Transistor, Pyre, and of course, Hades I & II.

In this intimate conversation, Trent and Amir will discuss Supergiant's impressive portfolio of indie classics, the design and narrative innovations that led to Hades redefining the roguelike, and how Amir and the team at Supergiant succeeded in the enormous challenge of delivering a sequel worthy of Hades' immense success.

An interestingly unique and at the same time familiar studio in the indie landscape, Trent and Amir will also discuss some of Supergiant's leadership and production philosophies that have forged one of the most critically, culturally, and commercially successful independent videogame studios of our time.
takeaway: All attendees will leave with a stronger understanding of the design philosophies behind Supergiant's games, especially the Hades series, and a glimpse into the studio and leadership philosophies that underpin Supergiant Games' success.
intended audience: Whilst many of the topics will cover design and studio leadership, this fireside chat is intended for all audiences, especially fans of Supergiant Games' impressive portfolio of work.
speakers: Trent Kusters(League of Geeks), Amir Rao(Supergiant Games)

session title: Live Sound Bath and Meditation (Presented by thatgamecompany)
description:
Tim Nixon (thatgamecompany) & Caio Jiacomini (thatgamecompany)

Quiet Room 213

Take a moment from the busy show to relax and enjoy the calming sounds of crystal singing bowls intertwined with the atmosphere of Sky: Children of the Light, before sinking into a short guided meditation to help refresh the mind and body. All are welcome.




**Doors Open at 9:30AM.**

session title: Your Steam Page Needs a Soul: How to Get Your Game's Magic Across on Steam
description:
A great Steam page can change an indie game's fate, turning obscurity into long-term sales, yet most still look like lifeless feature lists. For indie developers, Steam pages are the single most important and direct way to reach players. Still, many copy bland and boring AAA layouts, formats that only work thanks to brand power and marketing spend, and describe their game like they're selling accounting software.
This session explores how to build pages that connect emotionally, convert quickly, and most improtantly set the right expectations for the almighty Steam algorithm. Drawing on backend insights and data from supporting over 30 game launches a year, and dissecting hands-on insights from standout examples like CloverPit, Summerhouse, Peak, Necesse, and Vampire Survivors. This talk shows why tone, honesty, and personality matter more than polish or safe feature lists. The talk also includes hard data from the backend, page-by-page breakdowns, and practical tips and tricks to optimize for visibility and conversion.
takeaway: Attendees gain a deep understanding of how to craft a modern Steam page that resonates emotionally and visually in a way that improves vibes, traffic, conversions, and algorithmic visibility. And most importantly sets the right expectations. The talk combines real-world examples, data, and actionable tips from real-life examples and recent steam changes from experience of launching dozens of indie games per year.
intended audience: This session is for anyone making a game with a Steam page, whether solo developer, a bigger dev team or a studio marketer. No prior knowledge is required. Ideal for indies who want their game to stand out in an overcrowded store and connect with players quickly and emotionally.

session title: Build Once, Play Anywhere: PlayFab Powers Xbox Cross-Platform Game Services (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description: As games increasingly launch across console, PC, and mobile, ensuring a seamless player experience on every platform has become crucial, yet it remains a complex challenge. PlayFab is reducing this complexity by offering a suite of cross-platform services for identity, multiplayer, progression, community, live ops, economy, and data. This session explores how PlayFab's cross‑platform capabilities help developers remove backend overhead, avoid fragmented player ecosystems, and reach larger audiences with less friction. Through real‑world examples from successful titles, we'll show how studios are already using these services to accelerate development and support players wherever they choose to play.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a clear understanding of how Xbox helps developers reach the broadest possible audience by lowering barriers to adopt cross-platform services for Xbox creators.
intended audience: Current Xbox partners and teams evaluating Xbox services will benefit most from this session. It is also valuable for game developers building or planning cross‑platform titles. Basic familiarity with backend systems is helpful but not required.

session title: Building a Winning Discord Strategy to Fuel the Success of Your Game (Presented by Discord)
description:
You already know that Discord is home to the world's largest and most engaged community of gamers, with over 200 million monthly active users. But did you know that Discord now has multiple, complementary solutions that can help fuel the success of your game across its lifecycle? Learn how a holistic Discord strategy can help you harness the power of your game's Discord community to drive deeper engagement, increased playtime, reveal meaningful insights, and open new opportunities for revenue.

We'll share the latest solutions that Discord offers game developers, from new commerce channels and the ability to power your game's social layer by integrating with Discord, to improvements to official game servers and our growing lineup of advertising formats. You'll also hear success stories from game studios that have leveraged Discord's suite of developer solutions to gain a competitive advantage.
takeaway: Whether you're kicking off development or managing a multiplatform release, you'll leave with an understanding of how to leverage Discord's full suite of developer solutions to drive greater engagement and growth, from community building to commerce integration.
intended audience: This session is designed for game developers, publishers, community managers, marketing professionals, and studio leadership seeking to maximize player engagement and monetization through Discord. Whether you're launching a new title or managing an established game, you'll discover actionable strategies for leveraging Discord's developer tools and community features throughout your game's lifecycle.
speakers: Tom Jacques(Discord), Mason Sciotti(Discord), Emory Irpan(Discord), Mark Mandel(Discord), Sota Proffitt(Discord)

session title: Building the Next Growth Frontier for Games: A Conversation with Brian Ward of Savvy Games Group
description: Join Brian Ward, CEO of Savvy Games Group, and Tom Giles, a Senior Executive Editor at Bloomberg, for an executive conversation on building a global games business from Saudi Arabia. Drawing on his journey from Activision Blizzard to establishing and scaling Savvy, Brian will share how the company has built a diversified portfolio over the past four years, balancing the development of homegrown studios and talent with international investment and partnerships. He will address core C-suite challenges - risk, scale, and relative studio autonomy - and share practical do's and don'ts for operating in Saudi Arabia and the wider MENA region.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA
speakers: Tom Giles(Bloomberg L.P.), Brian Ward(Savvy Games Group)

session title: Fight and Ye Shall Receive: How CWA's Game Worker Union Campaigns Are Changing the Industry! (Presented by Campaign to Organize Digital Employees (CODE-CWA))
description:
Over the last few years, thousands of game workers across the US and Canada have won certified unions and bargained first contracts at major game studios like Bethesda, Blizzard, SEGA, Paizo, and more with the Communications Workers of America (CWA)!

Last year at GDC 2025, the industry also saw the launch of United Videogame Workers (CWA Local 9433), a direct-to-join union established with CWA open to any game worker in North America.

As the industry continues to face unprecedented times, CWA game union members have returned to GDC to discuss the historic rise of the game industry labor movement, how unions are making positive changes in our industry, and visions for the future!

If you've ever wondered about the specifics of how unionization could change or benefit your workplace and our whole industry, this talk is for you!

A moderated audience Q&A session will follow!
takeaway: Attendees will hear directly from some of the 4000+ game workers who have built their unions with CWA and will leave with a better understanding of the movement and how game worker unions can achieve tangible change through organizing and bargaining both at their specific studios and across the industry.
intended audience: Game workers at all levels interested in learning about the current labor union landscape of the games industry, those who are curious about industry unionization, and those who are tired of feeling helpless at the hands of the game industry's recent struggles and want to advocate for a better tomorrow.
speakers: Josiah Clark(Blizzard Entertainment and CWA Local 9510), Juniper Dowell(Bethesda Softworks and CWA Local 2108), Carolyn Jong(United Videogame Workers CWA Local 9433), Aurelia Augusta(United Videogame Workers-CWA)

session title: Offering a Helping Hand: Experimenting with AI-Powered Assistants in Games (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: Onboarding players into Total War: PHARAOH, which has deep, complex mechanics, can be a challenge. Discover how Creative Assembly built an experimental AI-powered assistant to help players of all skill levels master their intricate gameplay in real time. We'll demonstrate how NVIDIA ACE models and tools enable developers to build contextually aware, on-device assistants that enhance the player experience without sacrificing privacy or performance.
takeaway: Audience will learn how teams can create an integrated game assistant system, based on language models, and capable of using both static game knowledge and dynamic real-time game state to answer user questions while staying in character.
intended audience: Designers, Programmers, Producers, Business Leaders, Technical Directors

session title: Theseus - A Live Platform Migration Story (Presented by Servers.com)
description: Multiplay is entering a new chapter. The server orchestration platform is joining Rocket Science Group, supporting a new phase for live game infrastructure and how studios build, scale, and operate multiplayer services. This session shares the 'Ship of Theseus' story told through the lens of migrating a live dedicated-server platform: keeping the player and developer experience consistent while evolving what is happening behind-the-scenes. We'll cover what 'migration at scale' really means - multiple platform variants, differing workflows, drop-in SDK replacements for Unreal, Unity, and custom engines, and updates to build distribution and supporting services - while live games and launches continue.
takeaway: The session focuses on practical techniques used to safely migrate a live platform without disrupting players or developers. Attendees will leave with a concrete checklist for planning and executing a live platform transition without turning it into a big-bang rewrite.
