intended audience: Perfect for game developers, composers, musicians, and anyone who loves the emotional impact of music in games.
speakers: Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming), Austin Wintory(Independent)

session title: Lunch and Entertainment in the Gardens
description:
Join us for lunch and entertainment in the GDC Gardens.

Stone Stage entertainment presented by MAGWEST (https://www.magwest.org/):



• 11:00 AM - 11:50 PM: OrchKeystra
• 12:10 PM - 1:00 PM: OMNI of SilvaGunner
• 1:20 PM - 2:00 PM: Cheap Dinosaurs






Review the full week's schedule here (https://gdconf.com/lunch-and-entertainment-in-the-gardens/).
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Ending the Skin-Weighting Bottleneck with METATAILOR v2.5 (Presented by METATAILOR)
description:
Manual skin-weighting and garment fitting remain the most significant labor bottlenecks in modern character production. As studios scale toward diverse, customizable wardrobes, the traditional 'one-off' approach to skin-weighting fails to keep pace with rapid development cycles. In this Power Talk, METATAILOR CEO Graham Henry introduces version 2.5, demonstrating a paradigm shift from static meshes to 'Smart Meshes.'
The session explores a new engine-agnostic wardrobe hub and official plugins for Unreal Engine and Unity that eliminate manual rework. Attendees will see a live demonstration of the METATAILOR Bridge, which enables seamless round-trip asset transfers and automatic, self-adaptive clothing fitting across disparate character proportions. By automating the technical burden of skin-weighting transfers and collision detection, this session provides a roadmap for studios to achieve AAA-quality character costuming while reclaiming hundreds of hours of manual art labor.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to eliminate manual skin-weighting by implementing an automated pipeline that supports cross-engine interoperability. They discover methods for converting static garments into self-adaptive assets that conform to body types in real time. The presentation provides a clear workflow for moving production-ready assets without losing skin-weighting data.
intended audience: This session targets technical artists, character artists, and studio leads optimizing character production at scale. It benefits those managing large-scale avatar systems or decentralized pipelines. A basic understanding of character skin-weighting and familiarity with Unreal Engine or Unity is the only prerequisite necessary for understanding the technical workflow.

session title: Inside Austria's Indie Game Revolution (Presented by Austrian Play Arcade Spielschön)
description: Discover how Pioneers of Game Development Austria (PGDA) is driving Austria's indie game movement. As the country's leading network of independent studios and creators, PGDA supports collaboration, visibility, and international growth for Austrian developers.
takeaway: This session offers a behind-the-scenes look at their members' success stories, creative culture, and how Austria is emerging as a hotspot for innovative game development.
intended audience: Indie game enthusiasts

session title: Small Team. Global Impact. The Courage to Build What's Next. (Presented by Games From Portugal - eGames Lab)
description:
What does it take for a small team, based on a remote island, to build products that reach millions worldwide?

This talk shares the journey of a Portuguese studio born in Madeira — a small island in the Atlantic — that grew from developing a local hiking app into a global mobile game company with over 40 million downloads. Without large funding rounds or massive teams, growth came through resilience, long-term thinking, and the courage to compete in one of the world's most saturated industries.

This is not a story about scale through size — it's about scale through clarity, adaptability, and belief. It's about building sustainable digital products, evolving from mobile success into immersive virtual reality, and constantly redefining what's next.

For founders, studios, and partners, this session is a reminder: you don't need to be big to create global impact — but you do need the courage to build beyond your limits.
takeaway: you don't need to be big to create global impact — but you do need the courage to build beyond your limits.
intended audience: This session targets founders, indie studios, developers, and publishing professionals interested in sustainable growth and global scalability. It benefits teams building with limited resources who aim to compete internationally. No prerequisite knowledge is required, though basic familiarity with the gaming industry enhances context.

session title: The Player Journey Revolution: Why Gaming's Next Billion Users Will Never See a Password (Presented by FusionAuth)
description:
We're watching the gaming industry commit suicide at the login screen.
Studios are hemorrhaging 47% of players before they even press start. Not because the gameplay sucks. Not because of bad monetization. Because we're forcing 2025 players through 2015 authentication flows.For example: 68% of Gen Z doesn't have Facebook accounts. Yet half of mobile games still default to Facebook Login. That's like requiring a fax machine to play Fortnite.This isn't another 'passwords are bad' lecture. It's a wake-up call with receipts. I'll show you exactly how Fortnite hit 100M players faster than any game in history (spoiler: they let people play first, ask questions later). How Discord accidentally became the identity backbone for an entire generation. And why the studios quietly implementing progressive authentication are seeing 3x conversion improvements while everyone else debates Web3.
takeaway:
The generational authentication divide is real and it's spectacular. Gen Alpha thinks passwords are what their parents use. Gen Z lives on Discord and TikTok. Millennials are scattered across Steam, Google, and Apple like digital refugees. Meanwhile, most games still ask for email and password; a combo that converts at 3-5% versus 12-15% for progressive approaches.

What's more wild? Epic, Riot, and Supercell already solved this. They're not talking about it at conferences. They're not writing Medium posts. They're just quietly capturing millions of players while their competitors argue about blockchain.
I'll break down their actual player flows – from anonymous to identified to monetized. You'll see how risk-based authentication lets players jump straight into gameplay for low-stakes actions while protecting the stuff that matters. How behavioral biometrics will make passwords extinct by 2027. And why time-to-first-play is the only metric that actually predicts revenue.

This isn't theoretical. These are implementation playbooks from games earning $100M+ annually. Real data. Real results. Real competitive advantage that you're probably sleeping on.

While everyone's distracted by AI NPCs and NFT marketplaces, the companies winning the next billion players are revolutionizing the most boring part of gaming: proving who you are.

Attendees will walk away knowing how to:
Turn that 47% abandonment rate into a 15% conversion machine using progressive authentication
Pick the right authentication method for each generation (hint: stop asking Gen Alpha for passwords)
Implement risk-based frameworks that remove friction without compromising security
Steal the playbooks Epic and Riot already perfected
Deploy WebAuthn, passkeys, and behavioral biometrics before your competitors figure out they exist
intended audience:
Primary: Product managers, game designers, and technical directors who are tired of watching players bounce at login
Secondary: Marketing pros wondering why their million-dollar UA campaigns die at the registration screen
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall

session title: The Algorithm Broke Entertainment: Why Hollywood and Games Are Losing Gen Z and How to Win Them Back
description:
After two decades building global franchises in Hollywood—including powerhouse series like The Walking Dead, The Boys, and Invincible, plus acclaimed hits like The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel, Fleabag, and Good Omens—Sharon Tal Yguado moved into games expecting a different set of rules, only to find the same forces at work. The former Head of Scripted Programming at Amazon and Head of Global Programming at Fox Networks Group now faces a familiar challenge in her role as Founder and CEO of Astrid Entertainment: algorithms, short form content, shrinking attention spans, and 'friend slop' experiences are reshaping how younger audiences engage with entertainment, flattening meaning and pulling Gen Z and Gen Alpha away from traditional media, including games.

In this must-attend session, Yguado examines how both Hollywood and gaming are losing the fight for attention to social platforms and why optimizing for algorithms has come at a creative and cultural cost. Drawing on her unique transition from building linear entertainment franchises at major companies like Amazon and Fox to creating interactive worlds, she explores what everyone in the business of making and marketing games should rethink now and how games can reclaim relevance by offering something algorithms cannot replace.

With a career rooted in franchise building, long-term world creation, and deep care for fans and fandom, Yguado brings unparalleled insights from both sides of the entertainment divide to this critical conversation about the future of interactive entertainment.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA

session title: You're the Controller: Creating Intuitive VR Handtracking Interactions
description: Join us as we explore the creation of an acclaimed VR hand-tracking game and discuss what we learned over the course of the three years production. Discover how we leveraged insights from over 500 playtesters, primarily non-gamers, to design intuitive gesture-based controls —distinct from traditional button-based commands. Learn about our meticulous tutorial design - inspired by martial arts and self-defense training - evolving level progression - ensuring mastery before introducing complexity - and continuous playtesting - beginning with early proofs of concept and refining through each development phase. Gain insights into the technological advancements in hand tracking for VR, AR et MR, and see what the future holds for hands-first gaming experiences on Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro and all other hand tracking- compatible devices.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insights into creating VR hand-tracking games, including designing intuitive gesture-based controls from over 500 playtester insights, developing martial arts-inspired tutorials, evolving level progression, and continuous playtesting. They'll also learn about technological advancements in hand tracking for VR, AR, and MR, and future applications on Meta Quest and Apple Vision Pro.
intended audience: The target audience includes VR developers, game designers, and technologists focused on hand-tracking interfaces. Beneficiaries are those interested in advancing hand-tracking tech and game design. Pre-requisite knowledge includes basic understanding of VR development, hand-tracking technology, and game design principles to fully grasp the session content.

session title: AI Companion System in Peacekeeper Elite (Presented by LIGHTSPEED STUDIOS)
description: This lecture will discuss how to create an AI companion in Peacekeeper Elite that combines high human-like realism, carefully designed emotional value, and rich functionality. This AI companion serves as a combat teammate that follows player commands precisely, a coop partner for open conversation and emotionally responsive interactions, and a close friend with memory and nurturing features. The core capabilities of the AI companion are divided into four main areas: command obedience, free chat, memory system, and nurturing system. Command obedience involves executing player instructions such as searching for supplies, scouting enemy positions, and assisting in combat, accompanied by corresponding behavioral and voice feedback. Free chat allows the AI companion to initiate conversations—for example, warning about nearby enemies or praising the player's achievements to provide emotional value—or to respond safely and appropriately to player-initiated dialogue. The memory system enables the AI companion to remember the player's significant actions and conversations, which in turn influences its subsequent autonomous behavior and chat responses. The nurturing system lets players experience growing intimacy with the AI companion and unlock personalized tags, thereby deepening the emotional bond between the player and the AI companion.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about a typical case of successful AI capabilities currently being implemented in the existing game. They will also learn how to achieve the four core capabilities of AI companions—command obedience, free chat, memory system, and nurturing system—through a divide-and-conquer overall strategy. Additionally, attendees will gain insights into the AI companion framework that integrates these multiple capabilities. Furthermore, they will learn how to customize training with LLMs to make AI companions highly human-like in behavior, language, and voice.
intended audience: This session is intended for anyone interested in the application of AI technology in games. No prerequisite knowledge is required.
speakers: BING XUE(LightSpeed Studios), WANG CHEN(LightSpeed Studios), JIAXIN ZHANG(LightSpeed Studios)

session title: Bring Your Apps and Games to General Spatial Computing with Project Swan (Presented by PICO)
description: Project Swan is PICO's flagship device for general spatial computing, designed to support a new generation of full-space and shared-space experiences. This session introduces the core OS and platform capabilities that enable developers—from XR specialists to non-XR app, web, and game creators—to build or adapt content for this emerging medium. It presents a new paradigm for spatial experiences in which games and apps coexist, allowing a primary experience to run alongside companion applications in a shared environment. Attendees will gain an overview of Project Swan's graphics performance, multimodal interaction system, and developer toolchain, as well as practical guidance on bringing existing apps or games into spatial computing workflows. Through concrete examples and live demos, the session highlights how native app developers and game engine users can start building for general spatial computing today.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to bring existing or build new games to Project Swan using PICO OS 6, including designing shared-space XR gameplay where games run alongside companion apps. They gain concrete insights into adapting 2D, PC, or full-space XR games using Unity, Unreal, and PICO Spatial tools. All attendees will also receive a PICO giveaway package.
intended audience: This session targets XR developers, game designers, and technical artists, as well as mobile and PC developers exploring spatial computing. Attendees benefit from experience with Unity, Unreal, or real-time graphics. Prior XR expertise is helpful but not required, as the session covers lightweight paths from 2D or desktop-style games to spatial formats.
speakers: Ke Jing(PICO Immersive US Inc./ Bytedance), Hogan Mastanduno(PICO Immersive US Inc./ Bytedance), David Campos Rodríguez(PICO Immersive US Inc./ Bytedance)

session title: DirectX: Bringing Console-Level GPU Tools to Windows (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description:
Join the DirectX team and our GPU hardware partners as we announce new cutting-edge tooling features in DirectX, PIX, and more – all with the goal of helping you make great, reliable, and performant DirectX games on Windows.
The talk will introduce major new DirectX features to help you with the biggest pain points of PC graphics development today. We will give you unprecedented insight into the ambitious GPU tooling work that is happening across the industry, both today and in the future. We will show the biggest wave of new features to come to PIX on Windows in its 10-year history. And our hardware partners will join us to showcase our joint collaborations - to DirectX, to PIX, and to the wide range of other GPU tools available on Windows today.
takeaway: Attendees will learn about a wide range of new GPU tooling features that we have been building with our hardware partners. They will learn how these tools can help them create great, reliable and performant DirectX games on Windows.
intended audience: This session is for anyone who is interested in learning about brand new features coming to DirectX, that will help them or their colleagues solve some of the most painful challenges in PC GPU programming today.
speakers: Austin Kinross(Microsoft), Budi Purnomo(AMD), Steven Tovey(Intel), Kevin Hawkins(Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.), Aurelio Reis(NVIDIA)

session title: GDC Side Quest Podcast Live: Amra Khullar & Shafik Quoraishee
description: In this live podcast session, dive into the world of mobile gaming with Amra Khullar and Shafik Quoraishee from The New York Times. They discuss the development of , NYT's latest game, and share insights into what it takes to succeed in today's mobile market.
takeaway: Attendees will gain valuable insights into mobile game development and strategies for creating successful products in a competitive market.
intended audience: Ideal for mobile developers and anyone interested in the future of mobile gaming.
speakers: Amra Khullar(The New York Times), Shafik Quoraishee(The New York Times), Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming)

session title: How Do I Get My Game on IGN?
description:
With over 490 million monthly users, 96 million social followers, across 100 countries, IGN Entertainment has been a trusted voice in games and entertainment media for over 30 years. Being featured across IGN offers developers a powerful opportunity to reach a global audience of highly engaged players.




From flagship editorial programs like IGN First, to viral moments across social and YouTube, to homepage coverage on IGN.com (https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/qhTRCNkE8qiE3J8klc4hwIygP97?domain=ign.com), there are multiple ways games can be showcased across the IGN ecosystem.




IGN is passionate about highlighting innovative and exciting games of all sizes. However, the volume of submissions is high, and standing out requires a thoughtful and strategic approach. This panel, featuring respected IGN editorial leaders and headlined by IGN Entertainment Co-Founder Peer Schneider, will provide practical, real-world guidance on how developers can successfully pitch their games to IGN.




Attendees will learn how to identify the right contacts, structure an effective pitch, create compelling trailers and assets, and better understand how IGN evaluates and selects games for coverage. The session will also explore how early editorial wins can grow into broader visibility across the IGN Entertainment portfolio.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insight into how IGN Entertainment operates editorially and how developers can most effectively collaborate with IGN. They will learn best practices for pitching games, preparing assets, and maximizing opportunities to reach IGN Entertainment's global audience of players.
intended audience: Independent developers and small publishers who have limited experience working with large media outlets, as well as developers looking to refine their pitching strategy and improve their chances of earning editorial coverage.
speakers: Peer Schneider(IGN Entertainment), Ryan McCaffrey(IGN Entertainment), Justin Davis(IGN Entertainment), Miranda Sanchez(IGN Entertainment)

session title: Implementing Real-Time Path Tracing in RE Engine for 'Resident Evil Requiem' and 'Pragmata' (Presented by NVIDIA)
description:
See how Capcom engineers brought real-time path tracing into RE ENGINE for Resident Evil Requiem and PRAGMATA. This talk will cover the challenges of adapting a modern AAA architecture for real-time path tracing and explain the advanced optimization techniques used to squeeze every bit of performance.

**** Live translations will be available for English speaking audience. Please bring your mobile device and headphones. ****
takeaway: Attendees will gain a deep understanding of the path tracing pipeline integration within the RE ENGINE. The session will break down the complexities of implementing this technology in a shipping AAA title, highlighting specific engineering challenges and the GPU optimization strategies required to achieve high-performance real-time rendering.
intended audience:
Graphics and Rendering Engineers looking to implement or optimize path tracing in their own engines.

Technical Directors seeking to understand the architectural implications of moving to full path tracing in AAA production.

Engine Architects interested in the latest GPU optimization techniques (SER, Radiance Caching) for high-fidelity rendering.

session title: Video Game Trivia
description: Join The MADE for Video Game-themed trivia (speedrun style!!) with prizes, fun, and team-based experiences for attendees.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Xbox Partner Panel: Maximizing Discovery and Reaching Beyond for the Next 25 Years of Play (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description: Hundreds of games launch every week, making discovery one of the most consequential and least understood challenges in game development today. This session brings together developers who've successfully launched on the Xbox ecosystem across 25 years—from AAA and third-party studios to indie and emerging creators—to share practical lessons on increasing visibility, reaching players, and sustaining engagement. Panelists will discuss how discovery works on Xbox today, how features such as Game Pass and Xbox Play Anywhere impact audience growth across console, PC, and cloud, and how developers can align creative and business decisions to maximize reach without compromising their vision. Attendees will leave with actionable insights they can apply to platform strategy, launch planning, and long-term player engagement as Xbox continues to support creators in the next 25 years of play.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with a better understanding on how to build player-first experiences that will in turn enable better discoverability in an increasingly crowded market. This session offers advice and insights from a range of game creators that will provide attendees with an inside look at how to leverage the Xbox ecosystem to reach new audiences.
intended audience: Game developers—especially creative directors, producers, and studio leads—focused on launch strategy, discovery, and audience growth.
speakers: Xalavier Nelson Jr(Strange Scaffold), Andrea Rene(Vivrato), Matt Jeffery(Rebellion), Marcus Morgan(Obsidian Entertainment)

session title: Building and Sustaining a Global Development Team: Lessons in Sustainable Game Production
description: A globally distributed development model is no longer an option, it's essential for sustainable innovation and long-term business health. In this session, Blind Squirrel Games Founder & CEO Brad Hendricks and VP of Production Steven Sargent share a rare inside look at how their studio has successfully built scalable systems through adoptable processes, tools, and key learnings. Discover how to optimize operations and production to increase velocity without burnout, strengthen cross-cultural collaboration, and maintain quality across every discipline, no matter your studio size.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with a clearer understanding of how to operationalize a strong distributed model without sacrificing quality or cohesion, build infrastructure for cross-cultural/time zone collaboration, use global bandwidth as a force multiplier, and future-proof their studio/teams against market volatility.
intended audience: This session is for studio leaders, producers, and developers interested in building sustainable, high-performing teams across regions. Attendees should have a general understanding of game development workflows, but no advanced technical knowledge is required. The talk is relevant to studios of all sizes exploring global collaboration and long-term operational strategies.
speakers: Brad Hendricks(Blind Squirrel Games), Steven Sargent(Blind Squirrel Games)

session title: 'CorgiSpace': What I Learned from Making 15 Games This Year
description: Independent game designer and publisher, Adam Saltsman, best known for Canabalt and Finji, has been making small games again, a lot of them, and learning a ton about game design and creativity in the process. This talk is about the four big lessons from the last 18 months (and 18 games). The first lesson is that doing things that are 'easy and not obvious' is fun and funny as a designer, and quickly leads you to new things that are fun to explore. The second lesson is that your ideas are better than you think... but your drafts or formulas are probably worse than you think. The third lesson is that game design is about noticing, about listening to your game as it emerges. And the fourth lesson is how important it is to have fun when you are making a game.
takeaway: Attendees will be inspired to try things that are easy and not obvious in their own work, improve their development processes by separating their ideas from their formulas, be more open to 'listening' to their projects as they come to life, and enjoy game-making more in the future.
intended audience: This talk is about game design but is relevant for creatives of all levels, especially anyone who feels like they are on a 'plateau' lately or are looking for new ways to approach their creative work.

session title: Create Playable Games by Chatting with AI - Tesana AI Live Demo (Presented by Tesana AI)
description: Tesana introduces a new frontier in game creation: playable, AI-generated game worlds built directly from natural language. In this session, the Tessala team demonstrates how anyone can move from idea to playable experiences in minutes, generating worlds, mechanics, environments, characters, and systems through simple prompts.
takeaway: Tesana's mission is to make it possible for anyone to make games. This talk explores how foundation game models enables anyone to explore, create, and actively shape playable experiences, not just consume them. Attendees will see live examples of Attendees will see live examples of dynamic world-building from prompts, AI-driven logic, and adaptive gameplay systems generated on the fly
intended audience: This presentation is for game developers, designers, indie founders, gamers and studio leads exploring AI game making and 'vibe coding' games. No technical prerequisite knowledge is required, though familiarity with games, engines, scripting, or generative AI concepts is helpful.
speakers: Johannes Vermandois(Tesana AI), Eric Heimer(Tesana AI)

session title: 'DEATH STRANDING 2':  Making of Voxel 3D UI Map
description: This talk presents a complete overview of the technical process behind the creation the 3D UI Voxel map for Death Stranding 2 developed by Kojima Productions. It walks through every stage of development from early game design and production requirements to capturing and processing open-world level data, as well as the voxel rendering technique used to visualize the map. This presentation explains how the 3D UI map was built without relying on artist created assets to be visually rich and memory-efficient while meeting strict performance constraints. It highlights the custom tools and workflows designed to process large scale open world data, the methods used to convert that data into engine ready assets, and the rendering strategies that made efficient voxel rendering possible.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to build a visually rich, memory-efficient 3D UI map using voxel rendering. The session provides practical insights into open world data capture, custom toolchains, and rendering techniques that meet real-time performance on consoles. The techniques and tools presented are not engine specific and can be adapted for use in other game engines.
intended audience: This presentation targets technical artists, graphics programmers, engine and tool developers, and advanced game development students. Attendees should have a basic understanding of 3D graphics, shaders, game engine workflows, and programming in C++. It offers practical, engine-agnostic techniques for efficient 3D UI map creation and voxel rendering.

session title: Evolve or Die: How LiveOps Scaled Our Indie Hit
description:
Computer Lunch's game 'Cell to Singularity' is a success. Amazing! Now what? Should they jump right into a new project? Create an expansion? Make a sequel? They look around the games industry and realize, the answer might be yes to all of those.
This talk describes how a small, self funded indie team with no experience in LiveOps ends up turning a game that takes six weeks to beat into an evergreen LiveOps title. They break down what that looks like on the ground, and describe the challenges a developer might encounter along the way.
takeaway: How does a small, self-funded indie team with no experience in LiveOps spin up and maintain a live operations game? Learn how the Computer Lunch team turned their weird little indie game into a sustainable evergreen property by constantly iterating, investing in producers, creating content templates, and leaning into marketing.
intended audience:
This session is for anyone who has had a hit, and is now wondering if they should move to a new project, or double down on their success.
It's also for any indie developers curious to learn about LiveOps, or anyone wondering if LiveOps is the right strategy for their game.
speakers: Kati Nawrocki(Computer Lunch), Andrew Garrahan(Computer Lunch)

session title: Finding Signal in the Noise: Unlocking Growth in a Turbulent Games Market
description:
The games industry is facing a generational moment of disruption—marked by platform shifts, rising development costs, shuttered studios, and evolving player expectations. But within every disruption lies opportunity.
In this fast-paced 30-minute session, President/CEO Yoshio Osaki will share proprietary data from IDG Intelligence—the leading global market research, analytics, and consulting firm in gaming. This State of the Industry lecture will break down the forces reshaping the industry, and spotlight actionable growth opportunities hidden beneath the turbulence.
Using exclusive IDG market intelligence, I'll highlight:
• The real state of player behavior across platforms
• Where the next billion-dollar IPs are likely to emerge
• Untapped revenue levers and business models
• Future-proofing your strategy amid volatility
This session will help you cut through the noise, identify the signal, and chart a smarter path forward. Don't just survive the storm—grow through it and come out stronger on the other side.
takeaway:
-Data-driven view of current industry challenges
-Actionable growth opportunities, especially during down cycles
-Emerging player behavior insights, and how studios can align with them
-Investment guidance: how to prioritize R&D and publishing investments to align with opportunities and mitigate downside risk
-Future-proofing strategies - IDG toolkit to unlock sustainable growth
intended audience:
Studio Executives/Founders – Navigating headwinds and identifying high-ROI opportunities.
Business Development & Strategy Leads – Platform partnerships and strategic planning.
Product/Publishing Teams – Content strategy with evolving market trends.
Investors & Corporate Development Leaders –Industry signals and M&A opportunities.
Marketing Professionals – Player behavior and segment-level dynamics.
Platform/Ecosystem Strategists – Cross-platform, device, and storefront trends.

session title: From Local Talent to Global Stage: Building High-Value Game Development Ecosystems (Presented by Essential Costa Rica)
description: Using Costa Rica as a case study, this session examines how strategic international exposure, commercial positioning, and coordinated public–private collaboration helped transform a small local game development scene into a globally trusted production partner. Drawing from the experience of Fair Play Labs and the international market development efforts supported by Essential Costa Rica, the speakers share practical insights on connecting regional talent to high-value projects and long-term global partnerships. The talk reflects on lessons learned from expanding beyond geographic limitations and competing in international markets without the advantages of traditional industry hubs. Attendees gain a real-world perspective on how emerging ecosystems can strengthen production capacity, increase global visibility, and sustainably support high-value game development.
takeaway: Attendees gain insight into how international exposure, commercial strategy, and public–private collaboration can elevate emerging game development ecosystems. Through a practical case study, they learn how regional talent connects to global partners and how sustainable capacity for high-value projects is built and maintained.
intended audience: This session is designed for studio leaders, producers, publishers, investors, business development professionals, and policymakers interested in global collaboration and emerging markets. It is relevant to organizations seeking new production partners as well as regions working to strengthen international market access.
speakers: Maykool López(Essential Costa Rica), Claudio Pinto(Fair Play Labs)

session title: Illuminating Roco's Legacy: Scalable Global Illumination from Theory to Practice
description:
This session demonstrates how, with advanced global illumination technologies as the foundation, and through close collaboration between art and technical teams — the team reimagines the classic browser game Roco Kingdom as a 64-square-kilometer open-world cross-platform experience. While retaining the original art style, developers leverage technological innovations to overcome key limitations in memory, bandwidth, and performance. This enables delivery of dynamic day-night lighting effects across multiple platforms and devices, while maintaining visual consistency as much as possible.

In addition, this presentation will focus on sharing the various technical challenges encountered during the implementation of global illumination in an open-world, cross-platform game — along with the team's corresponding solutions, strategies, and insights.
takeaway: Learn how the R&D team upgraded a classic IP browser game into a 64-square-kilometer open-world cross-platform game using advanced engine and rendering technologies. This includes solving the challenges of time-of-day (TOD) global illumination through a hybrid precomputed-realtime solution; overcoming memory, bandwidth, and performance constraints via technical and art pipeline optimizations; and achieving seamless transitions between TOD and scene lighting, as well as consistent art quality across multiple platforms. These practical processes and experiences are highly valuable for game developers seeking to revitalize classic IPs.
intended audience: Targeted towards engine tool developers, technical artists, and other professionals with rendering backgrounds.

session title: Launching 'Phasmophobia' on Consoles (and Somehow Not F**king It Up)
description: When Kinetic Games set out to launch Phasmophobia on consoles—nearly five years after its massive PC success—the challenge was clear: reach new audiences without losing what made the game special. In this tactical session, the speaker shares how the team grew Phasmophobia from 19 million to 25 million copies sold, detailing the marketing strategies, platform partnerships, and creator activations that drove success. Attendees will learn how to tailor messaging for console-first players, work effectively with Xbox and PlayStation, and navigate internal team dynamics while staying true to a marketing vision. This talk is a practical guide for marketers, indie devs, producers, and publishing teams looking to bring established games to new platforms—without messing it up.
takeaway: Discover how Phasmophobia expanded from 19M to 25M copies sold through strategic console marketing, creator activations, and platform partnerships—without losing its core identity. This session offers practical insights on cross-platform launches, community engagement, and navigating team dynamics while staying true to your marketing vision.
intended audience: This session is for marketers, indie developers, producers, and publishing teams launching successful PC games on console. It offers tactical insights into platform partnerships, creator strategy, and team alignment—ideal for anyone navigating cross-platform releases or expanding games with established communities. No prior knowledge of Phasmophobia required.

session title: Short Form Futures: How AI Turned MMO Players into TikTok Creators
description: Sword of Justice Mobile turns the endless-swipe habit of TikTok into a fully playable loop inside an MMO. In this session, NetEase designer Chang Liu shows how the team builds StageHub—a vertical 3-D feed—and QuickShoot Stage, where players import a live-action clip or in-game cut-scene, let AI extract motion and camera, swap avatars, and refilm the scene in under 60 seconds. One year after launch, the feature delivers five million daily swipes, 600-second sessions, and a 70 percent 'Shoot the Same' conversion—14× higher than mainstream short-video remix rates. A certified-badge quest line and seasonal film festivals sustain creator output, while one-click export to a PC timeline editor lets players sell pay-per-episode shorts on TikTok, generating seven-figure USD revenue that largely flows back to them. Attendees learn the data-backed UI patterns, incentive loops and monetization blueprint behind this 3-D TikTok for MMOs.
takeaway: Attendees gain a concrete playbook: a 60-second AI retarget pipeline, a 3-D swipe feed with a 70 % remix tap rate, a badge system paired with real-time trend pushes keyed to TikTok hotspots that sustains creator output, and an export-and-earn loop that turns player shorts into fresh revenue and traffic.
intended audience: The talk targets community managers, live-ops designers, product leads and monetization PMs who run live service games or creator programs. A basic grasp of free-to-play KPIs and short-video culture is assumed; no AI or animation pipeline expertise is required to apply the lessons.

session title: Six Months to a Playable Demo: Building Aelwater as Indie Devs (Presented by Yeariver)
description: This session shares the development journey of Aelwater, a narrative-driven exploration game, created by a two-person team over six months to reach a playable demo and early vertical slice. The speaker presents the practical tools, workflows, and decision-making frameworks that enabled rapid, playable-first development within tight time and scope constraints. The talk explores how those constraints shaped key design choices—from the game's memory-driven narrative about grief and acceptance to the symbolic glyph puzzle mechanics that reinforce themes of memory, loss, and reflection. It also examines a narrative-first production approach, where gameplay systems, exploration structure, and puzzle design were built to support emotional pacing and environmental storytelling. Through concrete examples of implementation in Unreal Engine, including modular narrative systems, data-driven localization pipelines, and rapid iteration practices, the session highlights how a very small team can build a cohesive narrative experience efficiently.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a practical framework for rapid indie game development, including scope management techniques, narrative-driven design workflows, and prioritization strategies that enabled a playable demo and early vertical slice within six months. The session highlights replicable workflows for balancing creative vision with production constraints while building a cohesive narrative experience with a very small team.
intended audience: This session targets solo developers, small indie teams, and producers interested in efficient game development processes. It particularly benefits developers working with limited time or resources who need practical strategies for shipping complete games.

session title: Teaching Games with Games X: Artificial Intelligence
description: From Oxford commas and em dashes to hallucinated quotations and counting fingers, AI is ubiquitous in our inboxes, media feeds, and classrooms. In 2026, it seems there is no stopping how large language models impact the way we design and play games together. After all, games and play have a long and intimate history with artificial intelligence. On one hand Turing's Imitation Game, Weizenbaum's Eliza, and Conway's Game of Life each develop AI in the idiom of games. On the other hand Deep Blue's deep chess searches, AlphaGo's efficient joseki, and OpenAI Five's deathballs in Dota 2 teach us new ways to play. The question then is not only what AI teaches us about games but also what games can teach us about AI. Considering this history, we invite six educators to share a single activity, exercise, technique, or tip that played with or against AI in the classroom.
takeaway: Considering the unique challenges of teaching games during the emergence of artificial intelligence as a global technology, audience members will hear microtalks from a panel of game educators and come away with concrete examples of playing, teaching, and making with and against AI.
intended audience: This presentation welcomes educators of all levels and teaching contexts as well as game designers and industry professionals navigating the impacts of climate change in and around games.
speakers: Stephanie Boluk(University of California, Davis), Patrick LeMieux(University of California, Davis), Rogelio Cardona-Rivera(University of Utah), Frank Lantz(Everybody House Games), Gillian Smith(Worcester Polytechnic Institute), Yuqian Sun(Ada Eden), Samuel Pizelo(University of Toronto, Mississauga)

session title: The State of Mental Health in the Games Industry
description: Let's talk about what's really going on with mental health in the game industry. This panel will share findings from the 2025 Mental Health in the Game Industry study, debuting at GDC 2026. Built by mental health experts who understand game dev, the study digs into what's stressing developers out, what support actually helps, and where studios are falling short. We'll share findings from the study, break down the data, and offer practical, no-fluff recommendations for making studios healthier, more supportive places to work.
takeaway: Attendees gain valuable insight into the current state of mental health in the game industry. The session provides practical, research-backed strategies to help create a healthier, more supportive environment for game developers.
intended audience: This presentation is for those passionate about improving mental health in the game development industry. It offers practical steps to support developers' well-being, addresses key challenges faced by studios and individuals, and provides actionable strategies to foster a healthier, more sustainable work environment.
speakers: Kelli Dunlap(Take This, Inc.), Elizabeth Kilmer(Respec Psychological Services)

session title: Why Games Fail to Scale: Stop Optimizing CPI and Start Fixing the Game (Presented by Game Lyft)
description: In 2026, the industry faces a scaling problem; teams try to scale games by 'optimizing CPI and eCPM,' but very few are actually fixing the game. Ad networks claim AI to boost eCPM, UA partners promise AI that lowers CPI, Studios burn time and money while core problems in retention, LTV, and content design stay unsolved. This session will address these problems and show how games win in the era of AI. GameLyft Introduces a target and LTV-first way to win, an 'Intelligent Growth OS,' a system that examines your game to pinpoint systemic issues in retention, monetization, design flow, and prescribes game-specific solutions tied directly to ROAS and higher revenue. We explore why traditional dashboards fail to drive growth and how this framework unifies Product, Monetization, and UA into one actionable scoreboard. GameLyft's philosophy is simple: it should only win when it helps your game achieve sustainable revenue growth
takeaway: Walk away with a practical 'Fix-Order' framework to convert noisy dashboards into decisions. Using GameLyft's approach, you'll learn to pinpoint root causes, rank high-impact fixes, and tie in-game red flags to ROAS and retention deltas, prioritizing the experiment phases that actually compound revenue and increase LTV sustainably.
intended audience: Founders, studio leads, and strategic partners managing game portfolios, it's for UA and product teams, tired of silos, who need a clear system to assess scalability. If you're responsible for prioritizing roadmaps, growing LTV, or evaluating portfolio growth potential in the AI era, this session shares a practical framework.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall

session title: AWS Game Day
description: Stop by City View at Metreon on Thursday and explore everything Amazon and Amazon Web Services (AWS) have to offer. Our showcase will feature games built on AWS, along with cozy Luna Lounge streaming areas. Unlock your creativity at our immersive Agentic Arcade, where you can leverage AI agents to help you build your own custom game. Connect with AWS gaming experts to explore the latest cloud solutions tailored for game development. Whether you're an experienced developer, an aspiring game creator, or student, stop by AWS Game Day to cap off the week at GDC.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Game Developer Podcast Live Recording - Game Writer Alexa Ray Correia
description: Game Developer's Bryant Francis interviews Game Writer Alexa Ray Correia live in the GDC Podcast Studio.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: House of Peeps Meetup!
description: The House of Peeps is a wholesome, supportive community of game developers from all walks of life. From students to hobbyists to professionals, come on down to meet some friendly faces and just have a nice chill social session.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: The Middle-Man Table
description: The Middle-Man Table is a facilitated speed-mentoring session that connects newer game developers with experienced industry professionals in small, low-pressure conversations. Each rotation pairs 1-3 emerging devs with a senior developer, producer, or hiring professional, with guided prompts to spark meaningful discussion and practical advice. Organized by Ryan Fontenot, game developer.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: GDC Pitch: Day 2
description: Five GDC Play studios have been selected to pitch their games to an opinionated panel of investors and publishers, in front of a live audience (no pressure!) followed by questions, advice, and feedback before the judges declare the day's 'Best Pitch' and award a free GDC 2027 Game Changer Pass. Each year, the theater fills to capacity, as the developers compete for the judges' praise.
speakers: Sigurlina Ingvarsdottir(Behold Ventures), Jason Della Rocca(Execution Labs), Walter Paleari(Makers Fund), Jakob Longer(Griffin Gaming Partners)

session title: Lunchtime Surgeries: Day 3
description: An opportunity for deeper conversations with audio track speakers over lunch in a smaller scale setting. Grab some food and then come back to sit at tables in the round with the day's speakers on the topics that are top of mind for you about the industry, the day's topics, and the questions at the top of attendee minds.
takeaway: Get your specific questions answered, meet other attendees, and ask speakers about their experiences, perspectives, and opinions of audio for games today and in the future.
intended audience: All are welcome
speakers: Damian Kastbauer(Audiokinetic), Scott Selfon(Meta Reality Labs Research), Crystal Lee(Schell Games), Sonia Mokhtari(Zynga), David Su(Naughty Dog), Alexis Smith(The Flight), Joe Henson(The Flight), Colin Walder(CD PROJEKT RED), Goncalo Tavares(EA DICE), Heather Plunkard-Serge(EA - Respawn), Bo Crutcher(Blindlight), Tim Friedlander(soundBOX:Studio Group), Howard Mostrom(Injected Senses LLC), Kadet Kuhne(Firaxis)

session title: AI-Powered UGC Creation in Peacekeeper Elite (Presented by LIGHTSPEED STUDIOS)
description: This session explores how AI technologies empower large-scale UGC creation in Peacekeeper Elite, enabling players to build complex content with minimal technical barriers. The talk presents four practical AI-driven UGC workflows currently applied in production. First, AI-assisted brick assembly allows players to input images or text, with the system intelligently selecting and assembling predefined building bricks into structures that match user intent. Second, the session covers AI-generated UGC avatars, including automatic model creation, rigging, and vision-based motion capture for animation and runtime driving. Third, it introduces 3D scene generation, where AI converts user inputs into images, performs semantic scene understanding, and transforms results into interactive 3D game environments. Finally, the talk demonstrates AI-based 3D relief generation, converting 2D line art into depth-enhanced height maps to enrich model surface detail. Together, these systems showcase scalable AI solutions for UGC ecosystems in live games.
takeaway: Attendees gain concrete insights into deploying AI pipelines for UGC at scale, including brick assembly, automated avatar & motion creation, text-driven 3D scene creation, and depth-enhanced asset detailing, with real-world production examples from Peacekeeper Elite.
intended audience: This session targets game developers, technical artists, and UGC platform designers interested in AI-driven content creation. Familiarity with real-time engines, 3D asset workflows, or game AI systems is helpful but not strictly required.
speakers: Zeyu Hu(LightSpeed Studios), Shengju Qian(LightSpeed Studios), Jingwei Xu(LightSpeed Studios), Keyang Luo(LightSpeed Studios)

session title: Evolving DirectX for the ML Era on Windows (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description: Machine learning is reshaping graphics, and DirectX is evolving to meet this moment. This session explores how we're enabling neural rendering on Windows by introducing linear algebra capabilities in HLSL, unlocking dedicated hardware acceleration for AI operations and allowing developers to embed lightweight models directly in shaders. We'll also look ahead to the challenges of scaling beyond hand-authored shader models and share our vision for how we will eventually allow any developer to integrate entire models into their engines. Join us to learn how these innovations lay the groundwork for the next generation of real-time graphics and what they mean for the future of gaming.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how DirectX advances ML on Windows, starting with enabling neural rendering through new Linear Algebra capabilities in HLSL. We will also share our vision for how we will eventually allow any developer to integrate entire models into their engines. The session presents practical examples and a forward‑looking view of scalable, real‑time graphics powered by ML.
intended audience: This session targets developers exploring how machine learning reshapes real‑time rendering. The audience is expected to understand modern GPU programming and HLSL fundamentals. Those familiar with shader authoring, model integration workflows, or engine‑level systems will benefit most.
speakers: Max McMullen(Microsoft), Hisham Chowdhury(AMD), Steven Tovey(Intel), Don Brittain(NVIDIA)

session title: How to Build Practical Applications Powered by Neural Shaders (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: Join us to explore the latest in hardware-accelerated RTX Neural Shading. Discover how the Slang shader language simplifies development and how to use Nsight Graphics to optimize neural shading workloads. We will demonstrate how to identify performance bottlenecks, restructure shaders for maximum gains, and build novel Vulkan and DirectX applications using the RTX Neural Shading SDK.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how Neural Shaders can benefit the graphics pipeline, how to build Neural Shading applications and how to optimze them to maximize image quality and performance.
intended audience:
Artists (Tech)
Engineers (Graphics, ML, Software)
Researchers (Graphics, AI)

session title: Live Sound Bath and Meditation (Presented by thatgamecompany)
description:
Tim Nixon (thatgamecompany) & Caio Jiacomini (thatgamecompany)

Quiet Room 213

Take a moment from the busy show to relax and enjoy the calming sounds of crystal singing bowls intertwined with the atmosphere of Sky: Children of the Light, before sinking into a short guided meditation to help refresh the mind and body. All are welcome.




**Doors Open at 9:30AM.**

session title: Base Camp: Day 2
description: **Formerly known as GDC Connect, this dedicated lounge in the Festival Hall offers space for both casual mingling and pre-arranged meetings scheduled through the event app.**
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Play Beyond the Game: How Cross-Sector Marketers Are Driving Growth and Innovation in Interactive Entertainment
description:
Games aren't just a vertical - they're a universe where global IP, fandom, and culture collide. This panel brings together senior marketers from AEG World Wide, Fox Entertainment, ESPAT TV, and SEGA to explore how today's leading entertainment studios are expanding their worlds through gaming and redefining what 'interactive entertainment' truly means.

Sitting at the intersection of film, streaming, games, consumer products, and global fan culture, the panelists offer a holistic perspective from representing franchises, platforms, and IP ecosystems that naturally cross over with games. They'll share how Hollywood and major entertainment brands are embracing gaming as a critical pillar of audience growth, cultural relevance, and future-forward storytelling, including how they build brands by leveraging fandoms, world-building, and cross-platform experiences that deepen emotional connection and drive engagement.
takeaway:
• Curated by The Global Entertainment Marketing Academy of Arts & Sciences, the leading organization advancing the craft, impact, and innovation of entertainment marketing worldwide, this session reflects GDC 2026's commitment to uniting marketers, developers, publishers, and investors in shaping gaming's next era. In this session, panelists will dive into:
• Transmedia storytelling: building immersive stories across games, film, music, and AR/VR
• Creator-powered campaigns: leveraging gaming influencers and community-led content
• Experimentation & data-driven marketing: optimizing campaigns using A/B testing, personalization, and live ops
• Cross-vertical partnerships: activating entertainment, tourism, and education sectors through gaming
intended audience: This is the session for anyone who wants an insider look at how the biggest studios are using gaming as the next frontier of storytelling, marketing innovation, and audience expansion.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA
speakers: Brenda Cruz(AEG World Wide), Justin Scarpone(SEGA), Scott Edwards(Fox Entertainment), Rhett Reese(Deadpool & Zombieland Film Franchises), Dante Simpson(Producer Call of Duty: The Film)

session title: How to Play for the Planet
description:
Across platforms and devices, energy efficiency is rapidly becoming a competitive advantage, driving longer battery life, smoother performance on lower-powered devices, and resilience against rising energy costs.

This will be an open conversation focused on a question that is quickly becoming unavoidable for our industry: What does the next generation of performance-per-watt in games look like and what leadership is needed to move from isolated efforts to shared progress on energy-efficient code?

Learn from Microsoft, PlayStation, and Unity what tools are currently available to get this competitive advantage in your games and see how Ubisoft has reduced the energy consumption two of their games without impacting the player UX.

There is enough time available to ask any questions you might have to some of the most experienced industry peers when it comes to energy efficiency.




Speakers:

• Laurent Gilbert (Unity)
• Daniel Jacobs (Xbox)
• Sophie Barteau (Ubisoft)
• Kieren Mayers (Sony Interactive Entertainment)
• Lisa Pak (Playing for the Planet)
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: The Queer Gayming Organizations Networking Mixer
description: Hosted by SF Gaymers, this session provides a safe space for queer attendees to take part in a casual networking event where those involved with a queer, LGBTQIA, or gayming organization can connect, share notes, and leave with new resources for their communities.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: AI-Augmented PlayTesting: Test Smarter, Scale Faster (Presented by Softtek)
description:
Testing a video game at scale rarely feels like an easy win. As games grow in complexity, flows and screens multiply, and release pressure increases, testing teams struggle to balance manual testing with automation that is costly to build and difficult to maintain.

This session explores how AI can scale, accelerate, and optimize test cycles while keeping humans in the loop. Using the FRIDA AI Playtesting Framework, we walk through a real testing flow—from defining goals to running automated tests and reviewing rich, contextual results. The talk highlights how testers can drive powerful automation without writing a single line of code.

This is Augmented AI Game Testing in action: testers think like players, chase edge cases, and push gameplay to its limits, while technology handles execution speed, regression cycles, and outcome tracking. Instead of sidelining human expertise, FRIDA Playtesting turns testers' intent into smart, scalable automation. The result is higher quality games, faster validation, and measurable ROI before players press start.
takeaway: Attendees learn how combining AI with human playtesting increases throughput, optimizes testing effort, and delivers faster, higher-confidence releases by transforming manual validation into scalable, intent-driven automation.
intended audience: This session is designed for Game Technology Leaders, QA/QV Directors, Producers, QA Leads, QA Engineers, and Game Testers interested in scaling automated game testing. Attendees should be familiar with game testing workflows, but no prior experience with automation frameworks or AI-driven testing is required.

session title: Ambient Diffuse Lighting in 'Ghost of Yōtei'
description:
Ghost of Yotei is larger and more detailed than its 2020 predecessor, Ghost of Tsushima. To achieve the increased scale, Sucker Punch Productions needed to significantly reduce the authoring burden on artists to get correct lighting results. To achieve the increased detail, we looked to leverage new ray tracing hardware present in the PS5. To solve both of these problems, we implemented new data structures for authoring and lighting ambient probes that were easier to author and integrated well with ray tracing.

In this talk, graphics programmer Eric Wohllaib will discuss Sucker Punch Production's algorithms for baking and placing ambient diffuse probes, tools artists used to prevent light leaks, and our solution for achieving ambient lighting that reacts to light changes at runtime and while editing. Additionally, he'll provide details on augmenting our ambient diffuse with ray tracing, including how we managed data structures required for ray tracing, our methods for getting material information from ray hits, and the algorithm for our screen space probe based ray traced global illumination.
takeaway: Attendees can expect to takeaway an understanding of how real time global illumination is accomplished in Ghost of Yotei, as well as challenges and considerations for implementing similar algorithms in their own code.
intended audience: This talk is intended for graphics programmers interested in global illumination algorithms and ray tracing. Prior experience with either is not required.

session title: Artists, Do You Want to Fight for Your Future?
description:
Milton Friedman said that in times of crisis, the solutions are picked from stories lying around. Right now, artists do not have a story of a future where they feel empowered and optimistic, one in which their opinions matter and their input is valued. These stories do not produce themselves. We need to organize, bind together, and create such a story. This is what this talk aims to accomplish.

Drawing on nearly twenty years as a professional game artist, art director, and technical art director, alongside almost a decade running an AI company serving major entertainment studios, Andrew examines how AI has already 'replaced engineers' inside his own company and what artists need to learn from that shift. He outlines concrete, actionable steps artists can take to ensure their voices are not drowned out by technology and to move from merely keeping a seat at the table to helping lead the art of tomorrow.
takeaway: Attendees leave with a clear understanding of how value flows in creative industries and how to ensure their contribution remains indispensable. They gain practical strategies to increase leverage, outpace automation, and build a sustainable creative career, while organizing together to actively shape how AI impacts production.
intended audience: This session is for anyone who is tired of feeling powerless in the face of the impending AI transformation of creative industries. It is primarily designed for artists, designers, and creative professionals who want to actively shape the future of their craft, though all are welcome.

session title: Beyond Adaptation: Designing IPs That Live Across Games, Series, and Everything In-Between
description:
Why do so many transmedia adaptations fall flat — and what can games teach us about doing it right? In this session narrative designer and transmedia strategist, David Daubitz, explores how starting with worldbuilding instead of plot creates IPs that travel better across formats. Drawing on 15+ years of experience in both AAA games and scripted series, he shares a practical framework for designing flexible, emotionally resonant story worlds.

Using real-world case studies, including Anno 117: Pax Romana and an original audio drama co-produced with Germany's national broadcaster SWR, the session reveals how interactive 'lived story' structures can inform better adaptations — and how mismatched tone, pacing, and genre expectations often lead to failure.

This talk is designed for creative leads, writers, producers, and IP holders looking to build narrative ecosystems that go beyond a single format — and let games lead the way.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to design story systems instead of single-format plots. They discover how gameplay verbs, emotional arcs, and world logic can anchor transmedia IPs. The talk offers tools to align tone, pacing, and character intent across media — enabling IPs that adapt, expand, and stay coherent beyond adaptation.
intended audience: Narrative leads, transmedia strategists, writers, and creative producers working on story-driven games or cross-media IPs who want to design more resilient, adaptable, and narratively consistent universes from the ground up.

session title: Building Boundary‑Pushing Games on Roblox: Faster Iteration, Bigger Audiences, Better Monetization [Developer Showcase] (Presented by Roblox)
description:
Four top Roblox developer teams break down how they ship bold game ideas. From new mechanics and visual styles to live-ops and social design, they'll share how they spot opportunities, prototype fast, and decide what games are worth scaling.
We'll dig into how these developers test with real players, use community feedback and data to guide decisions, and grow audiences over time. You'll hear how they structure experiments around retention and engagement, design monetization that players value, and recover quickly when ideas miss.
Expect concrete examples of pipelines, iteration workflows, and Roblox tools that help teams move faster, learn faster, and build durable, player-first businesses.
takeaway: Attendees will leave armed with the playbooks of top Roblox developers and with actionable insights on how to approach building top experiences and new novel content for Roblox's 144 million users.
intended audience: This session targets developers, designers, producers, product managers, and live-ops leads who build or operate games on or off Roblox and want to sharpen their approach to experimentation, analytics, and monetization. Basic familiarity with Roblox Studio and at least one launched experience on the platform is recommended for getting the most value from the discussion, but all are welcome.
speakers: Alex Hicks(Twin Atlas), Janzen Madsen(Splitting Point Studios), Justin Sousa(Roblox), Yinson Yam(Rivals), Matthew Hufton(99 Nights in the Forest)

session title: Co-Ops Are the Future: A Step-by-Step Guide to Founding Your Own Worker Cooperative Game Studio
description: This session offers a practical guide to founding and running a worker cooperative game studio, with insights for both U.S. and international developers. Presenters will walk attendees through key operational, procedural, and legal considerations including founding processes, ownership structures, compensation models, and funding strategies. The session explores the strong benefits of choosing a cooperative model and when that model makes sense. We will also explore strategic differences between funded and bootstrapped teams, and how to structure projects for long-term sustainability. Real-world examples and testimonials from real co-op studios provide concrete lessons on co-op governance, hiring and separation practices, and common pitfalls.
takeaway: Attendees will take away a practical and actionable set of steps for how to found, build and ship sustainably with a cooperative studio. Attendees will also receive examples of important documentation like governing bylaws, as well as experiences shared from existing game development worker co-operatives ranging widely in scale.
intended audience: This session is for game developers interested in forming their own studios, particularly those focused on equity, worker agency, and collaborative structures. While no prior knowledge is required, attendees who have spent time thinking about studio organization and governance will gain the most from the discussion.

session title: D2C Crystal Ball: Why Platform Fees Could Actually Be Great for the Future of Web Stores (Presented by FastSpring)
description:
The shift to direct-to-consumer revenue in gaming has created an unprecedented opportunity to diversify revenue channels, increase profit, and harvest unfiltered data, primarily driven by recent court rulings and regulations. With Japan's Mobile Software Competition Act, however, we see a shift. Unlike Epic v. Apple and Epic v. Google in the US, which currently allow steering to web stores without any fees paid to Apple or Google, the new policy in Japan has introduced platform fees on linked payments. This is a likely precedent as similar fees are included in the proposed global Epic v. Google settlement. What are platform fees, when do they apply, and what does this mean for the future of D2C?

In this chat, D2C leader Chip Thurston shares:

-The current state of play for D2C

-How platform fees have been introduced in Japan How platform fees will likely be introduced in other regions

-Why platform fees will accelerate growth in D2C

-What game publishers can do to maximize D2C% today and in the future.
takeaway: This talk will enable attendees to understand what they need to do today to prepare for the future of D2C.
intended audience:
-Product leaders working to establish and/or grow their D2C revenue.

-Business development leaders looking to understand the D2C state of play.

-Executive/finance leaders eager to accelerate their profit margin growth via D2C.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall

session title: Developing Together: Engagement and Emotionally Connected Design Through Sound and Music (Presented by the IGDA)
description:
The Interactive Audio Special Interest Group (IASIG) is dedicated to advancing the art and technology of sound in games—an essential element of what makes games engaging, emotional, and unforgettable. Why do certain pieces of music evoke powerful memories? Why are orchestras around the world bringing game scores to the concert hall? And why does that one sound effect keep echoing in your mind? This lively roundtable will bring together voices from across the game development community—composers, sound designers, game and narrative designers, animators, programmers, producers, and more—to share experiences, explore creative approaches, and discuss the latest trends.
Moderated by Jennifer Miller Hammel (Host/Curator/Producer of Arcade on Classical California) and Steve Horowitz (IASIG Chair / Audio Director, Nickelodeon Paramount), the session will spotlight standout moments in game audio history and examine how they can inspire future innovation. We'll tackle questions like: Does iterative design improve player retention? How might AI reshape—or disrupt—the creative process? Join us for a collaborative conversation aimed at deepening our understanding of each other's art, workflows, and pipelines—while celebrating the role of sound in crafting unforgettable game experiences.
takeaway: It only takes a few seconds of playing your favorite game on mute to realize how important sound is when it comes to creating an immersive experience. Music and Sound are often the most beloved elements of our favorite games and just hearing their soundtrack can fill us with joy and nostalgia. Game designers, animators, programmers and producers imagine everything about a game, including its plots, characters, visual designs, and layouts and meet with different teams to create a cohesive vision. From this meeting of designers and producers in the field, attendees will take away a more complete understanding of the issues that audio designers and game designers face when working together in teams. Animators, programmers and designers will all gain a better understanding of how great sound can make their games more fun & increase playability and in turn, audio designers will gain a better understanding of how audio and audio system development fits into the overall production workflow.
intended audience: Calling all music composers, game designers, sound designers, animators, audio producers, programmers, and technical implementation specialists! This roundtable is for anyone involved in the game-building process. It's a must for industry professionals, students, and educators alike who are navigating the waters and steering toward smoother production and deeper immersion in the field. Together, we'll foster community, celebrate diversity, and share perspectives that strengthen collaboration across every corner of the industry.
speakers: Steven Horowitz(IASIG/GAI/SFSU), Jennifer Miller Hammel(Classical KUSC-Arcade)

session title: Freelancers and Independent Contractors Roundtable: New Freelancers
description: Shocking studio closings and project cancellations have left the industry volatile, and the prevalence of AI is a growing concern, forcing freelancers to rethink their business strategies and places in games. This roundtable will cover how freelancers are adjusting to these changes and what has and hasn't worked for them. Most importantly, this roundtable is a community of freelancers and independent contractors offering support and guidance. Everyone is encouraged to share practical advice and lessons learned through their hard-earned real-life experiences. Those who are new to freelancing or interested in freelancing are welcome, as well as more experienced freelancers.
takeaway: During this roundtable, attendees will share what they've learned about their businesses, with the aim of helping each other develop better business practices, advocate for themselves, and improve their quality of life. The moderators will provide topics and facilitate discussions that are pressing issues for all freelancers.
intended audience: This roundtable is designed for devs interested in becoming freelancers, freelancers of all experience levels, devs who are looking to work with freelancers, and anyone who cares about the freelance community.
speakers: Toiya Kristen Finley(Schnoodle Media, LLC), Souha Al-Samkari(Truant Pixel)

session title: From Sibelius to Game: Crafting Adaptive Music for 'Kingdom Come: Deliverance'
description: This talk explores the technical and creative processes behind the music of Kingdom Come: Deliverance II, where I served as a music programmer, and soundtrack contributor. Using our proprietary Sequence Music Engine and music logic module, we authentically scored the game's 1400s Bohemia setting with segment-based adaptive music driven by in-game variables. Our workflow centers around the notation program Sibelius and our custom tool Converdi, which streamlines the production by converting the score symbols to preliminary MIDI streams per individual VSTs and by extracting the precise timing data necessary for the segment transitions. This enabled us to spend more time on the creative aspects of music and less time on production and implementation.
takeaway:
* Production should start with a complete score, and not just the MIDI exports therefrom
* Custom automation tools can streamline the music creation workflow and reduce the production time
* Resequencing is more suitable for classical and medievalesque music than layering
* Rapid music prototyping allows for early testing of adaptive music directly in the game
intended audience: Game studio producers responsible for music workflow, game audio professionals, music technologists, composers, and developers interested in adaptive music systems, and technical composition workflows.

session title: Games for Diplomacy: Empowering Global Game-Making Communities
description:
How can games strengthen international connections and empower emerging creators? This 60-minute microtalk session brings together experts using games as tools for diplomacy, civic engagement, and community-building around the world.

Moderator Dr. Lindsay Grace introduces the concept of 'games as diplomacy,' drawing on collaborations with colleges, NGOs, and embassies across India, Turkey, Cuba, Malaysia, and more. Susanna Pollack highlights how Games for Change's international chapters support creators in Latin America, Africa, and Asia—building networks, funding pathways, and mentorship for long-term impact.

Maria Burns Ortiz shows how global game jams can grow local ecosystems while teaching skills in combating disinformation, exploring participatory decision-making, understanding IP rights, and preserving cultural heritage. Paul Fischer and Leo Olebe connect these efforts to policy and industry, sharing lessons from government partnerships and global platforms that uplift new developers and advance shared civic goals.
takeaway: Attendees will learn practical models for using games, game jams, and global networks as tools of diplomacy—gaining examples, partnership ideas, and infrastructure strategies they can adapt to support civic engagement, cultural exchange, and community-building in their own regions.
intended audience: Developers, educators, community organizers, policymakers, and anyone interested in how games can support diplomacy, civic engagement, and global creative ecosystems. Ideal for attendees seeking practical models, partnerships, and inspiration for using games to connect communities and empower emerging creators worldwide.
speakers: Lindsay Grace(University of Miami), Susanna Pollack(Games for Change), Maria Burns Ortiz(The Global Game Jam), Paul Fischer(Accenture), Leo Olebe(Olebe Media Group)

session title: Hybrid and Remote Work Roundtable Day 2: Remote
description:
What does it take to build and nurture a remote working environment? What are the challenges of working with and coordinating teams across the globe? How can we foster a sense of belonging and inclusion across cultures and timezones? What are the challenges to hiring for and leading a hybrid team?

Many companies are beginning to consciously offer fully remote work environments. With globalization leading to faster and more reliable access to internet, these development opportunities are becoming more feasible with each passing year. Yet they present their own unique challenges.

Engage with other industry professionals on the topic of remote working arrangements—including hiring, team leadership, accountability, company events, IT solutions, and more. Challenge the status quo of working a 9-to-5 office job with this collaborative and innovative approach development.
takeaway: Attendees will expand their knowledge on remote work solutions through a collaborative discussion. Join veterans and new team leaders alike to contemplate the challenges and celebrate the successes of this powerful workplace solution.
intended audience: This is for team leaders (of large or small teams) interested in dissecting how to create and support remote working environments. Whether the team is already remote or considering the change, this roundtable is open to all. Regardless of roundtable topic, both hybrid and remote teams welcome as both types share many topics.
speakers: Jessica Damerst(Unknown Worlds), Trevin York(Dire Lark)

session title: Level One: Women+ Entering Game Development (Presented by the IGDA)
description: Curious about breaking into the game industry but don'Äôt know where to begin? Then this beginner-friendly Women in Games+ workshop is designed for you! No prior experience is required. Participants will build a playable game prototype while learning foundational industry workflows, terminology, and development practices. Using accessible tools such as Flow Lab (browser-based), GameMaker, or Unity, attendees will gain hands-on experience and insight into real-world production pipelines. Participants should bring a laptop. For GameMaker or Unity sessions, please install the software in advance. Walk away with a functioning prototype, new technical skills, and a supportive community of Women in Games+ creators.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Machine Learning Roundtable Day 2: Game Development and Operations
description:
Machine Learning and generative approaches are changing how we build games, operate them, and even how we write code day-to-day.
