This round table focuses on how recent advances impacted development and operations in practice over the past year. What tools and workflows stuck. What promised productivity but fell apart at scale. What fundamentally changed how teams work, collaborate, and ship. And what we quietly rolled back.

The goal is to share lived experience from the trenches. Not predictions. Not hype. Just what it feels like to make and run games with modern ML in the loop.
takeaway:
Attendees gain a grounded understanding of how ML and GenAI are being used today in game development pipelines and live operations. This includes tooling, automation, coding workflows, DevOps, LiveOps, and internal platforms.

Rather than focusing on abstract potential, this session highlights concrete wins, real costs, and operational lessons learned. Attendees should leave with a better sense of what is worth trying next year, what requires caution, and what is probably not ready yet.
intended audience:
This round table is aimed at ML practitioners working in game development and operations. This includes AI and ML engineers, technical directors, leads, tools engineers, backend and DevOps engineers, and anyone shaping development workflows.

You do not need to be an ML expert, but you should care about how ML changes how games are made and operated. If you have adopted, evaluated, or resisted ML-driven tools in the past year, you will have something to contribute.

session title: Next Global PowerHouse in Game Development (Presented by TechDestination Pakistan)
Redefining What Romance Can Be in 'Love and Deepspace'
description: Launched as a passion project, Love and Deepspace reimagined emotional storytelling in games and grew into a global success. This session examines the creative and technical innovations behind its popularity, including the integration of real-time action with relationship-building, high-fidelity 3D characters with cinematic presentation, and deeply resonant themes that engage players worldwide. The design emphasizes combat, everyday life, and emotional narrative to expand the depth of interactive fantasy. Continuous player feedback, ambitious technology, and thoughtful storytelling ensured both polish and accessibility, demonstrating how creative vision and bold design can shape a global phenomenon.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how Love and Deepspace expanded romance by combining action combat, immersive 3D life-simulation elements, and emotionally rich storytelling. The session offers a blueprint for transforming passion projects into global successes through innovative gameplay design and authentic creative direction.
intended audience: Audiences of all experience levels are welcome.

session title: Spectrogram Spectacular!: The Art and Science of Dialogue Editing with Spectrograms
description:
Using spectrogram editors in the world of dialogue editing can be a bit like finding patterns in the noise. There is both an art, and a science, to the language of information contained within. We will dive deep into the mysterious universal language underneath the words actors speak, to empower people at all levels of editing experience to be more efficient and effective in their craft.

Through video & audio demonstrations, using samples from well-known characters, as well as some surprise guest voices, we will highlight the differences between spectrogram behaviors and the tools available in different DAWs such as ProTools, Adobe Audition, iZotope RX, and others. Comparisons of the similarities, differences and nuances gained from waveforms vs. spectrogram views will highlight how visual editing and repair can be a powerful tool alongside classical auditory editing of dialogue to make the work more efficient.
takeaway: Attendees will come away understanding the fundamental principles of what sound looks like on a visual 2D plane. How to identify different aspects of a spectrogram, how they translate to audible sound, the basic tools to cleanup/adjust/repair audio as well as some advanced tools and techniques.
intended audience: This talk accommodates everyone from those who have no prior experience with spectrograms to experienced dialogue designers. This includes VAs, engineers, editors and technical designers. We will take the audience through the very basics of understanding spectrograms through developing a deeper understanding of what sounds, words, and noises look like.
speakers: Katherine Tole(Plant Based Audio), Matthew Kulewicz(Socket Sound, LLC)

session title: The Monetization Paradox: How Doing the Opposite of What's Expected Drove Revenue Growth
description:
We're taught to believe that successful monetization comes from pressure: tightening economies, stretching missions, pushing players toward paywalls. But what if the opposite were true? But what if generosity outperformed all of that?

Naama Frenkel shares how her team broke conventional wisdom by giving more away and saw real revenue gains as a result. Through four case studies from live mobile games, she'll demonstrate how this approach successfully drives engagement, trust, and monetization figures that exceed expectations. This session offers both inspiration and a toolkit; not for side currencies or ad tweaks, but bold changes to your core loop.
takeaway: PMs, monetization designers, and LiveOps leads will receive first-hand insights that may lead them to rethink the fundamentals of how they design for long-term player engagement, ditching short-term monetization for long-term player loyalty.
intended audience: This talk will resonate with monetization designers, PMs, LiveOps leads, and anyone looking to challenge the assumptions that still dominate free-to-play revenue strategy.

session title: The Scalable Game Asset-Assisted Production Pipeline of 'Honor of Kings'
description:
Driven by the demand for immersive and visually stunning experiences, developers face the challenge of creating detailed assets efficiently and cost-effectively across multiple platforms. Traditional asset creation methods are time-consuming and expensive, making them inadequate for this evolving landscape.

This presentation explores how AI techniques, such as differentiable rendering and Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC), enhance asset production quality and efficiency. Specifically, within our game Honor Of Kings and other titles, we utilize Differential Shading Language (Slang) for material fitting, optimizing textures for low-end devices, and incorporating tools for artists to swiftly iterate designs. Innovations in geometry processing, including Software Occlusion Culling and Level of Detail (LOD) simplification, leverage rendering loss and visual importance to maintain high-quality visual experiences while reducing resource consumption.

By adopting these cutting-edge AI technologies, we significantly improve the creation and deployment of game assets, addressing the diverse challenges of modern game development.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insight into how AI technology can be employed to power game asset production for both improving the quality and reducing time consumption to generate high-detail or low-end devices runnable game assets, in several specific domains like material making, geometry production, etc.
intended audience: This presentation is intended for developers who want to employ AI technology to power their game asset production pipelines, as well as engineers who are interested in implementing a differentiable rendering pipeline in game engine. Cursory knowledge of rendering and artificial intelligence is recommended.
speakers: Zhipeng Tan(Tencent Games), Leon Zhang(Tencent Games)

session title: Think Outside the Cube: Designing Games for the First Cubical Console (Presented by WOWCube)
description: Cube Your Curiosity, what if the controller itself became the game? WOWCube merges the tactile joy of a Rubik's Cube with the power of a connected digital console with 24 screens. Join us to explore how this new interface opens up endless creative opportunities for developers — from interactive puzzles to 360° storytelling.

session title: Veterans Q&A for New Writers and Narrative Designers
description:
There is no hard-coded set of rules for video games writers and narrative designers, and often the form and function of the job is dependent on the culture of the studio you work with. Be it for indie, AAA, or somewhere in between, the idea of 'best practices' feels nebulous when processes everywhere can vary so wildly. But something we can all agree on is that in order to persist and thrive in your craft, being open to learning and growth is critical.

We've assembled a panel of video game writing super-veterans with decades of experience across the industry. We have worked on major IP from Call of Duty to Marvel to Dungeons and Dragons, hot new IP such as Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, and smaller indie teams. We've been full time studio hires, contract project writers, participated in writers rooms large and small and even lead or directed project narratives. We all come from vastly different backgrounds and experiences, from how we got their start, to the game genres we've written for, to where we are now. Now is the moment to ask a group of seasoned professionals every burning question you have about game writing and how to grow your craft and career.
takeaway: The audience will take away new insight into all aspects of game writing and working as a game writer within a studio environment, lead by questions from those in attendance.
intended audience: This will be most beneficial to entry level and junior writers, but will offer wisdom and advice for any writer looking to better their craft and ability to collaborate within teams.
speakers: Alexa Ray Corriea(Gearbox), Diandra Anne Lasrado(iwot games), Jennifer Svedberg-Yen(Sandfall Interactive), Evan Narcisse(Brass Lion Entertainment), Whitney Beltran(CD Projekt RED)

session title: Building Growth Through Partnership: A Practical Avia Playbook for Brand + Performance on Meta (Presented by AviaGames)
description: This session is about the operating model behind brand + performance. Avia and Meta will share a practical playbook for partnership-led growth—how teams co-define objectives, co-build creative, and run a shared testing and learning cadence so brand storytelling and UA execution work as one system. Attendees will walk away with an actionable framework for aligning stakeholders, accelerating iteration, and improving efficiency through a consistent measurement mindset.
takeaway: Attendees learn how Meta and AviaGames work together to run brand and performance as one marketing system. They take away high-level principles for partnership-led growth—how teams align objectives, co-build creative, and maintain a shared testing-and-learning cadence that improves iteration speed and measurement consistency.
intended audience: This session is for anyone working on growth and marketing, including brand, UA, creative, analytics, and partner teams. It benefits practitioners who want brand storytelling and performance execution to work together through clearer alignment, faster iteration, and a shared measurement mindset. No advanced expertise is required.
speakers: Ping Wang(AviaGames), Benny Chu(Meta), David Cheang(Meta), Minyu Sun(AviaGames)

session title: Discord Integration Best Practices: Social Design Principles That Move the Needle (Presented by Discord)
description:
Learn best practices for implementing the Discord Social SDK directly into your game to maximize player engagement and retention. This session provides a practical playbook for integrating Discord-powered social features—including voice chat, rich presence, and friend lists—and designing your game systems around these capabilities.

Discover proven strategies from successful titles: when to prompt for account linking, how to design friction-free onboarding flows, where to surface social features for natural adoption, and which game moments create the strongest social hooks. The presentation addresses critical design decisions like party formation, voice channel integration, and activity feed optimization.

Attendees gain actionable frameworks for structuring Discord integration, designing multiplayer experiences that leverage Discord's social graph, and measuring impact through retention curves and engagement metrics. Leave with clear guidance on building Discord into your game and designing around social features to create stickier, more engaging player experiences.
takeaway: Attendees learn specific integration patterns that increase account linking conversion rates, discover timing strategies for introducing Discord features that maximize adoption without disrupting core gameplay, and understand how to instrument and measure the impact of social SDK integration on player retention metrics like DAU and play frequency.
intended audience: This session targets client engineers implementing social platform SDKs and producers evaluating third-party integrations for player engagement. Technical attendees benefit from concrete implementation guidance while production staff gain frameworks for integration decisions. Basic familiarity with SDK integration concepts and player engagement metrics is helpful but not required.

session title: Evolution of Game Development: Making Games Locally with Global Teams (Presented by Virtuos)
description:
As production scales and creative ambitions rise, North American studios increasingly rely on global talent to accelerate development, reduce risk, and expand creative capacity. This panel explores how Virtuos and Pipeworks leverage distributed teams across Asia and Europe to empower local studios and deliver higher-quality AAA content.

Through real-world case studies, we will break down how co-development has evolved from a linear outsourcing workflow into an integrated, multi-studio creative model. This session will discuss the cultural, technical, and organizational shifts required to make globally distributed development not just efficient, but truly collaborative. Topics include: establishing shared ownership across time zones, aligning pipelines and tools, ensuring quality and consistency at scale, and fostering long-term trust between teams.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a transparent view of what global collaboration really looks like today, and how studios can build resilient, cross-regional partnerships that support innovation, stability, and creative excellence. As our industry continues to globalize, understanding how to harness worldwide talent will be essential to the future of game making.
intended audience: All
speakers: Dennis Cooper(Virtuos), John Doyle(PlayStation Studios), Jason RM Smith(Game Caviar), Kay Arutyunyan(CounterPunch - A Virtuos Studio), Lindsay Gupton(Pipeworks - A Virtuos Studio)

session title: GDC Side Quest Podcast Live: Gordon Bellamy
description: In this special live recording of the GDC Side Quest Podcast, sit down with Gordon Bellamy, CEO of Gay Gaming Professionals (GGP), to discuss the importance of LGBTQ+ representation in the games industry. With two decades of leadership experience, Gordon share his insights on how representation shapes the industry, why advocating for your peers is essential, and how fostering inclusivity benefits everyone.
takeaway: Attendees will leave inspired to champion inclusivity in their own spaces, with a deeper understanding of how representation and advocacy can drive meaningful change in the games industry.
intended audience: Ideal for developers, industry leaders, and anyone passionate about diversity, equity, and inclusion in gaming.
speakers: Gordon Bellamy(USC), Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming)

session title: IGDA Foundation x Unity Presents: Resume and Portfolio Review
description:
Join industry experts for a resume & portfolio review at GDC 2026! Get feedback, level up your career, and make connections.




Looking to break into the games industry or level up your career? Join IGDA Foundation and Unity's Portfolio & Resume Reviews for expert feedback from industry professionals. You'll be matched with a reviewer based on your discipline—whether you're a programmer, artist, designer, producer, or another role.




In a 15-minute, one-on-one session, receive personalized feedback on your resume, or portfolio to help you stand out in the industry. Don't miss this chance to refine your materials, gain insider insights, and make meaningful connections to advance your career!




Please note: we have limited time and spaces, so availability is first come first serve for these reviews
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Lightspeed Mocap LA: Creating a State-of-the-Art Mocap Studio from Scratch (Presented by LIGHTSPEED STUDIOS)
description: This session is a look behind the scenes of the creation of a state-of-the-art motion capture studio built entirely from the ground up. It outlines the full journey of LightSpeed Mocap LA—from construction and technical infrastructure to assembling the team and designing a robust, scalable pipeline. The talk highlights how this LA-based studio collaborates with game developers to deliver high-quality data rapidly, balancing production demands with innovation. Attendees gain an inside look at the LA studio's facility: including the stage layout and gear, workshop, audio suite, and supporting spaces, as well as the organizational practices and backend systems that keep everything running smoothly.
takeaway: Attendees gain knowledge of the new facility's services and physical technology, as well as practical insights into designing a mocap studio and pipeline quickly from the ground up. Discussions of pipeline design strategies and organizational frameworks will leave the attendees with an understanding of the studio's approach to streamlining processes.
intended audience: This presentation benefits technical and cinematic directors, mocap supervisors, pipeline engineers, and production leads using motion capture in their development. Developers and designers integrating performance data into game pipelines will gain practical guidance on facility design, workflow structure, and cross-team collaboration, making it valuable for anyone interested in mocap production.
speakers: Kristin Gallagher(LightSpeed Studios), Christopher Jarvis(LightSpeed Studios)

session title: Manicured Characters: Path-Traced Hair and Skin in AAA Games (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: Explore how Indiana Jones and the Great Circle uses NVIDIA's Vulkan extensions to achieve cinematic, real-time strand-based hair (RTX Hair). This session breaks down the implementation of strand geometry into the Bounding Volume Heirarchy (BVH) using Linear Swept Sphere (LSS) primitives, alongside optimizations for shadows, reflections, and multi-bounce scattering. Additionally, we'll demonstrate how path-traced subsurface scattering (RTX Skin) —as seen in Half-Life 2 RTX—integrates into a modern pipeline to deliver ultra-realistic skin for both gameplay and cutscenes.
takeaway: Attendees gain a practical understanding of real-time path-traced character rendering, including integrating Linear Swept Sphere primitives into BVHs for strand-based hair. They learn production-ready hair scattering techniques and how path-traced subsurface scattering fits into modern character pipelines while balancing quality, performance, and memory.
intended audience: Graphics Programmers; Technical Artists

session title: To Each Their Own Hell: The Journey of Writing Complex Characters in 'Limbus Company' (Presented by Project Moon)
description: Trauma and anxiety are some of the core psychological experiences inherent in human nature. Every person has a personal hell to confront, and in 'Limbus Company', the central narrative theme lies in how each character faces their own trauma in pursuit of their 'true' self. In this talk, Project Moon's Lead Scenario Designer Eunkyong Han revisits both the complex and multilayered personae that have successfully resonated with the game's players, and those that have not. She presents a case-by-case comparison between characters with deeper personal histories and those who, despite the writers' best efforts, ultimately received less narrative growth. This session will explore the diverse approaches to breathing life into fictional characters and how their personal skeletons in the closet serve to sustain the overarching narrative journey of the game.
takeaway: The audience will gain insight into how character writing and narrative design can reinforce the immersive experience of players. Through this session, attendees will also observe how the human trauma and anxiety, the hell that every person must face, can be used as psychological foundations in delivering a more compelling and visceral characterization.
intended audience: This session is aimed at sharing experience and concerns with developers and scenario writers at any level looking to create character-driven narratives. Anyone interested in game scenario and storytelling is welcome.

session title: Unpub Playtesting
description:
Playing new games is fun, but helping create them is even better! Play the tabletop games of the future today in our playtesting area hosted by Unpub. There's no registration required for players — seats are first-come first-served.

If you are a designer looking to have your game playtested, you must have reserved a table in advance. Reservations are now closed. More info: https://www.unpub.org/gdc (https://www.unpub.org/gdc)
location: Tabletop Free Play & Playtesting Lounge, West Hall Level 3
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: WICKED PROBLEMS: Balancing Complex Systems
description: Contemporary game designers often face the challenge of designing large-scale 'heterogeneous' systems. In genres like Roguelikes, RPGs, MOBAs, CCGs, and strategy games, there are interacting systems of abilities, skills, equipment, etc. - that create design challenges of nearly infinite complexity. This workshop focuses specifically on concepts and methods for solving these kinds of wicked design problems, using hands-on exercises as the basis for learning and practicing skills. We will look closely at how network effects happen and try out a number of approaches for balancing massively complex problems in a many-to-many character battling game. Along the way, we will discuss and learn about relevant game design concepts like the advantages and disadvantages of automated testing, or how to leverage systems as part of content design. Please come to the session prepared to actively play and participate.
takeaway: Using a 'learning by doing' approach, we will explore a range of skills and ideas related to balancing complex systems, including: design methodologies (establishing the WHAT, HOW, and WHY of a balancing process); the use and pitfalls of balancing against 'standard/vanilla' units; debates around machine-automated vs. human-designed approaches; and more.
intended audience: This session is for anyone who wants to deepen their skills and knowledge for solving complex design problems. Designers and developers of any level - those just entering the industry to design leads and creative directors - have found the exercises useful. The core ideas are relevant to just about any kind of game.

session title: Cancelled: Next Gen-Game Intelligence: Deploying Agentic AI on AWS (Presented by Perella)
description: Explore how agentic AI enhances engagement, from NPCs with persistent memory to adaptive in-world guidance. Perella fuels the next generation of game development through systems that guide players and empower developers. We'll showcase real use cases: Advanced gameplay analytics that inform content, dynamic NPC interactions, and player-centric tutorials. Whether you're a partner strategist or a developer, discover how intelligent characters scale engagement and make game worlds more alive.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with a clear understanding of how agentic AI transforms both game development and player experiences. They'll gain insight into deploying AI tools that analyze player behavior, create advanced NPCs, and offer personalized, inclusive in-world guidance.
intended audience: This talk is aimed at partner alliance leaders and developers in game studios. Attendees should be familiar with game design or development workflows, but no deep AI expertise is required.

session title: Leading Global Games Businesses in Uncertain Times (Presented by Testronic)
description:
In an era defined by shifting market forces, geopolitical tension, and evolving player expectations, the global games industry is navigating unprecedented unpredictability. This session will explore practical strategies on how studios can build resilience, maintain quality, and scale responsibly when the ground keeps moving beneath them.

We will examine the pressures impacting development, launch, and live-operations – from regional regulation and economic volatility, to rising expectations for culturalisation, stability, and trust. Testronic's global test floor, powered by its Centres of Excellence , enables teams to respond with agility, delivering technically robust and culturally relevant player experiences at scale. This session will also discuss the opportunities for new services and solutions being introduced to assist studios around the world.

Guided by a player-focussed, partner-first, outcomes-led approach, Testronic supports studios in consistently delivering strong player experiences by reducing risk, accelerating time-to-market, and safeguarding brand equity.
takeaway:
- Practical strategies to navigate geopolitical, market and industry volatility
- How to maintain quality and cultural relevance at global scale
- Why player-focused, outcomes-led partnerships drive resilience
- How global Centres of Excellence accelerate outcomes and safeguard the player experience
intended audience: This talk is for producers, studio leaders, and project owners navigating global uncertainty. It will deliver insights for teams seeking practical strategies to maintain quality, reduce risk, and scale responsibly while delivering culturally relevant, player-focused experiences across regions and platforms.

session title: Tacos & Beer & Friendship (Presented by Game Over Productions Inc.)
description:
Join us as we share the motivation and story behind ¡Lucha Loka!™—an upcoming co-op beat-'em-up currently in development for next-gen consoles.
It's a tale of tacos, beer, and friendship. Set against the backdrop of modern-day Mexico, follow Lucho and his Lucha Libre amigos as they battle an ancient cartel deity whose dark ambitions threaten to succumb the country to eternal damnation.
It's fiesta time!
takeaway: Finding the satire and irony of it all in difficult situations makes us human. It is our expectation that participants will develop a comprehensive understanding of our culture, the present challenges we face, and the rationale behind the creation of this game.

session title: Test Every Idea, Build the Best One: Amplify Creative Exploration Without Developer Bottlenecks (Presented by Bezi)
description:
Traditionally, prototyping has been a filtering process. Designers pitch ideas, developers evaluate feasibility, and only a handful make it to playable form. The rest? Shelved because there isn't time. At the same time, developers spend time building prototypes that never make it to production instead of focusing on the architecture, systems and polish required to ship.

This session explores how teams use tools like Bezi to change that dynamic. By removing technical bottlenecks, designers can translate ideas directly into playable mechanics in minutes, not days. Designers get creative freedom. Developers get to focus on production-quality work. More experimentation, more creative exploration, better games.
takeaway: Attendees will see real workflows to implement mechanics from scratch with natural language, how to iterate on existing systems faster to find the best version of an idea, how accessible prototyping can compound creative impact, and the compounding impacts of accessible prototyping on game quality.
intended audience: Game designers, producers, and technical leads facing iteration bottlenecks. Teams with dedicated prototyping workflows where designers depend on developers to implement ideas, limiting creative exploration. Studios seeking to accelerate pre-production without sacrificing quality or pulling engineers off core systems work.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall

session title: Expanding the 13%: Directing Game Access in Game Education
description: The University of Washington School of Information, building on the work by the Video Game History Foundation's '87%' study, is researching how the current game access landscape is impacting game education. With legacy games legal availability already being inconsistent, and the growing volume of games released in the market annually, we set out to discover what games included as examples in game education programs are, what they have in common, and what hinders their access and preservation. This session is the presentation of the phase 1 findings with data captured from interviews with leading game designers, educators, researchers, and historians.
takeaway: Attendees will be the first to see what traits are shared among high relevance titles for game education programs, and the barriers to accessing and preserving games for game education programs.
intended audience:
Game educators, researchers, historians, and students.
No prerequisite knowledge is necessary for understanding this presentation.

session title: Building Global Entertainment Worlds from Gaming IP
description:
Join Justin Scarpone, Executive Vice-President & Global Head of Transmedia at Sega, and acclaimed moderator Andrea Rene (Executive Producer at Shortie Media and co-founder of What's Good Games) for an illuminating conversation on how leading gaming companies are evolving into entertainment powerhouses that captivate audiences across film, streaming, merchandise, live shows, location-based entertainment, and beyond.

Drawing from his remarkable 30-year career spanning Disney's global entertainment empire and Scopely's mobile gaming success, Justin will share exclusive insights on Sega's ambitious transmedia strategy, including the phenomenal success of the movie franchise, which has become the catalyst for explosive growth in merchandising, animation, experiences, and streaming content.

This session offers an exclusive look into Sega's revolutionary approach to IP expansion, transforming beloved video game characters into global entertainment brands that resonate across generations and cultures.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA

session title: Creating IP Through Understanding (Presented by LIGHTSPEED STUDIOS)
description:
In the process of developing original intellectual properties, designers often prioritize uniqueness, which can sometimes lead to overdesign and a dilution of the core concept. This challenge is particularly common among students, who may skip essential research, jumping straight into a blank slate, resulting in less effective IP designs.

Strong IPs aren't solely about being different; they often revolve around familiar ideas, subtly altered to provide a fresh experience. When an audience's expectations are drastically changed, the learning curve can become steep, risking disengagement and potentially harming the IP's success.

To create a compelling IP, it's crucial to identify its fundamental selling points first. Once this foundation is established, designers can thoughtfully introduce unique elements.

In this session, we will explore a well-known genre, dissecting its distinctive characteristics. By understanding what makes these genres iconic, participants will gain insights that will help them develop their own unique concepts for their next standout IP.
takeaway: The key takeaway for designers is that effective design thinking, especially in IP creation, requires a solid understanding of your subject. Conducting thorough research before delving into form exploration is essential for successful outcomes.
intended audience: This lecture is for game developers, narrative designers, creative directors, and IP planners, especially those who wish to systematically learn about world-building, character development, and gameplay integration. A basic understanding of game development or design is required, but no specific background in IP creation is necessary.

session title: Designing for the Living Room: The Spatial Architecture of Mixed Reality (Presented by Meta)
description: As we move from the virtual worlds of VR to physical room-aware Mixed Reality, the core challenge for developers is no longer just building a world, but building inside of the user's physical world. In this session, Doug North Cook (CEO & Creative Director of Creature) explores the design philosophy behind his team's game Starship Home and games released under Creature's game label that leverage these features like Thomas Van Bouwel's Laser Dance and Cubism, among others, to illustrate how architectural theory can help solve the constraints of real-world spaces. Attendees will learn how to design for the unique affordances of mixed reality while creating embodied gameplay that feels natural and intuitive on standalone hardware. This talk provides a framework for leveraging Meta's MR APIs to create ''Playful Architecture'' that transforms any environment into a dynamic stage for immersive play.

session title: Discord Ads: The Emerging Must-Buy Platform for Gaming Marketers (Presented by Discord)
description: Discord presents its evolution into an essential advertising platform for gaming and gaming-adjacent brands. This session explores how Discord's unique position as the communication hub for 200+ million gamers worldwide creates unparalleled opportunities for authentic brand engagement. Attendees learn about Discord's expanded ads product suite launching throughout 2026, including advanced targeting capabilities and expanded measurement solutions that prove campaign effectiveness. The presentation features real client case studies demonstrating how leading gaming advertisers achieve measurable results by reaching highly engaged communities at scale.
takeaway: Attendees gain a concrete understanding of Discord's advertising capabilities and 2026 product roadmap. The session provides actionable insights through client case studies showing proven campaign performance, introduces new measurement tools for demonstrating ROI, and explains how Discord's community-first environment delivers engagement metrics that outperform traditional gaming advertising channels.
intended audience: This presentation is suitable for anyone who is looking to advertise with Discord, partner with Discord or simply learn more about our Ad product suite.

session title: Smooth Sailing: Using Asset Health Metrics to Optimize 'Voyagers of Nera' (Presented by Virtuos)
description:
In this session, Eoin O'Grady, Technical Director at Black Shamrock, explores the benefits brought by our asset validation tool in Voyagers of Nera, which gave visibility on the state of assets, leading to targeted optimizations and ensuring a stable early access release of the game.

Join this demonstration on how our tool reviews assets for optimal configuration, elevates visibility on sub optimal assets, and fixes content en masse. We break down key features every asset validation tool should have, and how these support optimization efforts for all different types of content. Finally, we share details on some of the specific rules we implemented for Nanite static meshes, skeletal meshes textures, blueprints, and more, and how this tool affected the efficiency of the development team and the performance of the game.
takeaway: The value of monitoring the health of your assets. How to automate monitoring a projects asset health and make it highly visible. The benefits of using tools to alter assets and enforce the health standards for your assets.
speakers: Eoin O'Grady(Black Shamrock - A Virtuos Studio)

session title: Game Developer's Bryant Francis interviews Marvel Rivals Publishing and Marketing Lead Yachen Bian and Marvel Games Executive Producer Danny Koo live in the GDC Podcast Studio
description: Game Developer's Bryant Francis interviews Marvel Rivals Publishing and Marketing Lead Yachen Bian and Marvel Games Executive Producer Danny Koo live in the GDC Podcast Studio.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: GDC Side Quest Live: AP Thomson & Jenny Jiao Hsia
description: Join us for a special live podcast featuring IGF-nominated *Titanium Court* developer AP Thomson and *Consume Me *creator Jenny Jiao Hsia, which won the Seumas McNally Grand Prize at IGF 2025. They discuss their creative process, the importance of creating games with social impact, and the challenges and rewards of working on deeply personal projects.
takeaway: Attendees will gain inspiration from the journey of these award-winning developers and learn how to create games that resonate on a personal and societal level.
intended audience: Perfect for indie developers, socially conscious creators, and anyone interested in the intersection of art and impact in games.
speakers: Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming), AP Thomson, Jenny Jiao Hsia

session title: It Takes a Village: Live at GDC Day 2
description:
'It Takes A Village' is a chat show now in its second season (hosted on YouTube) where we shine a light on the people who make our games, on themes of collaboration, community and co-advocacy. It's a love letter to our unsung heroes, a thank you to those who uplift others, inspiration to those interested in starting a career in games, and provides unique industry insight. Host Samantha Béart will chat with guests about how they got into games and the details of their craft, then moderate an informal audience Q&A. It is a wholesome fireside chat where developers can recognise and learn from those who've helped the industry, as well as share advice on navigating what can often be a challenging industry to work in.

Hosted by Samantha Béart, a classically trained and BAFTA-nominated actor, best known for their role as Karlach in 'Baldur's Gate 3'.
takeaway: Attendees will gain deep industry knowledge in not only the specialisation of each guest, but also their perspective of the games industry as a whole. They'll feel inspired to nurture their own communities in whatever form those communities might take.
intended audience: This is for anyone who's interested in intimate, behind-the-scenes chats with games industry professionals.

session title: All Rays Lead to Rome: Next-Gen Graphics in 'Anno 117: Pax Romana'
description: As a builder game, Anno 117: Pax Romana faces a unique set of technical challenges when it comes to rendering its highly dynamic world. Visuals range from overlooking entire cities with giant amphitheaters and aqueducts to observing tiny details in the daily activities of residents in small settlements near foggy marshes. This session provides an overview of some key engine improvements that were made during the development of Anno 117 and the most important graphic features that helped shape the look of the game. In particular, it will cover a scalable raytracing implementation that is based on gathering hit feedback on the GPU to manage acceleration structures. This algorithm allows for large and highly dynamic scenes with many deformable meshes, even on consoles.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insights into the rendering technology behind Anno 117: Pax Romana. They will learn about methods that facilitate scalable rendering of large player-built scenes, in particular some strategies for efficiently implementing raytracing that can be applied to similar genres.
intended audience: The target audience is mainly graphics and engine programmers. Basic knowledge of graphics and engine technology is required.

session title: Animation Summit Hotseat Challenge
description: Join us as we try to create a functioning animation set & implementation to get through an obstacle course live! It'll be a little bit gameshow, a little bit art & technical tutorial and a lot of fun.
takeaway: You will see animation and systems assembled at ludicrous speed, with a high level overview of what an animation pipeline looks like. We'll also be constantly interrupting the participants with questions and challenges, which we are sure they will love.
intended audience: Everyone! Come on down to watch the the high pressure artistic brilliance leading to something that may end up being actually playable! Bring your serious and irreverent questions for animators & designers so we can pester and distract them at every turn.
speakers: Taylor Rohrig(Half Mermain), Sole Gonzalez(Super Spline Studios), Chandrahas Tanguturi(Firaxis Games), Matt Lake(Independent), Frank Gordon(Compulsion Games), Nathaniel Winckler(That's No Moon)

session title: Ask Video Game Lawyers Anything (Legal AMA): This Normally Costs Money! Day 2
description:
This roundtable session allows you to ask any burning legal questions* you may have from a group of industry lawyers. This is a group of video game enthusiasts who happen to be lawyers (or, sure, vice versa). They met at video game and lawyer conventions (yes, video game lawyer conventions are a thing) and have been speaking for years about legal topics near and dear to their favourite digital pastime. The topics to be discussed if nobody's brave: influencer advertising; loot boxes; IP law; corp stuff; the rise of AI; web3 (if we have to); trademarks and other intellectual property; but we'd love to answer YOUR questions and give out legal info on video game and law topics.

* The speakers can't give specific legal advice; and aren't insured for that! Use hypotheticals, people! Of course there's a disclaimer here... we are lawyers, after all.
takeaway: An engaging and entertaining look inside the discussions video game lawyers have with clients, as well as an appreciation of the issues that arise in games. Above all else, talking to lawyers can be scary and we want to demystify that! Getting good legal advice is critical to games success.
intended audience: Anyone interested in the games business! We put these on to make interacting with lawyers approachable and non-scary; we hope you come with legal questions that have been on your mind!
speakers: Ryan Black(DLA Piper (Canada) LLP), Yan Perng(Blizzard Entertainment), Angelo Alcid(Microsoft Corp.), Brandon Huffman(Odin Law & Media)

session title: External Development (Outsourcing) Roundtable Day 2
description: External Development, or Outsourcing as it is commonly referred, plays a huge role in the game development industry and with it comes a huge amount of challenges. The External Development Roundtable is a moderated forum discussion surrounding external development topics which are mainly generated by the participants. Whether it's team/leadership buy-in, career growth, best practices, vendor relations, etc. this roundtable will provide a forum for questions, ideas and solutions from industry peers on the front lines of external development. Attendees from all experience levels and disciplines are welcome and will benefit from the discussion. Attendees are strongly encouraged to participate in the conversation.
takeaway: Attendees should leave with an increased awareness of the challenges and solutions that come with external development and how different studios, developers and service providers approach these challenges. They will have a better understanding of external development which they can apply to their own pipeline for more successful external engagements.
intended audience: The primary audience for this session are game developers and producers, outsource/ExDev managers and studio team leadership across all disciplines as well as service providers who are interested in candidly discussing topics related to the challenges and successes of Outsourcing and External Partnerships in game development.

session title: Fast Pre-Production for Gameplay & Cinematics (Presented by Cartwheel)
description:
Pre-production is where ideas move, or stall. In this session, Cartwheel shares how AI-assisted motion tools can dramatically speed up early exploration for gameplay and cinematic teams. We'll walk through real examples of going from reference to usable animation in minutes, generating fast blockouts for combat moves, traversal, cutscenes, and character moments without disrupting existing Maya, Blender, or Unreal pipelines.

Attendees will learn where AI fits today, where it doesn't, and how teams are using rapid motion prototypes to test ideas earlier, reduce iteration time, and unblock downstream departments. We'll cover practical workflows, common pitfalls, and how studios of all sizes are integrating lightweight AI tools into their pre-production stack to move faster without sacrificing creative control.
takeaway: Attendees learn how teams use AI-driven motion tools to generate fast, reliable animation blockouts for gameplay and cinematics, reducing early-stage iteration from days to minutes. The session shows concrete workflow e.g., turning reference footage into prototype combat moves or character beats while clarifying where AI meaningfully accelerates pre-production and where traditional methods remain essential.
intended audience: This session benefits gameplay animators, cinematic animators, technical animators, and directors exploring faster pre-production workflows. Attendees should have a working understanding of traditional animation pipelines in Maya, Blender, or Unreal. No AI expertise is required, only familiarity with early-stage animation or motion prototyping.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall

session title: Flipping the Script: Overhauling Sucker Punch's Writing Pipeline for 'Ghost of Yōtei'
description: Before Sucker Punch had even finished shipping our previous game, Ghost of Tsushima, we knew we desperately needed to improve our writing processes for the next project. Integrating scripts required massive amounts of manual busywork, and writers had little context on how their work would actually be used in the game. So for Ghost of Yōtei, we took a big swing and decided to build a new tool from scratch which let writers write their scripts directly into mission rigging - hoping this could eliminate the busywork and get writers more closely integrated into the design process. As it turned out, not only did it end up massively helping both writers and designers, but the tool quickly expanded to help manage recording, auditing, script reviewing, and localization. So come learn what we did and why it worked, as well as where we stumbled along the way and what we learned from it.
takeaway: Attendees should leave with an understanding of how Sucker Punch improved our writing pipeline, what specific design decisions and processes worked or did not work, and why. Attendees should leave with concrete ideas for how they could improve their own writing processes and the ways tooling could help.
intended audience: Dialogue producers, writers, and tools programmers who are interested in the higher-level design of pipelines and tools for writing, rigging, and/or recording dialogue.

session title: From Developer to Publisher: The Benefits of a Japanese Studio's Decision to Pursue Small-Scale Projects
description:
Game development has become larger in scale of late, requiring more manpower and longer project spans. As a result, young game creators who join mid-to-large studio are finding it harder to obtain the full scale of experiences they need, and studios are facing more challenges to train the next generation of game creators.

One of the reasons CyberConnect2, a well-established game development studio in Japan, decided to undertake a self-publishing project was to counter this issue. Fuga: Melodies of Steel was born to help provide a wide range of experiences to newer developers.

This session will introduce the problems encountered and knowledge gained for using self-publishing as a training tool.
takeaway:
・The advantages for development studios to undertake small-scale projects
・Problems and solutions encountered when using small-scale projects to train development staff
・Tips and tricks for self-publishing
intended audience: Game industry professionals who focus on growth and the training of personnel, development studios currently considering self-publishing projects, and independent developers.

session title: Fully Destructible Bricks World: 10 Deadly Schemes to Render 10 Million Bricks at 60 FPS on an Average Machine (Presented by DisBrick.com)
description:
A game with 10 million bricks at 60 FPS, with thousands of different complex shapes, is now live, beta but effective & fun, and we want to share some tip, nothing ground-breaking, but incredibly efficient when consistently combined; here are the 10 deadly schemes:
1) KISS: Keep It Simple & Stupid
2) The world resolves around you, Grid Y
3) Stop data waste now
4) Oh my LOD !
5) Size matters: don't go too far…
6) Triangles were not created equal !
7) What happens in the GPU stays in the GPU
8) Unity is real !
9) Textures are pressure
10) DX11 is dead: long live DX12
takeaway: Use of grid, data compression, LOD with specific data structures and specific shaders, extensive use of compute shaders, differentiating types of triangles, dissociating brick moves & frame rendering from collision & occlusions, reorganizing mesh data, releasing texture pressure, taking advantage of unique Unity features (DOTS, SRP) and DX12 (broadcast, mesh shaders).
intended audience: All programmers, ideally intermediate to advanced.

session title: Gaming the System: Creating Your Own Path to a Narrative Design Career
description: Game narrative is an extremely difficult discipline to teach, learn, and break into. In this session, narrative designers Luca Azenaro-Acero and Zoe Calamar discuss their paths into the industry and the ways they prepared themselves for their narrative careers when their formal education could not. They provide ways to self-teach the most important skills you need to be a writer and/or narrative designer, strategies to network meaningfully, and how to wring every last drop out of the resources offered to industry hopefuls without working yourself to death. If you want to learn how to DIY an education and set yourself up for a successful game narrative career, this is the session for you!
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to develop the skills needed for a career in game narrative, with or without a formal education.
intended audience: This session is intended for anyone hoping to start a career as game writers and/or narrative designers, be they students who have never worked on a game before or industry professionals looking to pivot.
speakers: Zoe Calamar(Escape Velocity Entertainment), Luca Azenaro-Acero(Perfect Garbage)

session title: 'LifeAfter': AIGC Paradigm Change in Mobile Game Art Production
description: This session unveils a systematic approach to effectively implementing AI in game development production pipelines, addressing the gap between emerging AIGC technologies and practical applications. Drawing from real-world implementation in 'LifeAfter,' we'll explore principles for successful AI integration: establishing effective human-AI collaboration workflows, building value-oriented technical services, and creating cross-domain integration strategies. Through concrete case studies, we'll demonstrate how AI asset optimization toolchains and intelligent workflow transformations delivered over $6 million in cost savings and 50% efficiency improvements. Attendees will learn how to develop scientific evaluation frameworks for AI initiatives, overcome common implementation challenges, and build production-ready solutions across creative design, art production, asset management, and performance optimization. Join us to transform cutting-edge AI possibilities into substantial business value for your game development process.
takeaway: Attendees will learn practical methodologies for identifying AI implementation opportunities in game development, specific techniques for leveraging AI in art production (texture optimization, model enhancement, environment enrichment), how to build a new aigc workflow and frameworks for building data-driven measurement systems that quantifiably improve creative workflows and production efficiency.
intended audience: Target audience: Game development professionals, particularly artists and technical artists (TAs), plus anyone interested in AI applications in gaming. Those familiar with current market AI tools will benefit most.
speakers: Xiudong Zhang(NetEase Games), Zhaoxing Yang(NetEase Games)

session title: Localization Roundtable (Presented by the IGDA)
description: How can localization unlock new markets for both existing and emerging IPs, and what does it take to strategically plan a global game launch? This roundtable will explore how to integrate localization thoughtfully into development pipelines, leveraging market research to uncover an IP's cultural strengths across diverse player communities. We'll discuss when a text-only approach is sufficient and when audio or other forms of localization can meaningfully enhance player engagement and explore how a game might sensibly be localized into other languages after launch. Participants are invited to share their experiences and engage in a lively discussion on best practices and insights for making games resonate authentically across languages and regions.
takeaway: Attendees at each roundtable session will interact with colleagues facing similar issues through the whole localization process, learning how problems have been overcome by their peers and gaining specific knowledge in the localization area.
intended audience: Localization professionals from all departments and all areas of expertise; developers and publishers willing to understand how to plan and execute game localization and culturalization as a part of the development cycle; students approaching the game localization field.

session title: Signature Sounds: Crafting a Cohesive Musical Identity Across Games
description:
This session explores the creation of signature sounds within specific games; music that not only reflects a game's emotional core and cultural tone, but also evolves gracefully through expansions, seasonal content, and sequels. Rather than focusing on composer-wide branding, this talk looks in detail at project-level musical identity: what it is, how it's built, and how it adapts.
Speakers will walk through concepts and show musical cues, guiding attendees through four major chapters of the creative and collaborative process.
takeaway: Attendees will gain practical strategies for developing and adapting a game's signature musical identity. These include identifying foundational stylistic pillars, adapting music for evolving content while maintaining cohesion, aligning musical choices with narrative and IP direction, and collaborating effectively with developers and stakeholders. They'll also hear real-world case studies that show how successful (and unsuccessful) signature sounds were built, challenged, and refined over time.
intended audience: This session is intended for intermediate to advanced game composers, audio designers, and music directors. Attendees should have a working knowledge of game music composition and production. Those looking to refine their artistic voice, build a consistent portfolio, or adapt their style across projects will benefit most from this panel.
speakers: Edouard Brenneisen(EB Music LLC), Garry Schyman(Garry Schyman Productions)

session title: Supporting the Party: Five Ways to Help Others Move Forward
description:
Most leaders in game development were promoted into 'leadership' but rewarded for managing systems and tools. We then collapse 'leader,' 'manager,' 'people manager,' 'coach,' and 'mentor' into one fuzzy blob and default to whatever is most comfortable. Usually that's directing or fast advice. The result is overmanaged, under-led teams and people who never develop the autonomy we say we want.


This talk defines three roles leaders actually play - Leader, Manager, People Manager - and five skills we use to develop others - Directing, Teaching, Advising, Mentoring, Coaching. You'll see when each is appropriate, where most leaders get stuck, and how to choose your stance intentionally based on urgency, risk, context, and the other person's needs. We'll walk through concrete stories from AAA production and real-time roleplays based on audience questions, so you can walk back into your studio with a practical, usable toolkit instead of more leadership buzzwords.
takeaway: Attendees will gain the ability to articulate and self-assess the roles and skills they use to lead, and some ways to expand their range as leaders.
intended audience: Leaders in game dev who are early in their career or looking to understand and grow their leadership skillset.
speakers: Benjamin Carcich(Building Better Games), Ryan Scott(2K), Shaina Grade(Obsidian Entertainment), Serge Knystautas(Stealth Gaming Company)

session title: Sustaining Revenue in Single-Player Games with a Lean GaaS Strategy: What Works, What Doesn't
description: This talk explores the implementation of lean GaaS methodologies in a single-player AA title, using EVERSPACE 2 as a case study. It covers a clever mix of free and paid DLC to boost long-term engagement, strategic use of Early Access, platform relations, and community building through Twitch and YouTube. Key insights include pricing strategy, wishlist conversion, performance marketing, and revenue breakdowns backed by real KPIs and sales data. Join for a detailed postmortem for studios balancing depth, trust, and visibility without a dedicated live ops team.
takeaway:
What the audience will learn:
- How to apply a lean GaaS model to single-player games without requiring a live-ops team
- Best practices for combining free and paid content to gain attention, drive retention, and maximize revenue
- How to optimize pricing strategy and adapt to community feedback
- Ways to maximize Early Access as both a funding and marketing tool
- Interpreting and leveraging performance marketing KPIs
- Building long-term community trust through transparency and platform presence
intended audience: This talk is ideal for producers (junior to senior), marketing and publishing managers, community leads, and studio execs working on single-player or premium titles. It offers hands-on insights into structuring content roadmaps, sequencing free and paid DLC, managing pricing and platform visibility, and growing a loyal fanbase without a live-ops team. Also valuable for product managers, business developers, and experienced designers aiming to apply lean GaaS principles to narrative or AA-scale games.

session title: The Dialogue Design Panel: A Role with a Million Names
description:
Dialogue Design is a growing specialization within Audio Design for games and it is quickly becoming an essential addition to any audio team. Otherwise known as Voice Design or VO Design, it expands far beyond essential casting, recording and post-production work, including the design thinking behind it, the systems which drive it, and the planning and strategic thinking to make it a reality.

Dialogue Designers wield voice as a creative tool to its greatest effect and this panel of experts will explore the many aspects of Dialogue Design for games which are often overlooked and not commonly discussed. Join us to hear how panelists approach their work, harness the psychological power of voice to support a project's creative vision and learn about both the creative and technical approaches our speakers use to bring voice to life in games.
takeaway: Through this panel, attendees will learn how Dialogue Design fits into audio teams and the game development process, the challenges, and creative opportunities in this growing field of game audio.
intended audience: Audio/Sound Designers and students, Audio Directors/Leads/Designers, Voice/Dialogue Designers, Voice Directors and Voice Actors, anyone working closely with VO such as Producers, Narrative Designers, Localization, Game Designers, QA, Cinematics Designers, or anyone interested in what Dialogue Design contributes to game audio development.
speakers: Charles Pateman(Independent), Kareem Shuman(Insomniac Games), Monet Gardiner(SIE - Playstation - Creative Arts), Heather Plunkard-Serge(EA - Respawn)

session title: Understanding & Managing Stress
description: This session provides a look at the science behind stress, why we feel it, how much we can handle before it becomes detrimental to our health, and how to notice when stress is becoming too much. Safe In Our World supports attendees in recognizing their personal stress signatures and identifying ways to prevent stress from becoming a bigger issue or leading to burnout.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Women Building the Future of Games
description: Join fellow female game creators, founders, and industry leaders for an inspiring and welcoming meetup focused on shaping the future of games. Hosted by Supernova Games, this community gathering brings together developers across mobile, PC, and indie studios to connect, share experiences, and exchange ideas on creativity, leadership, and sustainable studio building.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Exercises that Play in Public: How to Design Collaborative Class Projects that Work Outside the Classroom
description:
Class exercises are rarely worth playing outside the classroom, but we can and should strive for them to be, especially in introductory classes! Working towards and creating a successful public-facing project creates more buy-in, instills a sense of mastery, and provides a valuable opportunity for students to see how their work performs in the wild.

Jason Eppink describes the exercise he developed for an introductory coding class–a collaboratively authored WarioWare-style adaptation of Pong–and ties it to prior projects he organized as a museum curator and designed as a participatory artist. He explains how these projects fit into a framework for designing collaborative class projects that are modular, accommodate a wide range of proficiency, and are achievable within a short time frame - with a core goal of making the game playable by a public audience.
takeaway: Attendees will learn a framework for developing collaborative, modular classroom exercises that succeed as public-facing projects.
intended audience: This session welcomes educators of all levels and teaching contexts. Educators who teach in project-oriented contexts may find this talk of special interest.

session title: Advanced Shader Delivery on Windows (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description: Shader compilation has long been a pain point for PC games, causing slow initial loads and disruptive stutter during gameplay. Advanced Shader Delivery introduces a new approach: distributing precompiled shaders through storefronts so players experience faster startup and smoother performance. In this session, we'll show how developers can leverage state object collection in their engines to automatically gather shader inputs, package them for submission through the Xbox PC app, and ensure updates stay in sync with game patches and driver changes. Come learn how this system streamlines deployment and improves the player experience across the PC ecosystem.
takeaway: Attendees hear about innovations in how shaders progress from source code to instructions running on a GPU through Direct3D 12, scaling to dozens of architectures including ones that don't even exist yet, without making customers wait for long loading screens.
intended audience: Developers with a high level understanding of GPU programming will benefit most, or people involved in packaging or distribution of games or other graphics-intensive content.

session title: From Film to Games: The Evolution of Performance Capture Technology (Presented by Qualisys)
description: A panel discussion bringing together four industry veterans at the intersection of motion capture and game cinematics to explore how real-time performance technology is transforming interactive storytelling. The session begins with the Qualisys origin story, revealing how 35+ years of motion capture innovation has positioned the company at the forefront of game development, and where it's headed in supporting the evolving needs of game cinematics. The session then transitions to an expert panel discussion featuring a cinematic director from a major game studio, a virtual production innovator bridging film and game workflows, and a mocap software developer whose tools have become industry standard across AAA productions. These seasoned professionals explore how real-time performance capture is transforming game cinematics, democratizing technology that was once accessible only to the largest studios and shaping the next generation of interactive storytelling.
takeaway: Attendees gain practical understanding of current motion capture workflows for game cinematics, learn how real-time technologies are reshaping production pipelines, and discover where industry experts see performance capture headed as interactive storytelling demands increase. The panel provides actionable insights for studios evaluating mocap technology and workflows for cinematic game development.
intended audience: This session targets game developers working in cinematics, animation, and narrative design, particularly technical directors, cinematics leads, pipeline engineers, and producers evaluating motion capture solutions. Game development students and educators in animation programs will benefit from understanding professional workflows. No prerequisite knowledge required, though familiarity with game development pipelines is helpful.
speakers: Ander Bergstrom(Qualisys), Vivek Kandasamy(Film Engineers), Alastair Macleod(Peel Software Development LLC), Rob Fernandez(Gearbox Software)

session title: Gaming AI at Xbox: Responsible Innovation for Gameplay Experiences (Presented by Microsoft Xbox)
description: At Xbox, we're committed to ensuring our innovations in AI are designed responsibly and continue to serve players, developers, and our community. Xbox has been listening and learning as we implement AI in ways that help us enhance gameplay experiences for players. This session will explore key learnings around how players have been interacting with Gaming Copilot (Beta) so far – what's worked, what hasn't, what we're exploring - and beyond. Attendees will gain insights into how AI could shape the way game creators think about gameplay experiences in the future, how AI helps solve design challenges while creating great player experiences, and how developers can get involved in shaping the future of AI in gaming in a responsible way.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insights into how AI could shape the way game creators think about gameplay experiences in the future, how AI helps solve design challenges while creating great player experiences, and how developers can get involved in shaping the future of AI in gaming in a responsible way.
intended audience: This session is for a general developer and game creator audience. Gain insights into how AI could shape the way we think about gameplay experiences in the future, how AI helps solve design challenges, and how developers can get involved in shaping the future of AI in gaming.
speakers: Sonali Yadav(Microsoft Xbox), Haiyan Zhang(Microsoft Corportation)

session title: Music & Players: The Data, The Art, and Impact
description: Music is one of the most transformative elements in game design, capable of shaping player experiences in profound ways. Top executives from Universal Music Publishing Group will delve into the distinct roles of licensed music, original scores, and production tracks, showcasing how each can amplify storytelling, enhance gameplay dynamics, and forge lasting emotional connections. Through real-world examples and compelling data, the panel will reveal how music drives emotion, reinforces identity, and leaves an indelible mark on how players perceive and remember games. Whether you're a developer, designer, or producer, this session will offer invaluable insights into leveraging sound to create deeper immersion and make your games truly unforgettable.
takeaway: Attendees gain a clear framework for using licensed music, original score, and production tracks to shape emotion, guide player perception, and strengthen game identity. The session provides actionable techniques and offers concrete tools for aligning sound with gameplay tone to create culturally relevant experiences.
intended audience: This session is open to anyone with an interest in music and its role in games, including developers, creators, and industry leaders. The content is accessible to all experience levels and focuses on understanding how music shapes emotion, identity, and player connection.
location: Blue Shield of California Theater, YBCA
speakers: Sebastian Platner(Universal Music Publishing Group), Fran Goncalves(Universal Music Group), Mandy Gabriel(Universal Music Publishing Group), Beau Ballew(Universal Production Music)

session title: State of RTX Rendering in Unreal Engine 5 (Presented by NVIDIA)
description: The NVIDIA Branch of Unreal Engine 5 (NvRTX) continues to push the boundaries of visual fidelity and performance. Join us for a deep dive into the latest updates, including RTX Mega Geometry, ReSTIR PT, and RTX Hair, plus an exclusive preview of upcoming features. Whether you are an artist, a programmer, or a technical generalist, this session provides the insights and practical guidance needed to master the latest NvRTX technologies.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a greater understanding of the features of NvRTX. The session will cover the progress we've made in 2025 and look ahead to new features in 2026.
intended audience: Graphics Programmers; Technical Artists
speakers: Richard Cowgill(NVIDIA), Kelsey Mihalatos(NVIDIA), Evan Hart(NVIDIA)

session title: Building a Global IP as an Indie Studio: Making 'The Pirate Queen'
description: *The Pirate Queen* began as a small, ambitious VR game and is evolving into an Emmy-nominated, multi-format franchise spanning games, publishing, TV, and audio. In this talk, executive producers Eloise Singer and Dave Ranyard break down how an indie studio can build and scale a world-class IP, sharing practical creative and production lessons for developers looking to grow their universes internationally.
speakers: Eloise Singer(Singer Studios), Dave Ranyard(Dream Reality Interactive)

session title: The Tinkering Process: Crafting the Steampunk World of 'Brass & Embers' (Presented by Questborne Studios)
description:
Questborne Studios presents Brass & Embers, a tabletop RPG set in a land reshaped by rapid technological change, with factions battling for the substance that powers it, sinister mechanical monsters that roam through the wilderness, and the mysterious remains of a forgotten civilization lost to time. This talk explores how the team built a game centered on hope, community, and rebellion during times of uncertainty.
Questborne shares the creative process behind developing Brass & Embers' mechanics, shaping a world steeped in political tension, and translating narrative-driven systems into clear, intuitive play. The session highlights the unexpected differences between designing video games and TTRPGs, the lessons learned through iteration and playtesting, and how the studio has been preparing for the game's upcoming Kickstarter launch in April 2026.
Attendees are invited to step into Calderwick and learn what it takes to bring a new world—and a new RPG—to life.
takeaway: Attendees gain insight into Questborne Studios' approach to building their TTRPG Brass & Embers—from designing mechanics that reinforce themes of tech upheaval and political tension to refining systems through playtesting. They leave with practical lessons on cross-medium design, collaborative storytelling, and preparing an indie RPG for a crowdfunding campaign.
intended audience: This session is for game designers, indie studio heads, writers, and developers interested in worldbuilding, narrative-centered systems, or shifting between video game and tabletop design. It's ideal for anyone curious about building game mechanics connected to an emotionally resonant settings; and the realities of indie development ahead of launch.

session title: Designer Notes Live Podcast Recording - Frank Lantz
description: Join us for a live recording of the Designer Notes podcast. Soren Johnson, founder of Mohawk Games, sits down with noted designers to find out why we make games by examining their careers as a whole. This episode features a conversation with Frank Lantz.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: GDC Side Quest Podcast Live: Festival of Gaming Happy Hour
description: Grab your coffee (or your favorite 4PM pick-me-up) and join us at the ! Hosted by Game Developer's Bryant Francis and Alissa McAloon, along with GDC Festival of Gaming's Sam Smith and Beth Elderkin, this session is all about celebrating the games we love and the incredible industry that brings them to life. Whether you're here for the industry insights or just want to vibe with fellow game professionals, this is the perfect way to wind down your GDC day.
takeaway: Attendees will leave with new connections and a renewed appreciation for the creativity and passion that fuels the games industry.
intended audience: Perfect for anyone who loves games, loves talking about games, or just wants to kick back and enjoy a fun, casual conversation with industry friends.
speakers: Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming), Alissa McAloon(Game Developer), Bryant Francis(Game Developer), Sam Smith(GDC Festival of Gaming)

session title: BumbleBear Arcade Lab
description: Play new arcade prototypes from BumbleBear, creators of Killer Queen. Then join us for a hands-on hardware show-and-tell with joysticks, buttons, I/O boards, and other arcade components.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: 32nd Annual IASIG Town Hall
description: Want to improve game audio workflow or the professional audio tools available to you? Join fellow game audio educators, sound designers, composers, audio programmers, and other audio professionals at the Interactive Audio Special Interest Group (IASIG) Town Hall. Get an update on current IASIG efforts, and share your own ideas, suggestions, and recommendations for improving the state of the art in audio for interactive media. If you do audio, come and be heard. The IASIG works as a Special Interest Group in both The MIDI Association and the International Game Developers Association representing Game Audio Developers in those organizations.
speakers: Dave Berol(Surfaceink Product Design & Development), Pat Scandalis(moForte Inc), Steve Horowitz(Game Audio Institute and SFSU), Kurt Heiden(Independent), Alexander Brandon(Funky Rustic, LLC)

session title: Animation Roundtable Day 3
description:
The Animation Roundtable aims to bring all disciplines together under a common cause- how to best implement and improve the field of animation in game development. The following questions are among those that will be addressed:

Who should be directing the animation in a game? Who owns each part of the animation pipeline after export? How can all disciplines work together better, to bring the best looking and feeling animation to their game? What workflow frustrations have been experienced and how were they resolved? What new technologies are available or coming online that can help animators excel at their jobs, and what challenges will those technologies bring? What is your best tip or trick that you think everyone needs to hear? What mistakes have you made that you think others can learn from? How can we all give and take feedback better, from all disciplines?

In addition, this roundtable discussion will focus on helping newcomers by dedicating the last part of each session to them, encouraging questions to help them get prepared (or jump-started!) in their animation careers.
intended audience: Audience could include animators, designers, technical animators, producers, and programmers who are excited to participate in a productive discussion about animation and team building in games.
speakers: Euna Park(That's No Moon), Shayna Moon(CD Projekt RED)

session title: Art Leadership Roundtable Day 3
description: The art leadership roundtable is an open discussion forum for topics concerning the leadership of creative teams. All attendees are welcome to learn from industry veterans as we all seek to navigate the ubiquitous challenges of inspiring teams to their best work, while navigating both change and uncertainty. Come to listen. Come to share. Join us as we learn from each other and build better creative cultures.
takeaway: The roundtable is an open forum for discussion of topics related to creative leadership. This includes setting direction, managing expectations, building healthy teams, as well as developing resilience and adaptability. The moderator manages the flow of topics as they are surfaced with the possibility that notes will be shared with attendees after GDC.
intended audience: This roundtable is open to all attendees. Most takeaways are frequently targeted at experienced developers who are looking to level up their managerial skills or build stronger leadership networks. Veterans are strongly encouraged to attend to share their wisdom and experiences.

session title: Common Dev Mistakes That Lead to Poor Localization by Agencies
description: Mediocre localization can erode immersion and make a game feel less polished - even when translations aren't technically wrong. Many issues start in development long before text reaches a translation agency. In this talk, Sven Lohse, Technical Director at ROCKFISH Games, uses examples from EVERSPACE 2 to show how localization-aware workflows can prevent common pitfalls. He will give developers, UI designers, writers, and project managers practical steps to improve quality across languages, strengthen player immersion, and help teams use their localization budgets more efficiently.
takeaway: Developers will gain insight into how language structure, cultural context, and translator workflows impact localization. They'll learn to design UI and manage dynamic text for better results, and explore how evaluating translation quality can encourage agencies to go beyond delivering just 'good enough.'
intended audience: Great localization is only possible when developers, UI designers, narrative designers, and project managers know what to look out for.

session title: From a Naughty Dog to a Wildflower: The Fears, Failures, and Freedoms Found
