description: A quick recap of real-time volumetric cloud rendering techniques will be followed by a behind the scenes look into BL4's open world clouds system. Gearbox will discuss the issues involved in shipping an open world cloud system, which responds to localized dynamic weather, is art directable through custom tools, and made use of standard Unreal Engine features. Practical examples will be used to describe the solutions, and lessons learned along the way. The speaker is an Art Director Director with 17+ years of game industry experience.
takeaway: An understanding of one AAA game's approach to creating and shipping a dynamic cloud system, using industry standard techniques. Through practical examples, attendees will learn about industry standard cloud rendering concepts and techniques, how these these can be implemented in Unreal Engine, and strategies to make clouds art directable, dynamic and performant.
intended audience: This presentation is targeted at artists, technical artists, rendering engineers, and anyone with an interest in real-time clouds, atmosphere and weather. Knowledge of Unreal Engine is beneficial but not necessary.

session title: Designing the Ideal Level Design Portfolio: What Hiring Teams Actually Look For
description:
This session explores how level designers can build strong, effective portfolios that clearly communicate design thinking, ownership, and measurable impact. Drawing from real hiring experience across AAA and indie development, the talk breaks down what hiring teams actually evaluate when reviewing level design portfolios and why many otherwise capable designers fail to stand out.


The presentation examines the difference between showing completed environments and communicating decision-making. It outlines how to structure compelling case studies, how to present greybox and shipped work with clarity, and how to demonstrate iteration, collaboration, and problem-solving within production constraints. Attendees will learn how to frame their contributions in a way that signals readiness for professional development environments.


The session also addresses common portfolio weaknesses, including overreliance on visuals, lack of documented process, unclear ownership, and poor structural organization. By the end of the talk, attendees will have a practical framework for evaluating and restructuring their own portfolios to better align with what hiring teams actually look for.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to structure a level design portfolio that clearly communicates design intent, ownership, and problem-solving. The session provides practical guidance on building strong case studies, presenting iteration effectively, and improving overall clarity and organization. Designers will leave with a concrete framework for showcasing their work in a way that aligns with real hiring expectations.
intended audience: This session is designed for aspiring, junior, and experienced level designers who want to strengthen their portfolios for hiring or career advancement. While a basic understanding of level design principles is helpful, the content is accessible to students, working professionals, and those transitioning into level design from other disciplines.

session title: Killer Art Portfolio or Portfolio Killer Part 2: Portfolio Reviews
description:
As a follow up to the panel 'Killer Portfolio or Portfolio Killer' this session is an opportunity for artists looking for advice to speak one-on-one with an industry professional. Immediately after the panel, reviews will be done in 10 minute increments giving attendees an opportunity to get constructive critiques and seek out advice without the stress of an interview. Ideally this session will last 3 hours to accommodate the number of people that have attended in previous years.



Whether it is your first job in the industry or you are looking to switch jobs, getting a job as an artist in games has never been more competitive. This session follows the 'Killer Portfolio or Portfolio Killer' panel discussion and will focus on one-on-one portfolio reviews from the panelists. Participants will receive constructive critiques and advice to give their portfolios an edge in this competitive market without the pressure of an interview.
takeaway: Attendees will have the opportunity to get direct feedback on their portfolios and come away with a better understanding of how to construct a portfolio that will get noticed and help them land their dream job.
intended audience: This session is ideal for anyone looking to create a portfolio to break into the industry as a game artist, educators, or those already in the industry looking to make a job change.
speakers: Jordan DeVries(Respawn Entertainment), Darren Bacon(Bungie), Eli Tuttle(Bit Reactor), Jeff Skalski(Yellow Brick Games Inc.), Jeff A. Johnson(inXile Entertainment), Ashley Sparling(K9 Digital), Greg Knight(Lucasfilm Games), Cutlar Nordyke III(Keywords Studios), Gary Riley(Raven Software), Greg Foertsch(Bit Reactor), Rembert Montald(Lightspeed LA), Sarah LeBlanc(Bit Reactor), Jade Law(Wardog Studios), Jeff Parrott(Blizzard), Inmar Salvatier(Maxis), Gaurav Mathur(gamedevmap), Piero Macgowan(Bit Reactor)

session title: The Future of Game Knowledge: Search Less. Ship More.
description:
At Ubisoft, knowledge is scattered across Confluence, Jira, GitLab, video platforms, and dozens of team silos. This fragmentation makes it harder to ramp up, debug, plan, or even stay aligned. So we built DocGPT: an internal, production-grade assistant designed to help teams search, understand, and reuse Ubisoft's knowledge using natural language.
Our goal was simple: reduce context switching and make internal knowledge more accessible. We co-developed our tech with production teams. Then it scaled. Fast!
Today, DocGPT supports workflows across multiple Ubisoft productions. It helps testers generate test cases from Design docs. It assists new developers with step-by-step guidance or by explaining custom pipeline code. It even supports managers by surfacing project status and Jira insights in plain English.
In this talk we'll share honest lessons from our rollout — from organizational acrobatics to surprising wins that reshaped our roadmap.
takeaway:
In this talk, you'll learn:
• How we built a secure, modular RAG architecture to serve real-time answers from game production data
• How we tackled challenges around proprietary knowledge , assistant customization, and knowledge freshness
• Take away a practical roadmap to help you build and deploy your own GenAI assistant in a production environment
• What worked, what broke, and how we adapted the mindset around GenAI to address long-endured pain points
intended audience: This talk is for producers, technical directors and other game leaders who are interested in reducing time spent in searching for information to reinvest their time in building games and shipping faster. No prior machine learning knowledge is required, but familiarity with AI/ML concepts is helpful.

session title: The Player-Centric Engine: CRM's Role in Enabling Cross-Functional Growth for 'Candy Crush Saga'
description:
Candy Crush Saga reaches millions of players daily, but staying loved in a crowded mobile games market takes more than new levels and features. This session reveals how King's CRM at Candy Crush Saga evolved from a messaging tool into a full lifecycle system, built in partnership with Product, Machine Learning, and other cross-functional teams.

You'll hear how CRM drives engagement, retention, and brand love - not just through messages, but also by designing personalized in-game experiences. From adaptive onboarding journeys tailored to player types to large-scale lifecycle campaigns, CRM has become a foundational part of how we design and deliver meaningful moments. This talk offers a blueprint for live games looking to grow and evolve with their players.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to build a player lifecycle system that integrates CRM, product, Machine Learning, and other cross-functional teams. They will also learn how to shift from one-off campaigns to contextual, personalized player journeys, and how to measure CRM's real impact on engagement, retention, and emotional connection with the game.
intended audience: This session is for live ops leads, product managers, lifecycle marketers, and game designers. It's ideal for teams facing increased competition and looking to reframe player strategies - enabling faster design, testing, and iteration of engagement systems that go beyond short-term tactics and campaign-based thinking.

session title: Zenith: Diffusion Model-Driven Map Generation
description: This session introduces Zenith, a hybrid pipeline that automates the creation of multi-layered top-down maps from 3D environments. By combining procedural geometry extraction with a multi-encoder ControlNet architecture, Zenith generates walkable areas and stylized layers—line art, shadows, and highlights—using depth, normals, and detail conditioning. By unifying multiple diffusion models, Zenith delivers visually consistent results without requiring large training datasets. We will explore how it enhances efficiency, share insights from artist feedback, and discuss practical lessons for integrating diffusion models into game development.
takeaway: Attendees will explore how Zenith streamlines the creation of stylized top-down maps from 3D environments by combining procedural geometry processing with a custom multi-encoder diffusion model architecture. The session will detail how Zenith generates walkable areas and artistic layers—such as line art, shadows, and highlights—using fine-tuned ControlNet models, while preserving visual consistency and reducing manual effort. Technical participants will gain insights into training strategies, multi-modal conditioning, and architectural design choices that enable high-quality outputs with limited data. The talk will also share practical lessons from integrating Zenith into production workflows, emphasizing how automation can enhance creative efficiency without compromising artistic control.
intended audience: Technical artists, tool developers, software engineers, graphics engineers, applied scientists, machine learning engineers

session title: Making Economics Playable: Designing 'Hidden Champion' to Teach Business Thinking (Presented by German Pavilion)
description: Hidden Champion makes economics playable: markets, pricing, logistics—your decisions sharpen economic thinking. You take charge of a company's international expansion and compete for regional market share. Every month you balance capacity, workforce costs, warehouse limits, product quality, and cash flow. Demand is shaped by marketing and sales offices, while fulfillment depends on transport choices (truck, rail, ship, air) and route planning across real-world cities. Higher quality can win premium customers but reduces factory output and increases handling risk, so you choose where to differentiate and where to scale. Disruptions like strikes, accidents, or bottlenecks force contingency planning and highlight resilience. Transparent numbers—margins, transport costs, penalties, and market share—let players test hypotheses, learn from outcomes, and iterate strategies. It's a sandbox for practical business trade-offs, turning abstract economic concepts into decisions you can measure. Players quickly see why pricing power, liquidity, and logistics efficiency matter—and how small decisions compound over time.
takeaway: Attendees learn how Hidden Champion turns expert economic concepts into measurable player decisions: setting prices against capacity and labor costs, allocating warehouse space, choosing truck/rail/ship/air routes across real-world cities, and reacting to strikes or transport disruptions. They see how margins, penalties, cash flow, and market share compound over time.
intended audience: This session benefits indie and AA game developers, producers, designers, and educators who want to teach economics through game systems. Attendees learn how Hidden Champion turns markets, pricing, and logistics into understandable gameplay and measurable learning outcomes. No prerequisite knowledge is required beyond basic familiarity with games and production pipelines.
speakers: Andreas Hinze(Infoboard Europe GmbH), Ankit Dhillon(BAD Spiele Studio UG)

session title: Multiplayer Game Dynamics: Behind the Scenes of the New York Times' New Game: 'CrossPlay'
description:
The New York Times Games team presents a technical breakdown of CrossPlay, a real-time multiplayer word game built as a standalone mobile app rather than on a traditional game engine. The session explains how CrossPlay's architecture supports fast matchmaking, responsive gameplay, and large-scale social interaction using native mobile frameworks. Speakers walk through the platform's guiding product pillars—Let Them Play, Learn & Grow, and Smarter Social—and how those principles translate into concrete engineering decisions.

Topics include fanout-based real-time systems, performance-driven UI design, AI-assisted learning tools, and a production-ready automated content moderation service. Attendees gain insight into how disciplined release processes, data-informed iteration, and trust-focused systems enable a modern multiplayer experience that balances speed, depth, and safety at scale.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to design real-time multiplayer systems using fanout architectures, manage performance as a user-facing constraint, integrate AI-driven player feedback tools, and roll out moderated social features safely. Concrete examples include matchmaking limits, stateless moderation services, and native mobile interaction patterns.
intended audience: Attendees learn how clear engagement pillars guide system design, from fast game loops and moderated social play. They gain practical approaches for tying real-time infrastructure, coaching tools, and trust systems directly to retention, progression, and long-term player value.
location: Monetization & Player Engagement Stage, South Hall
speakers: Shafik Quoraishee(The New York Times), Amra Khullar(The New York Times)

session title: The New Cinema
description:
Step into the future of entertainment with Neville Spiteri, CEO of Wevr, he recently unveiled the groundbreaking XR experience, . Neville will share the journey behind creating the world's first free-roam, location-based XR adaptation of Antoine de Saint-Exupéry's beloved masterpiece. Neville is dedicated to showing how immersing in virtual worlds can be fun, fulfilling and transformative.

Discover how Wevr, in partnership with HTC VIVERSE, has reimagined storytelling by blending cutting-edge technology with the timeless magic of . From walking across asteroids to standing under a star-filled Sahara sky, this immersive experience transforms the way families connect with stories—offering a shared, embodied adventure unlike anything seen before.

Join Neville to explore how Wevr's vision of interactive storytelling is shaping the 'New Cinema'—a format that is physical, social, and emotional - designed for spaces where people already gather.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a glimpse into the broader implications of XR storytelling and how projects like are paving the way for more immersive, location-based experiences in cities around the world. They will also discover the concept of 'The New Cinema' - entertainment that is physical, social, and emotional.
intended audience: This is intended for game developers and anyone interested in immersive/XR

session title: GDC Side Quest Podcast Live: Gabe Cuzzillo
description: Head to the GDC Commons for a live recording of the GDC Side Quest Podcast with Gabe Cuzzillo, one of the creative minds behind the IGF-nominated game . In this session, Gabe will share his journey as an indie developer, discuss the challenges and triumphs of creating unique games, and explore the growing trend of developers pursuing smaller, passion-driven projects.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insights into the indie development process, the challenges of publishing, and the opportunities that come with breaking away from traditional industry structures.
intended audience: Perfect for aspiring indie developers, industry professionals curious about the indie scene, and anyone interested in the creative process behind unique games.
speakers: Gabe Cuzzillo(Independent), Beth Elderkin(GDC Festival of Gaming)

session title: 'Dead by Daylight': Reflecting on 10 Years of Terror
description:
Join us for an exclusive panel discussion hosted by Dork (Sr Community Manager –Behaviour Interactive Inc.) featuring Mathieu Côté (Head of Partnerships), Dave Richard (Senior Creative Director), Sarah Robinson (Associate Art Director) and Eric Pope (Community Management Director) as they reflect on the first decade of Dead by Daylight. From its humble beginnings to its rise as a global gaming phenomenon, hear firsthand stories, insights, and behind-the-scenes perspectives on the game's evolution, design challenges, and enduring community impact.

Whether you're a longtime fan or curious about the making of a hit multiplayer experience, this is your chance to go beyond the screen and discover the journey that shaped a decade of thrills.
takeaway: Attendees will get a glimpse behind the scenes of how Dead by Daylight came to be within Behaviour Interactive, from a small indie passion project to the 'House of Horror' it is today. The audience will learn from the challenges and successes that made Dead by Daylight part of the most well-established horror multiplayer games of today.
intended audience: This talk is suitable for anyone in the gaming industry interested in learning more about the life of a live-service game from making game design decisions to signing licenses and prioritizing a strong community relationship. Whether you work in game design, community management, marketing… or you are simply passionate about video game stories, there's something for you.
speakers: Mathieu Cote(Behaviour Interactive Inc.), Dave Richard(Behaviour Interactive Inc.), Eric Pope(Behaviour Interactive Inc.), Dork Dork(Behaviour Interactive Inc.), Sarah Robinson(Behaviour Interactive Inc.)

session title: Experimental Games Showcase
description: The Experimental Game Workshop Showcase, which featured games like 'Baba Is You', 'The Longing', 'Braid', 'Untitled Goose Game' and 'Katamari Damacy', is back for its 24th year at GDC! In this fast-paced, game-packed session, panelists will showcase a selection of surprising and intriguing prototypes made by innovation-minded game developers from all over the world. By demonstrating games that defy conventions and traditions in search of new genres and ideas, this session aims to ignite the imagination of all game makers. Come see what's happening in the world of Experimental Games, and be inspired!
speakers: Andrea Lucco Borlera(Independent), Andy Wallace(Hypnogenesis), Allen Riley(Hypnogenesis), Laureline Chiapello(NAD-UQAC (Université du Québec à Chicoutimi)), Isaac Cohen(Cabbibo), Nathan Leigh(cirqueSaw), Nicole Orabona(cirqueSaw), Tristan Hilaire(KMLO), Carol Mertz(Independent), Stefano Sanitate(Santa Ragione), Liz Ryerson(Independent), Cí Malone(The Crayon Eating Company), Bjarke Alexander Larsen(Independent), Mark LaCroix(Noble Robot), Cory O'Brien(Independent), Tommy Maranges(Independent), Hanwen Xu(Games for my Computer LLC), Hatim Benhsain(Games for my Computer LLC), Ben Landon Elliot(Bonk Interactive)

session title: G.A.N.G. Town Hall
description: Celebrating G.A.N.G.'s 24-year anniversary, G.A.N.G. President Brian Schmidt will discuss the organization's activities over the past year including the G.A.N.G Scholars program, Student Game Music and Sound Competition, live meetups, webinars, GRAMMY submission assistance and more. There will be an open discussion with the audience on how to further improve the future of the organization and to look at what the future holds. This meeting is open to anyone interested in promoting excellence and advancements in interactive audio. Over 3000 people representing over 25 countries around the world have joined G.A.N.G. since its inception in 2002.

session title: How to Advocate for Accessibility on Your Team: A Toolkit for Developers and Marketing Pros
description: Marketing campaigns are about building hype – revealing just enough information to pique fans' interest, while saving key details to share at high-impact brand moments. But gamers with disabilities can't look forward to a game's launch until they know whether or not they'll be able to play it. This conversation between Ubisoft Senior Corporate Communications Manager, Jessica Roache, and Disabled Accessibility Consultant, Steve Saylor, will seek to encourage developers and marketing teams that there are clear benefits to highlighting a game's accessibility early – and creating a successful, inclusive marketing campaign. For teams who haven't yet explored accessibility, this talk will provide actionable advice on how to pitch the business case for accessibility to their leadership.
takeaway: Attendees will take away specific next steps on how to be an accessibility advocate and drive real change within their organization, no matter their job title. They'll learn tips to deal with naysayers and improve accessibility in their sphere of influence, from creating an accessible presentation to pushing for an accessibility improvement in-game.
intended audience: The session will be directed toward people at any stage of their career, no prior knowledge needed. Content is most useful for those who work cross-functionally on or with business or marketing teams.

session title: Lessons Learned in Running a Game the Hard Way: How Blizzard Revitalized 'Overwatch'
description: Running a live-service game is one of the toughest challenges in development. In this session, Aaron Keller, Game Director of Overwatch, shares the lessons Blizzard learned the hard way about sustaining and evolving a large-scale live game. Drawing from Overwatch's transformation, Keller explores how Team 4 analyzed the game's position in the market, identified the needs of a mastery-driven audience, and turned experimentation into a repeatable process for change. He discusses how bold, analysis-driven updates, improved reward systems, and consistent communication helped rebuild player confidence and deepen engagement. The talk offers concrete insights for any developer managing a live-service title — from reading your players to turning every update into a learning opportunity.
takeaway: Attendees learn actionable methods for running and evolving live-service titles. Aaron Keller details Blizzard's approach to analyzing player behavior, using in-game experimentation as a design tool, and aligning updates with audience motivations. The session offers real examples from Overwatch on balancing innovation with consistency to sustain engagement and system health over time.
intended audience: This session targets developers, producers, and designers working on live-service or community-driven games. Attendees benefit from understanding how large-scale teams sustain engagement and adapt to player feedback. No specific prerequisite knowledge is required—only experience or interest in systems design, live operations, or long-term product development.

session title: Analytics and Data Science Roundtable (Presented by the IGDA)
description: Data science and analytics have transformed how games are designed and supported, but meaningful insight takes more than dashboards and KPIs. This roundtable explores how we connect data to decisions: framing the right questions, balancing quantitative and qualitative insight, and ensuring analytics serve creativity rather than constrain it. Participants from industry, academia, and research will share challenges, compare approaches, and discuss how to make data more actionable, reliable, and aligned with design intent. This session is hosted by the IGDA Analytics and Data Science SIG.
takeaway: Participants will gain a deeper understanding of how analytics can inform creative and strategic decisions in game development. They will leave with practical ideas for asking better questions, designing more meaningful metrics, and communicating data-driven insights effectively across disciplines.
intended audience:
This session is suitable for:
Game analytics and data science professionals seeking peer discussion and new perspectives.
Designers, producers, and researchers looking to apply data more effectively in development.
Students and academics studying the intersection of data, player behavior, and game design.

session title: Game Audio Programmer Roundtable Day 3
takeaway: Attendees will have the opportunity to discuss audio programming, and to ask and answer questions with other people who are interested and knowledgeable: their colleagues.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended for anybody who is interested in game audio programming, in any discipline. Experts are encouraged to attend all three days to ask and answer questions, and everybody is welcome to come and join in the discussion - or just listen!
speakers: Guy Somberg(Blizzard Entertainment), Tomas Neumann(CD Projekt Red)

session title: It Takes a Village: Live at GDC Day 3
description:
'It Takes A Village' is a chat show now in its second season (hosted on YouTube) where we shine a light on the people who make our games, on themes of collaboration, community and co-advocacy. It's a love letter to our unsung heroes, a thank you to those who uplift others, inspiration to those interested in starting a career in games, and provides unique industry insight. Host Samantha Béart will chat with guests about how they got into games and the details of their craft, then moderate an informal audience Q&A. It is a wholesome fireside chat where developers can recognise and learn from those who've helped the industry, as well as share advice on navigating what can often be a challenging industry to work in.

Hosted by Samantha Béart, a classically trained and BAFTA-nominated actor, best known for their role as Karlach in 'Baldur's Gate 3'.
takeaway: Attendees will gain deep industry knowledge in not only the specialisation of each guest, but also their perspective of the games industry as a whole. They'll feel inspired to nurture their own communities in whatever form those communities might take.
intended audience: This is for anyone who's interested in intimate, behind-the-scenes chats with games industry professionals.
speakers: Samantha Beart(Independent), Jackie I. Ko(Broadsword Online Games)

session title: Technical Artists Roundtable (20th Year)!!! Day 3
description: The role of a technical artists has grown from a clean-up artist and data wrangler to someone who is in charge of complex content creation pipelines and asset tracking systems. Increasingly, the lines between programmer and artist are blurring for technical artists. Tool creation, scripting, shader creation, working with both programmers and artists, and managing workflow are all skills required of a modern technical artist. This roundtable brings together technical artists from across the industry to share their knowledge, experiences, and tips for being the best at their jobs.
takeaway: Participants will learn what common skills effective technical artists share and how to apply those skills to their specific company. Participants will leave with a better understanding of tech art and the value it will bring to their projects.
intended audience: This roundtable is open to technical artists who strive daily to improve workflow, increase efficiency, and maintain art production pipelines. Artists and programmers are welcome too, as their thoughts and expectations will help define the roles of all technical artists.

session title: Tools Roundtable Day 3: Build
description:
The Tools Technology Roundtables focus on discussion of best practices, novel techniques, and technical trade-offs for building game development tools and services. Game development tools are constantly evolving, so come to share what works (and what doesn't) at your studio, and listen to others' take on how to keep their creative teams producing awesome games.

Day 3: 'Build' focuses on processing assets through to the game in all its forms: build systems, caching, distribution, scalability, optimization, disc mastering and patching.
takeaway:
A better understanding of current techniques and trade-offs for building game development tools, plug-ins, pipelines, and services.
Advice in selection between various techniques, languages, and frameworks.
Advice in handling increasingly complex teams, asset pipelines, and requirements.
intended audience: Engineers, technical artists, and technical producers.

session title: 'Borderlands 4' Launch Load Testing
description: Gearbox Software shares how its online engineering team prepared the backend platform for the launch of Borderlands 4 through large-scale, full-system load testing. Rather than validating individual features in isolation, the team built a unified test that simulated real player behavior across all online systems, from matchmaking to cross-platform play. This session details how the team generated realistic data, leveraged AWS and Serverless technology, and balanced test fidelity against cost and complexity. Attendees learn how Gearbox overcame late-stage technical challenges such as spot instance terminations and adopting Serverless Redis, and how these efforts led to both platform stability and improved team readiness. The talk provides practical takeaways for studios looking to conduct high-fidelity load testing at scale and highlights how holistic testing can strengthen both technical confidence and cross-team collaboration before launch.
takeaway: Attendees learn how Gearbox engineers built a unified, game-wide load test to simulate live player behavior and scale AWS and Serverless technologies. The session provides concrete methods for designing, executing, and optimizing load tests capable of supporting seven-digit CCU titles and ensuring backend stability under real-world launch conditions.
intended audience: This session targets engineers and technical leaders interested in backend load testing for large-scale online games. Attendees benefit from practical insights into system design, cloud scalability, and load test architecture. Familiarity with common cloud concepts, such as AWS services, serverless architecture, and distributed systems, helps maximize understanding but is not required.

session title: Game Education Case Study Microtalks
description: This session has a series of microtalks from educators working in different disciplines that make up games, including game development, technical art, design, and game studies. Come learn about how to get students to write better papers, why learning about old game consoles still has value for our programmers, encouraging 3D artists to explore technical art, and how to have students meaningfully connect with community partners. There is something here for everyone, as presented by Seth Hudson (George Mason University), Tom Tesch (Howest University of Applied Sciences), Jingtian Li (University of the Incarnate Word), and MJ Johns (UC Santa Cruz).
takeaway: Attendees will come away with concrete recommendations for course and curriculum design across a wide array of disciplines.
intended audience: Any game educator should find this session thought provoking and worth attending for the conversations and connections it inspires.
speakers: Jingtian Li(University of the Incarnate Word), Seth Hudson(George Mason University), Tom Tesch(DAE - Howest), MJ Johns(University of California Santa Cruz)

session title: Getting Your Foot in the Door Part 2: Getting Hired in Japan
description:
We presented at GDC 2025 and gave an overview between the differences in the working environment and company culture between Japan and the West. This is a follow-up panel that focuses on practical advice for raising the listeners' chances at being hired in Japan.

The main points will discuss what Japanese companies look at when they screen applications, what they look for in portfolios, and what questions are commonly asked during interviews.

We encourage all attendees with an interest in applying for jobs in Japan to check out the panel! Of course, some aspects will also be applicable to those searching for jobs in other countries as well.
takeaway:
・Broad stroke difference between Japan and North America during the hiring process
・Common evaluation criteria for portfolios in Japan
・Advice for getting hired in Japan, from frequently encountered interview questions to ways to improve your chances
intended audience:
・Aspiring game developers who like Japanese media
・Game creators or students who wish to work in Japan
speakers: Taichiro Miyazaki(CyberConnect2 Co.,Ltd.), Mimmy Shen(CyberConnect2 Co.,Ltd.)

session title: Grappling with Success: Smooth Movement on an Indie Budget
description: Great traversal doesn't require a AAA team, just intentional design and clever use of physics. In this talk, Salaar breaks down the core principles behind responsive, satisfying movement in indie games. Learn essentials like input buffering, defining move sets and metrics, leveraging physics for movement and animation, and building mechanics like grapples, wallruns, dashes, and jetpacks that feel great from input to pixel. Whether you're building a tight platformer, an expressive FPS, or just want your character to feel good to control, this talk offers concrete that scale to small teams and limited budgets. Perfect for indie devs looking to make movement a core part of their game's identity.
takeaway: Attendees gain practical techniques for designing responsive, satisfying movement in indie games. Salaar covers input buffering, movement metrics, and physics-driven traversal, showing how to build and tune mechanics like grapples, wallruns, dashes, and jetpacks, even with limited resources. Ideal for teams making movement a core pillar of gameplay.
intended audience: This session is for designers and engineers building games where movement is a core pillar. While tailored for small teams, the principles apply to projects of any scale. Attendees should have a basic understanding of game development and algebra.

session title: How 'Ghost of Yōtei' Was Pre-Mixed with the Help of a Sound Sensei
description: This session will showcase how automating asset integration can enable control over the final mix early on in development. Trying to maintain a mix during a game's development is tedious and time consuming work and can be problematic during the final mix if not done correctly. For Ghost of Yotei, Sucker Punch's Audio team decided they would try to solve these problems by creating a tool called Sound Sensei, which would be responsible for maintaining proper asset standards while fitting seamlessly between asset rendering and implementation. We will go over how Sound Sensei works for setting up mix rules, as well as how those rules get automatically applied to the corresponding assets non-destructively, and how this allows for adjusting the mix at any point during development without needing to reopen a DAW.
takeaway: Our hope is that audience members will come away inspired with insight into how automated processes, standardization and internal tool development can dramatically simplify workflow and elevate a project's sonic cohesion.
intended audience: The intended audience would be anyone interested in developing workflow improvements or designing audio systems at a AAA scale. Or anyone who would like to learn about the different considerations and tradeoffs the audio team must make during game development.

session title: Monetization Systems That Work for Players and Studios
description: Monetization is often seen as a business necessity, but in this session, it is treated as a game design system. Drawing from over a decade leading monetization and live operations on The Elder Scrolls Online, and now as Co-Founder and CEO of Sackbird Studios, Lee Ridout shares a principle-driven approach to building revenue systems that scale without eroding player trust. The talk covers practical frameworks for pacing content, structuring store portfolios, using scarcity responsibly, and measuring success beyond short-term revenue — all while keeping the player experience at the center.
takeaway: Attendees gain actionable strategies for pacing monetization content, managing product portfolios, and communicating across disciplines. Drawing from The Elder Scrolls Online, Lee Ridout shares tested frameworks for designing player-respectful stores, using scarcity without manipulation, and measuring success beyond revenue—tools developers can immediately apply to live service systems.
intended audience: This session is for game designers, product managers, live ops leads, and interested developers working on monetized games. Attendees should have a basic understanding of live service systems. Those seeking to align monetization with design values and improve collaboration across disciplines will find practical, immediately applicable insights.

session title: Supporting the Games Ecosystem
description: A community focused session for people who run non profit game support services, game developers interested in charitable gaming initiatives and collaborative studio models, and anyone who wants to help shape a more accessible games industry. Run by Wee Tattie Studios.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: Experimental Games Showcase Demos
description:
**Join us to demo games from the Experimental Games Showcase LIVE!**

**This is your chance for hands-on game play and the opportunity to talk to the developers that made the games. **

The Experimental Game Showcase, which featured games like 'Baba Is You', 'The Longing', 'Braid', 'Untitled Goose Game' and 'Katamari Damacy', is back for its 24th year at GDC! In this fast-paced, game-packed session, panelists showcase a selection of surprising and intriguing prototypes made by innovation-minded game developers from all over the world. By demonstrating games that defy conventions and traditions in search of new genres and ideas, the Experimental Game Showcase aims to ignite the imagination of all game makers. Come see what's happening in the world of Experimental Games, and be inspired!
speakers: Stefano Sanitate(Santa Ragione), Ben Landon Elliot(Bonk Interactive), Hatim Benhsain(Games for my Computer LLC), Hanwen Xu(Games for my Computer LLC), Tommy Maranges(Independent), Cory O'Brien(Independent), Mark LaCroix(Noble Robot), Bjarke Alexander Larsen(Independent), Cí Malone(The Crayon Eating Company), Liz Ryerson(Independent), Andrea Lucco Borlera(Independent), Carol Mertz(Independent), Tristan Hilaire(KMLO), Nicole Orabona(cirqueSaw), Nathan Leigh(cirqueSaw), Isaac Cohen(Cabbibo), Laureline Chiapello(NAD-UQAC (Université du Québec à Chicoutimi)), Allen Riley(Hypnogenesis), Andy Wallace(Hypnogenesis)

session title: Game AI Roundtable Day 3
description:
The Game AI Roundtable is organized into three sessions, with each session having its own focus.

Session 3 focuses on machine learning and generative AI, including LLMs. Discussion will cover current uses, challenges, ethics, and the evolving role of these technologies in games.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a clearer understanding of current game AI practices, from established techniques to emerging ML and LLM approaches. Each session offers a different perspective, with day 3 looking forward towards the future.
intended audience: Day 3 is targeted towards those who are working with or interested in Machine Learning and large language models.

session title: Leveling Up Kids' Privacy: Navigating Global Requirements (Presented by the IGDA)
description: In Children and Safety: An Ever Changing Landscape, we will discuss the rapidly shifting online social environment and protections that exist for children and vulnerable people of all ages. Participants share and discuss children's safety strategies in legal considerations to design/UX strategies to technical tools, moderation practices and more with real world examples and a closing exercise. Attendees are encouraged to share personal experiences or insights related to moderation and design with online safety in mind.
takeaway: There is a ton of regulatory movement here, but a 'wait and see' approach to compliance is extremely dangerous given how aggressive laws and regulators are becoming. Games will have to adapt to survive, but they will need to be smart about which battles to pick -- staying compliant while still respecting user privacy and minimizing friction.
intended audience: Product manager, business owners, legal professionals, compliance officers

session title: Tool Design Roundtable Day 3
description:
For day 3:
This Tool Design roundtable is about the UX of in-house and external toolsets. Come and meet, discuss, and find out what everyone has been doing with Tool Design in the last year.
Examples of topics are:
1. What user research methods do you use?
2. Do you have a dedicated user researcher on your team?
3. What surprises has your research uncovered in the last year?
takeaway: Attendees will share their experiences, challenges, and successes with tool design. They can expect to leave with a list of actionable methodologies, and real world experiences from other teams, that your team can adopt and learn from to improve in-house tool design.
intended audience: This roundtable is intended for producers, tool programmers, tool designers, and technical artists who wish to discuss and improve the efficiency of in-house tool design.

session title: Working with Stakeholders Roundtable: Peer Mentorship
description:
Working with stakeholders, especially difficult ones, can be a challenge for the field of UX. There are many factors that come into play - underappreciation of the expertise, business needs, deprioritization of accessibility design, time constraints, upper leadership demands, lack of UX maturity, etc. What methods have worked well for you when having to work with stakeholders unfamiliar with UX? What are some ways you've been able to grow UX maturity for your team?

This roundtable presents an opportunity for UX individuals, who are not in leadership positions, to bring their stakeholder & process challenges to ask the group for peer mentorship advice. As well, this space is open to those who'd like to share their stakeholder success stories.

session title: Designing ARGs to Foster Meaningful Community Connection
description: How can an alternate reality game do more than entertain and instead create genuine connection between players? This session examines how to design ARGs that encourage collaboration, conversation, and shared discovery across physical and digital spaces. The speaker outlines practical approaches to structuring multi-layer puzzle arcs, integrating narrative with real-world environments, and designing roleplay that invites meaningful interaction rather than passive consumption.
speakers: No speakers found for this session

session title: IGDA Annual General Meeting
description: The International Game Developers Association's Board of Directors and Executive Director provides developers with a summary of the association's progress over the past year, as well as an overview of what's in store for 2026. There will also be a Q&A period to get feedback and input from the community and a chance to meet the new board members. The IGDA is a non-profit professional association committed to advancing the careers and enhancing the lives of game developers worldwide.
speakers: Jakin Vela(International Game Developers Association), Parker Wallace(International Game Developer Association)

session title: Level Design Portfolio Review
description:
This session is a live, interactive portfolio review focused on practical and actionable feedback. Selected attendee submissions will be reviewed in real time by experienced level designers with hiring and production experience across AAA and indie development.
The review will focus on clarity of design thinking, communication of ownership, case study structure, and overall presentation effectiveness. Attendees will gain insight into how portfolios are evaluated during actual hiring discussions, including what stands out positively and what raises concerns.
Rather than theoretical advice, this session provides a transparent look at how design portfolios are assessed in practice. Participants will leave with a clearer understanding of how to strengthen their own work and better communicate their readiness for professional opportunities.
takeaway: Attendees will gain insight from live portfolio critiques conducted by experienced designers with hiring and production backgrounds. Through real examples and structured feedback, participants will see how portfolios are evaluated in practice, what strengthens an application, and what weakens it. The session offers actionable observations that designers can immediately apply to improve their own portfolios.
intended audience: This portfolio review session is intended for aspiring and early-career level designers seeking direct, practical feedback on their work. It is especially valuable for students, junior designers, and career switchers preparing to apply for roles and looking to better understand how their portfolios are perceived by hiring teams.

session title: Player Hearing Safety: Accessibility's Close Cousin
description:
Recent studies have shown that frequent gamers play with headphones more than 50% of the time. While increasing immersion (which is what we try to do!), the prolonged use of headphones at high volumes is a significant contributor to hearing damage.
There are things we as game developers can do to help mitigate impact on players' hearing health, and it turns out many are small tweaks to things we currently do for player accessibility. Accessibility and player sensory health are close cousins, from a developer perspective.
This talk will look at some the basics of hearing safety and go over the work the World Health Organization has done in hearing safety and health in esports and video games. We will also show how incorporating simple recommendations for game software and systems can have an impact on our players' hearing health, and how your game may be doing something very similar already.
takeaway: Attendees will come away with better understanding of the risks of hearing damage for our players, and how many mitigation efforts are extremely close to many things we as an industry are already doing for accessibility. Attendees will also receive an overview of the recently released, 'Global standard for safe listening video gameplay and esports,' published by the World Health Organization.
intended audience: This talk is for anyone involved in or interested in games accessibility, player sensory health, as well as people who design, architect or implement systems related to accessibility.

session title: Press Startup: How to Raise Serious Funds, Ship Killer Products and Build Lasting Value like a Tech Business
description:
Why do trending tech companies seem to attract extravagant funding where viable games studios can struggle? Does the tech world really know anything we don't and - if so - can we steal their ideas?

This is a unique opportunity to hear from veteran games professionals who have made the treacherous leap into tech, working on everything from world-class distribution platforms to pioneering medical technology.

Find out what the startup ecosystem can teach the games industry about funding, pitching, product management, exit strategies and more. The panel will even probe the most unspeakable question of all: should you consider starting a tech business yourself?
takeaway:
Attendees will gain concrete and practical insight into the business building, funding, and operational strategies used by tech startups.

They will have the opportunity to think about their games business in a broader context, evolving beyond a pure product focus to longer-term concerns.
intended audience: This is a general session suitable for anyone in a commercial or operational role, including indie developers and studio founders. It may be of particular interest to anyone looking to develop a product or service element to their business alongside pure game development.
speakers: Paul Kilduff-Taylor(Mode 7), Ian Hardingham(Oxford Brain Diagnostics), Gina Jackson(Pitchify), John Graham(Elbow Grease Games), Oscar Clark(Arcanix)

session title: Writing Games for a Distracted, Polarized, Media-Illiterate World
description: In a media landscape shaped by algorithms, distraction, and polarization, can writers still craft stories that require critical thinking? This talk argues not only that they can, but that they must. We'll explore how trusting the player's intelligence and rewarding deep engagement are essential for pushing our medium forward, and how these types of narratives survive studio politics and market pressures. Drawing from projects ranging from Call of Duty to Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, this talk examines the state of media literacy, the power of subtext, and the role of discourse in a game's legacy. Attendees will leave with a renewed understanding of how to create meaningful, resonant work without losing their jobs in the process.
takeaway: Attendees will gain tools for using 'narrative subterfuge' to create layered, thoughtful narratives in a fractured media environment. They'll learn how to advocate for deeper storytelling, push back on flattening feedback, and survive the internal politics that often hinder ambitious narrative work.
intended audience: This talk is for writers, narrative designers, and creative leads who care about the cultural impact of their work. No advanced technical knowledge is required, but attendees should have experience with game development pipelines and a desire to challenge default storytelling assumptions in today's attention economy.

session title: Canceled: Animating Vi for '2XKO'
description:
In this session, David Helsby, Principal Animator on 2XKO, shares how Riot reimagined Vi's animation for a new gameplay context while preserving her iconic personality and presence. Attendees will learn how gameplay timing, stylized posing, and hybrid 2D/3D techniques were used to make Vi feel punchy, responsive, and authentic in a fast-paced tag fighter. The talk explores key animation decisions around her glove proportions, attack cadence, and emotional expression—along with examples of what didn't work and how the team aligned across disciplines to make hard calls. David also dives into the creative and technical challenges of adapting a beloved character with an existing fanbase to a new format, offering lessons for animators working on stylized, gameplay-first characters. This is a focused, production-grounded talk aimed at animators, tech artists, and developers interested in the intersection of expressive character work and responsive gameplay systems.

In this session, David Helsby, Principal Animator on 2XKO, shares how Riot reimagined Vi's animation for a new gameplay context while preserving her iconic personality and presence. Attendees will learn how gameplay timing, stylized posing, and hybrid 2D/3D techniques were used to make Vi feel punchy, responsive, and authentic in a fast-paced tag fighter. The talk explores key animation decisions around character design, animation execution, and emotional expression—along with examples of what didn't work and how the team aligned across disciplines to make hard calls. David also dives into the creative and technical challenges of adapting a beloved character with an existing fanbase to a new format, offering lessons for animators working on stylized, gameplay-first characters. This is a focused, production-grounded talk aimed at animators, tech artists, and developers interested in the intersection of expressive character work and responsive gameplay systems.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how Vi was animated in 2XKO to maximize gameplay responsiveness and impact, blending 2D and 3D techniques in a unique rendering style. The talk explores how iconic Vi elements were adapted, what failed in early iterations, and how team collaboration shaped a stronger final result.
intended audience: This session is ideal for gameplay animators, tech artists, and developers working with stylized characters. Attendees should have a working understanding of animation principles and real-time implementation. Those familiar with cross-discipline production or adapting legacy characters will benefit most from the production insights and animation-focused case study.

session title: Canceled: Art Production Pipeline in 2026 and Beyond Roundtable (Presented by the IGDA)
description: In this fifth edition of the Game Art SIG's GDC Roundtable, we bring together professional developers, educators, and students to discuss the rapidly evolving landscape of game art production. Alongside the hot topic of A.I. and its impact on creative processes, careers, and markets, the roundtable invites participants to reflect on their evolving art production pipelines—mapping emerging artistic trends and sharing effective strategies to stay ahead with the latest creative tools.
takeaway: Attendees will gain inspiring insights from artists working across AAA and indie studios. The discussion will help identify areas for personal development, legal awareness, and A.I. literacy—while sparking new ideas and professional connections.
intended audience: This roundtable is aimed at developers working in design, concept art, and other areas of game art production. It is equally relevant for students, newcomers to the industry, and seasoned professionals.

session title: Canceled: Automating 'Marathon': How Bungie Went from 10 Automated Tests to Over 100 New, Reliable Tests to Deliver a High-Quality, Stable, Live-Service Game
description: This presentation provides a brief history of client automation at Bungie, from Destiny to Marathon. We give an in-depth view of the 'blackboards' system leveraged to improve our ability to verify data at runtime and write automation for feature areas of Marathon, expanding our coverage from 10 to over 100 automation tests. Demonstrate how using a simple state machine can provide a strong foundation for stability and leverage that for hardware evaluation. Finally, we discuss how we're using automation to speed up development of Marathon as a live-service game through regression testing and how we're working to improve communication between QA, engineering, and design teams.
takeaway: Attendees to this presentation will learn how vitally important it is to incorporate automation into a live-service game as early as possible. Specifically automation that can verify data at runtime for feature area verification or use simple state machines emulating player behavior.
intended audience: The target audience for this presentation are QA engineers, game/client engineers, and QA leads.

session title: Canceled: Behind the Scenes: The Making of a Gameplay Trailer
description:
This presentation invites attendees to step into the shoes of a 'Gameplay Capture' team, experiencing the game trailer creation process from marketing brief to final delivery with opportunities for audience collaboration at key points in the talk.

Attendees will be shown capture session footage and other real documents and clips demonstrating how a Capture Team is structured, its typical processes and some of the wider trailer workflows and collaboration required to create compelling gameplay sequences live in game while navigating technical constraints and rapidly evolving game content.

We'll look at breaking down the marketing brief, creative ideation, and the iteration, tools and processes that go into individual shots and sequences during trailer production. The session showcases game capture as the specialized discipline it is, combining creative visual storytelling and technical problem-solving with cinematography and gameplay, exposing attendees to the wide variety of techniques used in Gameplay Capture.
takeaway: Attendees learn the systematic approach and methodologies behind game trailer production from initial content research and marketing briefs to the finished shot, getting an in-depth understanding of how game capture teams navigate technical constraints while filming live inside an in-development game.
intended audience: This session targets marketing and creative professionals working with gameplay trailers, developers curious about the capture process, and individuals interested in game capture careers or virtual filmmaking workflows. Content creators, cinematographers, and teams collaborating with game capture agencies will also benefit. No prerequisite knowledge is required.

session title: Canceled: Camera Deobscura: Building a Flexible Camera System
description:
Video game cameras are notoriously difficult to implement well, but are crucial to get right. Having a solid foundation for a camera system may not guarantee that your game will receive rave reviews for its cameras, but it's an important starting point. This talk will walk through the architecture and implementation details necessary for building a camera system that is capable of supporting various types of cameras, applicable across a variety of game genres.

Specific topics include view representation, blending methods, priority handling, designer-friendly content authoring, spatial and temporal interpolation, value smoothing, and debugging tools.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to build a camera system that can support various types of games, is friendly to designers, and produces a smooth result for players.
intended audience: Programmers with a basic knowledge of video game / vector math, who are interested in learning about the engineering of cameras.

session title: Canceled: Cinematic on a Shoestring: The Technical Design Behind 'Mouthwashing'
description: In this session, Jeffrey Tomec explores the technical side of building a cinematic narrative experience around a writer's vision. He breaks down Mouthwashing's 'film-first' development workflow, where scenes are rapidly built from a screenplay. Multiple scenes from the game are dissected to showcase the tools and techniques used throughout development. He also vents about how painful the dialog system is to work with—and why you should make a better one.
takeaway: This session explores how Wrong Organ transforms screenplays into interactive scenes while preserving a writer's cinematic intent. Attendees gain insight into tools and workflows that balance reusability and one-off solutions, including branching logic systems, cutscene tools, and examples like Mouthwashing's bespoke enemy AIs and dialog pipeline failures.
intended audience: This presentation is targeted towards indie developers who are interested in the more systemic side of creating narrative experiences. Having some programming or technical experience is recommended but not necessary. Though some Unity-specific terms will be used, most of the concepts in this talk are engine-agnostic.

session title: Canceled: Connection and Creativity at Scale: Inside King's 1,000-Person Game Jam
description:
Go behind-the-scenes of King's company-wide Feature Jam - an AI-powered event that brought together over 1,000 employees across disciplines and countries to prototype new in-game features. From junior talent to the company's senior leadership, everyone got involved, showing how AI can be democratized in a fun way.

Designed to lower barriers between technical and non-technical roles, the Jam paired AI tools with expert guidance, enabling all to participate meaningfully. The result was a pipeline of playable prototypes with real business potential to kickstart discovery for future features in King's biggest titles.

This session explores how AI can serve as both a force multiplier for organizers and a creative co-pilot for teams - raising the ceiling of what could be built in just four hours. You'll learn what worked, what didn't, and how to design your own inclusive, high-impact, AI-powered innovation events at scale.
takeaway: Discover how to run large-scale innovation events that are inclusive, engaging and high-impact. Learn how AI can make innovation events more accessible and impactful - lowering barriers, increasing engagement, and turning creativity into real discovery - whether you're planning your own Jam or sparking team-wide collaboration.
intended audience: This session is for anyone interested or involved in the planning, hosting, or running innovation events - whether you're a seasoned facilitator or simply curious about the power of Game Jams to drive innovation, connection, and engagement across diverse teams.

session title: Canceled: Designing Content Creator Campaigns That Don't Burn Out at Launch
description:
In this session, Lurkit's CMXO breaks down how to build and execute full-lifecycle content creator strategies that drive sustained success across pre-launch, launch, and post-launch phases. Using Dune: Awakening, Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, and Ready or Not (Console) as case studies, the talk showcases how Lurkit deconstructs games to identify the right creator archetypes and audiences—sometimes uncovering surprising fits, like the synergy between DayZ creators and Dune.

Attendees will learn how to negotiate, onboard, and manage both organic and paid creators, ensure high compliance and engagement, and prevent campaign pitfalls like gray market reselling or inflated viewership.

The talk also covers how to use creator programs and scalable missions to maintain long-term buzz post-launch. This is a hands-on, practical look at what makes creator campaigns work—and how to build creator pipelines that last.
takeaway: Attendees learn how to deconstruct games to identify ideal creators, manage large-scale organic and paid campaigns, prevent key misuse and fake viewership, and use creator programs to sustain post-launch engagement—drawing on real-world examples from Dune: Awakening, Ready or Not, and Clair Obscur: Expedition 33.
intended audience: This session is intended for marketing professionals, influencer managers, community leads, and publishing teams looking to build or optimize creator marketing strategies.

session title: Canceled: Don't Ship the Wrong Game!
description: Shipping a good game that no one desires is a sad affair. It's also easy to accidentally make a production too large. This session will share how we avoid this by building many prototypes, evaluating them against real people and then go into an appropriately scoped production - staying happy devs in rough times. Maybe most importantly, we will share how we validate prototypes to find the games with potential among the crowd, as it is simply not something predictable or plannable.
takeaway:
You get strong pointers for creating prototypes quickly and especially for validating a prototype for its chances on the market.
You'll see three typical cases of how a project ends up going wrong (undesired, misaligned, overdeveloped), and how you can avoid them.
Ideally, you will come out with a solid plan on how to quickly spot if a game might not end up being successful, so you can kill it and focus your time on one that shows promise.
intended audience: Anyone who is in some way responsible for what game they make or have a strong influence on the development process (prototyping, scoping, production, direction).

session title: Canceled: Every Indie Funding Opportunity
description: This session condenses insights from dozens of game industry veterans on their experiences across hundreds of funded games into one rapid-fire review of every known indie funding opportunity. This session will cover the most relevant examples for various funding opportunities, the pros & cons of each, and how to decide which options are most (and least) viable and worth pursuing for your situation. Come hear familiar opportunities paired with fresh insights, discover new and potentially off-the-wall options you'd never have thought of, and leave with a couple of galaxy brain funding ideas of your own. You'll be sure to learn something new about game development funding options, whether you're funding your 1st project or your 15th.
takeaway: Attendees will gain practical insights on how to identify the best funding options available to them at a time when it feels like all the funding opportunities have dried up.
intended audience: This session is for any indie dev who is looking for funding, as well as those who want to better understand what funding options are available from $3K to $30M. No prior experience in funding a studio or project is necessary, but a passing familiarity with business development is helpful, and even those experienced in BD will walk away with new ideas for funding their projects.
speakers: Trent Kusters(League of Geeks), Sunni Pavlovic(Windflower Games)

session title: Canceled: High-Performance Gameplay Networking for 'Marathon'
description: We will present the gameplay networking techniques that allow us to deliver high-precision, responsive player movement, actions and shooting mechanics in Bungie's upcoming extraction shooter, Marathon. We will cover our dedicated server model, how clients can predict actions for responsiveness, and how to validate that cheating clients cannot compromise the security of the game world. Specific techniques will be presented for physics synchronization, weapon-fire networking and network layer security.
takeaway: Attendees will learn techniques for networked gameplay that can be applied to make the player experience smooth, resilient and secure in action games developed in any engine.
intended audience: Engineers that work on networked gameplay in action games.

session title: Canceled: Preserving Play: How the Wordle, Connections & Strand Archives Fuel Ritual and Connection in NYT Games
description: The Wordle and Connections archives in the NYT Games app do more than store past puzzles: they help achieve badges, spark group chats, and build rituals. In this talk, Android engineer Vanessa Johnson shares how the native layer of the NYT Games app supports discoverability, accessibility, and shareability in hybrid rendered games. From calendar style puzzle recovery to badge achievements and social links, attendees will see how archive design plays a central role in engagement. This session breaks down UI patterns that encourage return play, enhance accessibility, and let players relive past moments whether they're solo streakers or part of a daily Wordle group chat.
takeaway: Attendees learn how Wordle and Connections archives drive engagement through shareable content and accessible navigation to past puzzles. They'll leave with UI patterns, notification strategies, and lightweight social features they can apply to any game with daily or repeatable content.
intended audience: This session is for UX designers, mobile engineers, and producers building games with daily puzzles, trivia, or content driven data. No advanced technical knowledge is needed. The talk includes engineering insight from an Android perspective but all design patterns apply across platforms.

session title: Canceled: Restarting from Zero: A Message to Creators Considering Independence
description:
As the game industry continues to undergo major restructuring, many creators may be contemplating independence. To mark the 10th anniversary of KOJIMA PRODUCTIONS, founder Hideo Kojima will reflect on the studio's journey and share his insights into building a studio, developing projects, establishing a brand, and connecting with fans, all from the perspective of an independent creator.

Attend this official Festival Keynote taking place Thursday, March 12, 2026 at 9-9:50am on GDC Main Stage. Early access to keynote seating for Game Changer passholders begins at 8:15am. Doors open to all festival attendees at 8:30am. We recommend getting there early to secure seats for you and your GDC crew. Your buddies will thank you later!

session title: Canceled: Riding the Current: Leveraging Today's Discoverability Trends
description: Oversaturation and lack of marketing funding to break through the noise are largely blamed for why games don't find massive or sometimes any commercial success. Over 50 games are released on Steam every day and publisher options are limited. Elaborate go-to-market strategies aren't paying off as often as they used to. But games are still in fact being discovered. So what else is missing? This talk aims to break away from the collective fear that commercial failures are dictated by lack of discoverability and dive into problem areas that can be addressed and actioned on in new projects. Topics include modern market trends, how game discoverability has evolved, and how this knowledge can be leveraged to tackle the bigger issues: product and audience capture.
takeaway: Attendees will gain a high-level understanding of current market expectations and positive visibility trends to redirect marketing efforts from improving game discoverability to product validation and audience capture optimization. The talk will highlight key areas of opportunity and include supporting research and case study references.
intended audience: This discussion is mainly geared towards indie dev teams releasing games on Steam/PC at a minimum, particularly early in development, seeking additional avenues to fine-tune their GTM strategies.

session title: Canceled: Sones: A Better Measurement for Game Audio
description: This session introduces a new method for measuring perceived loudness in games using the Sones method. Traditional approaches such as LUFS and RMS have significant limitations in measuring how loudness is perceived in game audio contexts. The presenters will introduce the algorithmic basis for the Sones approach, demonstrate its application with a prototype program, and explore its potential to improve loudness normalization and audio consistency in gameplay. The session will include a comparative demo, and discuss real-world implementation challenges.
takeaway: Attendees will learn how to evaluate loudness with greater perceptual accuracy and understand the value of adopting the Sones method for game audio analysis or implementation.
intended audience: Audio designers, mix engineers, and tech audio programmers working on dynamic or complex layered mixes.
speakers: Akshay Balakrishnan(Interleave Inc.), Josh Osiris(Interleave Inc.)

session title: Canceled: Sound Design x AI: Honing Your Craft While Navigating Emerging Technologies
description:
What does it mean to be a Sound Designer in the age of artificial intelligence (AI)? What role can Sound Designers uniquely play? This session examines AI tools and technologies through the lens of sound design, and how attendees may be able to apply their skills and hone their craft as Sound Designers when working with AI.

Session topics include an overview of AI audio technologies with specific utility for sound design and a discussion of common audio tools that utilize AI. The presentation will also dive into VR case studies where audio intersected with AI, and discuss development challenges and creative solutions that improved overall audio quality.
takeaway: Learn about audio technologies and tools that utilize AI and how they may positively impact your sound design workflows. Everyone from professional Sound Designers to non-Audio people can learn how AI may be applied to prototyping, analysis, extending functionality, workflow acceleration, and more.
intended audience: This session is intended for all levels and disciplines, with particular focus toward Sound Designers, Audio Programmers, Audio Directors, Software Engineers, creators, and technology enthusiasts. Prior knowledge of AI is not required, and the presentation aims to provide all attendees with a baseline foundation for understanding the content.

session title: Canceled: Working Abroad in Gaming
description: As game development becomes increasingly global, more professionals are relocating across borders in pursuit of opportunity, growth, and new experiences. However, the realities of working abroad are rarely discussed openly, such as navigating visas, adapting to new work cultures, and rebuilding community. This panel brings together developers who have lived and worked internationally to share candid stories and practical insights about the personal and professional impact of relocation. Moderated by a developer who has personally relocated for work, the session will foster open discussion around the challenges and rewards of working internationally. Whether you're considering your first role abroad, have already made the leap, or are hiring across borders, this panel offers an opportunity to learn from peers, exchange resources, and gain a deeper understanding of what it means to build a truly global career in game development.
takeaway: Attendees gain practical insights into building a game development career abroad, learning how to navigate visas, relocation logistics, and cultural adaptation. They hear real-world experiences from international developers and leave with actionable advice, shared resources, and new connections to support their own global career journeys.
intended audience: This panel is for developers considering or currently working abroad, as well as hiring managers and team leads who support international staff. No prior experience or background knowledge is required - just an interest in global mobility, collaboration, and building a game development career across borders. All disciplines and experience levels are welcome.
speakers: Lianne Lardizabal(Riot Games), Chris Tom(Riot Games), Yao Wen Yeo(Riot Games), Bruno Carlucci(Riot Games), Willy Koswara(Riot Games), Louis Carl Pepin(Riot Games)
